[Page 7]
This book is dedicated to so many school teachers, university professors,
colleagues, collaborators, students, friends and family members who have taught
me not only the secrets of science, and in particular of nanolithography, but also
the secrets of a full life. And especially to those who like spending their time
around me: my wife Anabel, my children Sofía and Isaac, my parents Cándida
and José María and my sister Helena, given that the long hours devoted to this
book have been mostly taken from our time for common recreation.

Appendix A
A-1
Nanofabrication
xiv

[Page 17]
Preface
Nowadays, researchers are frequently requested to contribute to journals and books,
and, unfortunately, we have to decline most of these invitations due to the lack of time
to take additional tasks. But, in this case, when IOP approached me to make a proposal
on a book about nanolithography, I realized that this time it could be different. It should
be different. For more than 15 years, I have been teaching nanotechnology and
nanolithography in postgraduate courses and summer schools. And I have always been
faced by the same question: can I select a single book on nanolithography to allow the
students to go deeper in the topic? A Master or PhD student following such courses has
generally many tasks to do and cannot afford to spend much time on bibliography.
However, the situation is such that most books on nanolithography go very deep into
some particular technique but do not show the global picture. This can be very
frustrating for the student, who does not wish to know all the details of a particular
technique but understand the fundamentals of the main nanolithography techniques
and know their main applications. Some review articles have addressed this global
picture, but these are normally limited to a few tens of pages at most, thus providing the
ﬂavour of the topic but without providing a solid view of it. With this background in
mind, I proposed to IOP that I should edit such a missing book.
The book covers in great detail the following nanolithography techniques: optical
lithography (chapter 2), electron beam lithography (chapter 3), focused electron beam
induced deposition (chapter 4), focused ion beam and focused ion beam induced
deposition (chapter 5), scanning probe lithography (chapter 6), stamp lithography
(chapter 7), stencil lithography (chapter 8) and ice lithography (chapter 9). From the
beginning, I proposed that all the co-authors should address in their chapters not only
the techniques but also the main applications. In my opinion, it is not pretentious to
state that nanolithography currently inﬁltrates most of the existing technology by
enabling the digital technology. Hiding behind the scenes, nanolithography allows
building the integrated circuits that rule (overrule?) our daily life. Apart from this
classical application of nanolithography, the reader will discover other applications in
a material’s nanopatterning, device prototyping, nano-optics and plasmonics, nano-
superconductivity, nanomagnetism, electrical nanocontacting, nanosensors, energy
storage and conversion, as well as in new exciting ﬁelds like biology, medicine,
robotics and neuroscience. The last three chapters of the book are in fact devoted to two
speciﬁc applications, which are thoroughly discussed: magnetic nanostructures (chapters
10 and 11) and two-dimensional materials (chapter 12). Chapter 3 also addresses in
great detail the application of electron beam lithography to two-dimensional materials.
In summary, I hope that the book will provide the reader with the key fundaments
of the main nanolithography techniques and their applications, and, at the same time,
it will open the reader’s mind towards recent developments and emergent applications
in the ﬁeld. Last, but not least, each chapter provides a student’s corner, with some
questions and exercises as well as further hints to go deeper in the topics. In an
appendix at the end of the book, the reader will ﬁnd the responses to the questions and
exercises.
xv

[Page 18]
Acknowledgements
First, I want to acknowledge the enthusiastic collaboration of all the book’s co-
authors, who made this project happen and helped to put together an immense
amount of knowledge in the ﬁeld of nanolithography with the hope that the book
will be useful to students and colleagues. By choosing contributors who lead their
respective research ﬁelds, each chapter is in its own right a precious piece of work.
Their collection is intended to constitute the reference book on nanolithography for
the upcoming generations of Master and PhD students as well as for researchers
seeking a comprehensive view of the ﬁeld. Thank you, my dear colleagues and
friends for your involvement!
Secondly, I want to express my gratitude to the colleagues from the Spanish
network on Nanolithography (NANOLITO) for sharing their knowledge on the
topic for the last 12 years in the course of summer schools and workshops.
Finally, I would like to highlight the managers, colleagues, technicians and
students working at the Laboratory of Advanced Microscopies (University of
Zaragoza, Spain). Thanks to their hard work, the lab is currently a reference place
for nanolithography and also this book will see the light.
xvi

[Page 19]
Editor biography
José Maria De Teresa
Physicist José María De Teresa is research professor at the Institute of
Nanoscience and Materials of Aragon (INMA, CSIC-University
of Zaragoza, Spain). After obtaining a PhD degree from University
of Zaragoza in 1997 under supervision of Professor del Moral and
Professor Ibarra, he carried out postdoctoral stays at IFW (Dresden,
Germany) and CNRS (Paris, France), where he worked under the
guidance of Professor Albert Fert, Nobel prize winner in Physics in
2007. During this postdoctoral period, he learnt to use lithography techniques, which
he later implemented in his current institute, where he leads the group of
Nanofabrication and Advanced Microscopies (NANOMIDAS). He coordinates the
Spanish network on Nanolithography (NANOLITO) and the FIB-SEM area in the
Spanish National facility for Advanced Microscopies. From 1st January 2021, he will
chair the Condensed Matter Division (CMD) Board in the European Physical Society
(EPS). His main research interests are nanofabrication with focused electron and ion
beams and its application to nanoelectronics, magnetic materials, nano-superconduc-
tors and new materials. He has published more than 200 research articles and given
about 100 invited conferences.

Jose´ Marı´a De Teresa
Chapter 1
Introduction to nanolithography techniques and
their applications
José María De Teresa
Nanolithography encompasses the set of top-down fabrication techniques that allow
patterning materials and building devices with nanoscale resolution. Nanolithography
is supplemented by thin-ﬁlm-deposition techniques as well as self-assembly and self-
organization to provide an immense variety of nanofabrication strategies. In this
chapter, the key concepts underlying nanolithography will be described and the main
nanolithography techniques will be introduced and compared in terms of the
achievable resolution, throughput, pattern complexity, process complexity and cost.
The main applications of nanolithography will be subsequently reviewed, with an
emphasis on the overwhelming role of nanolithography in the semiconductor industry,
in nanotechnology and in emergent applications.
1.1 Key concepts in nanolithography
1.1.1 Top-down versus self-assembly and self-organization processes
Nature has generally opted for self-assembly and self-organization processes to build
complex structures, with the fabrication of proteins from amino acids and peptide
bonds as one illustrative example. This is generally known as the bottom-up
approach. On the other hand, when faced with the problem of building artiﬁcial
structures, our species (and other species not far from us in the evolutionary chain)
relies either on combining building blocks that are smaller than the targeted
structure or on extracting it from a larger structure, which is known as the top-
down approach. One example of the former case is the use of bricks to build a house
and one example of the latter case is a Greek sculpture modeled from a marble
block. The bottom-up versus top-down dichotomy also comes up when processes
need to be designed in order to produce small-sized materials and build small
devices.
doi:10.1088/978-0-7503-2608-7ch1
1-1
ª IOP Publishing Ltd 2020. All rights,
including for text and data mining (TDM), artificial intelligence (AI) training, and similar technologies, are reserved.

[Page 23]
In ﬁgure 1.1 an example of the usefulness of the self-assembly and self-
organization bottom-up strategies to synthesize Ag nanowires is shown. First, two
different polymers (PMMA and PS) are mixed in solution and spin coated on a
substrate. Due to speciﬁc interactions amongst these polymers and the substrate
(which will be reviewed in detail in chapter 2), in certain conditions the two types of
polymers tend to form the intertwined patterned displayed in ﬁgure 1.1(a). As can be
identiﬁed in the zoom-in image of that pattern, a local order between both polymers
exists, forming nanowire-like domains with periodicity in the range of 50 nm. This
pattern becomes very useful if, subsequently, Ag is evaporated at room temperature,
which, after landing on the PMMA-PS layer, tends to cluster on top of the PS
polymer due to the lower diffusion mobility of Ag on PS compared to PMMA.
Thus, Ag follows the underlying nanowire-like structure of the polymer scaffold that
extends undisrupted along various microns. However, the formed Ag nanowires
present some unwanted features, as a closer look at ﬁgure 1.1(b) reveals. A
signiﬁcant nanowire roughness is observed, as well as nanowire interruption at
certain points and deviation from straight shape, all of which are commonly sources
of deterioration of the physical properties of the nanowires. In addition, the long-
range order of the array of nanowires fabricated following this strategy is not
Figure 1.1. (a) Spin-coated ﬁlm of PMMA-PS block copolymers, with 50 nm periodicity. (b) Scanning electron
microscopy micrograph after evaporating Ag on the ﬁlm shown in (a). Due to diffusion effects, the Ag atoms
tend to cluster on top of the PS polymer, forming self-assembled Ag nanowires. Reprinted and adapted by
permission from Springer [1]. Copyright (2001).
Nanofabrication
1-2

[Page 24]
achieved, which is necessary in certain applications such as those in the semi-
conductor industry.
Top-down approaches, which represent most of the content of the present book,
aim to develop strategies and processes leading to the fabrication of artiﬁcial
structures with ﬁne control of all dimensions (length, width, height) and other
features such as roughness, edge shape, materials inter-diffusion, etc. One example
of the accuracy achievable with currently existing top-down lithography techniques
is shown in ﬁgure 1.2. In that example, the technique called focused electron beam
induced deposition (FEBID) is used to decompose the Co2(CO)8 precursor material
by means of a scanning electron microscope (SEM), forming arrays of cobalt
nanowires with ﬁne control of their periodicity (down to 30 nm). As can be observed
in ﬁgure 1.2, due to the underlying physical mechanisms governing the growth in
FEBID (to be explained in chapter 4), neighbour cobalt nanowires are not isolated
one from another. However, a post-growth Ar+ milling process allows the nanowire
isolation, as shown in the corresponding publication [2].
In general, top-down approaches make use of the synergic use of thin-ﬁlm
deposition techniques (sputtering, molecular beam epitaxy, pulsed laser deposition,
Figure 1.2. Arrays of cobalt nanowires grown by FEBID with ﬁne control of the nanowire inter-distance: 30,
40, and 50 nm. The top-down SEM micrographs of the arrays are shown in (a), (d), and (g), whereas cross-
sectional transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the arrays are shown in low magniﬁcation in (b),
(e), and (h) and in high magniﬁcation in (c), (f), and (i). Reprinted with permission from [2]. Copyright (2014)
American Chemistry Society.
Nanofabrication
1-3

[Page 25]
chemical vapour deposition, thermal evaporation, electrodeposition, etc) and
nanolithography techniques. Two approaches are commonly followed. In the ﬁrst
approach, a thin ﬁlm (or multi-layered ﬁlm) is grown on a substrate, with accurate
control of the layer’s thickness. Depending on the homogeneity of the growth
technique and the growth equipment used, the area of the substrate can reach large
values in the range of tens of cm2 or beyond. In a second step, a lithography
technique is applied to pattern the ﬁlm to micrometric or nanometric lateral
dimensions, removing material on particular locations (to be described in chapters
2 and 3). In the second approach, the nanolithography step is ﬁrst performed on
the bare substrate, which is followed by the growth of the materials. This is the case
of the so-called lift-off technique (to be described in chapter 3). A combination of
both approaches in multi-step processes is commonly needed in order to build
complex devices. Various examples of this will be described throughout the present
book.
Moreover, smart combinations of bottom-up and top-down approaches have
been developed. In the ﬁrst example, an array of metal dots (Au, Cu, Ni) is created
by means of top-down photolithography. These metallic dots act as catalyst centres
for the growth of Si nanowires by means of vapour-liquid-solid (VLS) technique.
Without the use of well-ordered patterned metal dots as catalysts (for instance using
randomly-distributed metallic nanoparticles), the Si nanowires would grow without
preferred direction or determined spatial periodicity. The well-ordered arrays of
vertical Si nanowires shown in ﬁgure 1.3 are well suited for application in photo-
voltaics [3]. In the second example, in a ﬁrst step, electron beam lithography (EBL)
plus immersion in a chemical solution are used to deﬁne long NH2-terminated
channels. When a drop of a solution containing dispersed GaP nanowires is poured
onto the substrate, they preferentially assemble on the NH2-terminated surfaces,
leading to a well-ordered array of GaP nanowires [4].
Figure 1.3. Triangular (left) and square (right) arrays of metal dots patterned by photolithography (top) and
the corresponding arrays of Si nanowires after VLS growth (bottom). Reprinted and adapted by permission
from Springer [3]. Copyright (2010).
Nanofabrication
1-4

[Page 26]
1.1.2 Resolution, cost, throughput and complexity
One of the most critical parameters in nanolithography is the maximum attainable
resolution. However, the resolution will change whether the targeted pattern is dense
or the features are isolated. In general, any nanolithography technique will suffer
from proximity effects and will require speciﬁc strategies in the case of dense
patterns. Conventionally, the resolution of a nanolithography technique is deﬁned as
the minimum distance achievable between two individual isolated or resolved
features. Nevertheless, the resolution is sometimes considered to be the minimum
lateral dimension of an isolated feature fabricated with the corresponding nano-
lithography technique. We inform the reader that throughout this book both
deﬁnitions will be indiscriminately used. Also, we would like to make the reader
aware that the phrase higher resolution or better resolution is commonly used to
express that smaller features can be fabricated. In the next chapters, the speciﬁc
limitations of each technique to achieve a better resolution will be discussed in detail.
However, in some cases the potential of a lithography technique cannot be fully
deployed in the laboratory due to practical limitations. Obviously, one of these
limitations can be the equipment or the process cost. For example, optical
lithography is nowadays capable of the fabrication of structures in the range of
10 nm. However, the required equipment, a stepper, is too expensive to be afforded
by a standard research laboratory. As a consequence, the use of conventional
equipment for optical lithography in a university lab will typically lead to a
resolution of 0.5 μm at best.
The concept of throughput, deﬁned as the capacity of a technique for mass
production, is borrowed from the industrial environment, where the cost per unit is
crucial. If the nanolithography technique allows the production of a large amount of
identical patterns in a short period of time, the technique’s throughput will be high.
The high throughput of a given process is beneﬁcial for its industrial implementa-
tion. From the perspective of throughput, the ideal nanolithography technique
should be able to achieve the simultaneous patterning of as many as features as
possible. In the next chapters, the reader will get detailed examples of techniques
such as optical lithography or nanoimprint lithography, which are well suited for the
fabrication of many features in parallel. However, other techniques such as electron
beam lithography or scanning probe lithography produce features in a sequential
mode, which leads to limited throughput. Whereas throughput is not an important
factor for the development of proof-of-concept devices and prototyping, it becomes
very important for the potential commercialization of new technologies.
The complexity of a lithography technique is generally associated with the
number and difﬁculty of the involved steps. In this book, we use this term very
broadly, encompassing aspects such as the number of processing steps required, the
degree of reproducibility of the process, the required human resources or user skills,
etc. All these aspects need to be considered before choosing the nanolithography
technique to use. It is also important to consider the capability of the technique to
fabricate complex patterns, which vary from one to another. In particular, not all
Nanofabrication
1-5

[Page 27]
nanolithography techniques have the capability to create three-dimensional (3D)
structures.
1.2 Nanolithography techniques using resists
1.2.1 Optical lithography (OL)
In conventional optical lithography (OL), a photoresist (a resist whose solubility in a
developer will change after photon irradiation) is ﬁrst patterned with the same
design of an optical mask. This involves three steps, as shown in ﬁgure 1.4: (1) resist
spin coating, (2) resist exposure and (3) resist development. The next two steps
consist in (4) transferring the pattern from the resist to the sample of interest by
means of an etching process of the unprotected part of the sample and (5) removing
the resist, which leads to achieving the goal of a clean patterned sample. Instead of
being grown before the lithography process starts, the sample of interest can be
grown at an intermediate step (after the resist development), in a process called lift-
off (see chapter 3 for details). Lift-off processes are in general advantageous given
that the etching step is no longer required. However, lift-off cannot be performed if,
for example, the material of interest needs to be grown at high temperatures that
cause the resist to deteriorate.
OL will be described in detail in chapter 2. Here, we will limit the discussion to a
few important points:
• Photoresists are of utmost importance, given that the pattern is ﬁrst trans-
ferred to them and they should endure either the etching process or the
sample growth and lift-off. Thanks to the availability of positive and negative
Figure 1.4. Various steps commonly performed in optical and electron beam lithography techniques: resist coating
(1), resist exposure (2), resist development (3), sample etching (4) and resist removal with the ﬁnal result (5).
Nanofabrication
1-6

[Page 28]
resists, which, respectively, increase or decrease their solubility in the
developer under photon exposure, the overall photolithography process can
be designed to produce either the same pattern present in the mask or the
supplementary one.
• The wavelength of the photon source will determine to a great extent the
maximum resolution of the process. The technology has seen the evolution of
light sources from NUV (near ultraviolet) to DUV (deep ultraviolet) to EUV
(extreme ultraviolet). Typical resolution with laboratory mask aligners is in
the range of 1 μm, whereas steppers target resolution in the 10 nm range.
• OL is well suited for large-area patterning given the availability of appro-
priate photoresists, light sources, lenses and masks to expose a large area of
the resist in a very short period of time (typically a few seconds). In addition,
a high throughput is achievable in OL with industrial equipment called a
stepper. A stepper is very expensive and ﬁne equipment with the capability to
handle the exposure of hundreds of wafers per hour, as required in the
industry of integrated circuits [5].
• Several variants of OL exist and the most important ones will be addressed in
chapter 2. Such related techniques exploit for example the use of dynamical
masks (instead of physical masks) [6], the properties of two-photon non-linear
absorption [7], coherent light interference [8], etc.
1.2.2 Electron beam lithography (EBL)
Similarly to OL, in the case of EBL the process relies on the change of solubility of a
resist after exposure. However, in EBL one needs to use an electron-sensitive resist
instead of a photoresist. EBL is typically performed inside an SEM or in equipment
designed speciﬁcally for this task. In both cases, a ﬁnely-focused electron beam is
scanned sequentially along the sample to expose the resist. The rest of the steps are
common to those already shown for OL (see ﬁgure 1.4).
EBL will be described in detail in chapter 3. Here, we will limit the discussion to a
few important points:
• The EBL process is intrinsically slow in comparison with OL given that the
electron beam needs to dwell a ﬁnite time on each exposure point. It is not
rare to see exposure times of several hours for a single sample. Some
developments for multi-electron-beam exposure are underway [9].
• In return for the long exposure time, a very high resolution (sub-100 nm) is
achieved routinely by EBL, reaching the realm of 10 nm with some
optimization and appropriate equipment [10].
• As occurs in OL, positive and negative resists are available in EBL as well as
the capability for performing lift-off processes that save the etching step.
• EBL is a technique well suited for laboratory research and prototyping,
considering that throughput is not an important issue and a very good
resolution is obtained.
Nanofabrication
1-7

[Page 29]
1.2.3 Nanoimprint lithography (NIL)
Although nanoimprint lithography (NIL) also uses a resist, resist exposure is
generally not needed, as shown in ﬁgure 1.5. Instead, a mould with the targeted
pattern is imprinted on the resist. By means of a high imprinting pressure and a high
working temperature, the resist adopts the shape of the mould. After sample etching
and resist removal, the sample is revealed with a pattern that is supplementary to
that imprinted in the mould [11]. Some variants of NIL include microcontact
printing, which relies on inks and self-assembly processes without the need of high
pressure and high temperature [12], and step-and-ﬂash imprint lithography, where
the resist is hardened during the imprint process at room temperature by means of
UV light exposure [13].
NIL and related techniques (also known as stamp techniques) will be described in
detail in chapter 7. Here, we will limit the discussion to a few important points:
• The mould fabrication and its quality is key in order to achieve high-
resolution patterning on large working areas. The ultimate resolution of the
technique will be largely determined by the features in the mould. As the
mould is fabricated with another nanolithography technique, it can host
features of only a few nanometers [14].
• The NIL process is capable of high throughput. In a single imprint step, a
very large sample area can be patterned if the appropriate mould and
mechanical system is used.
• In general, some residual resist remains on the sample’s surface after the
imprint step, which calls for an additional step to eliminate it. Thus, the
etching step becomes double: a breakthrough etch and a transfer etch.
Figure 1.5. Various steps commonly performed in nanoimprint lithography: resist coating (1), mould imprint
(2), sample etching (3) and resist removal with the ﬁnal result (4).
Nanofabrication
1-8

[Page 30]
• Stamp techniques are well suited for industrial applications given their
relative simplicity and their capability for high throughput, good resolution
and large-area patterning. However, the patterns cannot be in general
complex. Despite this limiting factor, its use is broad in a few niche
applications and some domains where hybrid fabrication processes are
required, such as in biotechnology or to create smart surfaces.
1.3 Direct nanolithography techniques
1.3.1 Focused ion beam (FIB)
The capability of impinging accelerated ions to sputter the atoms at the surface of
materials is very convenient for subtractive lithography. Thanks to the development
in the early 1980s of the technology of the production of ﬁnely-focused ion beams in
ion microscopes, this became a nanolithography technique with very high lateral
resolution, in the sub-10 nm range [15]. FIB commercial sources were overwhelm-
ingly based on Ga+ given its stability and easy of use. Scanning a FIB on the surface
of a material produces material removal with the desired pattern and with great
nanoscale precision, as sketched in ﬁgure 1.6. The conceptual design of the FIB
microscope leverages from the SEM, integrating similar parts: the source, the
extraction and acceleration, the optics, the scan coils, the sample stage, the electron
detectors, etc. Interestingly, combined FIB-SEM equipment was soon commercial-
ized, providing the user with all the imaging, nanofabrication and analysis
capabilities of both technologies in a single platform. For this reason, the FIB
technology has become very popular to carry out certain tasks such as cross-
sectional imaging, TEM lamella preparation, material nanopatterning and circuit
edit.
Figure 1.6. Sketch of the FIB equipment and some of its applications: creation of local milling (hole) and
growth of 2D and 3D nanostructures with the assistance of a gas injection system.
Nanofabrication
1-9

[Page 31]
The FIB technique will be described in detail in chapter 5. Here, we will limit the
discussion to a few important points:
• FIB is capable of direct removal of material without the cumbersome use of
resists. Being a direct nanolithography technique, the number of processing
steps is minimized compared to OL, EBL and NIL.
• Being a sequential nanolithography technique, FIB is intrinsically slow, with
much lower throughput than OL or NIL techniques.
• The Ga+-based liquid metal ion source has become the most widespread type
of source in FIB equipment. However, in recent years, new developments in
sources such as those based on gas ﬁeld ion sources (He+, Ne+), plasma
sources (Xe+) and metal-alloy sources represent a further step in terms of
resolution or throughput.
• As the ion–matter interaction is stronger than the electron–matter one,
harmful effects can be produced on the remaining material, changing its
physical and chemical properties.
• Niche but key applications have been found for FIB technology in the
semiconductor industry, in nanotechnology and in materials science.
1.3.2 Focused electron/ion beam induced deposition (FEBID and FIBID)
Soon after the ﬁrst commercialization of FIB equipment, a related technique was
developed and coined focused ion beam induced deposition (FIBID). FIBID
requires a gas injection system (GIS) to produce, out of a precursor material
delivered in gas form, a local deposit by precursor dissociation triggered by the FIB
irradiation [16], as sketched in ﬁgure 1.6. The main advantage of this technique is the
selective growth of a material on the area of interest in a single step. Given the high
resolution of the FIB technique, the deposits can be grown with high lateral
resolution [2]. Although the main property of interest in FIBID is commonly a
high electrical conduction [17], other functional properties such as ferromagnetism
[18], superconductivity [19] or good optical [20] and mechanical performance [21]
can be obtained. It was soon realized that FIBID is suitable for the growth of 3D
structures [22], with superb resolution recently obtained thanks to He+-FIBID [23].
The sister technique of FIBID uses a focused electron beam instead, being known
as FEBID (focused electron beam induced deposition). FEBID is carried out inside
a SEM with the assistance of a GIS. FEBID targets the nanoscale growth of
functional materials, similarly to FIBID, but with the advantage of the much lower
damage created on the substrate given the lower linear momentum of electrons
compared to ions. In return, the growth rate and the metal content in the deposits
are generally lower for FEBID compared to FIBID [24].
The FIBID technique will be described in chapter 5 in the context of FIB
technology, whereas FEBID will be described separately in chapter 4. Here, we will
limit the discussion to a few important points common to both techniques:
• FIBID and FEBID, using, respectively, a focused ion or electron beam to
dissociate a precursor gas, are direct additive nanolithography techniques
allowing the growth of speciﬁc materials on targeted locations without the
Nanofabrication
1-10

[Page 32]
need of resists. They can be used on substrates not appropriate for resist-
based processing such as cantilever tips [25].
• The purity of the materials deposited by FIBID and FEBID can be an issue
and intense research exists on puriﬁcation methods during or after growth
[26].
• Both, FIBID and FEBID, are appropriate for the growth of 3D structures
with very high lateral resolution.
• Being sequential techniques, FIBID and FEBID are intrinsically slow and are
not appropriate for large-area nanopatterning. Recent developments using
cryogenic temperatures to condense the precursor material improve the
growth speed a few orders of magnitude, the techniques being known as
Cryo-FEBID [27] and Cryo-FIBID [28].
• Applications of FIBID and FEBID are generally connected to (a) circuit edit
and mask repair in the semiconductor industry, (b) protection for cross-
sectional FIB-SEM imaging and TEM lamella preparation, and (c) proto-
typing and nanodevice fabrication in nanotechnology ﬁelds.
1.3.3 Scanning probe lithography (SPL)
The tip of a scanning tunneling microscope (STM) or an atomic force microscope
(AFM) provides not only imaging capabilities down to the atomic resolution but
also nanofabrication capabilities with such great resolution. A paradigmatic
example is the fabrication of a quantum corral by manipulation of individual
atoms on a surface by means of a STM [29]. Although the speed of such fabrication
has recently improved [30], it is a process with great difﬁculties in being scaled up
and integrated with the semiconductor industry. The use of AFM for scanning
probe lithography (SPL) also suffers from throughput issues, but it is more
suitable than STM for this task given the less stringent requirements of the
technique: no ultra-high vacuum conditions, conductive surface or very-ﬁne
control of the tip-to-sample distance are required. SPL based on AFM can be
carried out through various mechanisms, offering a wide range of possibilities [31].
Thus, the AFM tip can produce local changes in the composition, height or
physical/chemical properties of surfaces via thermal effects, mechanical effects,
deposition, chemical effects, etc. [32]. The principle of the technique is sketched in
ﬁgure 1.7(a) and the example of fabrication of Si nanowires based on oxidation
SPL is shown in ﬁgure 1.7(b) [33]. A derived technique that has become very
popular is dip-pen nanolithography, where the tip deposits speciﬁc inks on targeted
locations with great resolution [34].
SPL techniques will be described in detail in chapter 6 and one application using
thermal SPL will be discussed in detail in chapter 10. Here, a few important points
can be highlighted:
• The main advantage of SPL techniques is the great resolution achievable,
which can attain atomic resolution by STM in very speciﬁc experiments [29],
and sub-20 nm resolution in a more routine way by AFM [35].
Nanofabrication
1-11

[Page 33]
• SPL techniques are very slow by nature, but combined with other large-area
lithography techniques can be of great interest for prototyping or niche
applications [36].
• SPL techniques based on AFM are ﬂexible with respect to the type of
substrate, environment conditions, driving force of the lithography (thermal,
mechanical, oxidation, deposition, etc), the absence of physical masks, etc.
Thus, a wide range of SPL techniques exists nowadays, with thermal SPL,
oxidation SPL and dip-pen being the most common ones [37].
• Intense effort is currently being deployed towards the fabrication of active
customized probes, which can be used for SPL applications. These efforts
include downsizing of self-sensed and self-actuated probes and upscaling of
cantilever arrays amongst others [38].
Figure 1.7. (a) Sketch of an atomic-force-microscope-based process called oxidation nanolithography.
Reprinted and adapted by permission from Springer [31]. Copyright (2014). (b) Si nanowires with different
shapes fabricated with sub-20 nm resolution (scale bar is 100 nm except in the central image) by this scanning
probe lithography technique. Reproduced from [33]. Copyright IOP Publishing. Reproduced with permission.
All rights reserved.

1.4 Other nanolithography techniques and hybrid approaches
In recent years, the ﬁeld of nanolithography has been enriched by new processes or
techniques that have expanded the horizons for nanofabrication. A few of them can
be mentioned: stencil lithography [39], nanosphere/colloidal lithography [40], roll-
to-roll lithography [41], Talbot lithography [42], soft x-ray lithography using resists
[43] or precursors [44], proton-beam lithography [45], plasmonic lithography [46], ice
lithography [47], fountain-pen nanolithography [48], crack lithography [49], grain-
boundary lithography [50], DNA lithography [51], self-assembled-monolayer lith-
ography [52], beam-pen lithography [53], scanning near-ﬁeld lithography [54],
nanoskiving [55], etc. Given the limited space and the focus of the present chapter,
only stencil and nanosphere/colloidal lithography will be introduced in detail in this
section. New developments in coming years in the ﬁeld of nanolithography could
promote the wider use of some of the above mentioned techniques.
1.4.1 Stencil lithography
In this technique, a thin plate with designed apertures, called a stencil mask, is
placed in proximity to the substrate and used in combination with material
evaporation, as shown in ﬁgure 1.8(a). The evaporated material is stopped on the
top surface of the stencil mask, except in the apertures. The material is thus grown
Figure 1.8. Sketches of two nanolithography techniques that allow the deposition of material only through
small apertures: (a) stencil lithography. Reprinted with permission from [56]. Copyright (2008) American
Chemical Society. (b) Nanosphere/colloidal lithography. Reprinted under the Creative Commons Attribution
License from [40].
Nanofabrication
1-13

[Page 35]
with speciﬁc patterns determined by those of the stencil mask [56]. With appropriate
stencil masks, nanopatterned materials can be grown at the wafer scale in a single
shot. Resolution down to 20 nm has been obtained and it can be used on
unconventional substrates like cantilevers [39], but the technique bears some
difﬁculties related to shadow deposition below the stencil mask and lifetime issues
due to the deterioration of the stencil mask caused by material deposition at the
edges of the holes, eventually blocking them (clogging effect). Developments such as
the use of dynamic stencil masks have enlarged the range of applications of the
technique [57], which will be discussed in detail in chapter 8.
1.4.2 Nanosphere/colloidal lithography
Under this name we include a set of techniques making use of a solution of
nanoparticles, which form a very thin layer on the substrate of interest, and are used
as a sacriﬁcial layer during the nanopatterning process. Due to the interaction
amongst the nanoparticles, they can self-organize forming a thin layer that leaves
holes in between them, as sketched in ﬁgure 1.8(b). The technique was originally
termed natural lithography [58]. Provided the monodisperse nature of the colloidal
particles and their hydrophilic character, they form a colloidal crystal with ordered
holes through which the material of interest will penetrate and be deposited on the
substrate [40]. For example, polystyrene latex nanospheres can be used. The material
deposited on top of the nanoparticles will disappear after the sample is immersed in
an adequate solvent and sonicated. The process is similar to a lift-off process
described in section 1.1.1. Advantages of this technique are the capability for large-
area patterning, simplicity, good resolution and the potential to be combined with
other lithography techniques. On the other hand, the technique presents difﬁculties
with respect to the limited shapes available for the patterned functional material, the
long-range order of the nanopatterns and the presence of point defects.
1.4.3 Hybrid approaches
In order to cover all the possibilities, the term hybrid can mean in this context:
(a) The use of two or more of the top-down nanolithography techniques to
complete the patterning of a material or to fabricate a nanodevice;
(b) The combination of a top-down nanolithography technique with a bottom-
up nanofabrication process to achieve a certain nanopatterning process or
nanodevice or the unconventional use of a top-down nanolithography or a
bottom-up nanofabrication technique with thin-ﬁlm or other growth
techniques with the aim of nanopatterning and building nanodevices.
Just to give the reader the ﬂavor of such hybrid strategies, we consider in the
following a few selected examples within each category:
(a) If two lithography techniques complement each other in terms of resolution
or patterning area, it makes sense to combine them in a single instrument.
This approach has been used to fabricate a single-electron transistor, as
shown in ﬁgure 1.9 [36], integrating direct laser writing for large-area
Nanofabrication
1-14

[Page 36]
exposure and thermal scanning probe lithography for high-resolution
patterning. Another example that can be found in literature is the
combination of electron beam lithography and scanning probe lithography
using a single calixarene resist layer [59], in a process that is coined mix-and-
match. This concept normally entails the consecutive use of a large-area
lithography technique to pattern non-critical dimensions and a high-
resolution lithography technique to pattern the smallest dimensions, with
appropriate protocols to align both lithography steps. Mix-and-match has
also been used to perform the consecutive resist exposure of chemically-
ampliﬁed resists by DUV optical lithography and electron beam lithog-
raphy [60].
(b) As will be shown in detail in chapter 2, directed block-copolymer lithog-
raphy combines top-down lithography and bottom-up self-organization of
Figure 1.9. Example of a hybrid lithography technique combining two classical lithography techniques:
fabrication of a single-electron transistor based on a quantum point contact (QPC) combining direct laser
writing and thermal SPL (t-SPL). Reprinted from [36]. Copyright IOP Publishing. Reproduced with
permission. All rights reserved.
Nanofabrication
1-15

[Page 37]
two polymers in order to produce high-resolution nanopatterning in large
areas [61]. Normally, the block-copolymer self-organization is randomly
oriented and lacks long-range order (see ﬁgure 1.1), but the previous top-
down patterning provides the platform for a directed block-copolymer
lithography. In another example to be described in chapter 5, FIB
irradiation of a Si substrate gives rise to the preferential growth of semi-
conductor Ge material on the irradiated areas, which can be used to
fabricate ordered arrays of semiconducting dots [62]. In another example
of this category that is shown in ﬁgure 1.10, the FEBID technique is used to
demonstrate the unsurpassed capabilities of carbon nanotubes for room-
temperature mass detection, in the zg range (1 zg = 10−21 g) [63].
Further examples of the broad topic of hybrid lithography can be found in the
following references by Saavedra et al [64], by Kim et al [65] and by Lu et al [66].
Figure 1.10. Example of a hybrid unconventional lithography processing for demonstrating mass nanosensing.
(a) Growth of carbon nanotubes by chemical vapour deposition, which become attached to the substrate by
Van-der-Waals forces and in some cases overhang the substrate. The resonance frequency of the carbon
nanotubes is measured in a SEM through the generated secondary-electron generation and a vector network
analyser. This resonance frequency becomes modiﬁed by the change in mass through FEBID deposition at the
carbon nanotube’s tip. The technique shows 13 zg mass resolution; (b) SEM micrograph of the Pt-C
nanoparticle (total mass of 1.33 fg) deposited by FEBID; (c) TEM image of the nanoparticle shown in (b).
Reprinted from [63]. Copyright (2019) American Chemical Society.
Nanofabrication
1-16

[Page 38]
1.5 Comparison of nanolithography techniques
In ﬁgure 1.11, four selected lithography techniques are compared in terms of ﬁve key
indicators: resolution, throughput, cost of the equipment, process complexity and
the capability to fabricate complex patterns. In the following, we discuss these
indicators:
• Resolution: it refers to the smallest features achievable with the technique, and
good resolution refers to sub-10 nm patterning. In the semiconductor
industry, optical lithography using steppers (industrial optical lithography)
shows capability for fabrication of integrated circuits down to sub-10 nm
resolution [5]. Electron beam lithography is also capable of sub-10 nm
patterning using appropriate SEM equipment, resist and processing [10,
67]. Nanoimprint lithography with suitable moulds and resists also leads to
sub-10 nm patterning [68]. Given the intrinsically small tip diameter, scanning
probe lithography has so far shown the best resolution in nanopatterning [31].
Other techniques with sub-10 nm patterning capability are FEBID [69] and
focused ion beam milling [70].
• Throughput: it refers to the amount of sample area patterned per unit of time.
García et al mention that high-volume production requires throughput values
of 1012 μm2 h−1, which means the processing of 100 wafers per hour [31].
Existing equipment with such throughput can be found in industrial optical
lithography, where steppers can handle such a high number of wafers per
hour. Here, we use a less stringent deﬁnition to consider a nanolithography
Figure 1.11. Comparison of the main lithography techniques in terms of resolution, throughput, cost of the
equipment, process complexity and the capability to fabricate complex patterns.
Nanofabrication
1-17

[Page 39]
technique as a high throughput one. We consider high-throughput techniques
those capable of processing a full wafer in a single lithography shot, such as
optical lithography and nanoimprint lithography. Electron beam lithography
and scanning probe lithography can improve the throughput by using
multiple-beam [9] and multiple-tip approaches [37], respectively, but are still
far from industrial optical lithography. Other nanolithography techniques
with high throughput are stencil lithography and nanosphere/colloidal
lithography.
• Pattern complexity: this concept refers to the capability of the technique to
generate complex patterns, ideally, in 2D and 3D. Optical lithography using
masks are good to generate arbitrary patterns in 2D but not in 3D. However,
two-photon-absorption lithography is well suited for 3D patterns. Standard
electron beam lithography can generate arbitrary patterns in 2D but shows
greater difﬁculty to achieve 3D patterning [71]. Nanoimprint lithography is
commonly used to generate relatively simple 2D patterns. The fact that the
patterning should be ﬁrst imprinted on the mould is an added difﬁculty.
However, recent developments in the ﬁeld are promising towards 3D nano-
patterning [72]. Scanning probe lithography is well suited to generate
arbitrary patterns in 2D but not so much for 3D despite the fact that thermal
scanning probe lithography has shown capability for 3D nanopatterning [73].
Regarding the rest of nanolithography techniques, FEBID and FIBID have
shown great capability to generate nanopatterns in 2D and 3D [23, 74].
• Process complexity: within this concept, we evaluate the amount of steps
required to perform the processing and the difﬁculty of these steps. Processing
in industrial optical lithography is very sophisticated. The industry of
integrated circuits has reﬁned the involved lithography processes for several
decades, collecting high-end knowledge on key aspects [75]. This has led to
working devices in sub-10 nm nodes, which seemed unthinkable a few years
ago. A huge complexity gap exists between an industrial optical lithography
process carried out using a state-of-the-art stepper and a standard optical
lithography process carried out using a mask aligner. In sharp contrast,
electron beam lithography and scanning probe lithography can attain similar
resolution to industrial optical lithography with much simpler processes, but
with immensely lower throughput. In the case of nanoimprint lithography,
the imprint process is relatively simple, but some extra complexity arises from
the mould fabrication and the resist leftovers. On the other hand, given the
absence of resists, the simplicity of focused ion beam milling for nano-
patterning should be singled out.
• Cost: here, one should mainly consider the lithography equipment cost. By
far, a state-of-the-art stepper used in industrial optical lithography is the most
expensive lithography equipment one can think of, and its cost can reach 100
million euros. This is why only a few companies in the world can afford such
investment in order to fabricate the latest devices in the microelectronics
industry. The cost of electron beam lithography and nanoimprint lithography
equipment is much lower, typically in the range of 1 million euros or less, but
Nanofabrication
1-18

[Page 40]
the equipment price will strongly vary with the handled wafer size. On the
other hand, an atomic force microscope with nanolithography capabilities
will be surely cheaper than that. Stencil lithography and nanosphere/colloidal
lithography excel with respect to the equipment cost, which is certainly low
compared to the other techniques.
Deﬁnitively, an appropriate lithography technique exists for each particular need,
and the user should consider all the key aspects discussed in this section in order to
ﬁnd the most suited one.
1.6 Applications of nanolithography techniques
1.6.1 Classical applications
The fabrication of integrated circuits is the paradigm of the need of lithography
techniques in modern technology. Since the monolithic idea was conceived and
developed by Jack Kilby and Robert Noyce in the late 1950s, integrating on the
same semiconductor block all the circuit elements needed for an electronic device
[76], the development of appropriate fabrication techniques was pursued. Optical
lithography was found to be the right technique for this fabrication, given its high
throughput. In addition, it could cope with the increasing resolution demands in
each new generation of electronic devices, as will be shown in chapter 2. In fact,
optical lithography is considered to be one of the cornerstones of electronics.
Modern electronics would not be possible without the extraordinary properties of
semiconductor materials and an enabling technology such as optical lithography.
Thanks to optical lithography, Moore’s law, which states that in each new
generation of integrated circuits the number of transistors doubles, would simply
have not been possible [5]. Taking into account that the total footprint of a single
transistor today is around 100 nm × 100 nm, or equivalently 1010 transistors per cm2,
it is difﬁcult to think that signiﬁcant further steps will come from size reduction.
Nevertheless, More-than-Moore and Beyond-Moore approaches are being inten-
sively investigated as potential replacement to the existing paradigms, with nano-
lithography techniques remaining as a cornerstone of all new technologies [77]. On
the other hand, nanolithography is not only important in the fabrication of the
microelectronic components themselves, but also in other related processes. One can
cite for example circuit edit and mask repair processes, where focused ion beam,
focused ion beam induced deposition, focused electron beam induced etching and
focused electron beam induced deposition are employed [78–80].
The reader will ﬁnd in the book many other classical applications of nano-
lithography beyond those in microelectronics. Let us cite here a few of them, but we
invite the reader to go through the details in the different chapters:
• Optical elements and devices: display panels, optical masks, diffraction
gratings, micromirrors, micro-lenses such as Fresnel lenses, optical wave-
guides, photonic crystals, plasmonic elements, etc.
• Magnetic elements and devices: for information storage, such as memory
elements and reading heads, for logics and communication devices.
Nanofabrication
1-19

[Page 41]
• Structural elements and functional surfaces: cantilevers for MEMS and probe
tips, micro-implants, nano-tweezers, surfaces with speciﬁc hydrophobocity/
hydrophilicity properties, etc.
• Superconducting devices: arrays of Josephson junctions for quantum com-
puting, high-frequency electronics, single-photon detectors, x-ray detectors,
etc.
• Elements and devices for energy storage or conversion: solar cells, batteries,
energy-harvesting devices, etc.
• Enabling nanofabrication for advanced imaging: TEM lamellae preparation,
3D FIB-SEM imaging, atom probe tomography, etc.
It should not be forgotten that nanolithography is commonly used in laboratory
research for the patterning of materials and the fabrication of proof-of-concept
devices. Although most of the investigations will not eventually lead to a commer-
cialized application, many resources and nanolithography equipment are devoted to
it. In fact, it is well known that the properties of materials change as their dimensions
become comparable to the relevant physical lengths such as the electron mean free
path, the magnetic interactions, the superconducting coherence length, etc. As a
consequence, research activities involving the study of the change in the physical
properties and the discovery of new phenomena at the nanoscale are ubiquitous and
call for patterning the materials of interest. Also, exploration of new devices with
potential for commercialization requires as a ﬁrst step to fabricate a small batch of
them. Such prototyping or proof-of-concept fabrication needs lithography infra-
structure. For a small number of units in the prototyping phase, the cost of
fabrication is generally not the main issue and various nanolithography techniques
can be generally used.
1.6.2 Interdisciplinary applications
A growing interest of nanolithography exists for interdisciplinary applications,
mainly within the ﬁelds of biology and medicine. Nanolithography enters the game
in two ways. First, it can be used to fabricate the electronic devices that provide a
digital readout of a certain measurement or allows applying a certain external
action. Secondly, given the micrometric or nanometric size of objects such as
bacteria, virus, proteins, DNA, etc, artiﬁcial objects of similar size are relevant to
interact with those entities. This has prompted several applications in the following
topics:
• bionsensing and medical diagnosis [81],
• DNA and protein sequencing [82],
• microﬂuidic and lab-on-a-chip devices [83, 84],
• medical treatments (drug delivery, hyperthermia, mechanical treatment, etc)
[85],
• medical imaging techniques [86],
• neuroscience [87],
• scaffolds for tissue growth and cell differentiation [88, 89].
Nanofabrication
1-20

[Page 42]
1.6.3 Emergent and future applications
One can think of the following emergent and future applications:
• The development of ﬂexible electronics has expanded the use of nanolithography
into new emergent applications in biomedicine (biopsy diagnosis, implants for
optical stimulation, cardiac patches, brain mapping), ﬂexible LEDs, ﬂexible
touch screens, and strain and temperature sensors [90]. More futuristic, but
approaching fast to reality, ﬂexible electronic components are being developed to
accomplish the interfacing between humans and machines, such as in the
artiﬁcial retina [91, 92]. Cyborgs will surely rely on lithography to some extent.
• New challenges in chemical warfare, contaminant substances, disease diag-
nosis and drug detection are fostering development under the umbrella of the
electronic nose concept, where a small device must be able to detect the
targeted substances and trigger a fast response. Nanofabrication is crucial
towards device miniaturization, the fabrication of the electronic components
and their integration. The electronic nose must ideally perform the sensing,
the signal processing and the signal transmission [93].
• With the rising importance of robots in our daily life, nanolithography will
play a key role in the fabrication of smaller and faster sensors and actuators
as well as suitable electronics for the advanced control and communication of
the robot’s components [94].
• The change of paradigm from classical computation to quantum computation
to perform certain computational tasks will bring about new fabrication
challenges to build the qubits as well as the optimized electronics. The scenario
is still wide open, but in most of the proposed approaches lithography is one of
the key enabling technologies [95–97]. Other alternative computation schemes
such as neuromorphic computing also rely on nanolithography [98].
• With the upcoming implantation of autonomous cars, nanolithography will
be a key player in the fabrication of advanced sensors and efﬁcient electronics
for data treatment, decision making and communication. Important in
business terms and technological demands, the Internet of Things will also
bring new challenges for sensing and microelectronic fabrication [99].
Other emergent ﬁelds could be further mentioned, but I leave the last word to the
reader’s imagination. Nevertheless, I can share my futuristic thoughts. Artiﬁcial
intelligence (AI) is expected to change our lives in the next decades. Recent
developments have unveiled its potential impact in better designs and fabrication
concepts of integrated circuits beyond conventional approaches. Who knows what
can happen if AI uses all its power to improve the integrated circuits on which the AI
runs itself…will the process converge or diverge?
1.7 End-of-chapter summary
In this introductory chapter, we have provided a broad view on the ﬁeld of
nanofabrication based on nanolithography techniques, with an emphasis on a few
aspects that are hereafter summarized:
Nanofabrication
1-21

[Page 43]
• Key concepts in nanolithography:
(a) Whereas bottom-up approaches rely on self-assembly and self-organ-
ization processes that merge smaller building blocks to achieve larger
structures, top-down approaches rely on creating small structures out
of larger ones.
(b) In general, top-down nanolithography is combined with thin-ﬁlm
growth techniques, providing capabilities for ﬁne control of dimen-
sions down to the nanoscale, high throughput and creation of complex
structures.
(c) The combination of bottom-up and top-down strategies offers addi-
tional opportunities for advanced nanofabrication.
(d) The choice of an appropriate nanolithography technique for each
particular case should consider aspects such as the resolution, cost,
throughput and complexity.
• Types of nanolithography techniques:
(a) Optical-based lithography includes: UV lithography in contact, prox-
imity and projection modes, extreme UV lithography, direct laser
writing, interference lithography, two-photon-absorption lithography,
plasmonic lithography, soft x-ray lithography, Talbot lithography,
beam-pen lithography, scanning near-ﬁeld lithography, etc.
(b) Electron and ion beam based lithography comprises: electron beam
lithography, focused ion beam, focused electron and beam induced
deposition (including precursor in gas, liquid or solid phase), ice
lithography, etc.
(c) Stamp lithography encompasses: nanoimprint lithography, microcon-
tact printing, step-and-ﬂash lithography, self-assembled-monolayer
lithography, roll-to-roll lithography, etc.
(d) Scanning probe lithography covers: oxidation nanolithography, ther-
mal scanning probe lithography, nanoscratching lithography, dip-pen
lithography, fountain-pen lithography, etc.
(e) Lithography based on small physical apertures includes: stencil lith-
ography, nanosphere/colloidal lithography, grain-boundary lithogra-
phy, etc.
(f) Other lithography techniques not categorized under the previous
epigraphs and mentioned in the present chapter are crack lithography
and DNA lithography.
(g) In many applications, two or more of these techniques need to be used,
which has been coined in section 1.4.3 with the term hybrid litho-
graphy approach. Commercial equipment exists in some cases that
integrates two or more of these lithography techniques.
• Applications of nanolithography:
(a) The classical application of nanolithography is the fabrication of
integrated circuits and other electronic devices, in which optical lithog-
raphy is widespread. In addition, various types of nanolithography
Nanofabrication
1-22

[Page 44]
techniques are used in research activities targeting the patterning of
materials and realization of prototype and proof-of-concept devices.
(b) In recent years, the penetration of nanolithography in interdisciplinary
applications has rocketed, especially in biology and medicine.
(c) A bright future is waiting for nanolithography in emergent applica-
tions such as new computation schemes, biomedicine, robotics, the
Internet
of
Things,
the
autonomous
car
and
human–machine
interfacing.

applications can be viewed at https://iopscience.iop.org/book/978-0-7503-
2608-7.

lithography techniques: FIB, FEBID, stencil lithography.
• Brainstorming: think about one application not mentioned in this chapter in
which you could apply nanolithography techniques. Try to make it real.
