Nanofabrication
Nanolithography techniques and their applications
Jose´ Marı´a De Teresa
Chapter 2
Optical lithography
Francesc Perez-Murano, José Ignacio Martín and José María De Teresa
Optical lithography uses optical radiation to deﬁne patterns in a photosensitive
material (photoresist). Patterning resolution in optical lithography is mainly limited
by light diffraction. Decreasing the wavelength of the optical radiation is the main
way to improve resolution. Systems based on extreme ultra-violet light achieve a
resolution close to 10 nm. In the most common approach optical lithography is
performed using optical masks to locally expose the photoresist, allowing for high
throughput processing. Greater ﬂexibility and 3D patterning is achieved by laser
and two-photon lithography. A further improvement in the resolution of optical
lithography without compromising throughput could be achieved by complementing
optical lithography with self-assembly methods.
2.1 Concept of optical lithography
Optical lithography is the most employed lithography method. The principle is
described in ﬁgure 2.1: optical radiation is used to expose a photosensitive material
in selected areas. As a result of the exposure, the photosensitive material experiences
a change in its physical and chemical properties, varying its solubility with respect to
a speciﬁc solvent. After the exposure, the latent pattern in the photosensitive
material is revealed in the development process, when a speciﬁc chemical agent is
used to either eliminate the exposed or the non-exposed areas depending on the
characteristics of the resist.
As is the case in all lithography methods, resolution is the main ﬁgure of merit to
describe the performance. Resolution is deﬁned as the minimum feature that can be
printed in the resist. In optical lithography, diffraction phenomena limit the
resolution. As diffraction is directly dependent on the wavelength of the optical
radiation, the shorter the wavelength, the better will be the resolution. Reducing the
wavelength of the optical radiation has been one of the ways to improve the
resolution of optical lithography, but at the expenses of increasing the process cost.
doi:10.1088/978-0-7503-2608-7ch2
2-1
ª IOP Publishing Ltd 2020. All rights,
including for text and data mining (TDM), artificial intelligence (AI) training, and similar technologies, are reserved.

[Page 51]
There are several key features in an optical lithography process, which deﬁne the
different variations from the global scheme represented in ﬁgure 2.1:
• use of an optical mask or direct exposure;
• wavelength of the optical radiation;
• positive or negative tone of the photosensitive material.
The chapter is organized as follows. Section 2.2 is devoted to optical lithography
using an optical mask, describing the different exposure modes, the optical radiation
used and the types of photosensitive materials. In section 2.3, optical lithography by
direct write exposure without a physical mask is reviewed. In section 2.4, the concept
of directed self-assembly is introduced.
2.2 Optical lithography using optical masks
2.2.1 Optical lithography exposure modes and their resolution
The most common way to perform an optical lithography process is by means of an
optical mask. An optical mask consists of a substrate (glass or quartz, for example)
which is transparent for the wavelength of the optical radiation used for the
exposure. On this substrate, non-transparent areas are deﬁned, conforming
the patterns that will be transferred to the substrate. The mask is located between
Figure 2.1. Sketch showing the concept of optical lithography.
Nanofabrication
2-2

[Page 52]
the optical radiation source and the sample coated with the photosensitive material.
When light crosses the optical mask, the non-transparent areas will block the light
and, in consequence, some regions of the photosensitive material will be exposed and
some areas not (see ﬁgure 2.2).
The scheme depicted in the ﬁgure is simple, but the practical implementation is
much more complex. In order to achieve micrometric and sub-micrometric
resolution the overall exposure process needs to be carefully considered. Specially,
an accurate control of the energy intensity delivered to the photosensitive material is
crucial to obtain the desired ﬁnal pattern.
As indicated above, the photosensitive material changes its physical and chemical
properties upon exposure. For each photosensitive material, there is an optimal
quantity of energy that induces the change of the photosensitive material properties,
which in turn depends on the wavelength of the optical radiation and on the
thickness of the photosensitive material layer. If the energy delivered is less than
optimal, the change in the solubility of the material will not be sufﬁcient, so the
contrast between the exposed and unexposed regions will be weak. If the energy
delivered is too large, then the photosensitive material may experience degradation
or other effects different than the expected change of solubility.
To achieve an accurate control of the energy delivered, the following aspects must
be fulﬁlled:
(1) the wavelength of the optical radiation has to ﬁt with the physical and
chemical properties of the photosensitive material;
(2) the intensity of the optical source has to be known and stable;
Figure 2.2. General scheme of optical lithography using an optical mask.
Nanofabrication
2-3

[Page 53]
(3) all the exposed areas have to receive the same amount of energy delivered;
(4) the position of the optical mask has to be fully parallel to the photosensitive
material.
Attending to these requirements, ﬁgure 2.3(a) presents the three general modes of
an optical lithography process: contact, proximity and projection. Figure 2.3(b)
shows an image of an extreme ultraviolet (EUV) projection lithography system. The
optical projection system is made of multiple mirrors instead of lenses, as this is
more appropriate for EUV radiation (see section 2.2.3). Figure 2.3(b) reveals the
complexity of these systems, which are only appropriate for high-volume manu-
facturing activities due to their high costs.
Figure 2.3. (a) Optical lithography modes using an optical mask: contact, proximity and projection.
(b) Photograph of an extreme-UV optical lithography system. The main elements are labelled.
Nanofabrication
2-4

[Page 54]
2.2.1.1 Contact optical lithography
In contact optical lithography, the mask is placed in mechanical contact with the
photosensitive material. In this way, the deterioration in resolution is minimized, as
the distance between the apertures in the mask and the exposed volume in the resist
is minimized. An approximate calculation of the resolution, R, in this case is the
following:
λ
=
·
R
d
(
)
(2.1)
1/2
where d stand for the thickness of the resist and λ is the wavelength of the optical
radiation.
If we consider a common example in a usual microfabrication laboratory, a
typical thickness of the photosensitive material is d =1 μm, and the wavelength of the
optical radiation in the near UV range, λ = 435 nm. Then, the minimum feature size
that can be printed is R = 0.7 μm.
Masked optical lithography in contact presents the drawback of inducing mask
damage. The accumulation of defects in the mask means that the mask cannot be
used for many exposures, drastically decreasing its lifetime. Consequently, the use of
this method should be avoided.
2.2.1.2 Proximity optical lithography
An alternative to contact optical lithography is proximity optical lithography. In this
case, the mask and the photosensitive material are separated by a ﬁnite gap,
preventing any mechanical contact. As a consequence the useful lifetime of the mask
is increased, but at the expense of decreasing the resolution. An empirical relation
for the resolution in proximity optical lithography is the following:
λ
=
·
R
k g
(
)
(2.2)
1/2
where g is the distance between mask and the photosensitive material and k is an
empirical constant which stands for additional detrimental effects that degrade
resolution, like lack of parallelism between the mask and the sample, or the ﬁnite
thickness of the photosensitive material layer. In consequence, k is always larger
than 1.
Assuming a typical situation, k = 1.1, g = 20 μm and λ = 435 nm, the best
attainable resolution in proximity optical lithography is R = 3.2 μm.
2.2.1.3 Projection optical lithography
Projection optical lithography overcomes some of the resolution limitations occur-
ring in contact and proximity optical lithography (such as the damage by
mechanical contact and diffraction) by inserting an optical system between the
mask and the photosensitive material. The optical system creates an optical image of
the mask on top of the resist material, eliminating mechanical contact while keeping
a good resolution.
Resolution in projection optical lithography is calculated as in the resolution in
any optical system, but including the speciﬁcities of the fact that a photosensitive
Nanofabrication
2-5

[Page 55]
material is exposed. In practice, the resolution is calculated attending to the
following semi-empirical relation:
λ
=
R
k NA
(2.3)
1
where NA is the numerical aperture of the optical system and k1 is known as the
technological constant which stands for all the improvements engineered to enhance
the resolution. Considering moderate values of the resolution in optical lithography
like k1 = 0.66, λ = 435 nm, NA = 0.7, an important improvement in resolution is
obtained: R = 0.4 μm.
Projection optical lithography was introduced in the 1970s [1]. Since then,
improvements in multiple aspects have allowed for a continuous enhancement of
resolution. Resolution in projection optical lithography (i.e., expression (2.3))
cannot be decoupled from the need to keep the exposure in focus. Depth of focus
(DOF) stands for the range of vertical distance over which the broadening of the
image becomes acceptable. DOF for projection optical lithography can be estimated
through the semi-empirical expression:
λ
= k NA
DOF
(
)
(2.4)
2
2
where k2 is a constant that, similarly to k1, depends on technological and process
parameters. Comparing expressions (2.3) and (2.4), it is observed that DOF
decreases when the resolution of the optical system improves. This imposes
important requirements for resist thickness and wafer surface planarization.
2.2.1.4 Improvements in projection optical lithography
Projection optical lithography is the method used to obtain high resolution.
Figure 2.4 shows the evolution of lithography systems used over the years to
improve the resolution.
In the following, the different advances introduced in projection optical lithog-
raphy will be described:
(a) Reduction optical lithography: step-and-repeat and step-and-scan systems
Introducing a reduction (or demagniﬁcation) factor in the optical system
presents several advantages: (i) if the reduction factor is n, the features on
the optical mask will be n times larger than the exposed features, relaxing
the requirements to fabricate the masks. (ii) As the ﬁeld of exposure
decreases, it becomes easier to keep the image in good focus, which is
critical when the DOF decreases for high-resolution systems. Masks used in
combination with reduction optical lithography are usually referred to as
optical reticles (see ﬁgure 2.5).
The introduction of an optical reduction factor implies that the whole wafer
area will not be exposed in a single illumination shot. In consequence,
reduction optical lithography is performed in combination with step-and-
repeat and step-and-scan systems in stepper equipment. In the step-and-repeat
Nanofabrication
2-6

[Page 56]
Figure 2.4. Evolution of the optical resolution during the years. Data extracted from [2].
Figure 2.5. Step-and-repeat and step-and-scan systems. In step-and-repeat, the reticle is fully illuminated
during the ﬁeld exposure. In step-and-scan, the reticle is scanned by the laser. In consequence, sample and
reticle are moved in a synchronous way, with a scanning speed ratio equal to the reduction factor of the optical
system. Adapted from [3].
Nanofabrication
2-7

[Page 57]
system, all the desired area of the reticle is exposed in a single shot, then the
sample is moved a ﬁxed quantity, and another exposure is performed. Each
different exposed area corresponds to a dice or chip.
A further improvement is achieved using step-and-scan systems. While
the size of the reticle would be the same for step and repeat and step and
scan systems, in the step and scan systems the reticle is only partially
illuminated. At the same time, the reticle and sample are being scanned in a
synchronous way: for a reduction factor n, the scanning speed of the reticle
has to be n times faster than the scanning speed of the sample. Illuminating
a smaller area of the reticle (and, in consequence exposing a smaller area of
the wafer) allows for a better focus capability and illumination uniformity.
(b) Decreasing the wavelength of the optical source
A direct implication of expression (2.3) is that resolution improves as the
wavelength of the optical radiation decreases. Three different ranges of
wavelength are employed: near ultraviolet (NUV), deep ultraviolet (DUV)
and previously-introduced EUV. In all cases, light sources of narrow
bandwidth are employed: as the optical properties of lenses and the opto-
chemical properties of the photosensitive materials depend on the wave-
length, it is necessary to use a speciﬁc wavelength to achieve an accurate
exposure.
• NUV optical lithography
NUV optical lithography uses optical radiation of 436 nm (g-line),
405 nm (h-line) and 365 nm (i-line). The lines correspond to the optical
emission spectra of mercury-arc lamps. The light sources for NUV
optical lithography have been obtained from mercury lamps with
special optical ﬁlters for many years. Presently, UV LED light sources
are being employed as they can offer wavelength tunability, optical
ﬁlters are not required, maintenance is much more simple, offer longer
lifetime and are much more eco-friendly. NUV optical lithography is
used for optical lithography in the proximity mode and also for step-
and-repeat systems.
• DUV optical lithography
DUV optical lithography systems use optical radiation of 248 and
193 nm, the latter being one of the most used in present systems. KrF
and ArF excimer lasers are used as optical light sources for 248 and
193 nm wavelengths, respectively. ArF excimer lasers generate laser
light from the stimulated emission of ArF complex created from the
ionized gas containing argon and ﬂuorine. Excimer laser operates in a
pulsed mode, with typical frequencies of kHz. DUV optical lithog-
raphy is usually performed in the optical projection mode using step-
and-repeat or step-and-scan systems.
• EUV optical lithography
EUV optical lithography provides the ultimate resolution in mask-
based optical lithography using 13.5 nm wavelength. A separated
Nanofabrication
2-8

[Page 58]
section is dedicated to EUV due to its future importance for the next
generation of semiconductor electronics.
(c) Increasing the numerical aperture NA: immersion DUV optical lithography
Increasing the numerical aperture requires increasing the size of lens or
reducing the focal distance. Both options present limitations as the need for
a minimum distance between the ﬁnal part of the lens and the resist to
account for the sample scanning and also to avoid damaging the lens from
the evaporation of material during the exposure.
A solution to it has come from immersion lithography, a key break-
through for decreasing the attainable resolution of optical lithography, as
the presence of a liquid with a refraction index n increases n times the value
of NA. In particular, pure water, with n ≈1.47, is suitable for this purpose
as it only absorbs ~5% of the incident light. It is usually compatible with the
lenses and the photoresist, and it does not contaminate the system.
(d) Decreasing k1:
As mentioned before, k1 accounts for adjusting the resolution value from
the technological constraints. k1 has a physical lower limit of 0.25 [4].
Additional difﬁculties arise when k1 is decreasing, such as the ampliﬁcation
of errors in masks that cannot be compensated by the demagniﬁcation [5]
and the need of a very ﬂat topography to compensate for the low depth of
focus [6]. Approaching these physical lower limits is achieved by several
improvements that cover many aspects of a lithography process:
i. Off-line illumination. Engineering the illumination of the mask
improves the resolution because higher NA optical system can be
used while maintaining a durable depth of focus. Engineered off-line
illumination implies stopping the light from the center along with
patterning the optical beam to obtain annular dipole and quadrupole
illumination.
ii. Optical proximity correction (OPC). Optical proximity effects are
important in dense patterns, and it is caused by the dependence of the
actual exposure dose on the geometry of the patterns of the mask.
For example, for the same mask illumination, a dense array of
exposed lines will receive more dose than a single-exposed line. When
features of different geometries and sizes exist, correcting for the
proximity effects requires adjusting the geometries, what is known as
OPC. For complex structures at the limit of resolution, OPC is
complex and requires dedicated modelling.
iii. Phase shifting masks [7]. Diffraction of light through small apertures
makes difﬁcult the deﬁnition of high-density features. When the
optical source is coherent, constructive interference between the light
transmitted from two adjacent apertures maximizes the intensity in
between the apertures. The underlying concept in phase shifting mask
Nanofabrication
2-9

[Page 59]
is the variation of the optical path through the mask to add a 180°
phase shift to the optical wave, promoting a negative interference in
between the apertures. In the case of alternating phase shifting mask,
a recession in the apertures allows the control of the phase difference
between the optical radiation through the apertures (see ﬁgure 2.6).
2.2.2 Photo-resists
Optical lithography is based on the selective exposure of a thin layer of a photo-
sensitive material that is spread over the surface where the patterning is going to be
realized. The photosensitive material is called a photoresist, because its main role,
after being exposed and revealed, is to protect the surface from the subsequent
pattern transfer.
Usually, a photoresist is composed of, at least, the following three components:
the resin, a photoactive compound and the solvent. The resin is the polymer that
remains on the surface after the exposure and development. The physical–chemical
structure of the polymer changes as a consequence of the exposure to optical
radiation. The photoactive component controls the photochemical reactions that
induce the structural change of the resin. The solvent dissolves the resin in a solution
of a certain viscosity. Usually, the photoresist is deposited on the surface by spin
coating, forming a thin and very uniform layer of polymer. The viscosity of the
Figure 2.6. Concept of alternating phase shifting mask. Calculation of the in-focus aerial image after passing
through a 4X stepper at DUV illumination (248 nm wavelength), and numerical aperture of 0.6 for a simple
subtracting alternating phase shifting mask (a) and a dual trench one (b). Figure reproduced from [8],
copyright SPIE.
Nanofabrication
2-10

[Page 60]
solvent along with the spinning speed deﬁnes the ﬁnal thickness of the polymer layer.
After deposition, an annealing process is performed to remove the solvent.
The resin and the photoactive compound determine the response of the photo-
resist to the exposure and development. In the case of a positive tone photoresist, the
resin becomes more soluble in the developer, so that the exposure regions are
eliminated. For a negative tone photoresist, the effect of the exposure is to decrease
the solubility. As a consequence, the exposed areas remain on the surface.
2.2.2.1 Positive resists
For the most used class of positive tone photoresist, the photoactive compound
inhibits the dissolution of the resin in the developer before exposure. Upon
irradiation, the photoactive compound triggers a chemical reaction that increases
the resin solubility [9]. The most used photoresist for NUV optical lithography is
based on Novolac resin [10]. Novolac resins are typically produced with form-
aldehyde, at molar excess of phenol, and an acid catalyst. Typical photo-active
compounds belong to the DiazoNaphtoQuinon-sulphonate group (DNQ). Their
presence in photoresist reduces the alkaline solubility in one or two orders of
magnitude. During exposure, DNQ is converted into a carboxylic acid, causing that
the development rate increases by three to four orders of magnitude.
The resists used for NUV optical lithography are not adequate for DUV optical
lithography, as they have to face two issues: the lower intensity of the DUV optical
radiation and the higher absorbance of NUV resists to DUV radiation. Chemical
ampliﬁed resists solve the problem of lower optical intensity, increasing the
sensitivity of the resist. They are based on the use of photoacid generators (PAG)
as photoactive compound. Upon exposure the PAG produces an acid, which
catalyzes the breaking of the protecting groups of the resin. As the acid actuates
as a catalyzer, the effect on enhancing the solubility of the resin is maintained after
the exposure. Concerning the requirement for transparency at DUV wavelength, the
resist
is
no
longer
based
on
Novolac
but
on
other
polymers like
poly
(hydroxystyrene).
2.2.2.2 Negative resists
For the most used class of negative tone photoresists, the exposure causes a cross-
linking of the polymer molecules, and, consequently, the solubility decreases in the
exposed volume. A review about negative resists can be found in [11].
2.2.2.3 Photoresist profile after exposure and development
The ﬁnal proﬁle of the resist is key to obtain the desired features on the sample after
pattern transfer. The resulting proﬁle is a function of the photoresist properties and
of the conditions of exposure and development and it is difﬁcult to predict. For the
resist properties, it is important to know the thickness, the optical refraction index,
the optical absorption and the resist contrast. These properties will deﬁne the
optimal conditions for exposure and development. Optimal conditions are those
allowing a perfectly-vertical proﬁle of the resist. Figure 2.7 schematizes the proﬁles
Nanofabrication
2-11

[Page 61]
that can be usually obtained for positive photoresist when the conditions diverge
from the optimal ones. Optimal conditions can be obtained from the datasheet of the
photoresist.
An additional factor inﬂuencing the ﬁnal proﬁle is the reﬂection of light at the
interface between the resist/sample interface. The additional dose arising from the
reﬂection causes an overexposure and, in consequence, a degradation of the
resolution. To avoid this effect, antireﬂection coatings are usually added to the
sample before the resist coating step.
2.2.2.4 Photoresist contrast curve
The contrast curve of a photoresist shows the dependence of the ratio of the
remaining photoresist thickness (d′/d0) as a function of the exposure dose (see
ﬁgure 2.8).
The photoresist contrast parameter, γ, is deﬁned according to the following

2.2.3 Extreme ultraviolet (EUV) optical lithography
As has been properly discussed in previous sections, the resolution of optical
lithography is proportional to the wavelength (λ) of the radiation. Thus, as early
as in the 1980s decade, it was proposed to use light with λ ~ 10 nm, that is, more than
one order or magnitude smaller than the ultraviolet radiation that has been used in
lithography for many decades. Initially, the possible use of this short wavelength
light was thought for proximity conﬁguration (see section 2.2.1), in the nominally
called soft x-ray proximity lithography. However, it was early noticed [13] that
projection could be the appropriate conﬁguration in order to obtain the necessary
high-quality lithography results. This technique is called EUV lithography [14, 15]
which, ﬁnally, after overcoming several challenges associated with the use of small λ
radiation, has recently become part of the commercial chip production system.
Those challenges have been related with each part of the lithography process: the
light source, the optical system, the mask, and the photoresist. Therefore, deep
modiﬁcations have been implemented in them with respect to those detailed in
previous sections, as will be described in the following.
2.2.3.1 EUV sources
As has been discussed in section 2.2.1, any light source used for optical lithography,
and, therefore, EUV radiation, must fulﬁl several requirements, related to its
stability, the homogeneity over the exposed area, and a high-enough intensity level
to transform the photosensitive resist.
Thus, in order to produce light with those characteristics and λ ~ 10 nm, it is
necessary to generate a high-energy plasma (typically in the order of ~105 K), as
other alternatives like synchrotron radiation are not very feasible for the solid angles
needed in wafer production. In any case, the production of a high-energy plasma
requires a special preparation. First of all, it must be considered that almost all the
matter absorbs the radiation at these wavelengths, so that it is mandatory to produce
and propagate the light in a system with the highest possible vacuum. Then, there
are two methods to generate this kind of dense plasmas: (i) by a quick pulsed
electrical discharge between two metallic electrodes in the presence of a ﬂow of a low
pressures gas or a jet of small liquid droplets, which is called discharge produced
plasma (DPP); (ii) by focusing a highly energetic laser on the ﬂow of liquid droplets,
which is called laser produced plasma (LPP). Nowadays, it is considered that LPP
method is more appropriate for chip lithography production lines due to its better
power scalability, while lower-cost DPP sources are planned for complementary
applications like mask inspection where low-power EUV radiation is used.
In general, to produce the required high-energy plasma, the presence of multi-
charged positive ions is necessary. Thus, xenon plasmas with typically Xe10+ ions
and, moreover, tin plasmas with ions ranging from Sn8+ to Sn13+ are generated to
get an EUV emission that can be very strong in a narrow band centered close to
λ = 13.5 nm. It is important to note that, in addition to the plasma production by
LPP method, the interaction of the laser beam with the tin droplets also results in tin
vapour and particulates that could quickly contaminate or, even more, coat the
Nanofabrication
2-13

[Page 63]
collector of the optical system that is close to the location where the plasma is
generated. Therefore, it is mandatory to implement a contamination mitigation
mechanism within the plasma production device (like the use of hydrogen as a buffer
gas in the chamber to efﬁciently decelerate the fast plasma ions and chemically react
with them, or the presence of a magnetic ﬁeld in order to guide and discharge the
ions along the magnetic lines of force) to avoid a too frequent replacement of the
optical collector interrupting the operation of the lithography system.
Then, in the LPP source of an EUV lithography system (see a sketch in ﬁgure 2.9),
a stream of Sn droplets is injected in an ultra-high-vacuum vessel from a heated
reservoir with highly-puriﬁed tin. The droplets have a diameter size of ~30 μm and
ﬂy at a speed larger than 50 m s−1, the ﬂow rate being several tens of thousands of
droplets per second. This droplet ﬂow is monitored with high-speed cameras in order
to ensure as much as possible the targeting of the droplets with the focused spot of
the laser pulses. The droplets are usually irradiated with a high-power CO2 laser
(~50 kW) that works, accordingly with the droplet ﬂow, at several tens of kHz.
Actually, in order to increase the efﬁciency of the LPP process, they are irradiated
twice; ﬁrst, the droplet receives a pre-pulse that modiﬁes its shape from a sphere-like
geometry to a ﬂattened disk; subsequently, the main laser pulse hits the deformed
droplet. It favours the conversion of the laser power into the plasma generation that
produces the EUV light. After it, the EUV radiation is collected by an ellipsoidal
mirror, focused in an intermediate focus, and, from there, transported out of the
(A) Droplet 
generator
(D) Laser
(E) EUV 
radiation
(F) 
Ellipsoidal 
collector
(G) Droplet 
catcher
(B) 
Vacuum 
chamber

Figure 2.9. Sketch of a LPP EUV-source. A stream of Sn droplets is injected from a generator (A) into the
vacuum chamber (B). The droplet position is monitored with cameras (C) to tune the targeting with the laser
pulse (D). It produces the plasma with EUV emission (E). With an ellipsoidal collector (F), this EUV radiation
is focused to transport it to the exposition area of the lithography system. In front of the droplet generator, a
droplet catcher (G) takes the droplet waste.
Nanofabrication
2-14

[Page 64]
source chamber by an optical system whose main characteristics are described in the
following. Finally, it must be noted that the conversion efﬁciency of the energy is
very low, approximately of about 5%; however, it can be enough to reach an output
power level of ~250 W.
2.2.3.2 EUV optical system
From the optical point of view, the fact that the radiation with λ ~ 10 nm is absorbed
for any solid material implies that the refractive optics (based on quartz or, even,
CaF2 lens) used in NUV or DUV stepper lithography systems no longer apply for
the short-wavelength EUV light. Instead, a reﬂective optics architecture must be
conﬁgured in order to transport the light and illuminate the wafers.
In this sense, although for λ < 30 nm the reﬂectance at normal incidence of any
natural material is only of ~1% as the refraction index is very close to 1, multilayer
structures made of two materials with very different absorption coefﬁcients can
result in much larger reﬂectance values. Thus, mirrors based on multilayer structures
are designed for EUV lithography, similarly to the case of x-ray telescopes in
astronomy. In particular, Mo/Si multilayers with the appropriate thicknesses and a
large number of Mo/Si bilayers (see sketch in ﬁgure 2.10) can provide reﬂectance
values up to 70% approximately for EUV radiation with λ = 13.5 nm, that is, the one
produced by the source of the lithography system [16]. Typically, the number (N) of
Mo/Si bilayers is of the order of 50. In general, for a constructive interference close
to normal incidence between the beams reﬂected at the different multilayer interface,
the thickness (t) of the Mo/Si bilayer must ﬁt t = λ/2, that is t ≈6.8 nm. To optimize
the reﬂectance, the thickness of the Mo layers is around a little more than half of the
thickness of the Si layers. Moreover, it is completely necessary that the deposition of
the layers is as perfect as possible in order to avoid losses at the interfaces due to
imperfections such as roughness or interdiffusion. Thus, the deposition of additional
ultrathin layer barriers of boron carbide at each interface results in a signiﬁcant
reduction of the interdiffusion and compound formation at the Mo/Si interfaces.
N  Mo/Si bilayers
Substrate
Mo
Si
Mo
Mo
Mo
Mo
Mo
Si
Si
Si
Si
Si
t
Figure 2.10. Sketch of the Mo/Si multilayer structure of the EUV mirrors. The number of bilayers is, typically,
N ~ 50. The thickness of each bilayer must ﬁt t ≈λ/2. To protect the multilayer from oxidation, a thin capping
layer of Ru is usually grown on top.
Nanofabrication
2-15

[Page 65]
Also, as the dispersion of the light due to roughness (σ) has, approximately, a
dependence ~ (σ/λ)2, considering the short EUV wavelength, the surfaces must
present a very high quality smoothness, with a roughness of only 0.1 nm (root mean
square). This requirement must be fulﬁlled along the complete curved surface of all
the mirrors of the optical system. This is a huge challenge taking into account that,
for example, the ellipsoidal collector of the EUV source has a large diameter of
about 600–650 mm to allow the collection of the EUV light emitted by the source in
a solid angle of ~5–5.5 steradians.
From the point of view of the architecture of the optical system, after the EUV
radiation has been redirected by the ellipsoidal mirror collector to the intermediate
focus aperture, the light arrives to the illumination optics part where, after several
reﬂections, it is focused on the EUV mask element and, from there, it is projected to
the wafers. In total, the number of multilayer mirrors where the EUV is reﬂected
from the source to the wafer in an EUV lithography system can be 12. In each
reﬂection, the incidence angle with respect to the mirror normal is small, about ~5°–
6°. The large number of reﬂections (n) implies that, although the reﬂectance in each
multilayer mirror is ~70%, the portion of the EUV light that arrives at the wafers
from the radiation source is small, (0.7)n ~ 0.01–0.02, that is, of only a few percent.
On the other hand, considering the resolution of the lithography system (see
equation (2.3)), the size of the multilayer mirrors together with their spatial
conﬁguration resulted in low values of the numerical aperture (NA ≈0.25) in the
ﬁrst EUV lithography tools. Increasing the surface size of the mirrors has led to
higher values of the NA and, therefore, to an improvement of the lithography
resolution. Thus, at the end of the 2010s decade, the ﬁrst EUV lithography
commercial systems were shipped having NA = 0.33, for a 13 nm resolution.
2.2.3.3 EUV lithography masks
Similarly to the other optical elements, due to the short wavelength of the radiation,
the mask objects for EUV lithography must work in reﬂection, so that they present a
complex structure that is very different from the masks used in NUV or DUV
optical lithography (see a sketch in ﬁgure 2.11).
First, as more than 30% of the EUV incident radiation is absorbed in the process,
the substrate of the mask must be of a material with a low thermal-expansion
coefﬁcient, such as TiO2-doped SiO2 amorphous glass, in order to prevent the
distortion of the image when the mask is heated by the energy of the absorbed light.
On top of the substrate, a Mo/Si multilayer with ~50 bilayers is deposited to act as a
reﬂector for the EUV 13.5 nm light, in the same way as in other lenses of the optical
system. This multilayer is capped with a thin layer of a protection material, like Ru,
in order to prevent oxidation as well to stop the subsequent etching process that will
be done on top to deﬁne the desired pattern. For this purpose, a ﬁlm of an EUV
absorber material such as tantalum nitride (TaN) or tantalum boron nitride (TaBN)
is grown on the protected multilayer. Also, it is usual to deposit a DUV
antireﬂection coating (ARC), for example a Ta oxide, to improve the inspection
contrast. Then, the absorber/ARC is patterned by electron beam lithography and an
etching process to deﬁne the desired design to be transferred to the wafer. Thus, the
Nanofabrication
2-16

[Page 66]
absorber patterned regions act as a ‘black’ area for the transmission of the EUV
light, while the free Mo/Si multilayered regions work as if they were ‘transparent’ for
the EUV radiation propagation. Finally, in the backside of the substrate, a coating
thin layer of a conducting material like CrN is deposited to help the electrostatic
ﬁtting to the mask stage.
It is important to note that, for the short wavelength of the EUV light, any tiny
defect in height causes a local difference in the reﬂection and, as a consequence, a
phase defect appears in the pattern transferred to the wafer. Therefore, it is
mandatory that the substrates are extremely smooth and highly defect-free before
the deposition of the Mo/Si reﬂective multilayer, as later it would not be possible to
repair them. Only defects associated with the absorber patterning can be relatively
straightforwardly ﬁxed, but it is not the case for the defects related with a height
inhomogeneity in the Mo/Si multilayer; in this latter case, it is only possible to detect
the defect area to avoid the use of that region for patterning. On the other hand, as
any material absorbs EUV radiation, after the fabrication of the mask it cannot
easily be protected with a pellicle like in the case for other optical UV lithography
masks. Then, without this protection, any dirt or contamination particle that
appears is directly in contact with the patterned reﬂective mask. Although it could
be carefully cleaned, it would imply a shortening of the mask lifetime.
Finally, another problem is related with the 3D aspect ratio of the mask; in this
sense, it must be considered that, for high performance, the thickness of the absorber
layer must be, at least, ~50 nm. Thus, due to the reﬂective optics conﬁguration, there
is a ﬁnite angle of incidence of the EUV radiation with respect to the substrate
normal; this angle is small, of ~6°, but results in shadowing effects at the edges of the
patterned structures that must be taken into account when the mask pattern is
deﬁned.
Figure 2.11. Sketch of the cross section of the structure of a EUV lithography photomask.
Nanofabrication
2-17

[Page 67]
2.2.3.4 Photoresist for EUV lithography
The use of EUV radiation also has implications in the characteristics required for the
photoresist [15]. First, after all the losses in the radiation power along the system, the
dose at the wafer is typically less than half of the one used in DUV lithography for
similar exposition times, requiring a resist with a low clearance dose to change its
solubility.
On the other hand, considering the chemical process itself, as discussed in
section 2.2.2, in the case of DUV lithography it is necessary to increase the
sensitivity of the resist by using PAG photoactive compounds that catalyse the
breaking of the groups of the resin, improving its solubility. For EUV radiation,
the process is different. Now, the energy of the EUV photons is much higher. As a
consequence, they are not absorbed by functional groups but by individual atoms.
Thus, the immediate result of the photon absorption is not a molecule in an excited
state, but the emission of a highly energetic electron from the individual atom. This
emitted electron then interacts with other atoms and functional groups when
travelling across the resist, with the possibility of secondary electron generation.
Both, primary and secondary electrons, can trigger the PAG catalytic action.
Finally, this fact helps the sensitization process of the resist as only a small fraction
of the radiation is absorbed directly by the PAG molecules, but they are still
activated by the electrons coming from other atoms in the whole resist layer.
Conversely, the effect of the secondary electrons can result in a noise contribution
to the line-edge roughness. Actually, each EUV absorbed photon changes the
solubility of a certain volume of the resist, which is called blur. A higher resist
resolution implies a smaller blur, but smaller blur indicates lower number of
reactions and, therefore, slower photospeed, so that there is a trade-off between
line roughness and photospeed.
Besides, in order to avoid the collapse of close patterned structures with high
aspect ratios as well as to work with a lower depth of focus, it is convenient that the
thickness of the resist layer is not larger than ~20–30 nm. For these values, it is a real
challenge to reach the appropriate light absorbance level. Then, it is necessary the
use of resists based on metallic nanoparticles or organometallic compounds, given
the high absorbance of heavy atoms.
Despite all the difﬁculties, very high resolution has been reached with EUV
lithography and, nowadays, there is commercial equipment shipped by ASML
company which, with a numerical aperture of 0.33 in the optical system and a dose
of 20 mJ cm−2, is able to produce 125 wafers (300 mm in diameter, with 96 dies) per
hour. It has become competitive as it simpliﬁes the multi-patterning immersion
process associated with high resolution DUV lithography, and, therefore, reducing the
relative cost per patterned pixel. With a further improvement in some of the
parameters (an enhancement of the conversion efﬁciency of the EUV radiation, an
increase in the numerical aperture of the optical system, the implementation of some
phase-shift strategies in the mask, better tuned resist characteristics, …) it is expected
that in the next years the EUV resolution can be further moved to reach the 7 or 5 nm
node values.
Nanofabrication
2-18

[Page 68]
2.3 Maskless optical lithography
2.3.1 Direct laser lithography
The high resolution and throughput of photomask-based optical lithography come
at a great cost due to the complexity and high price of steppers as well as the long
fabrication time and cost of masks. In 1989, a new method was proposed to expose a
photoresist without the need of optical masks [17]. This method, called direct write
lithography (DWL), relies on a laser beam, which can be focused down to 1 μm or
less, and an XY-moving stage holding the resist-covered sample, as sketched in
ﬁgure 2.12. In DWL, the light produced by the laser exposes the resist in a sequential
way across the full area of the sample. Thus, in DWL the total exposure time is
much longer than in conventional optical lithography using a photomask. The
simple scheme shown in ﬁgure 2.12 is indeed more complicated in commercial
equipment in order to enhance the writing speed. In the ﬁrst publication on DWL by
Rensch et al [17], the lens system included a rotating polygon mirror that allowed a
fast raster scan of the laser beam along one direction, being in practice a laser
scanner. Another approach to produce a fast raster scan of the laser beam along one
direction is to use an acousto-optic modulator. More recently, other approaches
have been introduced in DWL technology. For example, the use of a spatial light
modulator (SLM) placed after the laser allows the dynamic projection of a given
pattern on a working area with typical linear length of a few hundreds of microns.
Figure 2.12. (a) Sketch of DWL. Light from a laser beam is projected through the lenses and the mirror
reﬂection onto the photoresist in order to produce its exposure. A computerized XY stage allows scanning the
full sample area sequentially. The CCD camera allows imaging the sample. (b) In some DWL systems, the
laser is raster scanned along one direction using a rotating mirror or an acousto-optic modulator. (c) In some
DWL systems, an array of micro-machined mirrors acts as a dynamic mask, projecting a pattern on a small
area (typically <1 mm2). Adapted and reprinted from [19] MDPI.
Nanofabrication
2-19

[Page 69]
Once the targeted pattern has been imprinted in the resist on a small area (<1 mm2),
the stage is moved to the next working area. An SLM can be considered a dynamic
mask that can be changed from a certain working area to the next one. SLMs can be
based on liquid-crystal technology or on an array of micro-machined mirrors
(sometimes known as digital light processor, DLP, or digital mirror device,
DMD) [18]. Nowadays, both concepts, that of a well-focused laser beam scanning
across the sample and that of an SLM projecting a dynamic mask, can be found in
commercial DWL equipment.
The resolution achievable in patterns exposed by DWL is ultimately limited by
the light diffraction, thus, of the order of the wavelength of the laser. Typical laser
wavelengths used in DWL are around 400 nm. As a consequence, the same or
similar photoresists to the ones commonly used in NUV optical lithography are used
in DWL. Processes have been developed using either positive resists or negative
ones. In ﬁgure 2.13, two examples of 2D structures patterned by DWL are shown. In
ﬁgure 2.13(a), representative SEM micrographs of high aspect ratio (AR, up to 40)
microstructures after DWL exposure, post-exposure bake (85 °C) and development
are shown. In this example, an epoxy negative-tone resist and a laser wavelength of
413 nm have been used [20]. In ﬁgure 2.13(b), SEM and AFM images of a process
for the fabrication of x-ray diffraction gratings are shown. The top SEM micrograph
Figure 2.13. (a) SEM micrographs of high aspect ratio microstructures fabricated by DWL. The bottom image
contains microstructures with widths of 1 and 2 μm and height of 40 μm. Reproduced from [20]. Copyright
IOP Publishing. Reproduced with permission. All rights reserved. (b) Fabrication of x-ray diffraction gratings
by DWL with 600 lines/mm. The top image is an SEM micrograph of the resist after exposure and
development whereas the bottom image is an AFM measurement of the structure after O2 plasma etching
and resist removal. Reprinted from [21], copyright SPIE.
Nanofabrication
2-20

[Page 70]
displays the 550 nm thick photoresist (S1805) on Si after exposure with line
periodicity of 600 lines/mm (line width ~0.8 μm) and development [21]. The bottom
AFM image has been taken in the same area after oxygen plasma, resist removal
and cleaning steps.
In general, the advantages of DWL compared to mask-based optical lithography
are the cheaper equipment and the absence of physical masks, making the technique
affordable and ﬂexible. On the other hand, the disadvantages are the long resist
exposure times and the worse resolution when compared to projection optical
lithography described in the previous sections. DWL has been a commonly used
technique in last years for the following applications:
• fabrication of optical masks and nanoimprint templates [22];
• fabrication of MEMS [20];
• fabrication of micro-optics components like diffraction gratings, waveguides
and microlenses [23];
• fabrication of microﬂuidic channels [24];
• fabrication of electrical contact pads and micro-contacts [25];
• patterning of magnetic or superconducting materials [26];
• fabrication of sensors [27].
Interesting developments in DWL have brought about the fabrication of non-ﬂat
structures, for instance exploiting the gray-scale lithography capabilities of the
technique. In gray-scale DWL, each pixel is addressed individually, providing the
desired light intensity (typically up to 1000 gray levels are used). As a result, the resist
thickness after exposure and development can be tailored from zero up to the
maximum value. This is very useful to fabricate certain structures such as micro-
lens arrays, MEMS or other mechanical devices containing several heights, blazed
diffraction gratings, light diffusers and reﬂectors, microﬂuidic channels, etc.
Moreover, 3D structures have been achieved by one-photon absorption DWL when
working in an ultra-low absorption regime [28]. In this case, at each incidence point
the laser is focused at different heights with different light intensity. However, the two-
photon absorption technique to be described in the next section is more appropriate
for the fabrication of 3D structures as it allows a higher lateral resolution.
DWL commercial equipment can be purchased from companies such as
Heidelberg Instruments or Durham Magneto Optics Ltd. A great variety of DWL
products are sold, from tabletop equipment for exposure of small substrates with
resolution down to 0.5 μm to large equipment that is capable of exposure on a
substrate size of 140 cm × 140 cm with writing resolution down to 0.75 μm. Such
large equipment is suitable for photomask fabrication to be subsequently applied for
the fabrication of large display panels, LEDs or touch screens. Interestingly, in the
same equipment, DWL can be combined with a scanning-probe-lithography (SPL)
module, providing the large-area lithography capabilities of DWL and the high-
resolution capabilities of SPL [29]. Moreover, the mix and match concept can be
used to take advantage of the complementarity of DWL with other techniques such
as electron beam lithography or nanoimprint lithography [30].
Nanofabrication
2-21

[Page 71]
Another strategy derived from DWL is the realization of interference laser
lithography, in which the initial laser beam is split into two beams, which are
driven to interference on the photoresist at an angle 2Θ. We show two different
approaches used to generate interference patterns on the photoresist, as shown in
ﬁgure 2.14. In the ﬁrst approach, see ﬁgure 2.14(a), the light from a laser beam is
split into two laser beams, which are driven to interference on the photoresist. In this
conﬁguration, the modulation of the light intensity proﬁle is given by λ/(2*sinΘ),
producing line-shape patterns with the same periodicity. If the sample is rotated 90°
and a second exposure is carried out, a chess-like pattern is produced. In the second
approach, a double iris at the exit of a laser generates two coherent laser beams,
which are driven to interference on the photoresist. In the sketch shown in
ﬁgure 2.14(b), one can see that besides the angle 2Θ formed by the laser beams, it
is possible to modify the α and β angles, which deﬁne the sample position with
respect to the light incidence angle. The 2D square structure displayed in
ﬁgure 2.14(c) shows periodicity of 1 μm and has been fabricated with Θ = 9.35°
and λ = 325 nm with two consecutive exposures with α = 0° and α = 90° whilst
keeping β = 0°. On the other hand, the 3D square structure displayed in
ﬁgure 2.14(d) shows periodicity of 2 μm and has been fabricated with Θ = 7.38°
and λ = 514 nm with three consecutive exposures with (α,β) = (60°, 0°), (0°, 30°) and
Figure 2.14. Two different approaches to carry out interference laser lithography are shown. (a) In the ﬁrst
approach, the light from a laser beam is split into two laser beams, which are driven to interference on the
photoresist. In this conﬁguration, the obtained line periodicity is given by λ/(2*sinΘ). Reproduced from [33].
Copyright (2007), with permission from Elsevier. (b) In the second approach, a double iris generates two laser
beams, which are driven to interference on the photoresist. (c) An example of a 2D structure fabricated
following the strategy shown in (b) and using the angle values mentioned in the text. (d) An example of a 3D
structure fabricated following the strategy shown in (b) and using the angle values mentioned in the text.
Adapted with permission from [34]. Copyright The Optical Society.
Nanofabrication
2-22

[Page 72]
(180°, 30°). Other more complex interference laser lithography schemes have been
developed [31], which have even achieved sub-100 nm resolution [32].
2.3.2 Two-photon lithography
The process resolution limit imposed by the laser wavelength in DWL (based on the
underlying one-photon absorption mechanism) can be overcome by two-photon
absorption lithography, as proposed by Wu et al [35]. For a given photoresist, the
number of photons absorbed per laser pulse and per molecule (n) is given by the
following formula:
λ
=
δ
τ
ℏ
n
p
f
c
NA
2
(2.6)
p
0
2
2
2
2
⎛
⎝⎜
⎞
⎠⎟
where p0 is the time-averaged laser power, δ is the two-photon absorption (TPA)
cross section, τ is the pulse duration, fp is the pulse repetition rate, NA is the lens
numerical aperture, and λ is the laser wavelength [36]. Due to the dependence of n
with (p0)2, the two-photon absorption is non-linear, which can be used to generate
signiﬁcant absorption only in the very small volume of the photoresist where the
laser is focused. The TPA phenomenon is sketched in ﬁgure 2.15 and was
theoretically predicted by Maria Göppert-Mayer in 1931 [37] and experimentally
demonstrated with the advent of lasers [38]. At the laser focusing point, the infrared
laser light intensity is high enough to generate a substantial TPA, which provides an
energy equivalent to that of a UV photon with twice the energy of a single infrared
photon. When the TPA takes place, it will trigger the chemical reaction that will
change the solubility of the photoresist in the developer. As the laser focus can be
displaced in X, Y and Z with respect to the substrate, complex 3D structures can be
fabricated with resolution beyond the diffraction limit. As will be shown later, 3D
structures with resolution better than 100 nm are doable by TPA lithography.
The important elements in TPA exposure are the following ones:
• The laser: a popular one is a Ti:sapphire laser with λ ∼800 nm, typical pulse
width of tens to hundreds of femtoseconds and repetition rates ∼80 MHz,
with average output power around one watt. Nevertheless, continuous-wave
(532 nm wavelength) lasers have also been employed for producing 3D
structures by TPA lithography [42].
• The resists: it can be a positive or a negative resist, but most of the processes
rely on negative resists. The negative resists consist of a pre-polymer resin
containing a photo-initiator that can be excited by a TPA (equivalent energy
to one UV photon). The excited photo-initiator starts a chemical reaction that
leads to the resist photo-polymerization around the exposed regions. Several
resists have been employed for TPA lithography [36]. One popular negative
resists for TPA lithography is SU8, which can be cast on ﬁlms up to 500 μm
thickness and can yield structures with high aspect ratio [43]. Positive resists
are useful in certain applications like in the fabrication of 3D microﬂuidic
channels [44].
Nanofabrication
2-23

[Page 73]
• The post-exposure steps. Each resist needs an appropriate developer, and, in
many cases, an additional post-baking step is required.
Once the TPA exposure and development has revealed a 3D polymer structure,
two routes can be followed. In the ﬁrst route, the polymer is functional and can be
straightforwardly used for an application. This is the case when the polymer stands
for structural, mechanical, magnetic or optical properties. A few examples are
shown in ﬁgure 2.16. In ﬁgures 2.16(a) and (b), the polymer constitutes a 3D scaffold
structure around which cell growth can proliferate. In ﬁgure 2.16(c), the polymer is
structured as a mechanical cantilever resonator. In ﬁgure 2.16(d) the hybrid nature
of the photoresist (polymer plus magnetic nanoparticles) provides magnetic proper-
ties to the micro-swimmers. In ﬁgure 2.16(e), the fabricated ring polymer, coupled to
a laser ﬁber, can be used for ultrasound detection.
In the second route, the polymer is not the functional material and another step is
required to grow the material of interest either inside or around the 3D-structured
Figure 2.15. (a) Mechanism of resist exposure by two-photon absorption. In a very small area of the resist, the
intensity of a well-focused laser can be high enough to produce the simultaneous absorption of two infrared
photons, with energy equivalent to that of a single UV photon. This effect brings about the exposure of the
UV-sensitive resist in a very small volume. Reprinted from [39]. Copyright John Wiley & Sons. (b) The
displacement of the laser focus inside the photoresist along the X, Y, and Z coordinates allows the precise 3D
exposure of the resist, producing a 3D structure after resist development. The scale bar is 2 μm. Reprinted by
permission from Springer Nature [40]. Copyright (2001) and from [41], Copyright (2010), with permission from
Elsevier.
Nanofabrication
2-24

[Page 74]
polymer. In ﬁgure 2.17, two examples of this are shown in which positive resists are
used. In both cases, after resist coating, exposure and development, holes with a 3D
structure are created in the resist. An electrodeposition or electroplating process is
capable of ﬁlling in these holes with the metal of interest. For the success of this step,
the substrate material should be conductive and the transparent indium tin oxide
(ITO) material is an ideal choice. In the last step, the resist is plasma or wet etched.
On the other hand, if negative resists are used, the TAP exposure produces a
polymeric 3D scaffold structure that can be coated with a functional material by
means of a conformal coating technique such as atomic layer deposition, sputtering,
chemical vapour deposition, etc. For example, strong, lightweight and recoverable
3D ceramic nanolattices have been grown following this approach [49].
The resolution of TPA lithography is essentially governed by the volume of the
region exposed with high intensity by the laser beam (voxel), which will change its
Figure 2.16. Examples of 3D structures imprinted on a photopolymer by TPA lithography: (a) Sea-shell 3D
polymer structures patterned by TPA lithography and growth of neural cells after 7 d of culture. Reproduced
from [45]. Copyright IOP Publishing. Reproduced with permission. All rights reserved. (b) 3D polymer
scaffolds patterned by TPA lithography and growth of bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells after 2 d of
culture. Reprinted from [46], Copyright (2017), with permission from Elsevier. (c) High-aspect-ratio cantilever
obtained from TPA lithography on an acrylic resin. Reprinted from [36]. (d) Micro-swimmer fabricated by
TPA lithography whose composition is a nanocomposite of a polymer and magnetic nanoparticles so that it
feels magnetic forces. Reprinted from [47], copyright John Wiley & Sons. (e) Fabrication by TPA lithography
of a 3D polymer micro-ring resonator integrated with a ﬁber laser. Reprinted with permission from [48].
Copyright The Optical Society.
Nanofabrication
2-25

[Page 75]
solubility in the developer. Important parameters determining this volume will be
the spatial distribution of the laser intensity in the photoresist (determined by the
quality of the laser and the optical lenses used to focus the laser beam on a small
point), the two-photon absorption cross section of the resist and the dimensions and
spatial distributions of the photoinitiators, pre-polymer and polymer molecules.
Sub-100 nm resolution is possible using photoresists such as for example SCR500,
SU8 and ORMOCER [52]. By means of optimization processes, certain structures
such as nanorods have been obtained with 30 nm resolution in SU8 [53] and 15 nm
in SCR500 [52]. In the latter case, see ﬁgure 2.18(a), such ultrahigh resolution has
been achieved by means of a repolymerization process in which a nanorod receives
directly a low light dose (not enough to be later revealed in the development process)
but indirectly (via close exposure areas) receives an additional dose so that the
structure is revealed in the development process. In addition, post-processing steps
such as plasma etching and pyrolysis can further reduce the structure dimensions, as
shown in ﬁgure 2.18(b).
In general, the advantages of TPA lithography with respect to related techniques
is the high resolution, the capability for 3D patterning and the absence of photo-
masks. On the other hand, the main disadvantage of the technique is the long
exposure times due to its sequential nature.
Figure 2.17. Two examples of TPA lithography with subsequent growth of the functional material. (a) On the
left side, the four steps to create four connected magnetic nanowires: positive photoresist coating (1),
photoresist exposure and development (2), electrodeposition of the magnetic material (3), resist etching and
cleaning (4). On the right side, the result of the process is shown. Reprinted from [50], copyright (2018) with
permission from Springer. (b) On the left side, polymer templates fabricated by TPA lithography are shown.
On the right side, the result of the process after gold electrodeposition and polymer etching is shown. Reprinted
from [51], copyright John Wiley & Sons.
Nanofabrication
2-26

[Page 76]
The main applications of TPA lithography nowadays are in the following
domains:
• Micro- and nano-optics related with photonic crystals: micro-lenses, Fresnel
lenses, non-planar or suspended components of waveguide structures, linear
couplers, Y splitters, Mach-Zender interferometers, diffraction gratings,
metamaterials, etc [36, 55, 56].
• Micro-mechanics for the fabrication of strong ultralight structures [49],
cantilevers, nanotweezers, microrotors, MEMS, etc [39].
• Micro-ﬂuidics for the fabrication of channels and integration in sensors [56].
• Biology for the fabrication of microneedles and 3D scaffolds for studies on
tissue growth [57].
• Electronics for the fabrication of conductive materials, microinductors or
microcoils [36, 39].
• Magnetic materials with 3D spin textures [50].
A few companies commercialize TPA lithography equipment, such as Nanoscribe
and Multiphoton Optics. The existing equipment can address 3D exposure patterns
with either large or small features, from the millimeter range to the sub-200 nm
range.
2.4 Directed self-assembly (DSA)
Optical lithography presents the limitation of the maximum resolution achievable, if
throughput and reasonable cost of ownership is maintained at levels compatible with
the progress of the semiconductor industry. Present lithography-based alternatives
to optical lithography do not guarantee their incorporation into the high volume
manufacturing chain because of their performance in terms of low throughput or
high defectivity.
A change of paradigm is to introduce self-assembly and bottom-up concepts: the
use of small building blocks to realize patterns (see chapter 1). In this case,
the resolution is dictated by the size of the building blocks. Combination of
Figure 2.18. (a) Sub-25 nm resolution obtained by TPA lithography on suspended ﬁbers of SCR500 resist by
repolymerization. Reprinted from [52] with the permission of AIP Publishing. (b) Reduction of the bar width
in a buckyball via a two-step process (the scale bar is 1 μm): (1) TAP exposure of SZ2080 resist and
development and (2) ten cycles of an oxygen plasma etching process. Reprinted from [54]. Copyright (2018),
with permission from Elsevier.
Nanofabrication
2-27

[Page 77]
self-assembly with lithography to position and align the building blocks at the
desired location is known as directed self-assembly (DSA).
DSA can be implemented in multiple ways, involving different kinds of nano-
meter scale objects as building blocks: carbon nanotubes, silicon nanowires, single
molecules, DNA origami, among others.
One of the DSA methods that have caught more interest by the industry has been
the DSA of block co-polymers, for several reasons such as: its good compatibility
with the materials and methods of the semiconductor industry, its simplicity in terms
of processing, its inherent high throughput and the demonstration of sub-5 nm
resolution. At the time of writing this chapter, DSA is considered as a future
complementary method to EUV optical lithography, aiming to improve line edge
roughness and decrease process complexity for the most advanced manufacturing
nodes in integrated circuit manufacturing.
In this section, the principles of DSA of block copolymers are presented, along
with recent examples of strategies for alignment of the block copolymers by
combination with optical lithography. One of the principal problems to date is
the insufﬁciently low defect density for the integration of block copolymer
lithography in high volume manufacturing processes [58].
2.4.1 Principles of DSA of block copolymers
Block copolymers are macromolecules constituted by two or more chemically
different polymer chains covalently bonded together to one single macromolecule
[59]. The chemically different blocks can either be merged in a linear or in a
branched manner. In that sense, a great variety of different block copolymer
structures may be thought of.
Driven by the repulsive force between chemically different molecules, the chains
self-assemble in periodic structures in the size range between few nanometers and
few tens of nanometers predominantly consisting of alternating phases of only one
component A or B (ﬁgure 2.19). A quantiﬁcation of the repulsive interaction
between two blocks is given by the Flory–Huggins interaction parameter χ, based
on the works of Flory and Huggins [60, 61]:
χ

)]

(2.7)
