Figure 2.19. Microphase separation in block copolymers: (a) difference between molecular order in a diblock
copolymer in disordered state (left) and the microphase separated state (right), (b) molecular architecture of a
PS-b-PMMA (polystyrene-block-poly(methyl methacrylate)) block co-polymer, with PS on the left side (red
colour) and PMMA on the right side (blue colour).
Nanofabrication
2-28

[Page 78]
where Z is the number of nearest neighbors of a unit and εij is the interaction energy
between two monomers i and j.
In general, the microphase separation in block copolymers is achieved in two
different ways [62]. On the one hand, thermal annealing can be used to heat the
block copolymers above their glass transition temperature, enhancing their chain
mobility in this way [63] and inducing microphase separation. Solvent annealing, on
the other hand, is the second strategy used to induce the self-assembly of block
copolymers [64]. The selectivity of the solvent plays a role in the orientation of the
block copolymer domains with respect to the substrate [65–67].
The most studied block copolymer architecture is A-b-B block copolymers, which
show a well-studied phase behavior, as shown in ﬁgure 2.20. In the phase diagram,
χN, where N is NA + NB, with NA and NB being, respectively, the number of
monomers of polymer A and polymer B, and χ is the Flory–Huggins interaction
parameter, is represented as a function of f, which is the ratio NA/N [68][69]. The
theoretical phase diagram of A-b-B diblock copolymers has been determined by
means of Monte Carlo simulations in [70] (see ﬁgure 2.20).
A symmetric block copolymer self-assembles in lamellar structures (denoted L),
whereas upon increasing the fraction of one polymer, fa, the material will ﬁrst pass to
a gyroid morphology and then to cylinders in a matrix of material B, before it will
ﬁnally self-assemble in spheres in a matrix of material B (see ﬁgure 2.20(b)).
Microphase separation occurs for all materials that are above the order–disorder
transition line in the phase diagram. The curve indicating the order–disorder
transition in the phase diagram is parabolic with a global minimum at χN = 10.4
for perfectly symmetric block copolymers. Based on that, microphase separation for
PS-b-PMMA block copolymers at 160 °C is a priori precluded for materials
consisting of less than 251 monomers. The smallest reported full pitch (total length
Figure 2.20. Phase behavior of diblock copolymers. (a) Phase diagram of diblock copolymers according to
Monte Carlo simulations. Reprinted from [70], with permission from AIP publishing. The parameter f (x-axis)
indicates the weight fraction of the chain of one of the blocks, for example PS. The term χN (y-axis) represents
the product of the Flory–Huggins interaction parameter χ and the chain polymerization N. Lamellar self-
assembly is expected in the areas denoted ‘L’ and the cylindrical morphology in the areas denoted ‘C’. Less
frequently used morphologies are the spherical and the gyroid phase, denoted ‘S’ and ‘G’, respectively. (b)
Sketches of the block copolymer self-assembly morphologies upon changing the relative fraction, f. Reprinted
from [71], Copyright (2007) with permission from Elsevier.
Nanofabrication
2-29

[Page 79]
of one repetition unit) obtained for PS-b-PMMA is 17.5 nm [72] or 19 nm [73],
although the utility of sub-22 nm pitch PS-b-PMMA materials for pattern transfer
processes is questioned by some authors [74].
For the energy minimization process in block copolymer thin ﬁlms, in general
four energy components are taken into account [50] (see ﬁgure 2.21(a)), namely
(1) the energy stored in the chain stretching/compression other than the radius of
gyration, (2) the A–B interface curvature, which contributes with a negligibly low
amount of free energy, (3) the interface free energy between the blocks A and B and
(4) the surface energy at the block copolymer–air and the block copolymer–substrate
interface. The self-assembly morphology is always such that the sum of the four free
energy components is minimal. For the self-assembly of thin ﬁlms in unconﬁned
states (e.g., on energetically homogenous, non-patterned substrates) the chain will
stretch such that pattern pitch minimizes the chain extension and the total surface
energy between the blocks A and B.
In the case of lamellar block copolymers, the decision about whether the material
self-assembles in vertical or horizontal lamellae (see ﬁgure 2.21(b)) depends on the
interface energy between the substrate, the material properties of the two blocks and
their interaction energy with the surface. Mansky et al have found that the energy at
the block copolymer-substrate interface of PS-b-PMMA can be tuned by using PS-r-
PMMA random copolymer brushes [75]. Random copolymer brushes whose
monomer ratio is similar to that of the block copolymer in question can be used
as energy neutralization layers and promote upright standing features. If one of the
blocks, however, wets the random copolymer brush layer in an energetically clearly
favorable manner, the block copolymer is assembled in horizontal features.
2.4.2 Strategies for alignment of block copolymers
The absence of long-range order and placement accuracy precludes, however, any
lithography application of the material in the semiconductor industry in its as-spin-
coated and annealed state. It is therefore crucial to induce long-range order in a
controlled manner. Guiding patterns that are usually deﬁned by top-down
Figure 2.21. Free energy in block copolymer thin ﬁlms. (a) Sketch providing an overview of free energy
contributions to the total free energy in a block copolymer thin ﬁlm, (b) vertically (left) and horizontally (right)
aligned block copolymer thin ﬁlms. When the interaction energy of the surface is similar for the two blocks,
vertically aligned lamellae are obtained, which appear as ﬁngerprint patterns in SEM images. If one of the
blocks wets preferentially the surface, then the lamellae self-assemble horizontally, without generation of any
surface pattern.
Nanofabrication
2-30

[Page 80]
lithography are required to induce long-range order in the material. In the following
we will brieﬂy refer to the most commonly used techniques to direct the self-
assembly of block copolymers: graphoepitaxy and chemoepitaxy, as it is sketched in
ﬁgure 2.22.
2.4.2.1 Graphoepitaxy
Directing the self-assembly of block copolymers with topographical patterns is
referred to as graphoepitaxy. The technique aims at fabricating topographical
structures in such a way that they energetically favour the block copolymer to
self-assemble in the desired structures, as depicted in ﬁgure 2.22(a). The energetic
advantage of ordered (e.g. directed) self-assembly in the created trenches with
respect to a disordered self-assembly is in the preferential wetting of guiding pattern
walls by one of the blocks. Furthermore, the creation of defects in block copolymers
assembled in commensurate trenches imposes a free energy penalty, which converts
the self-assembly in lamellae parallel to the guiding pattern walls into the minimum-
free-energy state. The probability to form defects increases with the distance between
the guiding pattern features because of the ﬁnite correlation length of the block
copolymers far away from the order-inducing surface.
This principle has been successfully employed by a number of authors, as for
example reported in references [76–78]. The best results are obtained when the width
of the conﬁning trench is exactly or close to an integer multiple of the natural pitch
of the block copolymer [79]. A suitable guiding pattern design permits, for example,
to direct the self-assembly of block copolymers in device-oriented features [80] and
induce long-range order in sub-10 nm half-pitch block copolymers [81] and sub-5 nm
half-pitch liquid crystals [82]. Another widely-used approach to make use of
cylindrical block copolymers is contact hole shrink applications [83].
2.4.2.2 Chemoepitaxy
The chemoepitaxy approach relies on the introduction of chemical patterns into an
underlying substrate (see ﬁgure 2.22(b)). A lithographic process is applied to locally
Figure 2.22. Scheme of most common techniques to direct the self-assembly of block copolymers: (a)
graphoepitaxy; (b) chemoepitaxy.
Nanofabrication
2-31

[Page 81]
modify the interface free energy between the block copolymer and the underlayer in
such a way that certain regions are more attractive to one of the blocks than to the
other. A characteristic feature of the chemoepitaxy approach is that the guiding
pattern must have a pitch close to η-times larger than the natural pitch of the block
copolymer. The factor η is an integer referred to as density multiplication factor of
the guiding pattern. The critical dimension of the guiding pattern features has to
correspond to the half-pitch of the block copolymer [84] or 1.5 times the block
copolymer pitch [85].
Several materials can be used as background layers to create the guiding patterns:
self-assembled monolayers [86], HSQ resist [87] and end-grafted homopolymers or
random copolymer brushes [84]. The most widely used technique consists of using
end-grafted homopolymers or random copolymer brushes (see ﬁgure 2.23).
Different approaches are used to create chemical guiding patterns. Ruiz et al
reported a viable technique based on the use of EBL and oxygen plasma
functionalization for cylindrical block copolymers with pattern density multiplica-
tion of 2L0 [89]. Later on, Cheng et al developed a technique to increase the density
multiplication factor of a lamellar PS-b-PMMA BCP, based on 193 nm immersion
lithography [87]. In order to achieve the defectivity level required by the industry,
Liu and Nealey in 2011 developed the so-called LiNe process. In this process, two
materials with different chemical afﬁnities are employed to create the chemical
guiding pre-patterns.
As shown in the scheme from ﬁgure 2.23, the process ﬂow starts by spin-coating a
cross-linkable polystyrene (X-PS) on a substrate and after a lithography step, the
material is etched. Then, a random hydroxyl terminated poly(styrene-b-methyl
methacrylate) (PS-r-PMMA-OH) brush is spin-coated and grafted to the substrate
between the X-PS guiding stripes, creating thus a chemical pattern. The X-PS
guiding stripes are more afﬁne to PS domains while the PS-r-PMMA background
stripes have equal afﬁnity for both domains [90].
Figure 2.23. Sketch of DSA based on chemical epitaxy using an alternate pattern of two polymers. Reprinted
with permission from [88]. Copyright (2011) American Chemical Society.
Nanofabrication
2-32

[Page 82]
Another common path to manipulate the surface free energy of a PS-r-PMMA
random copolymer brush layer is the combination of a lithography step with its
exposure to UV light or an oxygen plasma [89].
2.4.3 Applications
The periodic structures deﬁned in that way can be used as bottom-up templates for
nanofabrication processes that require very high resolution, such as bit patterned
media for hard disk drives [91–93], integrated circuit ﬁnFETs [80], contact holes [83]
and patterning of 2D materials [94].
2.5 End-of-chapter summary
Optical lithography is the most employed lithography method. Optical radiation is
used to expose a photosensitive material (photoresist) in selected areas. Because of
the exposure, the photosensitive materials experience a change in their physical and
chemical properties, varying their solubility with respect to a speciﬁc solvent. If the
solubility of the photoresist increases upon exposure, the resist is named positive
resist. Conversely, when the exposed photoresist areas remain after development, the
photoresist is called negative.
In optical lithography, diffraction phenomena limit the resolution. As diffraction
is directly dependent on the wavelength of the optical radiation, the shorter the
wavelength, the better will be the resolution.
The most common way to perform an optical lithography process is by means of
an optical mask. An optical mask consists of a transparent substrate, on which
opaque areas deﬁne the patterns that will be transferred to the substrate. Depending
on the position of the mask with respect to the sample, three modes of optical
lithography exist: contact, proximity and projection.
Projection optical lithography is the way to obtain the better resolution. Over the
years, multiple tricks have been implemented to improve the resolution: decreasing
the wavelength, increasing the numerical aperture, engineering the illumination,
optical proximity correction, phase shifting masks, among others.
Extreme ultraviolet optical lithography is a particular case of optical lithography
in which the wavelength of the optical radiation is 13.5 nm. Major challenges have
been solved to make it practical: the type of sources, the optical lithography systems
and the materials for the masks and the photoresists.
An alternative approach to optical lithography consists in exposing the photo-
resist without using masks (mask-less optical lithography). The two main methods to
perform mask-less optical lithography are direct write laser and two-photon
lithography.
Direct write lithography relies on a laser beam, which can be focused down to
1 μm or less, and an XY-moving stage holding the resist-coated sample. The
resolution achievable in patterns exposed by this technique is ultimately limited by
the light diffraction, thus, of the order of the wavelength of the laser. Typical laser
wavelengths used are around 400 nm. As a consequence, the same or similar
photoresists to the ones commonly used in NUV optical lithography are used.
Nanofabrication
2-33

[Page 83]
Another derived strategy is the realization of interference laser lithography, in which
the initial laser beam is split into two beams, which are driven to interference on the
photoresist at an angle.
The process resolution limit imposed by the laser wavelength can be overcome by
two-photon-absorption lithography. The two-photon absorption is a non-linear
process, which can be used to generate signiﬁcant absorption only in the very small
volume of the photoresist where the laser is focused. As the laser focus can be
displaced in X, Y and Z with respect to the substrate, complex 3D structures can be
fabricated with resolution beyond the diffraction limit.
Pushing the limits of optical lithography towards sub-10 nm resolution without
decreasing throughput will require the combination with self-assembling methods
based on small building blocks. Combination of self-assembly with lithography to
position and align the building blocks at the desired location is known as directed
self-assembly. One of the directed self-assembly methods that have caught more
interest by the industry has been the directed self-assembly of block co-polymers.
Driven by the repulsive force between chemically different molecular chains which
are covalently bonded, thin ﬁlms of block copolymer molecules self-assemble in
periodic structures in the size range between a few nanometers and a few tens of
nanometers. The self-assembled ﬁlm consists of alternating phases of the chains that
form the block co-polymer. For a di-block copolymer, large area lamellar or
cylindrical patterns are formed just by spin coating the solution and a short thermal
annealing. Long-range order in a controlled manner of these patterns can be
achieved by pre-deﬁning chemical (chemoepitaxy) or topographical (graphoepitaxy)
patterns on the surface. Applications of these technologies have been demonstrated
in hard disk drives, integrated circuits and 2D materials.
2.6 Student’s corner
• In a good approximation, we can assume that the value of the technological
constant, k1, has been decreasing linearly over the years, with a constant
decrease rate of 0.017/year. If the value of k1 was 0.8 in 1980, indicate which
information can be obtained about the rest of the parameters that deﬁne the
resolution in projection optical for the six cases indicated in ﬁgure 2.4.
• The parameter γ determines the contrast of a photoresist after exposure and
development, and, in consequence, it is a parameter that inﬂuences the
resolution. Evaluate the interplay between photoresist contrast and optical
exposure. For this, indicate the extension of the dimensions of a pattern for
three photoresists with γ = 1, 5 and 10, and assuming that the proﬁle of the
optical exposure at the edges of the exposed area follows a Gaussian like
distribution with σ = 1 μm (case 1) and σ = 3 μm (case 2). Make the analysis
for a positive photoresist.
• In an EUV lithography system the light is reﬂected in 12 multilayered mirrors
from the intermediate focus of the EUV source to the exposed wafer. The
reﬂectance of each mirror is 0.68, the exposure dose of the used photoresist is
40 mJ cm−2, each die is 50 mm × 30 mm in size, and the exposition time is 1.5 s.
Nanofabrication
2-34

[Page 84]
If the energy conversion efﬁciency at the light source is 4%, estimate the power
consumption at the EUV source.
• In the case that you had to fabricate a prototype of a planar circuit including
superconducting and/or magnetic materials, with patterning resolution of
0.8 μm, would you choose NUV optical lithography, EUV optical lithog-
raphy, laser direct write lithography or two-photon-absorption lithography?
Why?
• If you were working in the MEMS domain and needing to fabricate
cantilevers with aspect ratio (height/width) of 20 and widths down to 2 μm
resolution, propose a lithography fabrication process based on optical
lithography, including the used technique, the photoresist tone (positive or
negative) and thickness, the post-exposure steps, etc.
• The following images correspond to SEM images of self-assembled patterns
of PS-b-PMMA block copolymer, where the darker areas correspond to the
PMMA phase and brighter areas to the PS phase. The block copolymer ﬁlm
has been deposited on a silicon oxide surface and the thickness is around 40
nm. In the three cases, a neutralization surface layer has been formed on the
surface before the block copolymer deposition to create the optimal neutral-
ization. Indicate:
i. Which images correspond to lamella forming block copolymers and
which images correspond to cylindrical forming block copolymer?
ii. Are the block copolymer molecules oriented parallel or perpendicular
with respect to the surface plane?
iii. What can be said about the presence of a neutralization layer on the
surface of the substrate?
iv. Which is the position of each block copolymer in the phase diagram of
block copolymers (ﬁgure 2.20(a))?

Nanofabrication
Nanolithography techniques and their applications
Jose´ Marı´a De Teresa
Chapter 3
Electron beam lithography and its use on
2D materials
Vito Clericò, Mario Amado and Enrique Diez
Electron beam lithography (EBL) is a fabrication technique based on a scanning
electron microscope (SEM) integrated with a lithographic system for the control of
the electron beam and/or the SEM stage. In this chapter, starting from the description
of a conventional ﬁeld emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM), we
introduce the basic concepts of EBL, its advantages and main limitations. In the
second part of the chapter, we report some possible applications in which the EBL
technique has a very important role, focused on the fabrication of nanodevices based
on 2D materials.
3.1 Introduction
Electron beam lithography (EBL) is a nano-fabrication technique based on the
controlled irradiation of a sample covered with a sensitive resist via a focused beam
of electrons. EBL is typically performed by a scanning electron microscope (SEM)
integrated with a lithographic system for the control of the beam and/or the SEM
stage. This technique appeared in the late 1960s arising from the necessity to fabricate
submicrometric devices, unreachable to other widespread methods such as optical
(ultra-violet) lithography, x-ray lithography or imprinting. A standard EBL process
consists essentially of three steps: exposure, development and pattern transfer. The
successful outcome of the ﬁnal process relies on the careful control of these singular,
yet cumulative, actions shown in ﬁgure 3.1.
The emitter, also known as gun, consists of a thermionic ﬁlament or a ﬁeld emission
(FE) source based on a single crystal tip, both of them typically manufactured with
tungsten. An FE gun produces a far more coherent and brighter beam compared to the
one obtained with the thermionic ﬁlament. Throughout this chapter, our focus will be
given to systems described as ﬁeld emission scanning electron microscopes (FE-SEM),
which are nothing more than an SEM equipped with an FE gun.
doi:10.1088/978-0-7503-2608-7ch3
3-1
ª IOP Publishing Ltd 2020. All rights,
including for text and data mining (TDM), artificial intelligence (AI) training, and similar technologies, are reserved.

[Page 91]
Once the electron-beam (e-beam) is produced, it must be focused to be used as a
‘nano-pencil’ to write on a thin layer of material sensitive to it (the so-called resist or
e-beam resist) that was previously deposited onto the surface on which the ﬁnal pattern
is expected to be transferred to. The resist ﬁlm can be of positive or negative tone,
behaving similarly to how slides and negative ﬁlms performed in analog photography.
The pattern drawn in the resist can be used either to tailor the material with an
etching process or to deﬁne electrical contacts by metal evaporation (ﬁgure 3.1).
The electron beam follows typically a Gaussian-like distribution and the higher the
e-beam energy the lower is the wavelength and consequently a minor diameter of the
beam, i.e. the size of your ‘pencil-tip’, is potentially reachable.
The SEM, considered as a microscope, has been primarily built for imaging
purposes where the size of the sample area visible when taking the image will depend
on the selected magniﬁcation. If one wants to inspect nanometric structures, the
maximum available magniﬁcation will be required, and, therefore, the image will
cover a reduced area of the sample. If you keep the magniﬁcation at its maximum
value and the e-beam of the SEM is used to write a pattern, the size of the region where
you can write (known as writing ﬁeld, WF) will coincide with the area of the image. Of
course, one can decrease the magniﬁcation and enlarge consequently the size of the
writeable area, but it comes at a cost. Generally, a higher area is accompanied by an
increase of the e-beam diameter, which reduces extraordinarily the accuracy of the
pattern. This is one of the great disadvantages of EBL compared to other lithographic
methods: writing accurate small nano-patterns is only possible in a micrometric scale.
When we aim repeating a sub-micrometric pattern in a large area, as the electronic
industry does in the fabrication of nanoelectronic devices in large wafers, the solution
is at hand and well-known from the ﬁrst textile manufacturers: stitching. In our case,
stitching means keeping the WF at its minimum size hence keeping your e-beam size at
its minimum too. After drawing the desired pattern in the WF, the physical motion of
the stage holding the sample is performed, repeating the process in an adjacent
unexposed WF of the same area. Stitching requires an extraordinary accuracy in terms
of alignment and positioning (sub-micrometric accuracy indeed) that can be achieved
Figure 3.1. Sketch of a standard EBL process to evaporate a thin ﬁlm only in the desired region of a substrate
with a positive resist.
Nanofabrication
3-2

[Page 92]
either using interferometric (automatic) stages or by manual alignment using again the
SEM and reference marks.
While truly EBL systems are designed to work only for lithographic purposes
(integrating very-high-energy electron beams and interferometric stages for high-
accuracy stitching), a good imaging SEM can be used for EBL and be good enough
for a correct lithographic process. A typical example of the ﬁrst species are the EBL
commercial set-ups sold by companies such as Crestec, Vistec or Raith, well-suited
for large-wafer technology or mass scale production, but with the major drawback
on their cost, which can rise well over 1.5 M€. Henceforth we will devote the
majority of this chapter to EBL using SEMs plus a patterning processor for EBL,
rather modest setups compared to dedicated EBL instruments, but nevertheless
more than enough for fundamental basic research such as preparing prototypical
state-of-the-art quantum devices. There are numerous commercial systems to be
purchased almost off the shelf, one of the most well-known is the Elphy+ from Raith
patterning system, which can be combined with an SEM Sigma from Zeiss, as we
will thoroughly present in this chapter.
As we have already mentioned, in order to produce a ﬁnal working device we will
typically require one or several steps of lithography combined with the auxiliary
evaporation of metals or dielectrics as well as any kind of etching (dry or wet)
process to deﬁne the ﬁnal geometry of the device. These processes are intimately
related; as an example, the selection of a particular resist will be affected by the
nature of the material to be processed, the size of the pattern and the required
accuracy, also having an effect on the procedure for the etching and/or evaporation.
This chapter will be divided as follows: we will start overviewing a conventional
FE-SEM set-up and introducing the basic (common) concepts in EBL as well as its
main advantages and limitations. We will show the steps for a successful EBL
process and we will brieﬂy introduce electron beam deposition of metals and
dielectrics as well as inductively coupled reactive ion etching as two of the processes
that might be needed for the ﬁnal completion of the desired device. We will ﬁnally
focus on some of most widespread device geometries when working with two-
dimensional (2D) materials, as a ﬁrst approach for the reader to the application of
EBL to 2D materials. In the last part we report general applications of EBL as well
as new developments of this technique.
3.2 Fundamentals of EBL
3.2.1 Chamber and electron column
The column of the FE-SEM deﬁnes the region where electrons are generated,
accelerated, focused and deﬂected. It possesses unique integrated beam booster
technology to keep the beam at a high potential in the full length of the column,
independent from the landing energy [1]. A schematic view of a typical FE-SEM
with Gemini column from Zeiss and its main components is shown in ﬁgure 3.2(a).
Electron microscopes use bundled high-energy electrons as the basis of their
operation that are generated in the source. These electrons are emitted in a ﬁlament
presented in position (1) in ﬁgure 3.2(a), controlled by the suppressor electrode
Nanofabrication
3-3

[Page 93]
(2) and collected by the extractor (3) and ﬁnally accelerated to the anode (4). The
electron beam passes through the borehole of the anode and the condenser (7) and
leaves the source before being collimated by the aperture (5).
In EBL, ﬁlaments can be composed of different materials, which is tightly
related to the diverse working principles and expected properties. Thermionic
ﬁlaments (W and LaB6) were commonly used in the pioneer systems, where a moderate
Figure 3.2. (a) Photo of the FE-SEM and the Raith nanolithography system with their main components.
(b) Schematic views of FE-SEM: Filament (1), suppressor electrode (2), extractor electrode (3), anode
(4), condenser lens (5), gun/aperture alignment system (6), multi-hole aperture (7), stigmator (8), liner tube
(9), In-lens detector (10), electromagnetic lens (11), scan coils (12), primary electron beam (13), specimen
(14), holder (15), column chamber valve (ccv). (c) Sketch of the positions for the In-Lens and ET-SE detector
(2) with respect to the specimen and column.
Nanofabrication
3-4

[Page 94]
vacuum (10−6–10−7 mbar) was sufﬁcient. These systems liberate electrons from the
well-known thermal electrode emission, that is, by increasing the temperature of the
ﬁlament until the energy of the carriers overcomes the work function of the material [2].
Schottky sources, also known as thermal ﬁeld emission sources, represent the
evolution of the thermionic ones and are based on tungsten ﬁlaments covered by
zirconium oxide. They are present in a vast number of the last generations of EBL
systems. Their operational temperatures are lowered and thus electron tunneling
due to electric-ﬁeld-induced emission prevails. The combination of sharp tips where
the electric ﬁeld at their apex is locally enhanced (which drastically lowers down the
energy barrier) and the ZrO coating that reduces the work function to be overcome
increases dramatically the emission current.
Once the electron beam has been produced in the source, it must be focused and
guided by the optics present in the column, where electrostatic or magnetic lenses
bundle and deﬂect the e-beam (see position 11 in ﬁgure 3.2(b)). Electromagnetic
ﬁelds will guide the beam of electrons, analogously to geometric classical optics.
Similar to optical microscopes where the resolution is limited by the diffraction
within the different lenses and depends on the wavelength of the light itself, in an
electron microscope the access to high resolution beam spots is allowed when the
electron wavelength is shortened.
The wavelength λ is related to the momentum according to the formula:
λ = h
p
(3.1)
where h is the Planck constant and p is the momentum of the electrons. Because the
momentum p is determined by the accelerating voltage V where

2
with e being the electron charge and m the electron mass. Therefore, the wavelength
of propagating electrons at a given accelerating voltage V can be determined by
λ =

2
Since the mass of an electron is m = 9.1 × 10−31 kg, the charge e = 1.6 × 10−19 C
and the Planck constant h = 6.626 × 10−34 J s we obtain:
λ =

1.228 10
1.5 nm
(3.2)
9
Resolution in microscopy is limited by diffraction effects to about λ/2. Therefore
formula (3.2) allows us to obtain the electron’s wavelength (and therefore the
nominal resolution of our electron microscope) for different voltages used in the
SEM. For a typical value of the accelerated voltage (5 kV) wavelength is 0.017 nm
without considering relativistic effects. We can include relativistic effects straight-
forwardly including the Lorentz factor [3] but the correction for voltages below
Nanofabrication
3-5

[Page 95]
300 kV is small so usually to obtain the nominal resolution we have restricted
ourselves to formula (3.2). However, in practice, other effects such as the aberration
of the lenses (spherical and chromatic), the spread of the primary electron beam
inside the resist and the generation of secondary electrons around the incidence point
are limiting factors compared to diffraction effects, as will be later discussed.
Multiple apertures are mounted in the vacuum tube. They can well serve both for
interrupting the beam or for limiting it (reducing the amount of beam hitting the
specimen). For imaging processes, the standard aperture used in the majority of the
SEMs is 30 microns, but especially for EBL different apertures (typically 7.5, 10, 20,
30, 60 and 120 microns) are accessible in any commercial system permitting one to
achieve a good compromise between resolution and exposure time.
SEM is a scanning microscope in which the scanning of the sample surface takes
place thanks to the deﬂection of the electron beam during their crossing through the
scan coils (or deﬂector plates) (see position 12 in ﬁgure 3.2(b)). Electron beam is
deﬂected in X or Y direction by the magnetic ﬁeld (or electric ﬁeld in the case of
deﬂector plate) that is created in the scan coils, so the electron beam starts to scan in
a raster way over a rectangular area of the sample surface with very high precision.
The size of the scan ﬁeld on the sample is chosen according to the magniﬁcation. The
magniﬁcation is given by the ratio between the dimensions of the ﬁeld on the SEM
display and the real size of the ﬁeld on the sample.
Since the SEM screen has a ﬁxed size, higher magniﬁcations are obtained
reducing the size of the ﬁeld on the sample.
With respect to a conventional SEM, an FE-SEM has a ﬁeld emission gun that
allows the use of highly focused and low-energy electron beams. This feature entails
an improved spatial resolution and the possibility to work at very low potentials
(0.01–5 kV). The use of a low potential minimizes the charging effect and reduces the
possibility to damage sensitive samples such as 2D materials.
The interaction between the beam and the specimen will give us back crucial
information about the sample (position, topography, size and shape, etc). When the
incident electron beam interacts with a specimen, it will partially transfer its energy
to electrons in the vicinity of the sample’s surface, which eventually can escape from
the specimen. They are coined secondary electrons (SEs) if they have low energy (less
than 50 eV) and backscattered electrons (BSEs) when they are more energetic (up to
the energy of the primary beam). SEs and BSEs can be used for imaging purposes
and for the exposure of the resist in the EBL process.
The electrons leaving the sample can be collected using different detectors in the
SEM. In-Lens detectors and Everhart–Thornley detectors (ﬁgure 3.2(c)) are the most
typical ones in commercial systems. They work with secondary electrons which can
be divided into two different kinds:
• SE1: They are directly generated when the sample is hit by the beam and
leave the surface in the vicinity (up to a few nm) of the impact point of the
incident beam.
• SE2: They are generated after multiple scattering inside the specimen and
leave the sample at a certain distance (up to a few μm) from the impact point
of the incident beam.
Nanofabrication
3-6

[Page 96]
In-Lens detectors (ﬁgure 3.2(b)) are commonly placed inside the electron column
of the microscope and collect SEs and BSEs that are emitted from the surface of the
sample, providing high-quality topography and compositional images of the surface
with a rather low applied energy (⩽5 kV), which is useful for non-conductive
samples.
Everhart–Thornley (ET) detectors (collecting SEs) are located in a lateral position
(ET-SE detector is shown on the bottom left of ﬁgure 3.2(c)) and are designed to
operate at working distances between 5–12 mm. ET detectors are mainly used for
topography imaging and are more sensitive when used to scan tilted samples.
A column chamber valve (ccv in ﬁgure 3.2(b)) separates the gun from the chamber
where the specimen is placed. With the use of an ion-getter pump (IGP), the typical
pressure is in the range of 10−10 mbar where the gun is located. The vacuum in the
main chamber for the sample is obtained thanks to turbo pumps with a typical
pressure of 10−6 mbar. There are several options to place and position the sample
in the different available holders. They can be ﬁxed either by pressure clamps,
directly screwed or glued using special double-sided conductive tape. Sample
holders typically host the different standards that are required for the calibration
of the current, focus and astigmatism.
A controlled three-dimensional movement is essential in a modern EBL system.
Access to numerous samples by accurate calibrated electromechanical displacements
and the possibility to tilt the sample holder in situ are beneﬁcial both for lithographic
and imaging purposes. In the system presented in this chapter, the instrument
includes three-axis (X, Y, Z) fully-motorized rotary stage with tilted option to rotate
along the Z-axis from 0° to 90°. Samples with diameters up to 4 inches are allowed in
the system discussed in this chapter.
3.2.2 Basic and lift-off EBL processes
A schematic view of the typical process for fabrication, in which EBL has a
fundamental role, is shown in ﬁgure 3.3(a). Firstly, the spin-coating of the resist
on the sample takes place (see section 3.3.1 for details). The used resists are
sensitive to the electron beam, so during the EBL process part of the exposed
resist becomes sensitized (step 1). In this way, after the developing process (step 2),
some part of the resist is removed, and the uncovered sample can be removed by
etching process (dry or wet etching), meanwhile the covered sample is protected by
the resist, that acts as mask (step 3). Finally, the residual resist is eliminated in the
last step 4, in which an immersion of the sample in resist removal (typically acetone)
takes place.
In ﬁgure 3.3(b) another common fabrication process is shown. In this case the
sample is not previously present on the substrate, but it is grown after the EBL
process. After exposure (step 1) and developing process (step 2), part of the sample is
grown directly on the substrate or on the resist mask (step 3). Finally, the sample is
deﬁned through the so-called lift-off process, where an immersion in resist removal
(typically acetone) removes the resist mask and the material on it (step 4), deﬁning
the rest of the sample on the substrate.
Nanofabrication
3-7

[Page 97]
Figure 3.3. (a) Basic nanofabrication process by steps. (b) Lift-off procedure.
Nanofabrication
3-8

[Page 98]
Some common examples of these processes will be discussed later in detail, in
particular the dry-etching process with ICP-RIE and evaporation with e-beam will
be described in section 3.4.
3.2.3 Resolution
Real resolution in an EBL process depends on different parameters/effects. Here we
report the main important contributions:
• The Beam size (d)
SEM resolution strongly depends on the SEM beam diameter. Its size,
apart from the diffraction limit, is determined by the electromagnetic lenses
used to focus the electron beam on the sample. The theoretical effective beam
size (d) is given by the formula [4]:

is the
diffraction limit contribution. Schematic representation of spherical and chro-
matic aberrations is shown in ﬁgure 3.4(a) in analogy with the optical glass
lenses.
• Beam-resist interaction and main limitations; proximity effect
When an electron beam collides with the resist, the interaction strongly
depends on the acceleration voltage as schematically shown in ﬁgure 3.4(b).
This interaction is complex and inﬂuenced by many factors, but in general,
resolution is typically worse at low voltage because the aberrations of the
microscope are greater. Working at high voltage is normally preferred to
achieve higher resolution, but there is also another important effect that has
Figure 3.4. (a) Analogy with glass lenses for spherical and chromatic aberration. (b) Schematic representation
of electron beam collisions in an EBL process.
Nanofabrication
3-9

[Page 99]
to be taken into consideration for the interaction between beam and resist: the
proximity effect (PE).
PE relates to the resist outside the targeted area of exposure that
unavoidably receives a non-zero electron dose. In fact, when the electron
beam collides with the resist or the substrate, the generation of forward (with
small angles) and backscattering electrons (with large angles), as shown in
ﬁgure 3.4(b), causes the exposure of resist far away from the original incident
point. In particular, backscattered electrons, with high energy, high scattering
angle and large travel lengths generate secondary electrons along their paths,
which holds major responsibility for exposure far from the incident point. PE
affects more speciﬁcally submicron structures, causing a nonuniform exposi-
tion distribution between pattern elements or inside a pattern element [5]. The
nonuniformity in the exposure between pattern elements might arise from a
poor selection of the pattern geometry in the CAD ﬁle or from the reduced
(limiting) space and size of the adjacent features to be exposed. In fact,
smaller elements receive less real dose than the larger ones, and also isolated
elements receive less dose than packed elements. The nonuniformity in the
exposure inside a single element is instead related to the difference in
the exposure between the center of the feature with respect to the edges
and the selection of the writing pattern within the single element. Scanning
(thus writing) along parallel lines until the completion of the polygon is the most
common pattern but diagonal or radial scans can also be performed when
necessary.
In the schematic representation of the PE in ﬁgure 3.4(b), it is clear that the
use of a higher beam voltage implies drastic changes in the PE effect owing to
the variations in the interaction volume of the electron beam in the sample
and in the forward scattering angle of the primary beam [6]. Also, the nature
of the substrate itself has a lot of inﬂuence on the interaction volume, thus, on
the backscattering process.
There are different ways to reduce PE, such as an appropriate choice of the
resist. In fact, the use of a thinner resist layer often reduces the forward
scattering. In addition, the use of multilayer resists, in which the upper layer is
used for patterning and the lower layer is used to reduce the backscattering
processes can be advantageous [7]. Moreover, a high-contrast resist (i.e. HSQ)
is typically less sensitive to PE.
Another possibility for the complete or partial correction of the PE error
consists in introducing element fragmentation with different doses in the
CAD ﬁle features, choosing the best scan direction [8] and changing shape/
dimension of the element. It can be done manually by the user (but it requires
a high level of expertise and it is very time consuming) or the PE correction
can be computationally calculated by the EBL software, basically simulating
the effect due to backscattering electrons, whose contribution is higher at high
voltage.
To reduce the backscattering contribution, it is also possible to perform a
background-dose PE correction (known as GHOST) [9, 10]. It consists in
Nanofabrication
3-10

[Page 100]
writing, through the use of defocused beam, the inverse of the intended image
(after the primary exposure), so that a constant background level of dose in
all the regions is obtained. Apart from the longer exposure time due to the use
of two lower dose scans, the technique is characterized by a low contrast in
the feature deﬁnition.
3.2.4 Scan modes, writing ﬁeld, DAC, stitching, beam stability
In scanning beam systems there are two main writing strategies for lithographic
purposes and they strongly depend on the beam. Standard Gaussian (round) beams
address the desired pattern and their features pixel by pixel and it can be done either
by a raster scan or a vector scan. The entire ﬁeld is scanned with the raster scan
method and the beam is blanked where exposure is not required. Instead, a vector
scan system scans only over the required positions and the beam is blanked as it
moves between different scanning places (ﬁgure 3.5(a)) [4].
Both modes have their advantages and drawbacks; as an example, a raster scan
would be slower than a vector one but the possibility of the scan to exceed the limits
of the pattern would be higher in a vector scan.
As mentioned in section 3.1, another important concept in the EBL process is the
WF, which is the largest area exposed without the stage movement and it depends on
the SEM magniﬁcation. Each WF is composed by a ﬁnite number of steps in the
exposure (exposure elements (EXELs)), determined by the deﬂection of the beam
according to a certain value of an applied potential. This potential is controlled by a
PC through a card for digital to analogic conversion (DAC), connected with the
deﬂection system of the SEM (ﬁgure 3.5(b)). For real systems, for a DAC of 16 bits
for each axis for example, the WF is subdivided in a writing grid of 216 × 216 = 65
536 × 65 536 EXELs.
Thus, WFs with lower size permit performing the EBL process with higher
resolution. For example, the nominal resolution with a WF of 100 μm is 2 nm1. WFs
are chosen by the user with the aim to achieve the required resolution minimizing the
stage movements. When the pattern size (or Working Area (WA)) needs the use of
Figure 3.5. (a) Raster scan and vector scan modes. (b) A schematic EBL set-up.
1 The nominal resolution of an EBL process depends also on the selected SEM aperture (beam current). Using
a WF of 100 μm, a resolution of 2 nm can be achieved when the aperture is set to 7.5 μm.
Nanofabrication
3-11

[Page 101]
more WFs, the ﬁelds are stitched through the stage movement. The accuracy of this
stitching depends on the precision of the stage movement. Typically, interferometric
stage guarantees nanometric stage movement, meanwhile mechanical stages are
characterized by hundreds of nanometers error during the stitching process.
During the exposition, acceleration voltage and beam current are ﬁxed. The real
dose is measured in terms of current per unit area and it is determined, as well as the
writing speed, by the time in which the beam remains in each EXEL. This time is
called area dwell time.
Hereafter, the used nanocontroller is the Elphy-Plus, a system from Raith
company which makes it possible to produce micro and nano structures by means
of a Gaussian electron beam vectorial writing in connection with the FE-SEM. The
hardware assembly is built into a 19’ rack consisting of a digital control unit and a
separate thermo controlled analog electronics box. It has X-Y beam shift inputs and
a video out to a monitor (BAS out). The electronics units are completely remote
controlled by a PC data system via an integrated small computer system interface
(SCSI). Apart of the ELPHY Plus rack, a nanolithography system should present a
beam blanker unit that will operate to avoid any exposure when the beam is in
transit from one pattern element to another (ﬁgure 3.5(b)).
The beam blanker hardware is installed in the SEM column. It consists of sets of
parallel plates where one is grounded and the other is connected to the high voltage
end of the system. When the voltage is zero, the beam of electrons is allowed to pass
through a spray aperture and so they can reach the sample. Conversely when a non-
zero voltage is applied, electrons are deﬂected, and the aperture does not permit the
electrons to reach the sample. The beam blanker control unit is a high voltage power
supply, accepting a TTL voltage from the ELPHY Plus rack.
The ELPHY Plus software includes all the features needed to expose micro and
nano structures (WFs, doses, etc) and it is integrated with a GDSII based editor
compatible with ASCII, DXF, CIF and CAD formats. The generation and edition
of patterns is graphic and based on the superposition of different layers where the
different elements are drawn. In these layers, diverse elements will be designed for
multilevel exposure where beam intensity, dose, exposure time, focus, writing ﬁeld
can be modiﬁed and unique to each layer.
3.2.5 Resists
The resist has a central role in every lithographic process. It is typically used as
sacriﬁcial layer and deposited on the main working material through a spin-
coating process (see section 3.3.1) [11]. Typically, the thickness of an EBL resist
can measure between 20 nm up to a micrometer. Resists are either organic or
inorganic polymeric materials dissolved in solvents and they can be classiﬁed in
two different classes according their tone: positive or negative tone. After the
exposition on the desired areas of the resist and the developing process, the
exposed resist remains on (negative tone) or leaves (positive tone) the surface of
the substrate ([ﬁgure 3.6(a)). The exposition to the electron beam affects the
molecular composition of the resist, in particular chain scission in positive tone
Nanofabrication
3-12

[Page 102]
resists and cross-linking in negative tone resist. The choice of a resist is typically taken
according to three principal characteristics: resolution, contrast and sensitivity.
Resolution is deﬁned by the minimum feature size (or minimum feature distance)
that can be resolved. The contrast is the capability in differencing between exposed
and unexposed areas. The sensitivity deﬁnes the alteration of the resist with respect to
the electron beam doses [12]. Contrast and sensitivity are strictly correlated and
expressed according to the equation [13]:
γ =
1
log D
D
100
0
where γ is the contrast value, D0 is highest dose exposition that does not affect the
resist, D100 is the minimum dose for a complete exposition of the resist. Even if it is
desirable to have high contrast and high sensitivity resist, an increase of contrast
results in the worsening of the sensitivity and vice versa. A typical plot of the
remaining resist thickness versus the exposure dose is shown in ﬁgure 3.6(b) for
positive and negative resists. Apart from the electron energy, the type and concen-
tration of the developer solvent, the developing time and temperature affect the
contrast and sensitivity.
In table 3.1, we report some common resists used for an EBL process and their
developers:
In section 3.3.4 we will compare two different EBL processes, using positive
(PMMA) and high negative (HSQ) resists, highlighting the main limitations and
problems related to a typical EBL process.
3.2.6 Cross-linking problem
Cross-linking might appear when the energy of the electron beam is much higher
than the resist ionization energy. Through this process, chemical reactions occur in
the resist, bringing about unwanted molecular bonds. The crosslinked resist cannot
be easily removed from the sample surface. Although a crosslinked resist can be used
Figure 3.6. (a) Negative and positive tone resists after electron beam exposition and developing process.
(b) Remaining resist thickness in positive (dark blue) and negative resist (red) [13].
Nanofabrication
3-13

[Page 103]
for some purposes, as an alternative dielectric ﬁlm for example [15], it is commonly
an undesired effect. Actually, cross-linking is frequently not an effect that appears
during the EBL exposure step2, but it is rather a ‘post-exposure’ problem related to a
second fabrication step, typically the dry-etching process or during metal evapo-
ration. In fact, as we will explain in the next sections, during an ICP-RIE or e-beam-
evaporation process, some electrons are generated and if they reach the resist mask,
they can crosslink it. The ﬁnal result is a permanent resist mask after a dry etching
process, or in case of evaporation problems during the lift-off process.
3.3 Steps for a successful EBL process
In this section the most common steps in an EBL process will be presented. After
introducing basic concepts about spin coating, we will present a process where the
WA is larger than the size of the writing ﬁeld (WA>WF). This is typical when
working with a wafer without alignment marks. Afterwards, we will consider pre-
structured samples (with pre-deposited alignment markers) in which we face the
inverse scenario, WA<WF. Both processes are carried out with the use of a standard
SEM stage, in which the movement on the sample takes place with a typical stage
error movement of 1 μm, far from the desired accuracy in a nanolithography
process. We will therefore present a common self-alignment strategy that will serve
to arrive at a ﬁnal writing accuracy of some tens of nanometers. Two different kinds
of resists will be used, both leading to two unwanted, though common, effects when
performing EBL: the crosslinking of the resist and the PE.
