2 Indeed, crosslinking the resist requires a rather high dose. For example, for PMMA resist the dose necessary
for the crosslinking is roughly ten times the standard one for a correct lithography, hence, crosslinking will
mainly happen because of indirect effects.
Nanofabrication
3-14

[Page 104]
During the EBL processes we used different WFs. All the WFs must be calibrated
beforehand, so a correspondence between the applied voltage and the beam
deﬂection in the (X,Y) plane is found. For the WF alignment, we used a sample
(Chessy sample) with calibrated distances. During the alignment procedure, differ-
ent marks are overlaid on the Chessy image. We move each mark to the correct
position and, at the end of this procedure, the WF becomes corrected and the
calibration values are stored in the pattern generator hardware.
Different SEM magniﬁcations will imply a change in the maximum area covered
by the WF. All the processes described in this section were carried out with FE-SEMs
where a ﬁxed beam accelerating voltage of 15 kV was set.
3.3.1 Spin coating
An EBL process should start with a thorough cleaning of the substrate that will
depend on the nature of the sample. As a standard procedure, substrates undergo a
two-stage cleaning. They are ﬁrstly rinsed in acetone in order to remove organic
impurities followed by ethanol or isopropanol washing, which removes contami-
nated acetone before it can form streaks on the surface. The cleaning process might
be carried out in a sonic bath cleaner that will help to remove precipitates on the
surface. It is nevertheless worth mentioning that such a cleaning processes will
strongly vary from sample to sample.
A clean uniform substrate is ready for the very ﬁrst step of every lithography
process, i.e. the spin coating. This process consists in covering the surface of the
sample with some drops of the chosen resist, via standard pipetting technique, that
will eventually turn into a uniform ﬁlm using the spin coater. The ﬁnal thickness of
the ﬁlm depends on the chemical composition of the resist, revolutions per minute
and total time of the spin coating process as well as the nature of the surface and
the size of the sample. Before the exposition, the resist must be baked by placing the
samples onto hot plates with a given (desired) temperature. The deﬁnition and dose
of the whole EBL process will strongly depend on the temperature and time of this
baking process.
In this section we focus the attention on one of the most common resists used in
an EBL process: the polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA). PMMA represents also the
best candidate for EBL on 2D materials, especially when graphene is involved [16],
mainly due to its easy treatment and to the chemical components of the resist itself
and its developer, a mixing of methylisobutylketone (MIBK) and isopropanol. In
fact, they are less aggressive than other resists and respective developer; moreover,
residual PMMA can be removed with an annealing process at relative low temper-
atures after the fabrication process. PMMA is typically dissolved in anisole or
chlorobenzene and its concentration in the solvent will affect the ﬁnal thickness of
the ﬁlm [17].
The quality and reproducibility of an EBL process strongly depends on the
condition of the resist coating, in particular its homogeneity. For this reason, a
preliminary check with a ﬁltered optical microscope is convenient. Figures 3.7(a)–(c)
display optical images of 1 × 1 cm wafers consisting of 300 nm thick SiO2 on Si that
Nanofabrication
3-15

[Page 105]
has been coated with the same homemade PMMA3 (molecular weight 996 K) but
with different concentrations in chlorobenzene: (a) PMMA 2% spun at 4000 rpm for
60 s, (b) PMMA 4% spun at 4000 rpm for 60 s, (c) PMMA 8% spun at 3000 rpm for
60 s. The optical contrast (after baking each of them at 160 degrees for 10 min) is
different, corresponding to the thickness (a) 70 nm, (b) 220 nm, (c) 800 nm.
Spin coaters are usually installed in a fume hood with an independent exhaust
system that guarantees the extraction of toxic gases. We have used a manual spin
coater LabSpin 6 from SÜSS MicroTec (maximum speed of 8000 rpm) and two
hotplates (ﬁgure 3.7(d)). In a spin-coating process, the resist is deposited by using the
spinner on a substrate with a uniform thickness of hundreds of nanometers. Finally,
the resist is baked on the hot plate. We also have an ultrasonic cleaner and a
magnetic hotplate, which can we employed for cleaning, lift-off and wet etching
purposes.
3.3.2 EBL on a bare wafer (WA>WF)
In this section we describe an EBL process on a bare sample in which a precise
alignment on a particular position of its surface is not essential. This is a good option
when it is necessary to deﬁne arrays of structures with nanometric sizes. As an
example, we describe the EBL process to create an array (1 mm2) of thousands of
nanoresonators with lateral sizes of 100 × 200 nm and a separation of 1 μm centered
Figure 3.7. Optical images of PMMA coatings with different concentrations on sacriﬁcial SiO2/Si wafers: (a)
PMMA2%, (b) PMMA4% and (c) PMMA8%. (d) A typical fume hood for EBL process. Including a LabSpin
spin coater, two hot plates, deionized water supplier and sink.
3 For the homemade PMMA, we will always refer to PMMA 996 K from now onwards unless stated
otherwise.
Nanofabrication
3-16

[Page 106]
on a GaAs substrate of 1 × 1 cm2. In this case an aperture of 7.5 μm and a WF of
100 μm were used, resulting in a nominal resolution of 2 nm. Actually, the resist
selected for this process, a commercial PMMA2% resist (Allresist© ARP679.02) has
far worse resolution than the nominal one. The standard CAD ﬁle for the
lithography process shows rectangular shapes of 100 × 200 nm with a selected
dose of 230 μC cm−2, repeated for a whole WF of 100 × 100 μm. Therefore, this EBL
process is performed in the limit WA>WF.
The process starts with three small manual scratches or indentations on three
different borders or corners of the sample similar to the shown in ﬁgure 3.8(a). A
scratch helps for the correction of SEM parameters (focus, aperture and astigma-
tism), since the dielectric nature of the resist on a bare sample will bother their
adjustment.
Firstly, the stage must be adjusted through the global coordinates. In this way, we
can unambiguously deﬁne the exact correspondence between the physical coordi-
nates (X,Y) of the stage (SEM stage coordinates) and the (U,V) coordinates of the
sample. The global origin can be taken (and it is commonly taken) in the left-bottom
corner of the sample and it is essential to compensate the difference between the (X,Y)
and (U,V) origins. A second global point is taken after a controlled movement on the
SEM X-axis (for example 1 cm) so that the angle between the two systems can be
adjusted as well4. From this moment it is possible to move the SEM stage using
the (U,V) coordinates because the internal electronics in the system will use a roto-
translation matrix to this end.
Figure 3.8. Several micrographs of the WA>WF process showing a scratch made on the bottom-right corner
of a coated sample (a). Spot grown on PMMA, used for optimization of the exposition parameters (b). Final
array of nanoresonators obtained through EBL without markers (c).
4 Here the axes of the sample are parallel to the axes of the stage, because we do not care about the orientation
of the array with respect to the sample orientation. In section 3.3.3, we will show a case in which we need to
carry out an angle correction for the compensation of the difference.
Nanofabrication
3-17

[Page 107]
The next step consists of deﬁning an array (for example, of 1 mm2), ideally in
the center of the sample and in which positioning scratches are ideally separated
5–10 mm5. A further correlation between the coordinates in the sample (U,V) and
coordinates in the CAD ﬁle (u,v) must be found. Firstly, the software needs to deﬁne
the ‘three points alignment’ adjustment of the bare sample. We can take the bottom-
left corner scratch as ﬁrst (U,V) point (0,0) mm, moving physically with the SEM
stage along the borders of the sample (so avoiding unwanted exposition of the
sample). Then, we choose the bottom-right corner scratch as second (U,V) point, it is
ideally positioned at (10, 0) mm. The third (U,V) point at (0, 10) mm is ideally
chosen at the top-left corner scratch of the sample. In each point we correct the
focus. In the case of PMMA resist, a useful trick can be used to ﬁx correctly the focus
(the scratch can have a different WD with respect to the resist coating) and check the
quality of the SEM parameters. In fact, it is possible to create a spot (burned area) of
very small size on PMMA resist. This process takes place only when the focus is
approximately the correct one, so we improve the focus repeating the process in
another position (ﬁgure 3.8(b)). Its shape gives us information on the astigmatism
parameter, since a circular spot means a good correction of astigmatism, whereas an
elongated shape is a signature of an uncorrected astigmatism. A good deﬁned
circular spot represents also a good candidate for the aperture adjustment through
the wobble adjustment, in which the electron beam is displaced back and forth across
the focus and it is corrected when the image of the spot ‘blinks’ in the same position.
Once a good spot is obtained, all the parameters are deﬁnitively corrected. For a
very precise EBL, in particular for the focus correction6, the ‘spot trick’ can be
applied for all the three-point alignment. Once the alignment is completed, it is
necessary to move the stage on the Faraday Cup and measure the beam current that
will serve us to calculate the real doses during the exposition. Therefore, after the
selection of the WF of 100 μm, we drag and drop the CAD ﬁle in the so-called
positioning list, we select the layer, step size, central dose, WA with the same
dimension and position of the WF, (U,V) pattern position and we calculate the dwell
time for the chosen step size7. For the ﬁrst WA, the center of the patterning is ﬁxed at
(U,V) coordinates close to the center of the sample. We repeat the import of the
CAD ﬁle in the positioning list as a matrix (10 rows × 10 columns)8 and, for each
one, the patterning center is shifted 100 μm in U and/or V, in order to obtain a total
matrix of 1 mm × 1 mm. The time for each patterning exposure (ﬁxing the resolution
5 We have supposed an ideal distance between adjacent scratches of 10 mm for simplicity (and only in one axis-
direction). This will vary between different processes and substrates.
6 We typically need to correct the focus at different points of the sample. Through Elphy Plus software it is also
possible to read in the focus value together with the coordinates for all three alignment points. So, we move the
stage to all the three points and re-adjust the focus on each point before reading in the coordinates. In this way
the focus will be changed for each digitally addressed (U,V) location.
7 The step size is the distance between two adjacent exposed points, the dwell time is the time for the exposition
at each point.
8 In this way, the WA will be larger than the selected WF at the end of the EBL process. It is also known as
multiple patterning process and it is largely used for dose array matrix (in which doses can be changed along
U or V directions).
Nanofabrication
3-18

[Page 108]
at 2 nm and the correspondent dwell time) is estimated around 15 min. So, the whole
EBL process (without considering the loss of time due to the movement of stage)
requires 25 h of exposure. Such a long exposure time is a major limitation is most
conventional systems. Therefore, when and where possible, the combination of EBL
with another faster lithographic technique like optical lithography [18] would be
preferred.
Once the exposure is ﬁnished, the last step for the EBL process is to develop the
ﬁlm. Here, we will perform it under cold environment (10°C) for 90 s and rinsing the
sample in commercial developer AR60050 from Allresist, with a subsequent stop for
30 s in room-temperature isopropanol.
The electronic micrograph shown in ﬁgure 3.8(c) shows the ﬁnal array of
nanoresonators (after evaporation and ICP-RIE processes to be later described)
[10]. One of the main limitations of EBL, stitching, can be noticed. Such misalign-
ment/error arises between two adjacent WAs due to the stage movement. In
ﬁgure 3.8(c) the position of the array on the right is shifted both in (X,Y) with
respect to the correct one (red circle indicates the correct separation between the
two matrices of array). In this case the shift is lower than 1 μm, but it depends on the
SEM stage precision (it could be also some micrometers). Stitching can be completely
overcome with the use of an interferometric stage, in which the movement of the stage
has precision of a few nm.
3.3.3 EBL in a wafer with alignment markers (WA<WF)
Here we report the process for an e-beam lithography with the use of pre-patterned
alignment markers. It is predominant when working with (sub)micrometric-sized
2D-materials such as exfoliated ﬂakes [20, 21], when patterning source-drain
contacts in nanowires [22] or carbon nanotubes [23] nanotubes or when several
self-aligned steps of EBL are required.
Here we describe one example in which a ﬂake of graphene is ﬁrst contacted by
EBL and through a second EBL process we deﬁne the shape of the ﬂake into a Hall
bar or a constriction. This is a typical example in which the use of EBL represents
the best available option provided that the features in the CAD ﬁles are not so small
that a very high EBL resolution is required. In fact, it is well known that the
fabrication process greatly affects the quality of graphene and PMMA (and its
developer) represents a very good choice in terms of resist because different
techniques can be easily employed to remove eventual contaminants [24–26].
First of all, it is necessary to design the ﬁnal area to be exposed in the CAD ﬁle
and for this reason it is necessary to take accurate distances and orientations of
the graphene ﬂake to the closest markers. This measurement can be very accurate
through SEM imaging (and preparing the CAD ﬁle directly on SEM image) or
with a rather lower resolution by using an optical microscope with sufﬁciently
high zoom precision (precision lower than 500 nm in the best available micro-
scopes). The use of optical microscopes is widespread for two main reasons. First,
they avoid damaging the sample or causing its propertie to deteriorate because of
the unprotected interaction with the electron beam. Secondly, because typical
Nanofabrication
3-19

[Page 109]
ﬂakes are larger than 5 μm width/length and the optical resolution is sufﬁcient.
During the preparation of the CAD ﬁle, in this case we need to deﬁne the features
with different layers for each exposure (contact, mask and pads) with respect to
the position of the markers, in which their position is deﬁned in the centers of a
square drawn with another layer. In this process the square side is 20 μm9.
Once the distances are taken and the CAD ﬁle is ready, the exposure process can
take place. Three different apertures and three different WFs were chosen in this
case. We used the aperture of 60 μm, the WF of 1000 μm (nominal resolution
200 nm) and a dose of 350 μC cm−2 to deﬁne the large metallic pads in this ﬁrst EBL
step. For the exposure of the contacts from the pads to the sample, the following
parameters were used: aperture of 10 μm, WF of 500 μm (nominal resolution
7.8 nm) and a dose of 300 μC cm−2. An aperture of 7.5 μm and WF of 100 μm
(nominal resolution 2 nm) were used for the exposure of the mask (and a dose of
270 μC cm−2) and for the bar or constriction (dose of 180 μC cm−2) that will deﬁne
the ﬁnal shape of the ﬂake after the subsequent reactive ion etching step. We have
used home-made resist PMMA4% in chlorobenzene for the ﬁrst and second steps
and homemade resist PMMA2% for the ﬁnal one. Both resists were baked at 160°C
for 10 min.
As the different EBL processes are quite similar, we will describe the one for the
exposure of the contacts. In this case, the knowledge of the orientation of the (U,V)
coordinates of the sample is essential. Therefore, it will be necessary to align the system
with the available markers (ﬁgure 3.9(a)). We choose two markers at the border of the
sample (far enough to the ﬂake to avoid undesired exposure) and rotate the SEM
stage until a perfect alignment is achieved between the two markers (ﬁgure 3.9(b)).
Similar to the EBL without markers, the global parameters (origin and angle) are
Figure 3.9. SEM micrographs displaying the orientation of the markers before (a) and after (b) the rotation of
the SEM stage for alignment. Final graphene device fabricated with two EBL processes through the use of
markers (c).
9 Fours markers are typically used for the alignment. All the squares centered in the markers have to be
contained in the chosen WF. Therefore, the distance between them must be lower than the WF size.
Nanofabrication
3-20

[Page 110]
ﬁxed using two different markers in this case, as well the SEM parameters and the
three-points alignment for the exact correspondence between (X,Y) coordinates of
the SEM stage, the (U,V) coordinates of sample and the (u,v) coordinates of the
CAD ﬁle. Instead of points or scratches we will now use three of the makers for the
local alignment. In fact, the distance between markers is well known as well as their
(U,V) local coordinates with respect to the position of the graphene ﬂake. After
measuring the beam current in the Faraday Cup, we open a position and drag and
drop the CAD ﬁle, this time selecting the layer for exposure (i.e. layer for contacts)
as well as the layer of the four markers around the ﬂake of graphene. Before
exposing the sample, the program opens several windows with the chosen dimen-
sions (in our case 20 × 20 μm, as previously stated) where the markers must be found
to inspect and correct in situ the misalignment. By centering each marker in the
center of its window we correct the error intrinsic to the displacement of the stage10
and by repeating this procedure several times before the real exposure, a nanometric
alignment can be achieved.
Once the different lithographic steps and subsequent evaporations, and etching
processes are completed we obtain geometries shown in ﬁgure 3.9(c). Nanometric
alignment was achieved between contacts and the graphene bar/constrictions [27].
The total exposure time was roughly 1 h, quite reasonable for a standard EBL
process and suited for its implementation when working with graphene and other 2D
materials (see section 3.5).
3.3.4 Two different resists: polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA) versus higher
resolution hydrogen silsesquioxane (HSQ)
Typically, in a standard low-energy EBL set-up, and for the vast majority of resists,
it is difﬁcult to deﬁne shapes with a precision below 25 nm. PMMA resist, in spite of
its versatility, suffers from this limitation. As described in section 3.2.5, there are few
resists capable of resolution below 25 nm. One of them is hydrogen silsesquioxane
(HSQ) resist. HSQ permits the deﬁnition of few-nm structures, even at low-energy
EBL [28], as well as lines and space with a resolution better than 10 nm [29].
In this section, we compare two different EBL processes with two different resists.
A commercial positive low-resolution PMMA (ARP679.02 from Allresist©) resist
and a commercial negative high-resolution HSQ resist (XR-1541-004 Dow Corning)
are compared. For both cases, we want to deﬁne a ‘bone’ structure with features
lower than 100 nm using the same beam voltage of 15 kV. For the ﬁrst process, with
ARP679.02 resist, the same recipe described in section 3.3.2 is used. For the HSQ
process, we have developed a higher contrast recipe. In detail, after a cleaning step in
hot acetone and isopropanol and a pre-bake at 150°C for 5 min, we use a plasma
cleaner process for 3 min before the coating. For spin coating we use 4000 rpm for
1 min with a baking at 80°C for 4 min. In the exposure, the dose for HSQ is 2000 μC
cm−2. The developing process is carried out rinsing tetramethylammonium hydroxide
10 Before exposure, the SEM stage moves (with a micrometric error in the movement) to the chosen (U,V)
coordinates for the patterning.
Nanofabrication
3-21

[Page 111]
at 25% in DI-water (TMAH25%) at 80°C and stopped in ﬂowing DI-water for 1 min.
The ﬁnal thickness of HSQ with this treatment is 50 nm.
In ﬁgures 3.10(a) and (b) the ﬁnal result of these two processes are displayed. The
very high contrast and very high resolution of the HSQ structure in comparison with
the PMMA one is very clear from the two SEM images11. In spite of its very high
resolution, HSQ has some limitations and problem in comparison with PMMA
resist. In fact, it needs a previous treatment of the substrate before coating, it is very
sensitive to the time between coating and exposure and it needs very high doses
during the electron exposure (an order of magnitude larger compared to PMMA).
Another limitation is related with its developer (for high contrast recipe). In fact, the
high concentration of TMAH and the temperature during development are too
aggressive for many types of devices (i.e. 2D materials devices).
3.4 ICP-RIE and E-beam evaporation
As we mentioned in section 3.1, in order to produce a ﬁnal working device we
typically require several EBL steps combined with (Joule, sputtering or e-beam)
metal deposition and wet or dry-etching to deﬁne the device geometry.
3.4.1 ICP-RIE
In this section we will restrict ourselves to describing one of the most common dry-
etching techniques for 2D-based systems: the ICP-RIE (inductively coupled plasma-
reactive ion etching). There are different dry-etching techniques of this kind: RIE,
ICP-etch, planar etch and deep reactive ion etch. Even though the above techniques
need a plasma source, the main difference amongst them lies on the conﬁguration of
the electrodes for the plasma generation.
A plasma is deﬁned as a medium of positive and negative particles. It is formed
when a gas becomes ionized due to radio frequency (RF) energy, microwave
Figure 3.10. SEM images of EBL processes to obtain a bone structure using either PMMA (AR679.02) resist
(a) or HSQ resist (b).
11 The two SEM image are taken at different magniﬁcation, nevertheless the imperfection of the SEM image
with lower magniﬁcation corresponds to the PMMA process (ﬁgure 3.11(a)) are evident in comparison with the
higher magniﬁcation SEM image corresponds to the HSQ process (ﬁgure 3.11(b)).
Nanofabrication
3-22

[Page 112]
radiation or electromagnetic (EM) ﬁelds with an energy that overcomes the binding
one for the electrons present in the atoms or molecules in the gas. This ionization
energy varies from one gas to another. As an example, in a commercial system
PlasmaPro 100 Cobra from Oxford Instruments, the energy ranges from 11.6 eV for
Cl2 to 15.7 eV for Ar. The use of the plasma permits performing high-temperature
chemistry at low temperatures where slight variations in the different parameters
(gas mixture, temperature, pressure or plasma power) will imply anisotropic or
isotropic etched proﬁle. Optical or EBL resist as well as previously deposited
metal laid on the surface of the sample will play the role of the mask. They must
be previously deﬁned by any kind of lithographic process, where the uncovered
areas of the sample will be effectively etched while the remaining ones, protected
by the mask, will be left untouched. Remnant mask will be typically removed
after the etching process (see ﬁgure 3.11(a) for a sketch of the whole process).
In an RIE process, a plasma is created in the system at low pressure by applying a
strong RF ﬁeld. Once the gas has begun to ionize, the free electrons strike neutral gas
molecules generating more electrons and ions. The interaction of these electrons
with the gas molecules could be multiple, such as atom ionization and dissociation of
gas molecules leading to formation of radicals and electron impact excitation. The
ions created in this process are extracted from the plasma by applying an electric
ﬁeld and impact on the sample, removing (etching) material from the sample of
interest.
Plasma can interact with the surface of the sample during the etching in different
ways, producing ion bombardment (sputtering), radical chemical reaction and ion
assisted chemical reaction. The etching processes can be isotropic or anisotropic.
The processes with a pure chemical etching (similar to the wet etching) do not need
ion bombardment for reaction and in this case there is no directionality on the
etching. For anisotropic etching (normally the desired one), the process needs ion
bombardment, so the reaction can take place and the obtained etching has a vertical
proﬁle. In an RIE, as well as an ICP-RIE process, chemical and physical reactions
take place.
Compared to RIE, in an ICP-RIE there is a physical separation between the
plasma generation (created by means of inductive coils) and the acceleration of the
resulting ions. This separation permits ICP-RIE a higher control during the overall
etching process with respect to the standard RIE, including a higher conﬁnement
Figure 3.11. (a) Typical plasma etching process. (b) SEM image of GaAs structure etched through an ICP-RIE
process with chlorine gas.
Nanofabrication
3-23

[Page 113]
and higher ion ﬂux uniformity with a more intense plasma and thus a deeper etching.
A typical vertical etching on a GaAs heterostructure through ICP-RIE is shown in
ﬁgure 3.11(b).
The Oxford Instruments PlasmaPro 100 Cobra system (see ﬁgure 3.12) is able to
perform high-quality etching through a plethora of different materials (dielectric,
metals, semiconductors, organics, etc) in wafers up to 100 mm (4’) diameter. A typical
set of gases for the ICP-RIE processes consists of O2, Ar, H2, CH4, SF6, C4F8, CHF3,
Cl2, BCl3 together with N2 and He for venting purposes. This system is equipped with
a cryogenic module allowing to work down to −120°C to perform cryo-etching. This
option plays a crucial role in the fabrication of graphene nanoconstrictions and other
nanodevices where low edge roughness is crucial. Therefore, we will devote a whole
sub-section in this chapter to this process applied to 2D material heterostructures. As
shown in ﬁgure 3.12(a), only the load-lock (for mounting/unmounting the samples)
and one of the two controller PCs are within the premises of the clean room. The main
body of the equipment (plasma chamber, gas lines, pumps, nitrogen tank, clean
solution scrubber system for the disposal of the toxic waste) are placed in an adjacent
technical room (ﬁgure 3.12(b)).
3.4.2 E-beam metal evaporation
A standard e-beam evaporator for the deposition of metals (Au, Cr, Ti, Al, Ni, Co, Pt)
and dielectrics (SiO2, Al2O3, HfO2) is shown in ﬁgure 3.13(a). It is a prototype designed
with the help of Tecnovac (Spain) equipped with a load-lock vacuum chamber isolated
from the main chamber with a manual guillotine valve. The samples are loaded/
unloaded using a mechanical valve. In the main chamber, a vacuum in the low 10−9
mbar can be achieved while in the load-lock it is possible to achieve 10−5 mbar in
approximately 40 min.
In an e-beam evaporation process, a high current passing through a tungsten
ﬁlament (see ﬁgure 3.13(b)) permits obtaining an electron beam which is accelerated
in a gun12 and focused onto the evaporation material using either permanent magnets
Figure 3.12. (a) View of the loadlock and ICP-RIE PC controller situated in a clean room environment.
(b) Main body of the ICP-RIE placed in an adjacent technical room.
12 The one presented in this chapter is equipped with a commercial Telemark 7 keV e-beam gun.
Nanofabrication
3-24

[Page 114]
or electromagnets. These systems have typically a carrousel with different pockets
where the crucibles contain the materials initially shaped in pellets, ingots or rods
(ﬁgures 3.13(c) and (d)). By a controlled ‘XY Sweeping’, the e-beam circulates along
the whole surface of the materials which are homogeneously evaporated when brought
to their melting or sublimation points.
The combination of long distance between the material and the sample (over 1 m)
and the operation at very low pressures ensures a high control on the evaporation
rate (less than 1 Å s−1) which is monitored by a quartz crystal controlled by a TTL
controller and creating high homogeneity thin ﬁlms on the samples but with the
major drawback of a high consumption of materials. A typical evaporation process,
in which the metal is heated up as the emission current is increased until its
conversion to a gaseous state, is shown in ﬁgure 3.13(e).
3.5 EBL applied to 2D materials
2D materials [30–32], a family that encompasses an enormous selection of natural and
artiﬁcial compounds with almost all the elements of the periodic table represented, are
single-layer-thick systems arranged in a crystalline manner. They were widely believed
to be thermodynamically unstable and, therefore, unsuitable to exist naturally in the
2D form but just embedded in the bulk in layered materials. The isolation of a single
layer of graphene back in 2004, obtained by mechanical cleavage using the scotch tape
method [33], represented the source of a sudden cascade of discoveries that rapidly
enlarged the 2D-family. Nitrides, oxides, chalcogenides, halides, carbides (among
others) have been exfoliated directly from layered 3D materials or artiﬁcially produced
in the lab since then. Most of these materials are binary compounds but single layered
(such as graphene or phosphorene) or ternary (CuSbS2) compounds. The wide
Figure 3.13. (a) E-beam evaporator designed by Tecnovac, with Telemark e-beam gun. (b) E-beam source
model 244. (c) 6-pocket crucible carousel. (d) Detailed reinforced graphite crucible containing Cr. (e) View of a
e-beam evaporation from an external window thanks to an internal periscope.
Nanofabrication
3-25

[Page 115]
variety of materials and compounds has implied an enormous assortment
of electronic properties present that includes semimetals, metals, semiconductor
and insulators with enormous potentiality to be used in nanodevices and optoelec-
tronic devices.
The majority of the devices described in this section have in common the use of
exfoliated graphene materials from the bulk by means of mechanical cleavage (for
example graphene, h-BN, MoS2 or MoSe2). Exfoliation of a bulk material aiming to
obtain a single layer of them represents the ﬁrst step for the bottom-up approach in
the generation of 2D-material-based devices (while the growth of artiﬁcial 2D-layers
such as hexagonal boron nitride would be the top-down approach). The mechanical
exfoliation [33, 34] consists in a peeling force applied to a piece of graphite (or any
other layered bulk material) by an adhesive tape that has been placed on its surface.
By exerting such (normal) force it is possible to overcome the Van der Waals
attraction dividing drastically the pristine bulk material into thinner aggregates.
Subsequent repetitions of such mechanical exfoliation would eventually lead to the
reduction of the material into single-layer ﬂakes. Once obtained, the peeled one-
layer-thick ﬂakes are ready to be transferred onto (typically) sacriﬁcial substrates.
SiO2 on Si has been widely used to this end where thickness of the insulating barrier
(usually 90 or 290 nm) is crucial for the identiﬁcation of the ﬂakes via optical
contrast [35, 36] or other non-invasive techniques such as micro-Raman analysis
[37–39], ellipsometry [40], etc. Once the ﬂakes are positioned and identiﬁed on the
desired substrate they can be processed further.
As graphene and the majority of the 2D materials are typically of a semi-metallic
or semiconducting behavior, they have been extensively processed following the
standard roadmap for III-V semiconductor compounds. We will show a thorough
and detailed study of some of the most common nanofabrication processes involving
2D materials in which one or several steps of EBL must be performed. We will
concentrate our efforts in the most typical structures and geometries found in these
low-dimensional systems that might serve the reader as the ﬁrst steps into the
generation of eventually more complicated devices.
3.5.1 Hall bars in graphene heterostructures
In this section we will show how to fabricate a Hall bar device in a hBN/graphene/
hBN/graphite heterostructure [41]. The encapsulation of graphene in hBN guaran-
tees a complete isolation of the graphene ﬂake from the external environment [42], so
the fabrication of high-quality graphene devices can be achieved. Furthermore, the
group of Klaus Ensslin [43] reported seminal results showing that the use of a
graphite backgate can improve the performances of encapsulated graphene devices.
Before a standard exfoliation of the single ﬂakes on SiO2, the substrate was
cleaned with acetone (ultrasonic bath for 3 min) then isopropanol (or ethanol) for
30 s and ﬁnally annealed in our rapid thermal process (RTP) furnace (AS-One
100-Annealsys) for 15 min at 400°C in vacuum. For exfoliation of graphene and
graphite, we previously put a thick natural graphite piece on a Scotch Magic tape
where it was folded onto itself. We transferred the thinner graphite pieces onto
Nanofabrication
3-26

[Page 116]
another adhesive tape with lower force adhesion, Ultron tape (1009R-60). In the
last step we transferred different graphene and graphite layers on the Si/SiO2
substrate through the peeling process. The use of Ultron tape (instead of the
direct use of Magic tape) guarantees a minor contamination of the graphene ﬂake
and of the substrate. Typically, single layer (SL) or bilayer (BL) graphene ﬂakes
are chosen. A graphite ﬂake with a thickness between 10 and 20 nm is also
selected.
On the other hand, the hBN ﬂakes have to be subsequently obtained for the
fabrication of graphene-hBN based heterostructures. Particular attention in the
choice of the bottom hBN ﬂake is requested [44] because the thickness should be
around 35–40 nm and well measured to avoid a problem during the etching process
(avoiding short-circuited graphite with ohmic contacts). Ultra-pure bulk crystals of
hBN used for exfoliation were bought from HQ Graphene. The exfoliation process
was similar to graphene, but for hBN we use only Ultron tape (1009R-60) and the
peeling occurs on a hot plate at 100°C after heating the substrate for 1 min. In this
way, relatively big ﬂakes with a minimum lateral size of 30 μm and a thickness
between 10 and 40 nm were obtained with less tape contaminant. The residual
contaminant of the tape was deﬁnitively removed after a standard cleaning process
(acetone and isopropanol) and an annealing at 350°C with Ar atmosphere in the
RTP furnace.
After exfoliation, a preliminary characterization was obtained by optical contrast
of the graphene, graphite and hBN ﬂakes. The number of layers for graphene were
conﬁrmed by MicroRaman measurements [45, 46], and proﬁlometer measurements
gave us the accurate information regarding the thickness and quality (roughness) of
hBN and graphite ﬂakes. After choosing the ﬂakes, the fabrication of the graphene/
hBN heterostructures takes places. Two different dry transfer techniques based on
polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) stamps and polycarbonate (PC) ﬁlm [47] or poly-
propylene carbonate [PPC] [48] are used. In the following we describe the PPC
technique in detail [41].
Fabrication steps for an hBN/graphene/hBN/graphite heterostructure
1. Preparation of the PDMS stamp. A mixture 1:10 of the bicomponent
SYLGARD® 184 was placed (after removal of air bubbles with various steps
of vacuum-vent in a bell jar) in a Petri dish and baked at 75°C for 4–5 h.
2. Preparation of the PC ﬁlm on microscope glass slide. Preparation of the
PDMS stamp with the PPC ﬁlm. A square piece of 0.8 cm2 of PDMS is cut
and placed in a glass slide. The surface of the PDMS stamp is functionalized
in an atmosphere of O2 through ICP-RIE (see section 3.4.1). A solution of
polypropylene previously dissolved in anisole (concentration of 9%) is spun
on the PDMS stamp and baked for 15 min at 100°C in vacuum. After its
preparation, PPC ﬁlm on PDMS can be also used as stamp for longer time
(typically 1 week).
3. Placing the PPC ﬁlm and PDMS stamp on a glass slide. A small piece
(9 mm2) of PDMS and PPC stamp is cut and stuck with transparent glue on a
glass slide. Everything is ready for the transfer process.
Nanofabrication
3-27

[Page 117]
4. Pickup of the hBN (bottom ﬂake). The stamp is placed over the Si/SiO2
substrate with the bottom hBN and by using the micrometer stages and the
optical microscope, the top hBN ﬂake (ﬁgure 3.14(a) 1A) is located and
positioned under the stamp. Then, the SiO2 substrate approaches the PDMS
stamp through the z-movement stage until a contact area appears
(ﬁgure 3.14(a) step 2A). If the contact area is too far from the ﬂake, the
tilt stage can be used to increase it. The temperature of the substrate is
increased up to 60°C and the PPC will extend and cover the whole ﬂake.
Turning off the heater the SiO2 substrate is removed with the z-movement
allowing to pick up the bottom hBN ﬂake by the stamp. In this step the ﬂake
has been transferred on the PPC ﬁlm(ﬁgure 3.14(a) 3A).
5. hBN/graphite. Then the graphite ﬂake (ﬁgure 3.14(b)) on Si/SiO2 is aligned
with (X,Y) stages and approaches the hBN bottom ﬂake until a contact area
(between SiO2 substrate and stamp) appears. Tilt in case the bubble is too
far from the ﬂake (4A, 5A in ﬁgure 3.14(a)). The bottom hBN ﬂake is
deposited on the graphite ﬂake. The temperature of the substrate with the
Figure 3.14. (a) Transfer steps process for hBN/Graphite heterostructure. (b) Optical images of bottom hBN
(top left), graphite (bottom left) and their correspondent heterostructure.
Nanofabrication
3-28

[Page 118]
graphite is increased up to 150°C which permits the PPC ﬁlm to melt and
enhancement of the adhesion between graphite and hBN. Therefore, the
hBN bottom ﬂake is deposited on the graphite ﬂake reaching the above
temperature (ﬁgure 3.14(a) 6A).
6. The result is a heterostructure of graphite/hBN on a Si/SiO2 substrate (see
ﬁgure 3.14(b)). The heterostructure was cleaned in anisole, acetone and
isopropanol and annealed at 350°C in Ar in our RPT for 15 min to remove
the contamination before the next steps of the transfer process.
7. hBN/graphene. Using a new stamp, we pick up the top hBN ﬂake from the
SiO2 substrate approaching the PDMS stamp, extending the contact area
with the temperature (until 60°C) and removing the substrate from the stamp
through z-movement (ﬁgure 3.15(a) 1B, 2B). Once the top hBN ﬂake stays on
the PPC ﬁlm (3B in ﬁgure 3.15(a)), we align and approach with (x,y,z) stage
the graphene ﬂake on SiO2 with the top hBN ﬂake (ﬁgure 3.15(a) 4B). After
the appearance of the contact area (5A in ﬁgure 3.15(a)), it is increased with
the temperature at 150°C, then through the melting of the PPC ﬁlm and
removing the substrate with z-movement, the top hBN ﬂake is released on
Figure 3.15. (a) Transfer steps process for hBN/graphene heterostructure. (b) Optical images of top hBN (top
left) graphene (bottom left) and their correspondent heterostructure.
Nanofabrication
3-29

[Page 119]
the graphene ﬂake (6B in ﬁgure 3.15(a)). Now the heterostructure of graphene/
hBN (top) was obtained and cleaned in anisole, acetone and isopropanol
(ﬁgure 3.15(b)). So the heterostructure of graphene/hBN (top) is ready to be
picked up and placed on the previously prepared heterostructure of graphite/
hBN (bottom).
8. Graphite/hBN/graphene/hBN. First the pickup of the heterostructure gra-
phene/hBN(top) takes place with a new stamp. Introducing the SiO2
substrate to the PDMS stamp, extending the contact area with the temper-
ature (until 60°C) and removing the substrate from the stamp through
z-movement. After this step the heterostructure of graphene/hBN(top) is
transferred on the PPC ﬁlm (steps 1C, 2C, 3C in ﬁgure 3.16(a)). Then the
alignment and approach of the heterostructure of graphite/hBN (bottom) on
SiO2 substrate with the heterostructure of graphene/hBN takes place until we
obtain a contact area (4C, 5C in ﬁgure 3.16(a)). Increasing the temperature
(150°C), the heterostructure of graphene/hBN (top) is transferred on the
heterostructure of graphite/hBN(bottom) (6C in ﬁgure 3.16(a)).
9. Cleavage and cleaning of the substrate. This is the best moment for the
cleavage of the substrate because the area with the heterostructure is easily
recognizable and the heterostructure is still protected by PPC ﬁlm. The whole
heterostructure is so cleaned in anisole, acetone and isopropanol and annealed
in our RTP furnace at 350°C in Ar atmosphere removes contamination and
PPC residual from the heterostructure and from the substrate.
Once the hBN/graphene/hBN/graphite heterostructure is obtained, the fabrica-
tion by EBL of the ﬁnal device is ready to be tackled. During this process a lot of
blisters or air bubbles were trapped within the heterostructure. It is important to
choose areas free of bubbles since it would reduce the quality of the encapsulated
graphene. The best area was chosen through optical microscope and Raman
spectroscopy (ﬁgure 3.16).
EBL Fabrication steps of hBN/graphene/hBN/graphite device in Hall bar geometry.
1. First EBL for the deﬁnition of the markers. For high-quality devices, it is
better to use wafers without pre-patterned markers to avoid contamination
due to a preliminary process (in particular evaporation). So, ﬁrstly the
deﬁnition of marker arrays is necessary. For this aim, we prepare a CAD ﬁle
in which there are markers with a separation of 80 μm. The dose of each
marker is 270 μC cm−2. After the coating and baking of the homemade
PMMA4% resist, the ﬁrst EBL process for the markers deﬁnition takes place.
In this case we used a WF of 1000 μm and the aperture of 30 μm with a
nominal resolution of 6 nm and the carried process is very similar to the
process described in section 3.3.2. This time the WA is the whole sample, so
typically 4 × 4 mm, but the size of the sample depends on the dimensions of
the sample holder. After exposure, a shorter developing time (30 s) in 1:3
MIBK: IPA is chosen to avoid damage of the resist.
2. Measuring distances and preparation of the CAD ﬁle. Once the markers are
deﬁned on the PMMA, we need to measure accurately the distances between
Nanofabrication
3-30

[Page 120]
the ﬂake and the closer markers, obtained by optical microscopy and taking
into account the lower optical contrast of graphene ﬂake due to the presence
of the hBN underneath it. In the CAD ﬁle, we deﬁne the mask Hall Bar (ﬁrst
EBL process) and then add layers for the metallic pads and graphite contacts
(second EBL process). The preparation of the CAD ﬁle in heterostructures
with graphite bottom gate is particularly complex, since it is very important
to avoid eventual cracks in the heterostructure.
3. Second EBL process for deﬁnition of an etching mask.
The second EBL process is very similar to the section 3.3.3, the main
difference in this case is the lower dose for the Hall bar mask (250 μC cm−2)
due to the presence of hBN ﬂakes. The presence of the dielectric nature of the
hBN could provoke charging effects and for this reason a smaller dose has to
be used. After a developing time of 2 min, the Hall bar mask is deﬁned (ﬁrst
step in ﬁgure 3.17(a)).
Figure 3.16. (a) Final steps of the dry transfer method for a h-BN/graphene/hBN/graphite heterostructure.
(b) Optical images of graphene/top hBN structure (top left) and the graphite/bottom hBN (bottom left) one
and the combination of both resulting in the whole heterostructure (right).
Nanofabrication
3-31

[Page 121]
4. Shaping the heterostructure into a Hall bar and deﬁnition of the pyramidal
proﬁles in encapsulated graphene.
Through ICP-RIE we etched the encapsulated graphene in the Hall bar
geometry and created the appropriate etching proﬁle for side-contacts in SF6
atmosphere with an etching rate of 3 nm s−1 on hBN. In our ICP-RIE recipe,
we used 40 sccm of SF6, a power in RIE mode of 75 W at 6 mbar of pressure
and 10 °C of temperature for typically 12 s. A controlled etching rate gives us
the possibility to etch only the top hBN ﬂake avoiding the full etching of the
bottom hBN (see second step in ﬁgure 3.17(a)). In this case, the etching does
not have a vertical proﬁle, as typically desired in a standard etching process,
but it is pyramidal [42]. To avoid possible oxidation of the atomic exposed
(to the external environment) graphene layer, it is important to minimize the
time between the etching and the following nanofabrication steps. It is
important to note that, after this process, the markers pattern is transferred
to the substrate. In fact, the silicon oxide is also partially etched (etching rate
∼1.5 nm s−1) with this recipe.
5. Annealing process for residual resist and bubbles removal.
After the etching process, the sample is cleaned in acetone and isopropa-
nol and annealed through RTP in Ar atmosphere at 380°C. In this way,
contamination of the resist and residual bubbles from the heterostructure are
removed. In fact, it’s well known that an annealing (below 500°C) reduces
the number of bubbles encapsulated in the graphene heterostructure [49].
6. Third EBL process for deﬁnition of a new array of markers.
The pattern of markers transferred on the substrate in the ﬁrst lithography
are not properly visible by SEM through a new resist coating, but their
a
Graphite Bottom Gate
Contact
10 µm
10 µm
10 µm
10 µm
b
c
Figure 3.17. (a) From the top to the bottom: deﬁnition of the Hall Bar through EBL, etching of the Hall Bar
through ICP-RIE process, deﬁnition of the contacts (and contact for the graphite bottom gate) with a second
EBL. (b) Final devices after evaporation of Cr/Au and lift-off process. (c) Four probe resistance in function of
the back-gate voltage at room temperature for an encapsulated graphene device.
Nanofabrication
3-32

[Page 122]
presence is still well visible by optical contrast (also through a new layer of
coating). For this reason, we repeat the markers array with the same EBL
process described in the point 1.
7. Adaptation of the CAD ﬁle to the new markers.
Without the use of an interferometric stage, the new markers are slightly
shifted with respect to the old markers (and the graphene Hall bar position).
The measurement of this shift can be properly measured by optical micro-
scope and the CAD ﬁle (in particular contact and pads) is so adapted with
respect to the new markers’ position.
8. Fourth EBL process for the fabrication of edge contacts.
Then, after a new deposition of the PMMA resist, with a new nano-
lithography process, we deﬁne the ohmic contacts and the backgate contact
(see third step in ﬁgure 3.17(a)). This process is again very similar to the
process in section 3.3.3 apart from the reduction of the dose at 270 μC cm−2
for the ohmic contacts.
9. Evaporation of Cr/Au and lift-off process.
Finally, after an evaporation of Cr (10 nm) and Au (50 nm) and the
subsequent lift-off process in acetone, the ﬁnal sample is ready to be charac-
terized (ﬁgure 3.17(b)). In this case, a controlled and low evaporation rate is
strictly necessary. In fact, only with low evaporation rate and pyramidal proﬁle
previously obtained, is it possible to achieve a good contact between the atomic
exposed layer of graphene and the evaporated metals, in particular the adhesion
layer metal (Cr) [50].
Using this fabrication method, it is possible to obtain edge contacts with a contact
resistance lower than 1 kΩ μm−1. A typical characterization of an encapsulated
device at room temperature is shown in ﬁgure 3.17(c), where we report a four-probe
resistance in function of the back-gate voltage. Encapsulated graphene devices have
very sharp and narrow charge neutrality point (CNP) in comparison with standard
graphene devices on SiO2 substrate and the ‘Dirac peak’ is typically very close to
zero (see red dashed line in ﬁgure 3.17(c)). Typical mobilities13 in encapsulated
graphene are higher than 100 000 cm2 (Vs)−1 at room temperature [41]. For
comparison, in graphene on SiO2 substrate, the mobility is always lower than
20 000 cm2 (Vs)−1 also at low temperatures and with chemical treatments to
reduce the inﬂuence of the substrate [27].
3.5.2 Quantum point contacts deﬁned by cryo-etching
It is important to highlight the cryo-etching process due to its novelty applied to
graphene [51]. Cryo-etching has been widely used in the fabrication of quasi-2D
13 The carrier mobilities are obtained through the ﬁt of the resistance in function of the carrier density with the
use of the ﬁeld effect formula, at zero magnetic ﬁeld. The carrier density is directly calculated by the gate
voltage [41].
Nanofabrication
3-33

[Page 123]
structures achieving an unprecedented control of the edge-deﬁnition. It was
successfully implemented in silicon-based devices, where it was possible to control
and deﬁne low-roughness sidewalls, observe the absence of sidewall scalloping [52],
and generate samples with a high aspect ratio and cleaning [53] in nanometric-size
structures [54]. The extension of the cryo-etching technique to graphene [51] allowed
us to build graphene nanoconstrictions or quantum point contacts (QPCs) with very
low sidewall roughness.
EBL Fabrication of a QPC in an h-BN/graphene/hBN heterostructure
1. First EBL: bar deﬁnition and deﬁnition of pyramidal proﬁle. A bar (instead of
a Hall Bar) was deﬁned on the graphene heterostructure by EBL, using
PMMA as a mask. The etching for the edge contacts is obtained by a dry
etching processing with the same recipe of the previous section. A standard
cleaning process and a rapid thermal annealing was performed at 350 °C in
Ar atmosphere for 15 min to remove eventual small non-visible bubbles and
the PMMA remnants.
2. Second EBL: Edge contacts. A second EBL is performed for the deﬁnition of
the edge contacts after the deposition of Cr/Au through e-beam evaporator
and lift off process.
3. Third EBL: Cryo-etching. We used PMMA but with lower concentration
(2%) in chlorobenzene (resist thickness 70 nm), a smaller aperture (7.5 μm
with a dose of 185 μC cm2) and a WF of 100 μm (using closer markers with a
separation of 70 μm previously deﬁned in the second EBL). In this way a
nominal resolution of 2 nm can be obtained. From a technological point of
view, the choice of a constriction with V-shape, in contrast to an ‘abrupt’
constriction or a ribbon, as well as the use of a thinner resist, permit the
reduction of the proximity effect, as explained in section 3.2.3. The cryo-
etching process was performed at −110°C in a controlled Ar/SF6 (10/40
sccm) atmosphere at P = 6 mbar for 18 s, P = 75 W. The use of cryogenic
temperatures and Ar allows us to obtain QPCs with very low roughness. Ar
gas, instead of the more common O2 gas for a cryo-etching recipe, reduces
the possibility of passivation to a minimum. The etching rate of the cryo-
etching recipe is 2 nm s−1.
Figure 3.18 shows a few SEM images of a typical encapsulated graphene QPC
etched by means of the cryo-etching technique (magniﬁcation of 450k) (a) and by
standard room temperature etching process (magniﬁcation of 300k) (b). The upper
panels display the original SEM micrographs while the lower part colored SEM
images help to visualize the borders of the constrictions. The higher roughness of the
second image is evident at the edges, in comparison with the very well-deﬁned cryo-
etched structure. Two devices are shown in the SEM image of ﬁgure 3.18(c), where
two different nanoconstrictions are deﬁned. The upper one has a width of ∼200 nm,
whereas the lower one has a width of ∼100 nm. The high quality of these QPCs
allowed almost ideal ballistic transport. Therefore, the cryo-etching technique paves the
way for the fabrication of more complex graphene-based nanostructured prototypical
devices with truly ballistic transport of high-mobility carriers [41].
Nanofabrication
3-34

[Page 124]
3.5.3 Dual-gated graphene FETs
Here we will describe the fabrication process of terahertz detectors based on dual-
gated graphene ﬁeld-effect transistors (FETs), using encapsulated graphene [55, 56].
Thanks to its high carrier mobility, frequency-independent absorption, gapless or
controllable gap according to the number of layers (monolayer or bilayer) graphene
is a very interesting material for the development of detectors working in the
terahertz region of the electromagnetic spectrum, still severely lacking in terms of
solid-state devices.
Fabrication of a dual-gated FET in a h-BN/graphene/hBN heterostructure
1. Fabrication of the heterostructure. By using a similar procedure to that
described in section 3.5.1, we obtain a hBN-graphene-hBN heterostructure.
2. First EBL: bar deﬁnition. For the bar deﬁnition, a nanolithography and
etching process similar to the Hall bar deﬁnition described in section 3.5.1
was carried out. After the deposition of the resist, a bar is deﬁned by means
of the lithography process (step 2 in ﬁgure 3.19) and etched with ICP-RIE in
SF6 atmosphere (step 3 in ﬁgure 3.19).
3. Second EBL: edge contacts. Edge contacts were fabricated after annealing in
Ar atmosphere at 350°C, through a second nanolithography process (step 4
Figure 3.18. (a) SEM image of a nanocontact etched by cryo-etching ICP-RIE. (b) Micrograph of a similar
constriction etched using standard (no cryo) ICP-RIE. A noticeable reduction of the roughness along the edges
can be found when using low-temperature RIE. (c) Tilted SEM image of typical devices with nanocontacts in
encapsulated graphene. Reproduced with permission of Springer.
Nanofabrication
3-35

[Page 125]
in ﬁgure 3.19) and subsequent evaporation of Cr/Au (5–40 nm) and lift off
process (step 5 in ﬁgure 3.19).
4. Transfer of a new hBN ﬂake. Before the deﬁnition of the top gates it is
necessary to cover the device with a dielectric ﬁlm to avoid preparing new
edge contacts instead of gates! In our clean room we do not have equipment
(i.e. atomic layer deposition, sputtering, etc) for dielectric deposition, which
would be the ﬁrst option. On the other hand, a few-layer hBN ﬂake could be
a very good choice to obtain the higher performances of the device. For this
reason, we transferred a new ﬂake of hBN on the device (step 6 in
ﬁgure 3.19).
5. Third EBL: Top gates. The top gates were deﬁned by another EBL process
(step 7 in ﬁgure 3.18) and evaporation and lift-off of Cr/Au (5/40 nm) (step 8
in ﬁgure 3.18). The ﬁnal device, an asymmetrical dual grating gate FET
based on graphene (ADGG-GFET), is shown in the ﬁnal (eighth) step of
ﬁgure 3.19.
A multi-ﬁnger dual-gate topology improves considerably the performance of THz
detectors based on plasma waves, as demonstrated theoretically and experimentally.
The use of asymmetric ﬁngers FETs based on graphene instead of a large antenna
tuned for THz frequency has a technological advantage with respect to a conven-
tional antenna in terms of space (see [55] and references therein for more details).
3.5.4 Hall bars in other 2D materials
In 2005, Novoselov et al [57] demonstrated that, beyond graphene, it is possible to
obtain other 2D materials by mechanical exfoliation. They are named Van der
Waals or 2D materials (hBN, MoS2, MoSe2, MoTe2, NbSe2, Bi2Sr2CaCu2O) [58],
highlighting their very small thickness in comparison with the other two dimensions.
Starting from a bulk 3D material, it is possible to obtain a 2D material through
Figure 3.19. Fabrication process of FETs in encapsulated graphene can be divided into three different steps:
bar deﬁnition, fabrication of the side contacts and top gates.
Nanofabrication
3-36

[Page 126]
mechanical exfoliation. This is possible because layered materials show strong
in-plane bonds, but weak van-der-Waals coupling between layers. Transition
metal dichalcogenide (TMD) materials attracted particular interest among the
2D materials [59]. They are thin semiconductors composed by a transition metal
atom (M) and a chalcogen atom (X). In this section we describe the fabrication by
EBL of hall bars in MoSe2 and MoTe2, as well as their preliminary character-
ization. They have a direct band gap between 1 and 2 eV and represent excellent
candidates for application in electronics and optics.
MoSe2 devices
We started with the mechanical exfoliation of the material from the bulk material
(grown by HQ Graphene). The bulk material was ﬁrstly peeled with Magic tape and
later transferred in the blue tape, where we peeled 5–6 times before placing the blue
tape on a SiO2 substrate (it was previously cleaned in acetone and isopropanol and
treated with plasma cleaner for 5 min).
The ﬂake is obtained and identiﬁed by optical microscope (inset in ﬁgure 3.20(a))
and a ﬁrst characterization was made by proﬁlometer (ﬁgure 3.20(a)), before the
Raman measurements (ﬁgure 3.20(b)). For example, for the ﬂake of the inset of
ﬁgure 3.20(a), the measured thickness was 5 nm and considering the thickness of a
monolayer ﬂake (9.5 Å), we can estimate a ﬁve-layer MoSe2 ﬂake. This was conﬁrmed
by Raman spectroscopy, comparing another 5 nm ﬂake in literature [60] with which we
found similarities. There is a prominent A1G peak on the material also in bulk as well
as other peaks (E1G, E1
2G, B1
2G). We tried to transfer with the PPC method (see section
3.5.1 for details) this ﬂake on pre-patterned contacts, but the ﬂake was damaged, as
presented in ﬁgure 3.20(c).
The device worked but we noticed a large electrical instability. For this reason, we
performed a different fabrication method on another 4L-MoSe2 ﬂake. In this case
we used EBL on ARP67904 (coating at 4000 rpm, bake 160°C for 10 min) and
evaporation of Ti and Au (5 and 35 nm). The ﬁnal device is shown in ﬁgure 3.20(d).
We measured the drain current as a function of the gate voltage from −30 to 40 V
at ﬁxed drain voltage (Vd = 2 V) (ﬁgure 3.20(e)) and the behavior of the current is
typical of an n-type semiconductor, as expected [61]. We fabricated also a 1L-MoSe2
device (shown in the inset of ﬁgure 3.20(f)) but it did not work probably due
oxidation. We estimated the gap with photoluminescence (PL) measurement
through Raman, as shown in ﬁgure 3.20(f). The measured gap was 1.57 eV [62].
MoTe2 devices
The mechanical exfoliation of MoTe2 is similar to that in the MoSe2 material. The
only difference consists in peeling 3–4 times with the Magic tape and placing a stripe
of the blue tape and peeling only once with the blue tape. In fact, this material was
much harder to be exfoliated than other 2D materials. We also tried to transfer
ﬂakes with thicknesses of approximately 20 nm (optical image in the inset of
ﬁgure 3.21(a)) on pre-patterned contacts. The transfer process was successful as
shown measurements were electrically unstable.
Nanofabrication

For this reason, we fabricated another device with the same process described in
the previous subsections. The ﬁnal device was designed on a quintuple layer ﬂake
(optical image in the inset of ﬁgure 3.21(b)) whose thickness was characterized by
Raman spectroscopy (as shown in ﬁgure 3.21(b)) where a 0.17 ratio between the
characteristic peaks B1
2G and E1
2G conﬁrmed the number of layers in the sample
[63]. After the EBL process, evaporation of Ti and Au (5 and 35 nm) and lift-off, the
ﬁnal device is shown in ﬁgure 3.21(c) (one contact was broken during the lift-off
process). A preliminary measurement of the drain current (Id) as a function of the
gate voltage (Vg) at ﬁxed drain voltage (Vd = 18 V) (ﬁgure 3.21(d)) shows the
characteristic ambipolar behavior of this material [64].
We also tried to transfer ﬂakes with thicknesses of approximately 20 nm (optical
image in the inset of ﬁgure 3.21(a)) on pre-patterned contacts. The transfer process
was successful as shown measurements were electrically unstable.
3.6 General applications and new developments in EBL technique
EBL plays a central role for the top-down nanofabrication approach. Its range of
applications in different ﬁelds is really wide, as shown in ﬁgure 3.22.
EBL technique is largely used for the contact deﬁnition of (sub)micrometic-sized
structures (nanowires, nanotubes, etc). A typical example is shown in ﬁgure 3.23(a),
where metal contacts are deﬁned on cobalt nanowires. The EBL process is carried
a

Figure 3.21. (a) Optical image a ﬂake of MoTe2 (inset) transferred on pre-patterned contacts. (b) Raman
spectra of a 5L-MoTe2. (c) Hall-bar-like device on the 5L-MoTe2 ﬂake. (d) Drain current versus gate voltage
sweep with a ﬁxed drain voltage (18 V).
Nanofabrication
3-39

[Page 129]
out after the identiﬁcation of the cobalt nanowires position randomly deposited on
the substrate. In this case the position of the single nanowire can be taken through
SEM and the design for the CAD directly prepared on the SEM image, achieving in
this way an alignment of the contacts with nanometric precision.
EBL is also used in the mask fabrication for optical and x-ray lithography [65].
The fabrication of photomasks is similar to a wafer fabrication. For optical
lithography mask a quartz/glass substrate is covered with a chrome ﬁlm and electron
beam resist. The resist is subsequently exposed through an EBL process and used as
mask for the etching of the chrome ﬁlm in the targeted areas. Features of the order
of ∼1 μm and line control of ∼50 nm is required for an optical lithography mask.
The process for x-ray lithography masks is very similar. The main differences are the
minimum feature size (∼100 nm) and line control (∼20 nm) required and the
materials used for the mask (Si, SiN, SiC, diamond with Au or W as metallic mask).
In the previous sections we introduced some of the limitations of the EBL
technique. New developments of EBL aim to overcome the problems related to the
technique itself. One of the major limitations is the difﬁculty in exposing a large area
due to the limited scan range of the electron beam (the WF) in a reasonable
exposition time. For example, the problems related to defective stitching between
different WFs was already addressed in section 3.2.4. This problem occurs even
when one wants to deﬁne features (for example a line) bigger than the WF size, and
it can be partially solved with the use of an interferometric stage with high
resolution, but a residual stitching error (∼10 nm) is always present. The use of
Figure 3.22. Range of applications of EBL for different ﬁelds.
Nanofabrication
3-40

[Page 130]
the ﬁxed-beam-moving-stage (FBMS) strategy addresses this problem [66]. In
ﬁgure 3.23(b) optical waveguides are deﬁned with this technique [67]. The wave-
guides (length of 2.5 mm and width of 700 nm) are exposed continuously,
maintaining electron beam and dose ﬁxed during the stage movement, thus over-
coming any stitching error.
But probably, the main important limitation, especially for a use of the EBL
technique in the industry, is related with its low throughput due to the long exposition
time to cover large areas. Multi-beam systems for parallel writing are much faster
than standard EBL system and potentially competitive with the optical lithography.
The basic idea is schematically shown in ﬁgure 3.23(c). A low-energy electron beam
(5 keV) is generated by a single electron source. Various electrons beams are obtained
and selected by a programmable aperture plate system (APS), basically composed of
a cover plate (for the division of the original beam), a blanking plate (for the selection
of the beams) and an aperture plate. Different beams with a typical size of 5 μm are so
accelerated up to 100 keV and focused through an electrostatic and magnetic lens on
the wafer with a ﬁnal beam size of 25 nm. The scanning of the beams on the wafer
takes place through the movement scanning stage. This technique was developed for
Figure 3.23. (a) Electrical contacts on cobalt nanowires fabricated through the EBL process. Permission by J
M De Teresa (unpublished). (b) Long optical waveguides exposed with the ﬁxed-beam-moving-stage (FBMS)
approach [67]. (c) Schematic representation of a multibeam system with the aperture plate system [68].
Reprinted with permission from Acta Physica Polonica A.
Nanofabrication
3-41

[Page 131]
the semiconductor industry, and some companies (Mapper Lithography in Delft,
IMS Nanofabrication GmbH in Wien) base their business on this new technology.
3.7 End-of-chapter summary
In this chapter, we have provided the reader with the basic knowledge and
understanding of the indispensable processes in and around EBL, with a ﬁnal
scope of their application for the generation of fully working 2D-material-based
devices. After reviewing the core concepts of an FE-SEM nanolithography set-up
(section 3.2) we have introduced the fundamental (common) concepts for a typical
EBL in the following section. Section 3.3 has been devoted to introducing the
steps for an EBL process as well as the auxiliary, though necessary, processes
essential to obtaining low-dimensional devices, i.e. plasma etching and e-beam
metal evaporation (section 3.4).
In section 3.5 we have combined all the knowledge previously acquired and given
a thorough and complete step-by-step study in the generation of some selected
devices based on graphene and related 2D materials: Hall bars, quantum point
contacts and FETs. We want to stress once more that the different recipes will
deﬁnitely vary between facilities, set-ups and devices to be fabricated but we do
believe that this chapter will be a good starting point for neophytes in the technique.
We encourage Master and PhD students that are taking their ﬁrst steps in EBL
lithography to adapt and optimize these recipes at their convenience.
In section 3.6 we introduce general applications of EBL and new developments
for the EBL technology, thought to overcome its own limitations. In the Student’s
corner we introduce brieﬂy some other applications of EBL in 2D-materials and
provide the reader with some introductory and seminal papers to continue their
study further. We have included a few questions for a student self-assessment where
they will challenge their acquired knowledge. The authors would like to thank the
reader for any feedback that could be remitted to improve this text.
3.8 Student’s corner
So far, we’ve dedicated this chapter to electron beam lithography using a speciﬁc set-
up but trying to introduce general concepts of this powerful technique. We have
thoroughly explained how to obtain some devices in nanoelectronics such as Hall
bars and FETs while the generation of quantum point contacts with smooth walls
has been introduced because of the unusual combination of EBL and cryo-etching.
Nevertheless, the student can go deeper in the application of EBL to 2D materials.
For that, here below we mention other devices and geometries relevant in nano-
electronics. Therefore, we do encourage the reader to take some time to go through
the proposed examples and literature.
• Nano-etched suspended graphene can be obtained by a combination of scanning
electron microscope (in this case present in a focused ion beam system) with a
selective gas injection needle that tailors pre-deposited chemical vapour deposi-
tion (CVD) graphene into line-cuts with a resolution down to ∼15 nm, whilst the
edge roughness stays below 5 nm [69].
Nanofabrication
3-42

[Page 132]
• Graphene nanoelectrode arrays can be obtained solely by the use of an EBL
set-up combined with a standard voltage ramp apparatus. After the spin
coating of MicroChem 950 PMMA,A5 with a speed of 4000 rpm for 45 s, a
CVD graphene sheet was patterned as follows: a 5 nm width dash line
between each pair of electrodes was designed on top of the graphene in order
to obtain 50 μm length windows creating a pattern of numerous nanocontacts
that are etched by standard oxygen RIE. Finally, electroburning technique
was carried out by applying a slow voltage ramp to the device until the
graphene links burn [70]
• Generation of unusual systems such as edge-free Corbino graphene geometry
where concentric circular electrodes with no physical edge connecting the inner
and outer electrodes is deﬁned by a series of self-aligned EBL in combination
with the van-der-Waals dry transfer technique [71]. Graphene nanoribbons
(GNRs) represented the ﬁrst benchmark attempts to study real ballistic trans-
port and the effect of the edges in low-dimensional systems. GNRs have been
largely generated by EBL typically using negative tone resists such as HSQ [72]
or MIBK-diluted HSQ [73] in order to attain samples with extreme length-to-
width ratios, much larger than present in QPCs.
• Josephson junctions represent a novel technological paradigm with various
applications in nanoelectronics. They consist of a normal (metallic or semi-
conducting) region known as weak link coupled to superconducting leads
harnessing dissipationless electrical current. Graphene-based Josephson junc-
tions have been possible due to the generation of EBL-designed (sub)micro-
metric devices. Such junctions could be as simple as two bare superconducting
electrodes in direct contact to the graphene sheet via edge contacts [74] or to
more complicated fabrication process involving a ﬁrst EBL step on a single
PMMA layer plus the deposition of the superconducting leads followed by a
second (more complex) lithography with a PMMA/HSQ mask combined with
an RIE process for the deﬁnition of the ﬁnal geometry of the system [75].

Choose the correct answer among the different options (more than one correct
answer is possible):
• An FE-SEM allows working at low potentials which is crucial when dealing
with…
A. Metallic ﬁlms;
B. Semiconducting ﬁlms;
C. Dielectric ﬁlms.

A. It is not possible to take tilted SEM images as the SEM conﬁguration is
ﬁxed (beam, detectors, etc).
B. In-Lens detector is the best choice.
C. ET detector is the best choice.

is removed in the exposed areas.
B. After the exposure and development processes, the negative tone resist
is removed in the exposed areas.
C. Development determines the tone of the resist.
• Choose the WF with the nominal higher resolution.
A. 100 μm
B. 500 μm
C. 1 mm

• If you need to expose an array (larger than 1 mm) of the same nanostructured

lithography?
A. Expose the whole WA in a unique (U,V) center.
B. A smaller area (according to the used WF) CAD ﬁle is prepared and
the whole WA is exposed thanks to the creation of a matrix of CAD
elements with shifted (U,V) centers.
C. The whole CAD ﬁle is prepared, but we translate the WA exposure at

• How can we overcome the stitching problem?
A. Through the use of an interferometric position stage.
B. Through the use of a micrometric position stage.
C. Through the use of markers.
• Suppose that during an alignment process with markers, their centers are not
visible in the windows that are opened by the EBL program. How would you
proceed?
A. I would repeat the 3-point alignment and probably I would need to
repeat also the global origin and angle correction.
B. I would change the dimensions of the markers square in the CAD ﬁle
(I would make them bigger so that I can see their center in the next four
markers alignment).
C. I would repeat the alignment process on the four markers as many
times as necessary. In the ﬁrst alignment, I would center each window
in the visible part of the marker so that in the subsequent alignments I
will ﬁnally identify the center of the markers.
• What are the advantages of the HSQ resist with respect to the PMMA?
A. Versatility.
B. Resolution.
C. Lower exposition time due to the lower used doses.

• How would you reduce the PE problem through fragmentation?
A. In an isolated feature by reducing the dose at the center and increasing
the dose at the edges.
B. In an isolated feature by increasing the dose at the center and
decreasing the dose at the edges.
C. In packed structures by increasing the dose between neighbouring
features.
• How would you solve a lift-off problem due to the crosslinking of the resist?
