IOP Publishing
Nanofabrication
Nanolithography techniques and their applications
Jose´ Marı´a De Teresa
Chapter 4
Focused electron beam induced deposition
Javier Pablo-Navarro, Soraya Sangiao, César Magén and José María De Teresa
In this chapter, the physical principles, applications and perspectives of focused
electron beam induced deposition (FEBID) are overviewed. FEBID is a single-step
direct-write nanolithography technique relying on a nanometre-size electron beam
to decompose the molecules of a precursor gas delivered onto a substrate. The
process is carried out inside a scanning electron microscope with a gas injector
system. The shape and particular dimensions of the resulting nanodeposit can be
tailored from the micron scale down to the nanometre scale. The absence of resists
makes FEBID extremely adaptable to virtually any kind of substrate material or
topography, including cantilevers and tips. The availability of precursors enables the
growth of a wide range of functional materials, whose physical properties (conduct-
ing, insulating, mechanical, superconducting, magnetic, optical) can be tailored to a
great extent, with relevant applications in nanoelectronics, nanophotonics, nano-
sensing and nanomechanics. Being an additive and site-selective technique, FEBID
offers unique possibilities as a 3D nano-printer and prototyping of complex
nanoarchitectures.
4.1 Introduction
The possibility of fabricating micro- and nanostructures paves the way for systems at
the nanometre scale with novel capabilities and better performance. In this regard,
micro- and nanolithography techniques are common methods used to produce
structures by transferring a speciﬁc pattern to a sample. These nanoscale objects are
fabricated following two different approaches: the bottom-up and the top-down
strategies (see chapter 1 for more details). The bottom-up approach refers to
architectures based on self-assembly or self-organization processes, such as the
electrochemical deposition and the block co-polymers technology with promising
applications and high tunability in terms of the size, shape and periodicity of arrays
of objects [1]. The top-down methods are basically rooted in the deposition of thin
ﬁlms onto which the motifs are patterned by lithography techniques [2, 3]. Several
doi:10.1088/978-0-7503-2608-7ch4
4-1
ª IOP Publishing Ltd 2020. All rights,
including for text and data mining (TDM), artificial intelligence (AI) training, and similar technologies, are reserved.

[Page 140]
types of top-down nanometre scale patterning lithography methods have been
developed: electron beam lithography (EBL) [4, 5]; focused ion beam lithography
[6]; near-ﬁeld scanning optical lithography [7], laser lithography [8], holographic or
interferometric lithography [9, 10], extreme ultraviolet lithography [11], x-ray
lithography [12], zone plate array lithography [13]; stamping techniques such as
micro-contact printing [14, 15], nano-imprint [16] and step and ﬂash imprint
lithography [17]; and others such as nanografting [18] or dip-pen lithography [19].
However, although these nanofabrication techniques have a great potential for a
wide range of applications, they also present some limitations such as the need of an
additional layer prior to the fabrication, the use of masks and lift-off processes,
material implantation, sputtering and deposition processes, the effect of elevated
temperatures for the fabrication, the inability to grow deposits with complex
materials and shapes in 3D or the use of several steps for the growth of the ﬁnal
nanostructure, among others. In this context, focused electron beam induced
deposition (FEBID) emerges as a direct-write fabrication approach for nanosyn-
thesis of functional materials. The method is similar to chemical vapor deposition,
but, rather than on thermal dissociation, it is based on the electron-impact
decomposition of gas molecules that are continuously refreshed at the area of
growth. In FEBID, the deposition is conﬁned to the area where the primary electron
beam impinges and to the surrounding area where secondary electrons are
generated. This technique has already demonstrated to serve as a robust nano-
fabrication method for electronic [20], magnetic [21], photonic [22], and mechanical
[23] devices.
4.2 Principles of FEBID
FEBID is a single-step nanolithography technique based on the decomposition by a
ﬁnely-focused electron beam of the molecules of a precursor gas delivered and
adsorbed onto the surface of the substrate, which gives rise to the deposition of a
solid material [24–27]. Before the consolidation of this technique, the potential of
electron beam induced processing for patterning was already hinted in the 1970s by
the local deposition of contaminants while performing scanning electron microscopy
(SEM) [28]. When the electron beam scans a region of the surface of the substrate, a
few-nanometre layer of material starts to cover the scanned area. This phenomenon
is produced by the decomposition of hydrocarbons present in the vacuum chamber
as residual gases, and adsorbed on the surface.
FEBID was formally introduced in 1984 by Matsui and Mori [29, 30], who
injected different precursor gas molecules inside the vacuum chamber on purpose,
producing micro- and nanostructures with different functionalities. Once the
precursor gas molecules are delivered locally near the surface of the substrate,
some of them are dissociated by the electron beam. The non-volatile part of the
molecule is deposited, whereas the volatile fragments are pumped out of the working
chamber. Nowadays, this method is capable of nanometre-scale resolution for the
growth of 2D [31] and 3D [32] architectures. The physical and chemical properties of
the deposit will depend on its obtained geometry and composition, which will be
Nanofabrication
4-2

[Page 141]
determined by the complex interactions between the electron beam, precursor gas
molecules, substrate and the nascent structure [33, 34]. For this reason, under-
standing the inﬂuence of the growth parameters on these interactions is a key point
to develop proper approaches for the fabrication of nanostructures with the
optimum targeted properties (ﬁgure 4.1).
The list of most relevant growth parameters in FEBID includes:
• the energy of the primary electrons, which is determined by the beam
acceleration voltage;
• the electron beam current, deﬁned as the electron ﬂow reaching the sample
surface;
• the dwell time, which identiﬁes the lapse of time for which the electron beam
is held still on a particular position or pixel;
• the pitch, which is the distance between two neighbouring pixels;
• the scan direction or patterning strategy, associated with the trajectory of the
beam within the pattern [34, 35];
• the number of loops, which is the number of single complete scans of the
established pattern;
• the refresh time, which is the period of time between two consecutive loops,
allowing the replenishment of precursor gas molecules in the deposition area.
In addition, the pattern dimensions and geometry, the chamber base pressure, the
precursor gas type, its temperature and ﬂux, the position of the gas injector system
(GIS) nozzle, the substrate material and its temperature are also decisive variables
for the fabrication of nano-objects by FEBID.
However, the simultaneous control of all the parameters playing a role in the
growth process of nanostructures is a daunting task [24], not only for the high
number of variables, but also because some of them depend on the others. With the
goal of learning how these parameters affect the growth operation and handle them,
the dissociation process caused by the electron beam deserves special attention.
Among the many distinct electron–molecule interaction processes [36], the elastic
scattering, vibrational and electronic excitation, dissociative electron attachment,
Figure 4.1. Sketch of the FEBID process. Example with the (CH3)3Pt(CpCH3) molecule.
Nanofabrication
4-3

[Page 142]
neutral and bipolar dissociation, and dissociative ionization can be mentioned.
Given this situation, it may be thought that the beam–molecule interaction is too
complex to have a thorough comprehension of the mechanism at molecular scale.
In fact, few experimental [37] and theoretical studies [38] in this regard can be found
in literature. However, simulations of the fabrication process by continuum models
and approaches of growth geometries by Monte Carlo method have already been
performed to clarify the intricacies of the FEBID growth process [39].
4.2.1 Electron–matter interaction
Since the basic principles of FEBID are closely related to SEM, whose basics are
explained in section 3.2.1, the physical events emerging from the electron–matter
interaction will be ﬁrst introduced and correlated with SEM imaging. First, the
elastic collisions give rise to a broadening of the primary beam which degrades the
lateral resolution. In addition, inelastic collisions lead to other physical events such
as the emission of x-rays and Auger electrons, phonon scattering, or cathodolumi-
nescence (visible light). Also, the electron–sample interaction generates secondary
electrons (SE) and backscattered electrons (BSE), both being used for imaging
purposes. The SE are generated by the inelastic scattering of the primary beam with
the valence electrons in the outer shells of the sample atoms. They are emitted with
typical energies lower than ~50 eV, having mean free paths of few nanometres. Since
only SE emitted near the surface can escape and be detected, they are mainly used to
obtain images of topographic contrast [40, 41]. On the other hand, the BSE are
generated by the quasi-elastic scattering of the primary beam with the atoms. Thus,
the energy lost by these electrons is very small, but the momentum transfer is large,
allowing the electrons to be scattered at high angles. As BSE are high-energy
electrons, they can reach the surface from deep regions of the sample, not giving
accurate topographic information. However, they provide images with composi-
tional contrast given that the scattering cross section of the BSE increases with the
atomic number, Z. The maximum resolution of an SEM image is mainly determined
by the primary electron beam voltage and current, but the interaction phenomena of
the electron beam with the sample is also relevant.
Regarding the physical phenomena involved in the growth of structures by
FEBID, Fowlkes et al noted that the most signiﬁcant contribution to the dissoci-
ation of the precursor gas molecules arises from the primary electrons (PE) and their
generated secondary electrons (SEI), with lower relevance from BSE and their
generated secondary electrons (SEII) [42]. Later, Smith et al stressed the important
role played by forward-scattered electrons (FSE) in the growth of 3D nanostructures
as well as the dependence of their effect on the primary beam energy and the
resulting growth regime [43]. Moreover, Arnold et al evidenced that SEII are
responsible for a halo of deposited material around the scanned area in 2D
structures, affecting the ﬁnal deposition resolution [44]. A similar study by
Schmied et al showed various competing effects in the case of 3D deposits, with
signiﬁcant broadening of the deposit upon decreasing primary electron beam
energies [45]. The FEBID process is thus complex and, in general, the precursor
Nanofabrication
4-4

[Page 143]
dissociation is determined by high-energy electrons (mainly arising from PE and
FSE) as well as by low-energy electrons (mainly SEI and SEII) (ﬁgure 4.2).
In brief, due to the complex electron–matter interaction, different broadening
effects with respect to the primary beam width can be identiﬁed in FEBID, as
represented in ﬁgure 4.3 [44]:
• The edge broadening, in the range of a few nanometres, which is mainly
caused by the SE generated in the growing structure by the PE, the BSE and
the FSE.
• The inner halo broadening, in the range of a few tens of nanometres, which is
mainly caused by the SEI generated by the PE in the substrate and reaching
the substrate surface.
• The outer halo broadening, up to a few hundreds of nanometres, which is
mainly caused by the SEII, generated by the electrons backscattered by the
substrate, that reach the substrate surface.
Figure 4.2. Types of electron emission contributing to the FEBID process. Adapted from Harald Plank, Graz
University of Technology, Austria, 2020.
Figure 4.3. Broadening effects induced by the electron–matter interactions. The d and s subscripts refer to
electrons emerging from the deposit and substrate, respectively. Adapted from [45] with permission of
Hindawi.
Nanofabrication
4-5

[Page 144]
Regarding the inner and outer halo broadening, the substrate atomic number, Z,
and the electron beam voltage, V, have a decisive inﬂuence:
• A higher Z value leads to a smaller interaction volume of the electron beam
in the substrate, thus reducing the outer halo.
• A higher V produces two effects. First, the primary beam is narrower due the
lower aberrations of the electron column lenses, thus diminishing the primary
irradiation spot and the inner halo produced by the SEI. Secondly, a larger
interaction volume of the electron beam with the substrate gives rise to SEII
emitted at further distances, thus enlarging the outer halo.
4.2.2 Monte Carlo method towards FEBID simulations
The Monte Carlo method can be used to comprehend the FEBID growth by
simulating the electrons trajectories and precursor gas dynamics and dissociation
[32, 43]. The interaction between the electron beam and the atoms of the substrate
and of the growing structures can be modelled via a strong interaction mechanism
leading to a multiple electron scattering process. The Monte Carlo method typically
implies two assumptions. First, the electron trajectory only depends on elastic
events, modelled with a relativistic-screened Rutherford scattering cross section.
Secondly, the electron loses energy continuously as a function of the path length in
the solid [25]. A large number of Monte Carlo scattered electron trajectories and SE
generation are simulated. After parametrizing the electron trajectories and calculat-
ing the scattering events, the motion and energy distribution of the electrons through
a material can be obtained as a function of the primary beam conditions and
substrate material [36], as illustrated in ﬁgure 4.4. SE generated close to the surface
Figure 4.4. Monte Carlo simulations using CASINO software [46] of electron trajectories in Si for a PE beam
with incident energy in the range from 5 to 20 keV in normal incidence, with SE and BSE in blue and red,
respectively. The number of simulated trajectories is 1000 at each energy.
Nanofabrication
4-6

[Page 145]
with sufﬁcient energy to escape are candidates to produce precursor dissociation,
thus, deposit growth.
4.2.3 Single precursor species model
In order to shed light on the physical and chemical phenomena behind the deposit
growth, the single precursor species continuum model of FEBID is enlightening.
Considering the precursor adsorbate density, n, and the radial coordinate with
respect to the electron beam position, r, the radially symmetric equation for the
adsorption rate is written as follows [47]:

A rough interpretation of the growth rate can be obtained as a function of tD. On the
one hand, for relatively small tD, typically on the order of nanoseconds, R increases
as a function of f . This growth regime is the electron-limited regime because the
growth is restricted by the inability of the electron beam to decompose all the
available precursor molecules on the surface (ﬁgure 4.5). On the other hand, in
the case of large tD, the dissociation rate exceeds the gas precursor replenishment
rate, giving rise to the precursor-limited regime.
Thus, the balance between the availability of precursor molecules on the growth area
and the electron beam current will determine the regime in which the growth occurs,
affecting not only the growth rate but also the composition of the nanostructures
[48, 49] and the sample morphology [34]. This balance can be also modiﬁed through
the variation of the precursor ﬂux reaching the growth area [48], which can be tuned
by several means such as varying the GIS temperature, changing the GIS valve
opening, modifying the distance between the GIS and the substrate, etc.
Figure 4.5. (a) Deposit height as a function of the dwell time for a ﬁxed total electron dose; (b) representation
of the deposit volume as a function of the electron beam current, indicating the electron-limited regime (ELR)
and the precursor-limited regime (PLR). (a) Adapted with permission from [48]. Copyright 2011 American
Chemical Society. (b) Adapted from [49] with permission of de Gruyter.
Nanofabrication
4-8

[Page 147]
It is also worth mentioning that the precursor molecule sticking coefﬁcient
(or equivalently, the precursor molecule residence time) is temperature dependent.
Thus, an increase in the growth temperature entails a lower residence time of the
precursor molecules, giving rise to lower growth rates [50, 51]. This is particularly
relevant in the growth of 3D structures, where heat dissipation is less efﬁcient [52].
On the other hand, if signiﬁcant heating of the growing deposit occurs, the
decomposition of the precursor gas molecules is more efﬁcient when the reached
temperatures are close to that of the thermal decomposition of the precursor [53–55].
However, if the deposit temperature exceeds the decomposition temperature of the
precursor, a spontaneous and uncontrolled thermal decomposition of the molecules
takes place.
4.2.4 General features of FEBID precursors, growth parameters and applications
The speciﬁc characteristics of the precursor are of utmost importance concerning the
physical and chemical properties exhibited by the FEBID deposit and its subsequent
applications [56]. The precursor material needs to be suitably chosen following
various recommendations, as discussed in reference [56].
The chemical stability is one of the ﬁrst requirements to fulﬁl. The precursor
material is stored inside a GIS container and delivered to the working chamber
through a narrow nozzle that is placed in proximity to the substrate growth area.
Although the precursor reaches the substrate surface in the gas phase, it can be
stored in solid, liquid or gas phase. The transfer from the GIS container to the
working chamber should be free from undesired chemical reactions or premature
decomposition. Sometimes, the lifetime of the precursor is too short or polymer-
ization reactions occur inside the container itself.
• The gas ﬂux needs to be precisely controlled and requires precursor materials
with appropriate vapour pressure values. Rough approximations suggest that
this variable should range between 0.01 and 50 mbar. The two main knobs to
control the gas ﬂux are the automatic or manual valve, which allows the
releasing of the gas into the chamber, and the GIS temperature, which can be
tuned in commercial equipment up to a few tens of degrees.
• In order to obtain high-purity deposits mainly containing the element of
interest, small precursor molecules are preferred, given that long molecules
are more prone to produce non-volatile residues inside the deposit. Along this
line of thought, precursor molecules based on carbonyl groups, phosphine
groups and halogen groups are normally used.
• A large sticking coefﬁcient of the precursor molecule, closely linked to its
residence time, favours a high growth rate. If the sticking coefﬁcient is very
low, the technique becomes too slow. On the other hand, a large sticking
coefﬁcient implies that the precursor molecules remain for a certain period of
time (a few minutes typically) adsorbed on the substrate surface, which can
hinder further sample processing.
Nanofabrication
4-9

[Page 148]
• The precursor molecule should not be hazardous (toxic, ﬂammable, corrosive
or carcinogenic) to minimize health risks during precursor handling (normally
performed under glove-box environment) and to avoid chamber and vacuum-
system contamination.
In table 4.1, the most important FEBID process parameters are listed. Finding an
optimum value for each parameter will ensure an efﬁcient process, but, at the same
time, it can be a Herculean task. The general notions presented in this chapter can be
applied for the fabrication of any type of structure, offering a starting working point.
However, an optimization or reﬁnement process should be performed for each
particular deposit shape and precursor material.
Due to the physical principles of FEBID described in this section, this technique
presents unique advantages with respect to other nanolithography techniques:
• FEBID is a direct-write nanolithography technique which implies that only
one lithography step is required, in sharp contrast to other competing
techniques such as EBL. Thus, FEBID can be useful in device prototyping
as a 2D (or 3D) nano-printer.
• FEBID does not require the use of resists. This implies that FEBID is
competitive when the use of resists is cumbersome, such as in the case of non-
ﬂat substrates or substrates whose physical/chemical properties are sensitive
to resist residues.
• FEBID can achieve extremely high lateral resolution. Using an SEM, 3 nm
dots have been grown. More generally, sub-100 nm resolution is routinely
achieved, in various cases reaching 10 nm resolution.
Table 4.1. Main FEBID tunable variables, and their impact on the growth parameters and the deposit

Nanofabrication
4-10

[Page 149]
• FEBID can fabricate 3D objects, which are hard to obtain with competing
nanolithography techniques. Recently, speciﬁc software has been developed
to apply this 3D growth capability to create complex 3D architectures. Please,
refer to section 1.4.5 for further details.
• Site selectivity and tunability in terms of material type and composition by
modifying the growth parameters are two key advantages of FEBID with
respect to other nanofabrication techniques.
In ﬁgure 4.6, particular FEBID examples exploiting these advantages are
collected.
4.3 FEBID precursors and deposited materials
In this section, we will ﬁrst review the most frequent precursors used in FEBID and
the properties of the resulting as-grown deposits. Secondly, various strategies to tune
the properties of the FEBID deposits will be revised. Such strategies, applied during
or after the FEBID growth, are mainly aimed at enhancing the purity of the
deposits.
Figure 4.6. Examples of FEBID deposits that exploit the advantages of the technique: (a) high-resolution Co
nanowire grown on a Si3N4 membrane, (b) suspended Co nanowire grown between two pads [57], (c) two-loop
Co nanowire, (d) Co@Pt core–shell nanowire built with various bends, (e) Co nanowire with an aspect ratio
around 100 grown on a Si substrate, (f) Co nanosphere grown on a cantilever and (g) Fe nanowire grown on
the tip of an atomic force microscope cantilever [58]. (a) Reprinted with permission from [48]. Copyright 2011
American Chemical Society. (c) Reprinted with permission from [59]. Copyright 2013 Springer Nature.
(d) Reprinted with permission from [60], copyright IOP Publishing. (e) Reprinted with permission from [61].
Copyright 2020 American Physical Society. (f) Reprinted from [62] with permission of Hindawi.
Nanofabrication
4-11

[Page 150]
4.3.1 Precursors and properties of as-grown materials
The growth of FEBID nanostructures with either metallic, magnetic, photonic,
superconducting or structural properties has been explored using a wide range of
precursors [63]. One of the potential issues of this synthesis technique is the existence
of chemical impurities in the deposit due to incomplete dissociation of precursor
molecules. In addition, the structures also contain different levels of contaminants
originating from the residual gases existing in the working chamber [64].
Nevertheless, purity can be normally improved by a precise control of the large
number of parameters involved in the growth [65], which in some cases enables the
fabrication of deposits with a high content of the desired element(s) [48, 66–69]. In
the following, the main precursors used in FEBID, their as-grown composition and
other physical properties are listed.
• Platinum. The Pt deposits are among the most common structures grown by
FEBID. Typically, these objects are presented in the form of conducting Pt–C
nanocomposites where the metallic particles are embedded in a carbonaceous
matrix, using (CH3)3Pt(CpCH3) precursor gas [70] and, to a lesser extent,
(CH3)3PtC5H5 [71]. Integrated generally by 80 at. % of C and 20 at. % of Pt,
and with a resistivity value around 104 μΩ cm, the signiﬁcantly higher growth
rate of this material in comparison with the rest of FEBID materials makes it
very useful for the standard lamellae preparation for transmission electron
microscopy.
• Tungsten. W deposits grown with W(CO)6 precursor yield to maximum
metallic contents of 50 at. %, showing low electrical resistivity values
around 270 μΩ cm [72]. The deposits are composed by an insulating matrix
where a disordered array of crystalline metallic nanoparticles (1–5 nm) are
embedded. One of the most interesting properties of these W-based granular
deposits relies on the capability to cover the full line between insulating to
practically metallic behavior as a function of the growth conditions [73].
WF6 molecule, one of the ﬁrst precursor gases which gave rise to pure metallic
structures under ultra-high vacuum conditions [42, 74], is also currently
used.
• Carbon. The organometallic nature of a great number of precursors induces
the deposition of different levels of C material as a side effect. To purposely
grow C deposits, C10H8 molecule can be used as standard precursor gas for
patterning transfer into the substrates with high lateral resolution [75].
• Cobalt. Metallic Co structures with polycrystalline microstructure, purity
higher than 90 at. % and resistivities around 40 μΩ cm were reported in the
last decade using Co2(CO)8 [48, 76]. These structures in the form of nano-
squares revealed a very well-deﬁned dependence of the composition with the
precursor gas ﬂux. At low precursor gas ﬂux, i.e., in the precursor-limited
regime, C and O coming from the molecule and the residual species in the
chamber are deposited. On the other hand, at high precursor gas ﬂux, i.e., in
the electron-limited regime, the molecules are refreshed too quickly to allow a
proper decomposition by the electron beam, thus incorporating non-metallic
Nanofabrication
4-12

[Page 151]
material. Finally, there is an intermediate precursor gas ﬂux range where an
optimized balance between the precursor ﬂux and the electron beam current is
reached, where the decomposition takes place under the most favourable and
advantageous conditions. Other molecules such as Co(CO)3NO have also
been used for the fabrication of deposits with 50 at. % of Co, showing non-
magnetic behaviour and higher resistivity values, around 6.3 × 103 μΩ cm at
room temperature [77].
• Iron. Fe deposits have been grown with metallic compositions above 75 at. %
with homonuclear Fe(CO)5 [78] and Fe2(CO)9 [79] precursors. In these
amorphous Fe:O:C nanostructures, resistivity values below 100 μΩ cm
were found at room temperature.
• Gold. The maximum Au purity limit of 30 at. % cannot be overcome for the
as-deposited materials based on C7H10AuF3O2 [80, 81]. Nano-objects in the
shape of nanowires present very high resistivity values of around 5 × 106 μΩ
cm at room temperature. Also, other Au precursors such as PF3AuCl [82] or
Au(CO)Cl [83], and even precursor molecules such as Au(CH3)2(C2ClF3O)
have been tested to improve the metallic content in combination with new
approaches, as explained in the next section.
• Silver. Planar Ag deposits with purity around 75 at. % have been fabricated
utilizing the precursor gas AgO2(CH3)2C4H10 [84]. The results indicate that
the use of low doses (30 nC μm−2) gives rise to the formation of Ag crystals,
whereas high doses (104 nC μm−2), which produce larger C atomic percen-
tages, result in Ag nanoparticles embedded into a carbonaceous matrix.
• Silicon and silicon oxide. Si deposits based on Si5H12 precursor gas [85], or
amorphous SiO2 deposits with additional amounts of C and O contaminants
have been obtained from the Si(OC2H5)4 precursor [86, 87], the most
commonly used gas for SiO2 deposition. In addition, the shapes and
architectures arising from this material also demonstrate the geometrical
versatility by the growth of 2D and 3D nanostructures, e.g., in the form of a
hand-fan architecture [88].
• Lead. Granular Pb deposits, with a 46 at. % Pb composition, based on the
dissociation of (CH3CH2)4Pb, also exhibit metallic behavior [89]. These
deposits present a resistivity value of 190 μΩ cm at room temperature.
• Other less frequent precursors: ruthenium C7H9RuC7H9 precursor has been
used to fabricate deposits where the as-deposited Ru composition is nearly
10 at. %, the rest being C [90]. Nickel precursors such as Ni(C5H4CH3)2
or Ni(PF3)4 [91] and copper precursors such as CuC16O6H26 [92], with
resistivities around 106 μΩ cm, have also been used to grow structures by
FEBID.
Bimetallic precursors. Using heteronuclear precursor molecules that contain two
metallic elements, it is possible to grow deposits of alloyed materials. A few
examples are collected here below:
• Cobalt-iron. Co-Fe bimetallic compounds have been explored through the use
of the heteronuclear precursor HCo3Fe(CO)12 [93–95]. Co3Fe alloy deposits
Nanofabrication
4-13

[Page 152]
with 80 at. % metallic content were obtained, with a resistivity value of 43 μΩ
cm at room temperature, comparable to those obtained in Co-FEBID [76]
and Fe-FEBID [78] nanostructures.
• Ruthenium-iron. The carbonyl-based complex H2FeRu3(CO)13 [96] has led to
deposits with maximum metallic contents of around 26 at. %, displaying a
lowest resistivity of 2500 μΩ cm, close to the metallic conduction regime. The
different behavior between HCo3Fe(CO)12 and H2FeRu3(CO)13 precursors
points to the relative thermal stability of the decarbonylated intermediates
with the CO ligands. In the ﬁrst one, the CO groups are thermally unstable in
the intermediate compound at room temperature, whereas in the second one
the CO ligands are stable and can be integrated into the structure [96].
• Silicon-manganese. Si-Mn alloys can be fabricated by using the SiH3Mn(CO)5
precursor [97]. In this case, amorphous Mn2Si grains are embedded in a
carbonaceous matrix, with relative compositions of approximately 35 at. % of
Mn and 18 at. % of Si, the rest being C and O. As has been observed in other
FEBID materials, whereas the composition has been found to be independent
of the electron beam voltage, the total metallic composition was improved
from ~40 at. % up to ~53 at. % by increasing the electron beam current one
order of magnitude, as well as the Mn/Si ratio, indicating a loss of Si in the
fabrication process. These deposits present a quasi-metallic temperature-
dependent behavior, with a resistivity value of 1.5 × 104 μΩ cm at room
temperature.
4.3.2 Improving properties
The low purity and the poor crystallinity are key limiting factors for capitalizing the
full potential of FEBID. In most cases, the purity of the deposits is not enough to
ensure a metallic behavior or the good performance of the magnetic, optical or
superconducting properties. To face this limitation, diverse puriﬁcation protocols
have been developed to increase the purity of FEBID deposits, the general strategy
being a multi-step synthesis approach [64]. In the following, details are provided on
the puriﬁcation methods and their degree of success when applied to various
materials.
Puriﬁcation methods. Various successful methods have been employed in order to
increase the purity of the element of interest, in general a metallic one, and to
decrease the unwanted elements, either originating from the precursor molecules or
from the chamber contamination. The most successful ones are as follows:
• In situ [98, 99] and ex situ [100–102] post-annealing treatments at high
vacuum and under controlled reactive gas atmospheres [103]. This procedure
is performed inside an SEM or TEM equipment using a heating stage and
controlling very precisely the temperature, the ramp rate and the annealing
time after stabilization.
• The use of substrates at high temperatures during growth [54, 104]. In
addition to the preservation of the deposit geometry, the metallic purity of
Nanofabrication
4-14

[Page 153]
the structures when using this method is enhanced, sometimes at the cost of a
lower growth rate.
• Post-growth electron beam irradiation [105, 106] and ion beam irradiation
[107]. Both strategies point towards a strong improvement of the electrical
resistivity of the as-grown deposits.
• Laser-assisted heating during deposition [108, 109]. The support of a
synchronized pulsed laser gives rise to laser-assisted electron-beam-induced
deposition (LAEBID), which has been demonstrated to produce thermal
desorption of the reaction by-products leading to an increase of the metallic
content.
• Post-growth Joule heating upon injection of high electric currents [110]. This
method has also been employed to promote a different microstructure, e.g.,
from as-grown nanogranular crystal phases to a polycrystalline one when
increasing the current.
• Supersonic jet delivery of precursor gas [111]. The high kinetic energy of the
inert carrier gas jet stimulates the desorption of the decomposition residues,
inducing an enhancement of the metallic purity. The highly-localized pre-
cursor delivery also increases the deposition yield.
• Combinations of all previous methods [112–115]. The exploration of post-
growth heating annealing in combination with gas atmospheres and electron
beam irradiation has been performed in order to improve the metallic content
of the as-deposited structures.
Puriﬁed materials.
Some important materials are discussed hereafter:
• Platinum. In order to increase the metallic content during growth, existing
strategies lie in the use of more than one gas during the deposition. The prime
example consists of Pt by using (CH3)3Pt(CpCH3) and O2 injection simulta-
neously, where metallic contents near 100 at. % and resistivity down to 60 μΩ
cm have been found at room temperature [112]. In fact, it is interesting to
highlight that the tuning of the precursor gas temperature leads to different
ﬂuxes. In these experiments, the resistivity was reduced from 88 μΩ cm to
60 μΩ cm by decreasing the GIS temperature from 40 °C to 28 °C, obtaining
pure structures. Also, an additional knob was used, based on the improve-
ment of the puriﬁcation of the Pt structures by increasing the O2/precursor
ﬂux ratio (always above 1 × 104) or increasing the beam current for a ﬁxed
ﬂux ratio. The PE, SE and BSE are quite likely to ionize O2, prone to react
with C residues into volatile species. Finally, it is also worth mentioning the
astounding success achieved in growing virtually-pure Pt deposits by post-
growth electron irradiation in O2 atmosphere [103].
• Gold. The growth of Au structures with metallic purity higher than 90 at. %
has been performed by a water-assisted process in combination with Au
(CH3)2(C2ClF3O) [116]. The use of water as an oxidative enhancer during the
deposition has enabled the formation of highly pure Au nanowires with
resistivity of 8.8 μΩ cm and associated current density of 715 kA mm−2.
Nanofabrication
4-15

[Page 154]
The atomic Au composition can be adjusted by modifying the water ﬂux. It
has been found that, although the total thickness of the deposit drops with
increasing water addition, the relative contribution of Au to the structure
volume increases. It is suggested that water favors the oxidation of the
carbonaceous part of the deposits as well as the residual gas compounds in the
chamber, improving the efﬁciency of the Au nanofabrication process since a
higher number of adsorption sites are available for the Au precursor gas
molecules. Also, other studies support the possibility of fabricating functional
Au plasmonic nanostructures by electron beam irradiation in water vapor
atmosphere [117].
• Other metallic materials:
In order to improve the electrical properties, different experiments in
deposits based on the SiH3Mn(CO)5 precursor gas revealed that the low-
energy electron irradiation allows the tuning of the conductivity. Speciﬁcally,
recent experiments show the dependence of the resistivity with the irradiation
dose. Deposits undergoing 44 nC μm−2 dose show resistivity values around
1.5 × 104 μΩ cm at room temperature, this parameter decreasing with the
irradiation dose [97]. Using an electron irradiation of 1000 nC μm−2 the
resistivity reaches 1.9 × 103 μΩ cm, and further irradiation gives rise to a
saturation in the conductivity. However, even though this value is two orders
of magnitude lower than the one obtained for FeRu [96] and CoSi [118], it is
still almost 50 times larger than the CoFe-FEBID one [93].
• Magnetic materials:
Deposits based on Co(CO)3NO precursor gas were performed on sub-
strates at low temperatures to understand the decomposition process [119].
Firstly, the NO and part of the CO ligands are decomposed, giving rise to a
decarbonylated intermediate. Then, the remaining CO groups can be decom-
posed by the electron beam, increasing the C content of the structure while Co
oxidized atoms turn up. At higher temperatures, the thermal reactions lead to
C removal, therefore improving the metallic purity. In subsequent studies, it
has been demonstrated that the deposition yield increases with the substrate
at 130 °C, nevertheless maintaining their high resistivity of 6.3 × 103 μΩ cm at
room temperature with a metal-insulator behaviour. The Co composition is
always at 50 at. %, after 400 °C annealing, the resistivity decreases two orders
of magnitude down to 63 μΩ cm, showing a clear metallic performance. In
addition, some of the initial CoO fcc nanometric grains were transformed into
Co hcp grains, with sizes between 10–15 nm, which facilitate the connection
between Co grains after the thermal treatment, eventually enhancing the
electrical properties [77, 120].
On the other hand, Co(CO)3NO-based as-grown nanowires with moderate
atomic content of Co, ranging 50%–55%, present non-metallic and non-
magnetic behavior even at low temperatures [120]. However, after performing
an annealing treatment at 400 °C in vacuum conditions, the resistivity of the
nanostructures decreases down to 62 μΩ cm, corresponding to a metallic
behavior at low temperature, retrieving the ferromagnetic properties.
Nanofabrication
4-16

[Page 155]
In the deposits formed from HCo3Fe(CO)12, compositional analysis
showed that carbonyl groups, and to a lesser extent the O, are removed
from the deposit by ex situ high-vacuum post-growth annealing process. The
atomic composition increases from Co:Fe:C:O = 41:13:26:20 for the as-
deposited nanostructures up to 52:17:22:9 after the thermal treatment. In
addition, the amorphous C microstructure also changes into graphite crystals
increasing the temperature up to 300 °C, and the semiconducting behavior
turns into the metallic one, where the resistivity is reduced by around two
orders of magnitude to 65 μΩ cm [95].
The widely-used puriﬁcation strategies have been generally applied to 2D
deposits, but also puriﬁcation of 3D objects has been attempted in vertical Co
and Fe nanowires based on Co2(CO)8 and Fe2(CO)9 [121, 122]. The 3D case
presents speciﬁc problems: for instance, the architecture stability can be
compromised due to the high-volume shrinkage occurring in low metallic
content deposits, eventually leading to a substantial modiﬁcation of the object
shape or even the collapse of the 3D structures [123] (ﬁgure 4.7).
• Superconducting materials:
The fabrication of superconducting structures at the nanoscale has been
generally tackled by focused ion beam induced deposition (FIBID) [124],
even though the physical damage and ion doping generated by this method
would prompt considering FEBID as an optimal approach. However,
signiﬁcantly fewer studies have focused their attention on FEBID super-
conducting materials because of the lack of reproducibility and difﬁculties in
Figure 4.7. Scanning transmission electron microscopy images of as-deposited and annealed 3D Co nanowires
under a post-growth puriﬁcation process (ﬁrst row of images). In the insets, the corresponding fast-Fourier
transform of the images reveals the increase of the crystallinity as a function of the temperature. Chemical
maps (following rows) show the spatial distribution of the relative composition of Co, C and O, increasing the
metallic content from ~72 at. % for the as-deposited nanowire to ~93 at. % for the 600 °C-annealed one. Scale
bars are 20 nm. Adapted with permission from [121]. Copyright 2018 American Chemical Society.
Nanofabrication
4-17

[Page 156]
the growth conditions. Nevertheless, Makise et al reported for the ﬁrst time
the fabrication of MoC nanowires using Mo(CO)6 in combination with H2O.
In these samples, superconductivity is suggested to be present due to the
resistivity decrease below 10 K [125]. Additionally, as previously mentioned,
Winhold et al showed the growth and characterization of Pb nanostructures
using (CH3CH2)4Pb with metallic behavior, with the resistivity dropping to
16 μΩ cm at 9 K, very close to the superconducting transition temperature
(7.2 K) [89]. Finally, Sengupta et al reported the growth of W nanowires with
critical temperature of 2 K [72].
Multi-precursor deposition. The use of more than one precursor gas has allowed
the synthesis of binary metallic alloys with outstanding properties. Some examples
are considered below:
• [CoFeSi2]n multilayers can be prepared by alternating different precursors
using a multi-channel GIS [126], where HCo3Fe(CO)12 and Fe(CO)5 are
combined simultaneously to obtain deposits with a Co/Fe ratio of 2. Also, by
depositing a Si-based dielectric layers with Si5H12 in between the CoFe ones,
CoFeSi2 Heusler compounds are grown.
• Fe–Si binary compounds have been fabricated by the alternating Fe(CO)5
and Si5H12 as precursor gases. The combination of post-growth annealing
and electron-beam irradiation in Fe/Si multilayers has led to FeSi and Fe3Si
metallic binary compounds by the intermixing of Fe and Si atoms [85].
• Au–Fe nanoalloys were grown under consecutive deposition of Au and Fe
using C7H10AuF3O2 and Fe(CO)5 precursor gases, respectively [127].
• CoSi–C binary alloys were synthesized using Co2(CO)8 and Si5H12 precursor
gases simultaneously, with the presence of CoSi and CoSi2 nanoclusters
embedded in a carbonaceous matrix in the ﬁnal deposit. The resistivity can be
modulated between 7 × 103 μΩ cm and 2.8 × 104 μΩ cm controlling the Si5H12
ﬂux or, in other words, the Si/Co ratio [118].
• CoPt–C
structures
arisen
from
the
combination
of
Co2(CO)8
and
(CH3)3CH3C5H4Pt present a total metallic purity of approximately 29 at.
%. This quite low relative metal composition leads to resistivity values around
105 μΩ cm. As a result of the growth, the deposits were composed by CoPt
nanoparticles embedded in a carbonaceous matrix. Following an electron
irradiation approach, the low purity and high resistivity of the nanostructures
based on Co2(CO)8 and (CH3)3CH3C5H4Pt can be improved. The CoPt
nanoparticles turn into face-centered tetragonal L10 nanocrystallites, while
the resistivity is reduced from 105 μΩ cm to 103 μΩ cm. Moreover, this speciﬁc
system, which is superparamagnetic in the as-grown state, becomes ferro-
magnetic at room temperature after the irradiation treatment. Along this line,
the accurate control of the electron dose permits the adjustment of the
electrical and magneto-transport properties [128].
• FePt–C nanorods were prepared using Fe(CO)5 and (CH3)3(C5H5)Pt pre-
cursor gases. These FePt–C deposits in the form of nanowires were submitted
Nanofabrication
4-18

[Page 157]
to post-growth annealing treatments at 600 °C for 120 min, leading to a
crystallization process. Several crystalline FePt alloy nanoparticles with face-
centered tetragonal microstructure and surrounded by a C shell constitute the
composition of the nanostructures [129].
• PtSi–C alloys with CH3CpPt(CH3)3 and Si(SiH3)4 precursors have been
grown [130]. The composition can be progressively tuned by varying the
precursor ﬂux. Although the highest metallic content was found to be ~36
at. % Si, ~13 at. % Pt, ~47 at. % C and ~4 at. % O, the minimum resistivity
was achieved for a sample with metallic content of ~42 at. %, when the Si/Pt
ratio was 1.5. However, even at ~20 K this sample did not show a metallic
behavior.
• Co–C deposits were obtained using Co2(CO)8 and C8H17OH precursors.
Utke et al showed that in multi-precursor FEBID deposition, the composi-
tional variation is driven by the adsorption and supply of the adsorbates,
together with the electron beam impact efﬁciency [68]. Thus, the control of
the purity in metallic nanostructures to achieve customized physical proper-
ties was shown in this material. By means of a two-adsorbate model, the
tuning of deposit composition by pulsed electron beams process was
demonstrated.
4.4 Applications
FEBID is becoming a key lithographic technique in nanotechnology and materials
science [131, 132]. Its unique capability for the growth of different types of materials
has been exploited in a broad range of applications, such as integrated circuit edit
and mask repair [133], creation of electrical contacts [134], growth of magnetic
nanowires [135], fabrication of plasmonic nanostructures [117], photodetection [136]
or gas sensing [137]. The last 35 years have witnessed the increasing interest of the
scientiﬁc community and industry in the development of advanced nano-objects
grown by FEBID for various applications, which can be classiﬁed attending to the
material’s nature. Following this criterion, the nanostructures can be classiﬁed in
metallic, superconducting, magnetic, photonic and plasmonic, structural, etc.
From all the applications arising from table 4.2, we are going to single out the
most relevant ones today:
• Mask repair. Highly-challenging critical features are required in the lithography
and even very small defects should be minimized during the fabrication. One of
the most demanding FEBID-based processes is photolithography mask repair,
allowing the removal of surplus mask material and addition of the missing
material. Binary, attenuated phase-shift or extreme ultraviolet and nanoimprint
lithography masks can be repaired overcoming charging or contamination
issues and ensuring cleaning lifetime [133]. For example, protective Ru ﬁlms
have been used for nanoscale repairs by deposition and post-puriﬁcation
process based on electron irradiation in combination with O2 atmosphere
[90]. Pt deposits based on (CH3)3Pt(CpCH3) have been used for lithography
mask repair [141, 142].
Nanofabrication
4-19

[Page 158]
• Tips for scanning probe microscopy. 3D FEBID deposits have been used
for general scanning probe microscopy (SPM) operation modes, such as
conductive-atomic force microscopy (AFM), electrostatic force micro-
scopy, Kelvin force microscopy or scanning thermal microscopy. High-
resolution capabilities were achieved by Pt–C, W, Au-based tips and their
puriﬁed versions thanks to the narrow diameters and especially due to the
small and sharp tip ends (<10 nm) [143–145]. In this line, the growth of
magnetic Co and Fe nanowire tips has also shown its potential application
in magnetic force microscopy (MFM) [58]. The superior behaviour of FEBID
tips with respect to standard tips has recently opened the door to the next
generation of commercial MFM tips. These tips work properly in ambient
conditions as well as in liquid environment, behaving appropriately in terms
of mechanical stability, resolution and sensitivity [145]. Besides, tetrapod
structures have proven to be an efﬁcient method for localized thermal
sensing, where the fast temperature response enables dynamic sensing [146].
In addition, the fabrication of nano-spheres on top of AFM cantilevers has
shown powerful sensitivity in ferromagnetic resonance force microscopy as
they minimize the remanent effects when measuring magnetic hysteresis
[62]. In the ﬁeld of nano-optics, scanning near-ﬁeld optical microscopy
(SNOM) [147], tip-enhanced Raman scattering nano-probes [148] and
AFM-based infrared spectroscopy [149] have considered the use of
FEBID pillars as potential basic elements.
• Electrical contacts to nano-objects. Due to the low resistivity values presented
by some metallic materials grown by FEBID, their implementation in
nanoelectronic devices is a signiﬁcant step forward. The growth of reliable
metallic connections for electrical characterization is a routine method
performed by FEBID. Commonly, Au [150], W [151] and Pt [152] contacts
have been used, minimizing the damage caused by other techniques such as
FIBID. In the case of superconducting materials, FEBID nanostructures
were found interesting only in MoC and PbxCyOδ materials [89]. In the latter,
Table 4.2. FEBID materials and applications examples.
Type of material
Property of interest
Applications
Metallic
Conductivity
Electrical contact to nano-objects [138]
Superconducting
Null electrical resistance,
superconducting vortices
Electrical contacts to nano-objects,
fluxtronics [125]
Magnetic
Magnetization, magnetic
forces, domain walls
Magnetic sensing, actuation
and storage [61]
Photonic and
plasmonic
light–matter interaction
Optical antenna, filters [22]
Structural
Mechanical properties
Nanotools, mechanical support,
templating, nanoactuators, tips [139]
Nanofabrication
4-20

[Page 159]
since the resistivity value at room temperature is seven times larger than the
bulk Pb, this compound is a potential candidate for the fabrication of metallic
contact electrodes.
• Nanophotonic applications. The FEBID community has focused much
attention on optical applications. These can be divided according to the
dimensional extension of the nanostructures. In the case of planar or 2D
nano-objects, photonic nanostructures have been fabricated, for example by
Au in the form of disks, or nanorods to be used as antennas [22]. In this sense,
a comparison between FEBID and other lithography techniques such as EBL
results in a similar qualitative behavior. W and SiOx elements can also be
used in photonic crystals for infrared ﬁlters [153] and plasmonic antennas for
SNOM [154]. Even Ag nanostructures have been grown for plasmonic
applications in the visible range [84]. On the other hand, 3D nanodeposits
with strong plasmonic activity can be fabricated by Pt–C [155] and pure Au,
the latter acting as nanoantennas or optical ﬁlters [65, 117].
• Magnetic applications (beyond tips). Magnetic nanostructured materials
attract particularly great interest because of their possibilities to be imple-
mented in future spintronic nanodevices. Speciﬁcally, they are potential
candidates for fast and low-power electronics based on domain wall conduit
for storing and handling information [156–158]. In addition, Co magnetic
nano-objects grown by FEBID have also been used for the fabrication of
electronic devices, e.g., nano-Hall sensors [20, 48]. The step towards 3D
nanostructures has given rise to nanoactuators controlled by the application
of external magnetic ﬁelds [21], artiﬁcial spin ice systems [159], complex
architectures based on hook-shaped nanowires aimed for domain wall
conduit devices [60] or structures where the control of the magnetic chirality
is possible [160]. Other materials such as Mn–Si nanostructures have proven
to be appropriate systems to study helimagnetism and skyrmionics [97],
opening a new pathway for application development. On the other hand,
FEBID can also be used for studies devoted to biomolecule immobilization,
such as the case of CoNi nanoparticles attached to the substrate using a
30 keV electron beam [161].
• Nanotools and other structural applications. FEBID versatility has allowed the
development of strategies aimed at fabricating and customizing nanotweez-
ers, enabling their manipulation by applying a voltage [139], as shown in
ﬁgure 4.8(e). Also, non-magnetic 3D scaffolds have been grown by FEBID,
and subsequently covered by a thin ﬁlm of evaporated magnetic material to
bring functionality [162]. In addition, FEBID Fe particles have been used as
catalysts for the site-speciﬁc synthesis of C nanotubes, which can give rise to
the production of novel nanocircuits or biosensors [163], Pt deposits have
been used for the fabrication of DNA tunneling detectors [164], and systems
based on Cu have been built and characterized, showing their applicability as
mechanical devices [23].
Nanofabrication
4-21

[Page 160]
4.5 Additional FEBID strategies and novel developments
Leveraging from the FEBID general framework presented so far, other FEBID-
related approaches have been explored. Here, we will focus our attention on six
derived strategies: (i) FEBID assisted by an electrically-biased patterned metallic
structure, (ii) FEBID under cryogenic conditions, called cryo-FEBID [165],
(iii) growth after electron-beam induced surface activation, called EBISA [166],
(iv) FEBID in liquid environment [167], (v) combined use of FEBID and atomic
layer deposition (ALD) [168], and (vi) the subtractive nanofabrication method
known as focused electron beam induced etching (FEBIE) [169].
• FEBID assisted by an electrically-biased patterned metallic structure. This
approach arises from the difﬁculties in the use of FEBID on insulating
substrates due to detrimental charging effects which ruin the resolution of the
beam. To overcome these issues and also to allow for the in situ modulation
of the geometry of the structures, an electrically-biased metallic structure
patterned on the surface of the substrate is used, giving rise to the so-called
Architectural Adjustment by Grid Overlay Nanotechnology-Chip (ARAGON-
Chip). As shown in ﬁgure 4.9, a perforated metallic layer enables the evacuation
of the charge produced during the nanostructure growth. On the other hand, the
tunable electric ﬁelds produce a signiﬁcant impact on the trajectories of the PE
Figure 4.8. Examples of applications based on (a) metallic contacts aiming to protect the SnO2 nanowire from
ion damage, (b) superconducting crystalline Mo and amorphous MoC nanowires, respectively, (c) remote
magnetomechanical nanoactuators based on Co nanowires, (d) free-standing Au plasmonic architectures and
(e) structural carbonaceous materials used as nanotweezers with a gap of 25 nm. (a) Adapted with permission
from [140]. Copyright 2006 Elsevier. (b) Adapted with permission from [125]. Copyright 2015 Springer Nature.
(c) Adapted with permission from [21]. Copyright 2016 Wiley-VCH Verlag GmbH & Co. KGaA, Weinheim.
(d) Reprinted with permission from [117]. Copyright 2017 American Chemical Society. (e) Adapted with
permission from [139], copyright IOP Publishing.
Nanofabrication
4-22

[Page 161]
and the generated SE, leading to the dimensional customization of the objects,
especially the 3D ones such as vertical nanowires [170].
• Cryo-FEBID. This technique relies on the condensation of the precursor
material on the surface of the substrate prior to the exposure to the focused
electron beam. After exposure, the condensate layer is heated up to a
temperature slightly above room temperature (e.g., ∼50 °C), to reveal the
formation of the deposit in the irradiated areas, whereas the unexposed ones
become volatile. The most important advance of cryo-FEBID is the much
higher growth rate in comparison with the original FEBID approach. In fact,
the growth rate has been found to be up to four orders of magnitude higher
compared to room temperature deposition. Owing to the need of a much
smaller electron irradiation dose to reveal the deposit, the FEBID process
speed is signiﬁcantly enhanced. The origin of this phenomenon is that in cryo-
FEBID the electron irradiation is more likely to break precursor molecules in
the thick (a few hundreds of nm) condensed precursor layer than in FEBID,
where the precursor molecules are in the gas phase and form an adsorbed
monolayer on the substrate (ﬁgure 4.10).
Bresin et al proposed for the ﬁrst time the growth of a material by cryo-
FEBID using (CH3)3Pt(CpCH3) precursor gas onto a substrate at −155 °C,
exposing the condensate and facilitating the desorption of the precursor
molecules in non-irradiated regions by heating the substrate to room
Figure 4.9. FEBID process carried out on an insulating substrate with an electrically-biased patterned metallic
structure (ARAGON-Chip). Example with the (CH3)3Pt(CpCH3) molecule. Reprinted with permission from
[170], copyright IOP Publishing.
Nanofabrication
4-23

[Page 162]
temperature [171, 172]. In this study, the thickness of the condensed precursor
layer was modiﬁed between 75 and 300 nm by tuning the GIS temperature,
distance to the substrate and valve opening time. The lateral resolution
strongly depends on the thickness of the condensed layer. High lateral
resolution of 40 nm was reported with a condensed layer of 70 nm in
thickness. However, the use of condensed layers around 280 nm worsened the
resolution 3.5 times [172]. Moreover, consecutive cryo-FEBID depositions of
different layers can be carried out to fabricate 3D structures or to combine
different materials and thicknesses. This provides great versatility and opens
many possibilities in terms of applications. For instance, mask repair, etching
and large-scale patterning processes could beneﬁt from this technique [173],
as well as the fabrication of metallic contacts.
• EBISA. This technique consists of a previous patterning by focused electron
beam over the surface of the substrate at high vacuum conditions with no
precursor gases introduced in the chamber. The areas scanned by the electron
beam become chemically active for the subsequent decomposition of the
precursor material. When the precursor gas is locally delivered onto the
substrate surface, autocatalytic growth takes place as the decomposition only
occurs at the irradiated areas [174]. Similarly to standard FEBID, the
nanostructure size is determined by the electron beam scan as well as the
interaction between the beam and the substrate. Different substrates such as
SiOx or TiO2 are commonly used [175, 176]. Also, additional steps can follow
the ﬁrst deposition by introducing different gases and generating thicker
specimens in the EBISA areas (ﬁgure 4.11).
• FEBID in liquid environment. This strategy consists of the decomposition of
solvent molecules by the electron beam, producing solvated electrons and
radicals in a domino-like effect [177]. In the standard process, the use of some
cells to retain the liquid is the most straightforward way to work under
vacuum (ﬁgure 4.12).
Figure 4.10. Diagram of the cryo-FEBID process. Example with the (CH3)3Pt(CpCH3) molecule.
Nanofabrication
4-24

[Page 163]
Due to the need of removal of high levels of contaminants in some metallic
deposits, liquid-phase processes provide an effective solution to obtain high
purity nanostructures. Several materials such as Pt [167, 178], Au [179], Ag
[180], Pd [181] or Cu [182] have been used to fabricate nanostructures by this
approach. For instance, in the last case, using aqueous solutions of copper
sulfate as precursor, with sulfuric acid which induces the elimination of
oxygen, deposits of 95 at. % of Cu have been grown [183]. Apart from the
high metallic content of the structures, growth rates up to 105 times greater
than that of standard gas-phase process can be reached.
• Combination of FEBID and atomic layer deposition. The combination of
FEBID with other lithography or deposition techniques could extend the
capabilities of both methods. One of the examples is the conjunction between
FEBID and area-selective ALD [168], the so-called direct-write ALD. In
several studies, ﬁrstly the patterns made of a FEBID seed layer with low Pt
contents are deposited, then this metallic part is activated locally for the ALD
growth. The method consists in a multi-cycle process involving the alternate
surface exposure to two different gas reactants, as shown in ﬁgure 4.13 [184].
This cycle approach opens up the possibility to fabricate layers or multilayers
Figure 4.11. Diagram of the EBISA process. Example with the Fe(CO)5 molecule.
Figure 4.12. Diagram of the FEBID in liquid environment process.
Nanofabrication
4-25

[Page 164]
of different materials. For example, selective Pd or Ru deposition on Pt-
FEBID seed layer has been demonstrated [185, 186]. Thanks to this
innovative strategy, high lateral resolution (down to 10 nm), high chemical
purity together with high conductivity, and great thickness control are
provided. Moreover, the low electron dose (∼10 pC μm−2) and the absence
of resists allow patterning on sensitive materials such as graphene or C
nanotubes, improving the wetting interaction with respect to other methods
[187, 188].
• FEBIE. Focused electron beam induced etching consists of a direct-write
subtractive nanofabrication technique where the combination of an electron
beam and a reactive gas is used to perform chemical dry etching with spatial
resolution down to 10 nm [189, 190]. The typical precursor gas molecules
used are H2O, O2, Cl2, NH3, NOCl, SF6 and XeF2, among others [169]. In
comparison with FIB, the use of an electron beam prevents beam damage and
Figure 4.13. Diagram of the direct-write ALD process. Example with (CH3)3Pt(CpCH3) and O2 molecules.
Figure 4.14. Diagram of the FEBIE process. Example with the XeF2 molecule.
Nanofabrication
4-26

[Page 165]
redeposition. This confers the technique a unique behavior for applications
such as nanostructures edition [191], photolithographic mask repair [192],
upgrading of tips devoted to SPM [193] or fabrication of nanometric pores in
membranes [194]. Besides, FEBIE has been widely employed to enhance
metallic purity of as-deposited FEBID nanostructures composed of Pt [195],
Au [196], Fe3O4 [197] or SiO2 [198] materials (ﬁgure 4.14).
4.6 End-of-chapter summary
Over the last decades, FEBID has demonstrated unprecedented capabilities for the
growth of customized nanostructured materials. The key virtues of this technique,
i.e., a maskless, resist-free direct-writing nanolithography, enable the design of
complex motifs on virtually any surface, and an easy adaptation to 3D printing
of nanostructures nowadays out of reach for most nanofabrication techniques.
This adaptability is sustained on the extensive repertoire of gas precursors available
nowadays. Some success cases of FEBID materials are particularly astounding.
Platinum precursor is routinely used as structural material, for 3D prototyping
and scaffolding as well as for nanocontacting. High purity cobalt has become the
standard for ferromagnetic FEBID nanostructures, with proven functionality for
scanning probe tip functionalization and sensing. Pure gold can be fabricated by
FEBID, with great promise for the design of custom-tailored nanophotonics
devices. Finally, further progress on FEBID technology is currently underway.
Some exciting developments described in this chapter, including FEBID in extreme
environments (cryogenic or liquid media), in combination with other techniques
(ALD), reactive etching or autocatalytic FEBID growth, are aimed to boost FEBID
performance and throughput, and open new pathways in the competitive market of
nanofabrication.
4.7 Student’s corner
• A video animation on the FEBID process can be visualized at https://
iopscience.iop.org/book/978-0-7503-2608-7.
• Which growth variables have an inﬂuence on the lateral resolution of FEBID,
and why?
• Which measures would you take to reduce the halo around FEBID deposits?
• Are insulating substrates suitable for FEBID growth?
• Growth rate is one of the key issues of FEBID. For a given electron dose,
which growth parameters could you modify to increase the growth rate?
