IOP Publishing
Nanofabrication
Nanolithography techniques and their applications
Jose´ Marı´a De Teresa
Chapter 5
Focused ion beam induced processing
Pablo Orús, Rosa Córdoba and José María De Teresa
Focused ion beam (FIB) technology makes use of a highly-focused beam of charged
ions to perform precise nanopatterning tasks by steering it over a sample, tracing
patterns deﬁned by the user. The physical interactions between the ions and the atoms
of the sample are exploited for nanostructuring purposes such as material removal or
modiﬁcation, and material deposition (when used in combination with a precursor).
Besides, FIBs are also used for imaging and compositional and structural analyses. In
this chapter, the instrumentation and technique fundamentals are reviewed, and a
selection of application examples is presented.
5.1 Introduction
Foreseen in 1959 by Richard Feynman on There’s Plenty of Room at the Bottom [1],
the continuous need for device miniaturization present in both the industrial and
research ﬁelds has led to the development of a wide spectrum of techniques in
nanofabrication. Since its inception in the 1970s [2–5], the technology of FIBs, which
represents the capability to obtain, steer, and focalize an energetic beam of charged
ions for different purposes, has established itself as a solid cornerstone within the
nanofabrication framework.
Focused ion beam induced processing (FIBIP, although it is common to refer to
both the beam and the technique as simply ‘FIB’) is an umbrella term that encompasses
several nanopatterning techniques that share the usage of an FIB as a primary drive for
the nanolithography process. These methods exploit the interactions that take place
between a focused beam of charged ions and the material it impinges upon. By itself, an
FIB can be used to selectively remove material from a surface, and to induce
crystallographic and compositional changes on it. In combination with a gaseous
precursor capable of adsorbing to the surface of the sample, the FIB can be used to
induce a decomposition reaction, with subsequent material deposition. This technique,
known as focused ion beam induced deposition (FIBID, IBID), ﬁnds application in
optical lithography mask repair, circuit editing, and growth of functional materials [6].
doi:10.1088/978-0-7503-2608-7ch5
5-1
ª IOP Publishing Ltd 2020. All rights,
including for text and data mining (TDM), artificial intelligence (AI) training, and similar technologies, are reserved.

[Page 179]
Depending on the processed material, the ionic species, and the beam conﬁg-
uration, FIBIP provides a working resolution range from up to hundreds of μm
down to a single nm. It offers great adjustability and ease of use in terms of patterning
of complex geometries at speciﬁc locations in a sample.
The object of this chapter is to provide the reader with a basic overview of
FIBIP techniques and their applicability. First, the basics of the FIB instruments
will be reviewed. After that, the theoretical foundations and experimental details
of the technique will be presented. Lastly, several examples of usage of different
FIBIP techniques, including milling, modiﬁcation, and material deposition, will
be reviewed.
5.2 The instrument
As the architecture and design of FIB equipment play a pivotal role in FIBIP, the
instrument will be described in detail hereafter. FIBIP is performed in either dedicated
equipment simply referred to as FIB instruments, or in hybrid microscopes that
combine an FIB with a scanning electron microscope (SEM). FIB/SEM microscopes
are extensively known by commercial names such as Dual Beam (Thermo Fisher
Scientiﬁc), Crossbeam (Zeiss), and MultiBeam (Raith). The commercial name Helium
Ion Microscope or HIM (Zeiss), is also commonly used to refer to FIB instruments that
make use of He+ and Ne+ ions in their columns.
Both FIB and FIB/SEM instruments share two main components: an ion column
and a process chamber. The ﬁrst houses on its upper part an ion source from where
the ions are extracted, while the lower part contains a series of elements used to
accelerate them, and focus and steer the beam. The chamber is where the processing
is performed, and is equipped with a stage holding the sample, and several detectors
and processing modules, including a gas delivery system used to inject the precursor
material into the chamber to perform FIBID, if applicable. Since the interactions of
beam ions with contamination and/or air particles are to be avoided, a pumping
system is used to maintain vacuum conditions during operation. Typically, at least
two levels of vacuum are maintained in the equipment: the operation chamber
vacuum (10−6 mbar, raised to 10−5 mbar during gas delivery), and the ion source
vacuum (10−8 mbar).
5.2.1 Historical development
In the ﬁeld of experimental sciences, the achievement of most scientiﬁc break-
throughs is intrinsically bound to the development of the speciﬁc instrumentation
that is required to perform the required experiments. As shall be discussed in the
following section, the beating heart of any FIB is the ion source—the constituent
used to extract the ions from a source material. With ample generosity, the develop-
ment of ion sources for FIBs can be traced back to the 17th century, when English
scientist William Gilbert reported that when an electrically charged fragment of amber
was brought close to a water droplet, it would pull some towards it, constituting a
conical shape [7]. One of the most signiﬁcant achievements in the development of FIB
instrumentation was the invention of sources based on the ionization of a material in
Nanofabrication
5-2

[Page 180]
liquid form, following the same physical mechanism that made Gilbert’s amber piece
pull water. These liquid metal ion sources (LMISs), initially intended to be used as ion
thrusters in space applications [8], would eventually become a main cornerstone in FIB
technology (ﬁgure 5.1a–c).
The development of actual, usable FIBs based on LMISs would ﬁnd its beginnings
in the 1970s, with Seliger and Orloff being two of the most prominent challengers.
Seliger et al reported in 1974 the usage of a 60 keV He+ beam for material modiﬁcation
[2]. The beam had a diameter of 3.5 μm, which would be considered enormous by
today’s standards. Complementarily, Orloff et al reported the successful ionization of
gaseous H2 and Ar to a much more focused beam, with a probe size of 0.1 μm [3, 4].
The ﬁrst reported usage of FIBIP dates back to 1979, with Seliger et al using an
improved 57 kV Ga+ source to mill trenches on an Au ﬁlm [5].
In parallel to LIMSs, strong effort was directed to the development of ion sources
based on the ionization of a material in gaseous form [10, 11]. In 1955, Müller et al [12]
reported the utilization of the so-called ﬁeld ion microscope, based on the ionization of
gaseous He. Roughly, the instrument consisted in a glass-blown recipient on which a
ﬁne tungsten tip, a ﬂuorescent screen, and gaseous He were placed. By biasing the tip
and the screen contrary to each other, ﬁeld-ionization of the He atoms was achieved at
the former, resulting in the He+ ions being accelerated towards the screen, where the
effective emission pattern of the tip would be recorded (ﬁgure 5.1d). This experiment
marks the very ﬁrst time in the history of humankind when atomic resolution was
achieved—individual tungsten atoms from the tip could be clearly discerned in the
ﬂuorescent imprint of the emission pattern.
Field-ionization of a gas thus proved quite a promising approach as a basis for
ion sources in FIBs, setting the grounds for what would later be known as the gas ﬁeld
ion source (GFIS). However, the rise and consolidation of LMISs somewhat slowed
down the research process, since this established approach was also considered, for
most applications, more practical and doable at the time. After a series of efforts
Figure 5.1. (a) and (b) TEM images showing the formation of the Taylor cone at a 20 μm wide Ga-wetted
tungsten tip acting as a LMIS. (c) High magniﬁcation of the former LMIS, with the emission area visible at the
apex of the tip. Reprinted from [9]. Copyright 2004, with permission from Elsevier. (d) Field-ion microscopy
image of the tungsten tip utilized by Müller. Each bright spot corresponds to one individual atom in the tip,
this image being one of the ﬁrst ever depicting atomic resolutions. Reprinted from [10] John Wiley & Sons.
Copyright Wiley Periodicals, Inc.
Nanofabrication
5-3

[Page 181]
developing a suitable and repeatable procedure for tip fabrication in the 1990s
[13, 14], the atomic trimer-based approach that would deﬁnitely stay as the core of
GFISs was presented in 2006 by Ward et al [15], with a probe size of 0.5 nm and
imaging resolution of 1 nm [16]. This technique proved to be commercially viable
and is currently present in modern HIMs.
Thanks to all these developments, both LIMS and GFIS-based FIB instruments
have become commercially available as of the present day. They can be found at
specialized facilities of both commercial companies and research centers, where they
can be used either as a supplementary tool (very commonly, for transmission electron
microscopy sample preparation), or as the primary means of achieving a patterning
goal. FIB equipment manufacturers include the companies Thermo Fisher Scientiﬁc,
Zeiss, Raith, Orsay Physics, Hitachi, and Tescan.
5.2.2 Ion sources
The ion source is the component of an FIB instrument where a source material is
ionized to extract the ions that will posteriorly be accelerated in the column,
constituting the beam. Depending on the ionization mechanism, several types of ion
sources exist. As anticipated, the most commonly used are LMISs [6] and GFISs
[17]. While the instruments are typically equipped with a single source capable of
providing a certain ionic species, customizable setups from several source materials
do exist, both in commercial and home-made forms.
Liquid metal ion source
An LMIS is based on the ionization of a source metallic material in liquid state. It
is essentially constituted by a metal reservoir connected to a tungsten needle with a
tip radius between 1 and 5 μm, and an extractor electrode (ﬁgure 5.2a). The source
material, stored in the reservoir, is heated up to its melting point and wets the capillary
needle, remaining in place as a consequence of the surface tension of the liquid.
Figure 5.2. (a) LMIS. (b) GFIS. Inset shows a scanning ion microscopy image of the tip edge of a GFIS. Each
bright dot corresponds to one of the atoms in the trimer. Reprinted from [18], with permission from Elsevier.
Nanofabrication
5-4

[Page 182]
The extractor electrode supplies a high electric ﬁeld (given by an extraction
voltage, with values commonly around 10 kV [19]) that induces an electrostatic force
on the liquid. As experimentally observed by Gilbert in 1600, the balance between
the action of the surface tension of the liquid source material and the electrostatic
forces results in the deformation of the liquid drop into a cone shape with a very
sharp tip (2–5 nm), referred to as Taylor cone. Atoms are then extracted at the point-
like tip of the cone by ﬁeld evaporation through the electric ﬁeld provided by the
extractor voltage, and then accelerated down the FIB column.
Several metals, such as Al, In, Au, or Bi can be used as source materials in LMISs
[20], although the most widespread material used in LMISs is undoubtedly gallium,
due to its outstanding properties for this purpose, such as low melting point (29.8 °C),
low vapor pressure, and long lifetime, among others [6]. Ga+ LIMSs are common-
place in commercial FIB/SEM microscopes.
The ﬂexibility of FIB technology makes the potential usage of ionic species other
than Ga+ highly appealing due to the technological and scientiﬁc interest they may
present. However, some pure species present an elevated melting point, making the
engineering of the source challenging. This obstacle can be mitigated by using as a
source in the LMIS a binary or ternary alloy of the wanted material [21, 22].
The working principle of a metal-alloy based LMIS (sometimes termed LMAIS)
is the same as a pure metal LMIS, with a signiﬁcant difference: metal-alloy LMISs
do not emit a single ionic species as LMISs do, and as a consequence require a
ﬁltering of the beam before it reaches the substrate. This is achieved by using a mass
separator (also known as Wein ﬁlter and ExB ﬁlter): placed in the column after the
source, a series of magnets electromagnetically separate the different beam con-
stituents according to their atomic mass. It is worth noting that the mass separator
enables extracting different ionic species from the same alloy.
A myriad of alloys can be employed in metal-alloy LMISs, containing elements
that cover about half of the periodic table [22]. Their operating temperatures fall
in the 100 °C–1000 °C range. Speciﬁc examples include Co–Nd [23] and Au–Si [24],
among many others.
Gas field ion source
A GFIS is based on the ionization of a source material in gas form. The main
constituent of a GFIS is a ‘supertip’—a nanofabricated tungsten crystal whose size is
sequentially diminished in a series of terraces down to a single group of three W atoms,
or trimer. The tip is positively biased against a counter electrode (around 35 kV) and
then the source gas of neutral atoms is made ﬂow in its vicinity [25] (ﬁgure 5.2b).
Polarized by the electric ﬁeld, these atoms are attracted to the tip. In close
proximity to the trimer, the high electric ﬁeld results in the appearance of ionization
disks, i.e., small regions in which electrons from the gas atoms tunnel into the tip,
effectively leaving them positively charged. As a consequence, they are immediately
accelerated away from the tip and down the FIB column [15, 25]. The source is
typically tilted so one of the three atoms in the trimer is aimed down the column [10].
Conventional gases used in GFISs are helium and neon. It is relatively common
for GFIS-based instruments to be equipped with reservoirs for both gases, which can
Nanofabrication
5-5

[Page 183]
be ionized using the same GFISs. As aforementioned, and due to the signiﬁcant
differences they exhibit with conventional Ga+ FIB instruments, FIB instruments
with He+ (and Ne+) GFISs are sometimes referred to as helium ion microscopes
(HIMs).
Less frequent architectures are capillary-type and impregnated-type GFISs [26].
These are respectively based on ionization occurring at the end of a small capillary
through which the gaseous source material is made ﬂow, and on the ionization
occurring at the end of a needle joined to a tube inside which the source material is
stored.
Other sources
An alternative approach to obtain an FIB is the usage of a plasma source (PFIB).
These sources, more typically used to obtain broad beams rather than FIBs, operate
by creating a plasma of a noble gas and then extracting the ions through an aperture.
One of the most relevant advantages of plasma sources is the enhanced amount of
beam current they are able to deliver while keeping a relatively small beam spot size
below 25 nm (signiﬁcantly enhancing the removal speed in FIB milling) at the cost of
shorter lifetimes and restrictiveness in the number of feasible source materials [27].
Xe, Ne, O, and Ar are amongst the species that are ionized in plasma sources.
Somewhat more ‘exotic’ designs include laser ion sources [28], from which the ions
are obtained by laser-induced ionization of a focused atomic beam also obtained
using a laser; electron-impact sources [29], on which an electron beam induces the
ionization of a gas; and magneto-optical trap sources [30], among others.
5.2.3 Ion column
The ion column is the constituent of FIB equipment on which the ions coming from
the source are accelerated, steered, and focalized to the substrate (ﬁgure 5.3). One of
the reigning parameters of the ion column is the acceleration voltage, i.e. the energy
acquired by the ions as they are driven down a potential difference in the column.
Apart from the acceleration voltage electrodes and the ion source itself, the
column is essentially equipped with two different elements: electromagnetic and
electrostatic lenses, and apertures. An aperture is a physical oriﬁce of a ﬁxed size
used to deﬁne the size of the beam and moderate the number of ions that go down
the column. Different beam currents are selected by choosing an appropriate beam-
deﬁning aperture.
Electromagnetic lenses are constituted by arrangements of electromagnets in
quadrupole or octupole-like dispositions, in such a way that the joint magnetic ﬁeld
they generate radially increases away from the center of the column. The Lorentz force
induced by the magnetic ﬁeld on the passing charged particles deﬂects those away
from the center back onto it, focusing the beam. Conversely, electrostatic lenses are
combinations of electrodes that deﬂect and focus the charged particles in a similar
way, but without resorting to magnets and as such, lacking magnetic hysteresis.
A common and relatively simple column setup is based on two main electro-
magnetic lenses—the condenser lens, located near the source, and the objective lens,
Nanofabrication
5-6

[Page 184]
located near the sample. Depending on the particle optics between the two, four main
regimes can be differentiated: semi-convergent and semi-divergent modes, on which
the beam respectively converges and diverges towards the objective lens, parallel
mode, on which no further beam refocusing is performed before the objective lens, and
crossover regime, on which the beam is focused into a point between the two lenses
and then diverges into the objective lens.
Other relevant elements of the FIB column are the beam blanker element, used to
rapidly steer the beam away from the axis when it is required to, and the Faraday
cup, a conductive cup-shaped device used to precisely measure the ion beam current
by quantifying the charge accumulation that occurs on it when the FIB is steered
onto it.
5.2.4 Gas injection system
The gas injection system (GIS) is the component of FIB instruments used to deliver
the precursor materials to the substrate surface during FIBID. The precursor
materials are stored in dedicated reservoirs, commonly in solid state, although
liquid and gas-state precursors exist. To inject the material into the chamber in gas
form, the reservoirs are heated to a certain temperature to sublimate the material,
which is then delivered to the chamber through a nozzle placed in close proximity to
the sample in terms of lateral distance and height (between 50 and 200 μm in some
cases). If there are multiple precursor materials in the system, each may have one
individual, dedicated nozzle, or they may all share a common one.
Figure 5.3. Diagram of a generic FIB instrument, depicting the fundamental constituents. The FIB column
has been placed in the vertical axis for simplicity, but in dual FIB/SEM instruments it is found angled around
52° with respect to the vertically-placed SEM column. Not to scale.
Nanofabrication
5-7

[Page 185]
The spectrum of materials that can be grown via FIBID is bound by the availability
of a corresponding suitable precursor. Other than obviously containing the element
to be deposited, precursor materials must satisfy several requirements to be able to
be used in FIBID [31, 32]:
• the vapor pressure of the precursor in the GIS must be high enough to ensure
a sufﬁcient ﬂux of precursor is delivered to the chamber;
• precursor molecules must have a sufﬁcient sticking coefﬁcient to the surface
to which they adsorb, and a subsequent residence time in that surface large
enough to ensure they can be decomposed under irradiation by the beam;
• the precursor material must be non-hazardous to both handling operators
and the equipment itself.
Table 5.1 lists some relevant precursor materials.
5.2.5 Process chamber and electron/ion detectors
The process chamber is the main compartment of the instrument. Samples are
placed on a moving platform referred to as sample stage, which can be maneuvered
in all three directions of space, rotated around its axis, and tilted up to a certain
angle.
Most systems and modules within FIB instruments, such as the FIB itself, the
GIS, or spectroscopy detectors, are calibrated to optimally work at a speciﬁc height
of the sample stage, called eucentric height. For FIB/SEM microscopes, the eucentric
height is set at the coincidence point between the two beams and the stage, which are
tilted one with respect to the other in an angle typically between 45° and 55° [34].
When tilting the stage while at the eucentric height, the working area remains in
focus. Since in most commercial FIB/SEM instruments the SEM column is placed in
the vertical direction and the FIB is angled with respect to it, tilting the stage to
Table 5.1. Typical FIBID precursor materials found in literature.

Nanofabrication
5-8

[Page 186]
match the inclination angle of the FIB is mandatory if normal incidence of the FIB is
required (as is the case for conventional FIB milling and FIBID).
Most FIBIP procedures, if not all, require (at least to a certain extent) obtaining
micro- and nanoscopic images of the sample, either for observation, precise position-
ing, compositional and structural analysis, etc. These images are retrieved following
the conventional principles of scanning electron microscopy, on which, succinctly,
pictures are obtained by collecting and analyzing the electrons that the sample emits
upon interaction with the beam of charged particles.
The speciﬁcs on the origin and nature of the different electrons emitted from the
sample will be discussed in a later section. For now, it will sufﬁce to say that mainly
two types of emitted electrons are relevant for imaging purposes: secondary electrons
(SEs), inelastically scattered as a consequence of some loss of energy of the incident
particle when it excites an electron in the sample, and backscattered electrons (BSEs),
exclusive to SEM, which are electrons from the primary beam that are elastically
scattered away from the sample after impinging on it. The signal associated with the
former mainly provides topographic information, whilst the contrast associated with
the latter is mostly related to the atomic number of the elements in the material that
is being imaged, thus providing compositional contrast.
FIB/SEM instruments (and some FIB equipment, such as HIMs) are equipped
with a series of electron detectors that collect these emitted electrons and process
their corresponding signal so that an image can be constituted. It is outside the scope
of this chapter to go into detail over their working principles and relevance, but a
basic overview is provided due to their relevance in most FIB processing techniques.
The most commonly found electron detector is the Everhart–Thornley detector
(ETD). The ETD is placed inside the chamber, above the sample stage at an angle of
about 30°. Its end is covered with a metallic grid named Faraday cage, to which a
positive bias is applied (variable, up to 250 V). In this way, SEs are attracted towards
it, inducing the emission of light by a scintillating material. This signal is further
ampliﬁed via a photomultiplier and eventually converted into an electric current
using a photocathode, from which the image is retrieved. This setup is used to
capture mostly SEs, although in SEM, BSEs emerging in the angle at which the ETD
is placed can be detected by it as well [35, 36].
The signal retrieved by the ETD also contains second-order SEs or SE-II (i.e., SEs
whose emission has not been directly induced by the primary beam, but by back-
scattered electrons or ions) which worsen the resolution of the image. Some micro-
scopes are equipped with objective lenses whose associated magnetic ﬁeld is strong
enough to reach the sample (provided the working distance is short enough), trapping
electrons in an upwards spiral trajectory. Electrons can thus be retrieved above the lens
using a through-the-lens detector (TLD), polarized at high voltage (10 kV). This allows
for efﬁciently separating second-order and backscattered electrons from primary SEs,
which comprise the image retrieved in the TLD. The electric signal is also extracted by
a scintillator-photomultiplier setup [35, 36].
The ETD and TLD detect BSEs only incidentally, since the energy of emerging
BSEs is much higher (in the order of keV) than that of SEs, so BSEs are detected
using dedicated detectors. Rather than using a voltage to attract the electrons, these
Nanofabrication
5-9

[Page 187]
are usually designed as ﬁxed pieces that cover a large area, towards which the BSEs
impinge directly. The signal can be obtained either via scintillation, or by inducing
electronic excitations in a semiconductor material [35, 36].
The previous detectors are commonplace in SEM, and are typically optimized to
detect electrons whose emission has been induced by a focused electron beam (FEB).
Although they can be used when the primary beam is an FIB (and indeed are, for
instance in HIM imaging [37]), specialized detectors mostly dedicated to FIB imaging
are available as well. Widely known as electron multipliers, these dynode-based
detectors capture the SE signal induced by an FIB and greatly enhance it, allowing
for image retrieval. A common example is the channel detection electron multiplier
detector (CDEM, also standing for continuous dynode electron multiplier) [6, 38].
The electric signal coming from the detectors passes through an image processing
board, which maps the intensity distribution of the electrons coming from the sample
and yields a black-and-white image that is visualized on the screen. The contrast
can be adjusted by modifying the ampliﬁcation parameters of the signal, and varies
depending on the type of signal that is being processed.
5.2.6 Additional modules
Some FIB and FIB/SEM instruments are equipped with additional modules that
are used to either complement the nanofabrication process in some way, or to
perform in situ additional characterization procedures. These include, but are not
limited to, a microprobe station to perform in situ electrical measurements on the
samples; a nanomanipulator used to handle pieces of extracted substrate material,
used in transmission electron microscopy (TEM) sample preparation; spectro-
scopy modules to determine the composition of the sample via secondary ion mass
spectrometry (SIMS) or energy-dispersive x-ray spectroscopy (EDX/EDS) (FIB/
SEM systems only, since it needs an electron beam to induce enough counts of the
characteristic x-ray emission for each element); a stage temperature controller; and
an electron ﬂood gun used to correct charge imbalance.
5.3 Basics of FIB processing
Accelerating the ions down the column provides them with a signiﬁcant amount of
energy that is redistributed as they reach the sample. The energy exchange that
occurs at the sample is the driving force for all FIB processing (ﬁgure 5.4).
The most common and straightforward application in FIB processing is ion-
induced milling, through which material is precisely removed from the sample as the
FIB scans its surface. Alternatively, ion irradiation may also result in permanent
modiﬁcation of the material properties via structural and compositional changes (e.g.
amorphization and ion implantation). Materials modiﬁcation is usually regarded as
an unwanted side effect inherent to the nature of the process (referred to as ‘ion
damage’ or similar). However, it can also be exploited for wanted purposes, such as
resist exposure for ion beam lithography, in a similar fashion as a focused electron
beam selectively sensitizes resist in an electron beam lithography process.
Nanofabrication
5-10

[Page 188]
When used in combination with a precursor material, ion irradiation results in
material growth. Deposition occurs as a consequence of several interactions taking
place between the ions from the beam with the substrate surface and the precursor
molecules.
This section is dedicated to present the relevant interactions taking place during
the different processes and the controllable parameters that can be adjusted to
optimize it.
5.3.1 Ion–solid interactions
As the beam is scanned over a material, several interactions initiated by the ions
from the FIB take place (ﬁgure 5.5). They arise as a consequence of the conservative
transfer of energy and momentum from the ions to the atoms in the sample.
Depending on the mechanism that governs the energy loss of the impinging ion,
the ion–solid interactions can be broadly divided into two categories: nuclear and
electronic. On the former, the interaction is governed by the elastic interaction of the
nucleus of the ion with the nuclei of the atoms in the sample, whilst for the second,
the interaction takes place between electrons from the ion with those present in the
atoms of the material.
The extent up to which these interactions take place is governed by a parameter
referred to as ion range, which indicates the average distance travelled by an ion until
it loses all of its impinging energy. The ion range deﬁnes a physical region within the
substrate on which the ion irradiation is relevant, called interaction volume. This
volume is pear-shaped (in most conventional operating conditions) and its bounds
are strongly dependent on the ionic species used to perform the irradiation, as well as
on the nature of the material that is being irradiated (ﬁgure 5.6). Differences in the
Figure 5.4. Typical applications of FIBIP. (a) Milling, (b) ion beam lithography, (c) FIBID, and (d) material
modiﬁcation by ion irradiation.
Nanofabrication
5-11

[Page 189]
interaction volume of the ions in the sample lead to substantial changes in the
processing and growth of the material [17].
Nuclear energy losses
Ionic nuclear energy losses take place via successions of individual, elastic collisions
between the nuclei of the ions with those of the substrate. Incident ions elastically
collide with atoms in the substrate, displacing them from their equilibrium position and
triggering a collision cascade, through which momentum is elastically transferred from
one atom in the substrate to a neighboring one. The atomic displacements taking place
during the cascade generally result in amorphization of the material.
In a collision cascade, if the momentum transferred to atoms close to the surface
of the substrate exceeds their binding energy, they get ejected from the bulk.
Figure 5.5. Summary of ion–solid interactions in FIB processing.
Figure 5.6. SRIM simulations of trajectories in a Si substrate of three ionic species under different acceleration
voltages, showing the interaction volume of each.
Nanofabrication
5-12

[Page 190]
This effect is called sputtering. Sputtered atoms from the substrate may then
redeposit in the vicinity of the point where they were ejected from. Collision
cascades can also result in the emission of charged ions from the substrate, referred
to as secondary ions (SIs), since they are the byproduct of a primary radiation
represented by the FIB itself. SIs are used as a compositional ﬁngerprint in SIMS.
Additionally, elastic collisions may result in the ions themselves getting ejected
back from the substrate after one or multiple collisions, in a process referred to as
backscattering. Both sputtering and backscattering are enhanced as the angle
between the FIB and the substrate is increased away from normal incidence.
A part of the ions from the primary beam will remain in the bulk of the substrate
once they have lost all their energy. This effect is referred to as ion implantation and
results in the presence of a signiﬁcant amount of the ionic species on the ﬁnal
composition of the deposit.
Electronic energy losses
Energy from the ions is also transferred to the substrate through electronic interactions.
With the energy of the FIB always being set above the ionization potential of the
substrate, the energy transfer results in the appearance of excitations in the form of
emission of the already mentioned secondary electrons as ionization products, emission
of electromagnetic radiation, and in the creation of plasmons and phonons.
Analogously to the aforementioned SIs, SEs get their name due to their emission
being a consequence of the irradiation with a primary beam, the FIB. As anticipated,
depending on where they are extracted from, SEs are termed SE-I, or ﬁrst-order, when
they are generated at the local area of irradiation, and SE-II, or second-order, when
they arise far from it. SE-IIs appear when the impacting ions are scattered away
from the irradiation point and induce new excitations in a different region.
SEs are of paramount importance in FIB/SEM microscopy, for the topographic
contrast in the images is obtained by processing of their associated signal. In
addition, SEs are mainly responsible for the material growth in conventional FIBID.
If the substrate is polycrystalline and the ions happen to impinge in a low-index
crystallographic direction, the reduced number of substrate nuclei in these directions
results in the ion undergoing much fewer nuclear interactions than it would if it had
impinged on a different crystalline direction. This effect, referred to as crystallographic
channeling, has two main consequences: the appearance of signiﬁcant contrast in SE
images between oriented and non-oriented directions as a consequence of the change in
SE yield (oriented directions appear darker); and a reduction on both the milling and
material redeposition rates in oriented directions, since the number of nuclear collisions
near the surface diminishes as the ions channel (consequently, milling in oriented
directions is slower, but sharper) [39].
Numerical models
The computational modelling of ion-substrate interactions, used to gain insight on
the expected behavior of a material under irradiation by an FIB, is performed by
developing statistical-based approaches named Monte Carlo simulations, which have
also proven useful for SE emission studies [40, 41]. Their speciﬁcs are outside the
Nanofabrication
5-13

[Page 191]
scope of this chapter, but some of them will be brieﬂy mentioned in this chapter for
the sake of completeness.
The program transport of ions in matter [42] (TRIM), developed in the 1980s, still
remains a relevant and consolidated approach as of the present day. The program
deﬁnes the trajectories of individual impinging ions by calculating the changes in
energy and trajectory that occur at each individual binary collision with the atoms in
the substrate, tracing a collision cascade. This approach allows for the extraction of
statistically signiﬁcant conclusions when a large number of paths is simulated.
Different conditions can be emulated by modifying the ion’s initial energy, or the
crystallinity and composition of the substrate, obtaining a reliable approximation of
the corresponding interaction volume. TRIM is implemented in an open source
software piece named stopping and range of ions in matter (SRIM) [43].
More recent and sophisticated approaches to modelling ion behavior inside a
material, which are used to account for observed behavior in complex FIBIP scenarios,
include the programs EnvizION [44–48], which expands TRIM to include the sputtering
effects taking place in the surface of the substrate; IONiSE [40], which focuses on
simulation of SE trajectories; and TRI3DYN [49–51], which implements the binary
collision approximation for 3D nanostructures.
5.3.2 Operating the beam
Beam scanning
In FIBID and in FIB processing in general, the patterns traced by the beam are
constituted by a series of small, identical ‘irradiation spots’ in close proximity. The
FIB is steered upon each point during a ﬁxed amount of time and then moves to the
next one (ﬁgure 5.7). With some texts referring to each of these points as pixels, care
must be taken since in general they do not correspond with the digital pixels of a
Figure 5.7. Diagram of FIB scanning of a rectangular pattern using different operation parameters. (a) The
pattern is traced by successive exposure to the beam of 20 irradiation spots (using a small spot size) with an
x-pitch and y-pitch equal to the spot size (equivalent to overlap to 0%), and using a serpentine scan. (b) The
design is patterned so as to have 12 irradiation spots and a larger spot size. The x-pitch and y-pitch are set to
half the spot size, resulting in a 50% overlap between successive irradiation spots. The scanning is performed in
raster mode. Note that if the dwell time is set to the same value in both scanning types, approach (b) will be
faster.
Nanofabrication
5-14

[Page 192]
bitmap. The scanning of the complete series of points is in most cases repeated
multiple times to deliver the required ion dose to the substrate [52].
The shape of each of the irradiation spots is roughly Gaussian [52] with a size
ultimately mainly determined by the working current, increasing for higher currents.
The scanning is usually set for the individual irradiation spots to overlap with each
other, eventually constituting a deﬁnite or arbitrary shape ﬁxed by the operator: a
line, a curve, a polygon, a digital image in the form of a bitmap, etc.
The shape ﬁdelity and lateral resolution of the technique are given by the dimensions
of the interaction volume, which depends on the conditions of each speciﬁc FIB
system-substrate combined scenario. For milling only, and with ﬂat and conducting
substrates, the resolution is mainly limited by the spot size of the beam, associated with
the ion beam current: using low ion beam currents, features of around 10 nm can be
milled with Ga+ ions, and down to 0.5 nm with He+ ions. On the other hand, the
resolution will be jeopardized if the substrate contains heavy elements, as they give rise
to a strong ion–substrate interaction very close to the substrate surface. For growth
purposes, the resolution worsens due to the large lateral range reached by the SEs
dissociating the precursor molecules and, in addition, it is eventually bottlenecked by
the spot size of the beam. In optimal conditions, the best resolutions of Ga+ and He+
FIBID are around 20 and 10 nm, respectively. Other limitations are practical in nature,
such as the pattern requiring a reasonable amount of growth time at a speciﬁc current.
The most straightforward pattern that can be grown by FIBID is a ﬁlm-like
structure, by repeatedly scanning the FIB over an area while constantly replenishing
the precursor. In-plane nanowires and nanowire-like structures are also common-
place when working with FIB. By focusing the beam on a single point under a
constant precursor ﬂux, it is possible to grow out-of-plane nanowires, commonly
referred to as ‘nanopillars’. As will be described in the following sections, FIBID of
more complex three-dimensional structures such as nanohelices [53] and nanotubes
[54] can also be achieved through speciﬁc adjustment of the growth parameters.
Adjustable parameters
Most of the parameters that govern FIBIP can and must be adjusted by the instrument
operator to optimize each growth procedure. Setting a growth protocol for a given type
of sample, consisting in a systematic tuning of these variables, is commonplace when
attempting the growth of a new or challenging geometry.
• The acceleration voltage is the potential difference used to accelerate the ions
down the ion column. The most commonly used value is 30 kV, although the
attainable range extends down to 0.2 kV and up to 60 kV depending on the
speciﬁc instrument. Generally speaking, higher acceleration voltages yield
higher resolution, at the cost of a reduced growth rate.
• The ion beam current is a measure of the (ion) charge delivered to the substrate
per unit time. It is adjusted by selecting an appropriate aperture and/or
modifying the gas pressure at the source if a GFIS is being used. In FIBID,
typical ion beam current values usually fall in the pA–nA range. Depending
on the equipment, currents as low as 0.1 pA and as high as 2 μA (in Xe PFIB
systems [55]) can be delivered.
Nanofabrication
5-15

[Page 193]
• The dose is a quantiﬁcation of the charge received by a material per unit area
in a given time interval. This word is used somewhat interchangeably with the
term ﬂuence, the difference between the two being essentially formal in nature
(while the dose accounts for the charge actually received by a material, the
ﬂuence accounts for the charge passing through a given volume, divided by
the cross-sectional area of that volume).
• The volume per dose is a parameter that accounts for the volume of material
that can be grown/removed per unit charge. It is deﬁned as the ratio between
the volume of the processed feature (a hole or a deposit) and the total charge
received by the substrate, which can be calculated from the dose.
• The dwell time is the time spent by the FIB on each individual irradiation
spot during scanning. For milling purposes, high dwell times (i.e. keeping
Table 5.2. Operation parameters in FIBID, with the main features of the processed feature they inﬂuence, and

the beam focused at an individual pixel for an extended period of time) can
lead to unwanted redeposition of sputtered material. In FIBID, high dwell
times generally produce precursor depletion and, subsequently, milling.
• The refresh time is deﬁned as the time required to complete a full scan of the
pattern, referred to as a pass. It amounts to the dwell time times the number of
points in the pattern, plus any extra time added between successive scans. As a
note of caution, in some cases the term is used to denote this additional wait
time between passes without including the time spent scanning the pattern.
• The pitch, also referred to as step size, is the physical separation between two
adjacent points on a pattern. The pitch is typically set below the spot size of the
beam, so the irradiated area overlaps between adjacent spots. A different pitch can
be deﬁned in both the x and y directions. The overlap is actually a controllable
parameter by itself, and in most cases is set to a value of 50% of the spot size.
The chemical composition and morphology of the material can also be tuned via
thermally-assisted methods, namely heating the substrate during and after growth
(annealing). Performing the deposition above room temperature is found to markedly
affect the composition of the material, increasing the metallic content and promoting
crystallinity, as observed in FIBID of copper [56, 57] and gold [58, 59].
Annealing is found to have a moderate purifying effect on Pt-FIBID nanowires,
slightly increasing the metallic content [60], but with the resistivity of the sample
remaining fundamentally unchanged [61]. In addition, rapid annealing treatments
are found to have a signiﬁcant bending effect on freestanding nanostructures [62].
5.4 Milling and materials modification
5.4.1 Milling
Standard milling
The most straightforward application of FIB processing is direct milling. Given the
heavy mass of ions compared to electrons, the linear momentum of accelerated ions is
high enough to displace and eventually remove atoms from a given material. Under
the same acceleration energy, the ratio of the linear momentum of two different
charged particles depends on the square root of their mass ratio. In the case of a light
ion such as He+, the ratio of its linear momentum to that of an electron is 85,
increasing to 355 for a heavier ion like Ga+. The impacting ion will produce a collision
cascade phenomenon and, as described in section 5.3.1, it will transfer energy and
linear momentum to surface atoms, milling the material. The efﬁciency of this milling
process can be quantiﬁed through the sputtering yield (number of sputtered atoms per
number of incident ions) or the sputter rate (sputtered volume/ion dose) and depends
on various parameters, the main ones being:
• The type of ion: the sputtering yield will increase with the ion mass, given the
higher momentum of the impacting ion. For example, the sputtering yield of
Si under 20 kV ion irradiation is 0.66 for Ne+ ions and 2.9 for Xe+ ions [63].
• The ion energy: in ﬁrst approximation, the sputtering yield will increase with the
acceleration energy given the higher momentum and energy of the impacting
Nanofabrication
5-17

[Page 195]
ion. For example, under Ga+ FIB normal incidence, the sputtering yield of Si at
20 kV is 1.94 [64], increasing to 2.4 at 30 kV [65]. However, at very high ion
energies, the sputtering yield decreases again due to deeper ion penetration in
the material.
• The angle of the ion with respect to the surface: the ion incidence angle can
have a profound inﬂuence on the milling rate given that the removal of a
surface atom requires a backscattered ion to approach the surface. As an
example, in the case of Si, the sputter rate increases from 0.24 μm3 nC−1 for
normal incidence (90°) to 1.6 μm3 nC−1 for a 10° incidence angle with respect
to the surface, decreasing to zero for parallel incidence (0°).
• The surface binding energy of the atoms in the material to sputter: as a rule of
thumb, the lower the binding energy, the higher the sputtering yield.
In table 5.3, the sputter rates of various materials under 30 kV Ga+ FIB normal
incidence are displayed.
The most important aspects to be considered for milling processes are:
• The achievable milling resolution. Given one ion species, a higher ion energy
will give rise to higher-resolution milling. The higher resolution is a consequence
of a more ﬁnely-focused beam occurring at high energy, due to the decrease of
the ion column aberrations, and the lower spread of the ion beam in the vicinity
of the surface. Comparing the available commercial FIB technologies, Ga+ FIB
columns are widely used and have improved to the point of achieving sub-5 nm
resolution [66]. More recently, He+ FIB and Ne+ FIB commercial columns can
Table 5.3. Sputter rate for various materials under 30 kV Ga+ FIB
normal incidence.
Material
Sputter rate (μm3 nC−1)
Al2O3
0.08
Cr
0.10
Si3N4
0.16
C
0.18
Pt
0.23
Si
0.24
SiO2
0.24
Cu
0.25
Fe
0.29
Al
0.29
Ta
0.32
PMMA
0.4
Ti
0.46
GaAs
0.61
InP
1.2
Au
1.5
Nanofabrication
5-18

[Page 196]
provide beam spots of the order of 0.3 and 2 nm respectively, which can be
useful for high-resolution milling applications [10].
• The milling throughput. As it increases with the impacting ion mass, Xe+ FIB
columns have been recently commercialized in order to achieve much higher
sputter rates than Ga+ FIB columns [67].
• The damage created in the material around the milling area [68]. Higher ion
energies result in damage in larger areas given a deeper penetration in the
material. Typical material damage includes amorphization, recrystallization,
vacancy creation, alloying, etc. This is why some processes, such as the TEM
lamellae preparation, start at high acceleration voltage for obtaining a large
milling rate, but they are concluded using a lower acceleration voltage to
minimize the damage in the ﬁnal structure [67].
Generally unwanted side-effects that can occur during FIB milling are:
• Part of the milled material is redeposited in the surrounding areas and can give
rise to electrical short-circuit, lack of straight milling proﬁles, surface rough-
ness, etc. This effect can be sometimes minimized by means of an appropriate
patterning strategy such as multi-pass versus single-pass beam scan, left-to-
right versus top-down beam scan direction, etc.
• When the milled sample is composed of various materials with different
milling rates, FIB milling produces rough milled surfaces, curtain effects, etc.
Gas-assisted FIB milling can minimize this drawback (see next section).
• Smoothly milled surfaces can prove challenging to achieve in a single material
with various grain orientations due to ion channeling effects along highly-
symmetric crystallographic orientations that decrease the milling efﬁciency.
Gas-assisted FIB milling can minimize this drawback (see next section).
• Ion implantation can be detrimental if the physical properties of the milled
material are severely affected. The material conductivity, magnetic or optical
properties can be dramatically changed.
• The formation of helium bubbles (accumulation of gaseous helium near the
surface) when performing He+ FIB milling on certain materials has been
observed.
Despite the abovementioned difﬁculties, FIB milling is the lithography of choice
in several applications requiring material removal given that it is a direct-write
technique that does not require the use of resists and is capable of nanometer lateral
resolution (ﬁgure 5.8). Some relevant applications include:
• Circuit edit in semiconductor industry [69, 70].
• TEM lamellae preparation and cross-sectional FIB-SEM imaging in materi-
als and life sciences [71, 72].
• FIB-SEM 3D tomography [73],
• Small hole drilling in membranes for DNA/protein sequencing and prepara-
tion of shadow masks [66, 74, 75].
• Cantilever tip sharpening for scanning probe microscopy [76].
• Atom probe tomography specimen preparation [77].
Nanofabrication
5-19

[Page 197]
• Creation of nanophotonic devices [78].
• Mask repair applications [79, 80], where the technique has to compete with
focused electron beam induced etching [81].
• High-resolution milling. Ga+ FIB milling has been used to produce Fresnel zone
plates with resolution down to 15 nm [82, 83], which is of interest to focus x-rays.
Remarkably, He+ FIB milling has been used on gold to fabricate nanoantennas
with a resolution of 6 nm [84] and on graphene to create nanoribbons with a
resolution of 5 nm [85].
Gas-assisted milling
• In order to enhance the FIB milling rate, the insertion of certain etching gases
(Cl2, XeF2, I2, …) onto the area of interest is frequently used [90]. This
approach is found in applications requiring high throughput, such as circuit
edit. The combination of an etching gas with an accelerated ion beam
produces a combined physicochemical etching process that can multiply the
milling rate of a factor up to 100, as recently shown [91], although milling rate
enhancements up to a factor of 10 are usually reported [65, 90, 92–97].
Figure 5.8. (a) 45°-tilted HIM image of the cross-section of a large trench (50 nm × 200 nm) milled by 5 keV
Ne+ FIB through a test sample for circuit edit purposes. Reprinted from [86]. With permission from Elsevier.
(b) SEM image after FIB milling of a beetle scale, which allows observing the sub-surface tubular structures
producing plasmonic effects. Image credit: Teobaldo E. Torres (LMA), unpublished, collaboration with S
Mouchet and O Deparis. Used with permission. For related research, see [87]. (c) TEM image of a nanopore
fabricated on a SiC membrane by Ga+ FIB milling. Reprinted from [88]. Copyright (2009), with permission
from Elsevier. (d) 6 nm gap gold nanoantenna fabricated by He+ FIB milling. Reprinted with permission from
[89]. Copyright (2014) American Chemical Society.
Nanofabrication
5-20

[Page 198]
• Cl2 and other Cl-based gases have been successfully used for milling rate
enhancement in FIB milling of Al, Si, GaAs, InP, Cu, Cr and C.
• XeF2 has been successfully used for milling rate enhancement in FIB milling
of Si, SiO2, Si3N4, C, Ti, Nb, Cr, Al and W.
• I2 has been successfully used for milling rate enhancement in FIB milling of
Si, InP, GaAs, Au, Al, Nb and CH3NH3PbBr3 (ﬁgure 5.9) [96–98].
The gas-assisted milling has been found to produce more homogeneous milled
structures as well as minimization of curtain effects and grain-orientation inhomo-
geneities [94, 99].
Cryo-FIB milling
It has been shown in devices based on semiconductors and fabricated by means of
FIB milling that the performance is enhanced when the devices are patterned at
lower temperatures [96, 100]. The reason for this improved behavior is that the use
of low temperatures signiﬁcantly reduces the diffusion of defects created during
FIB milling.
Recent results show that the thickness of the amorphization layer created
during FIB milling of the topological-insulator Bi2Se3 material is much reduced
(to be published), showing great potential for the direct FIB patterning of
topological circuits.
Cryo-FIB milling in cross-sectional FIB/SEM imaging or during lamellae prepara-
tion has been found to be of great interest when soft solid-state materials are present
or when organic and inorganic materials are present in the sample of interest [101, 102].
It has also proven extremely useful in life sciences for frozen biological materials
(ﬁgure 5.10). Adequate work protocols allow these materials to be subsequently
imaged by FIB/SEM or TEM [103, 104].
Figure 5.9. SEM micrograph of an I2-assisted Ga+ FIB-milled InP-based trench waveguide. Reprinted from
[96]. Copyright (2010), with permission from Elsevier.
Nanofabrication
5-21

[Page 199]
5.4.2 FIB irradiation for materials modiﬁcation
Local modification in physical properties induced by FIB irradiation
In this section, a few examples of modiﬁcations in physical properties arising from
FIB irradiation are listed, in order to show how versatile this technique is, and
selected examples are shown in ﬁgure 5.11.
• In semiconductor materials, Ga+ irradiation on porous Si ﬁlms was found to
produce selective photoluminescence on the irradiated areas [105].
• In magnetic materials, several magnetic properties can be tuned by FIB
irradiation such as the magnetic-domain-wall propagation ﬁeld [106, 107], the
coercive ﬁeld and the magnetic anisotropy [108–110], the pinning of magnetic
skyrmions [111], etc.
• The mechanical properties of certain materials can be tuned locally by FIB
irradiation, as shown for Cu nanopillars [112].
• Nitrogen-vacancy centers can be controllably created in diamond for advanced
quantum sensing by N+ FIB irradiation [113, 114].
• Taking advantage of the great precision of FIB, it is nowadays possible to
produce single-ion implantation within a diameter of 100 nm in a determin-
istic way [115], which can be useful in semiconductor and quantum devices.
• The electrical conductivity of insulating diamond-like carbon material can be
tailored through FIB irradiation, with resistivity values as low as 103 μΩ cm
being achieved [116].
• Spin-ﬁltering regions in non-magnetic InGaAs layers have been created by
Ga+ FIB implantation [117].
Resist exposure for ion beam lithography
Ga+ FIB was used in the past to expose various types of resists in order to explore
the performance for ion lithography. A good result was found using the SAL601
resist with 30-kV Ga+ FIB irradiation [118]. Line widths with resolution better than
100 nm were found with sensitivity of 8 × 1011 ions/cm2.
Figure 5.10. (a) SEM image of a hydrated frozen mouse optic nerve after Cryo-FIB milling. Reprinted
from [104]. Copyright (2013), with permission from Elsevier. (b) SEM image of a hollow-ﬁber-supported
membrane section after Cryo-FIB milling. Reprinted from [102]. Copyright (2015), with permission from
Elsevier.
Nanofabrication
5-22

[Page 200]
Figure 5.11. (a) FIB irradiation of a 113 nm thick diamond-like-carbon (DLC) ﬁlm with various ion species accelerated under 30 kV, which allows for decreasing the
electrical resistivity of this material. Reprinted from [116]. Copyright (2014), with permission from Elsevier. (b) HSQ resist lines exposed by means of He+ FIB.
Reproduced with permission from [122]. Copyright 2012 American Vacuum Society. (c) Circular-shape carbon nanomembranes fabricated by means of He+ FIB-
irradiated self-assembled monolayers due to cross-linking. Reproduced with permission from [126]. Copyright 2014 Zhang et al, licensee Beilstein-Institut. (d) Degree of
ferromagnetic behavior (measured through the Kerr ellipticity) of Fe78Ni22 thin ﬁlms submitted to Ga+ FIB irradiation using 1 scan or 100 scans for a given ion dose.
Reprinted from [129]. (e) Microresonator fabricated by Ga+ FIB irradiation of Al2O3 and subsequent selective etching. Reproduced from [141]. Copyright IOP
Publishing Ltd. All rights reserved. (f) Selective growth of Ge quantum dots by pre-patterning the Si surface by means of Ga+ FIB irradiation and subsequent Ge
evaporation and sample annealing. Reproduced from [142]. Copyright IOP Publishing Ltd. All rights reserved.
Nanofabrication
5-23

[Page 201]
The topic has recently seen a revival given the very good results obtained for resist
exposure by He+ FIB irradiation [119–122]. Using HSQ negative-tone resist and 35 kV
He+ FIB irradiation with 1 pA current and around 100 pC cm−1 line dose, resolution
values down to 4 nm in half-pitch dense patterns have been obtained [122].
Growth of carbon nanomembranes using FIB irradiation
Previous results on organosilane self-assembled monolayers (SAMs) submitted to
FIB irradiation with various ions (Ga+, Si+, Au+, Au++) and subsequent exposure
to Pd solutions showed the capability for selective metallization [123]. In addition,
in thiol-based SAM-gold systems, the Au–S can be selectively broken under ion
irradiation, completely removing the organic molecule [124].
More recently, carbon nanomembranes with thickness in the range of 1 nm were
fabricated by the combination of SAMs and He+ FIB irradiation [125, 126]. The ion
irradiation produces the selective cross-linking of the SAM ﬁlms, producing a carbon
nanomembrane (CNM). These CNMs have found applications in TEM supports,
diaphragms in pressure sensors, gas and liquid ﬁlters, mechanical resonators, biosens-
ing, and as protective layers or to combine with other 2D materials [125]. It has been
found that the complete cross-linking of aromatic SAMs requires He+ FIB irradiation
doses around 850 μC cm−2. 1D or 2D cross-linked structures can be generated
depending on the FIB scan strategy. The transfer of the CNMs to other substrates
can be performed using PMMA resists [126].
Modification of crystallographic phases by FIB irradiation
Using Ga+ FIB irradiation, it has been shown that sub-100 nm ferromagnetic
patterns can be imprinted on a paramagnetic Fe60Al40 matrix [127], and then erased
by an annealing procedure. This phenomenon correlates with the disorder effect
caused by the ion bombardment on Fe60Al40, which transforms the original B2
phase into a chemically-disordered A2 phase. This transformed phase can be
viewed as an FeAl alloy that harnesses ferromagnetic behavior, becoming stabilized
when Al is substituted by Ga. Moreover, the sample surface remains smooth after
this phase transformation [127]. This scenario has been conﬁrmed by spectroscopy
measurements carried out on the same Fe60Al40 material submitted to Ne+ FIB
irradiation [128]. Another similar example is that of paramagnetic fcc Fe0.78Ni0.22
ﬁlms. After Ga+ FIB irradiation, the irradiated areas transform into a ferromag-
netic bcc phase [129]. In this material, it has been reported that the transformation
occurs following three regimes. First, the ion irradiation produces stochastically
small bcc nuclei, with the number of nuclei being equal to the number of incident
ions. As the ion irradiation proceeds, these nuclei grow in size, extending throughout the
whole irradiated area. After the maximum saturation magnetization is reached within
the irradiated area, further ion bombardment leads to a decrease of the magnetization
due to defect creation, atomic intermixing and material sputtering [129]. On the other
hand, He+ FIB irradiation has been used on non-ferromagnetic ordered L12 fcc Fe3Pt
ﬁlms, achieving disordered ferromagnetic patterns with resolution down to 10 nm [130].
It has been recently shown that Ga+ FIB irradiation induces the transformation
in SrFeO3-δ (δ ~ 3) ﬁlms from the perovskite (PV) phase to the brownmillerite (BM)
Nanofabrication
5-24

[Page 202]
phase [131]. This phenomenon has been explained by the preferential sputtering
produced by the ion bombardment, which leads to an oxygen-deﬁcient phase,
SrFeO3-δ (δ ~ 2.5). The resolution of this patterning has been found to be ~10 nm.
The PV and BM phases display different physical properties [132] which could be
of interest for the development of useful devices.
Use of FIB irradiation to obtain insulating paths on conductive materials
Due to the defects created introduced by FIB irradiation, it is possible to create
insulating paths in ﬁlms of certain conductive materials in order to create a micro/
nano-device. This strategy has been used to generate GaAs quantum nanowires with
width down to 20 nm [130]. The non-irradiated central part of the nanowires had a
sufﬁciently low number of defects so that the expected universal quantum ﬂuctua-
tions were observed.
More recently, it has been shown that He+ FIB irradiation on high-TC super-
conducting YBCO ﬁlms can be used to tune the material conductivity at the local
scale [133]. In fact, FIB doses in the range of 1016ions/cm2 have been used to fabricate
Josephson nanojunctions as well as SQUID magnetic nanosensors [134, 135].
Material modification by FIB irradiation for subsequent selective etching
It has been found that Si areas irradiated by Ga+ FIB behave as hard masks for
subsequent wet etching and reactive ion etching processing [136]. The etch selectivity
between the irradiated and non-irradiated areas can be very high, which has been
used to fabricate nanopillars with an aspect ratio of 15:1 (height of 600 nm and
width of 40 nm) [137] and narrow suspended nanowires [138]. Given that the etch
selectivity can be precisely controlled by the ion irradiation dose, this technique can
be used for gray-scale lithography and has been used to fabricate tailored 3D
nanostructures [139].
Other materials have been irradiated by Ga+ FIB and present similar phenom-
enology. For example, quartz also presents etching selectivity as a function of the
ion irradiation dose, which has been used to fabricate stamps for UV nanoimprint
lithography [140]. Similarly, Al2O3 presents etching selectivity with Ga+ FIB
irradiation, which has been used to fabricate microresonators [141].
FIB irradiation for subsequent selective material growth
It has been observed that local FIB irradiation of certain substrates produces
subsequent preferential growth of semiconductor materials on the irradiated areas. For
example, Ge nanocrystals grow preferentially on Ga+ FIB irradiated areas of Si and
SiO2 substrates [142, 143], which can be used to fabricate ordered arrays. Similarly, it
has been found that InAs dots grow preferentially on Ga+ FIB irradiated areas of
GaAs, which is of interest for devices based on semiconductors [144].
Besides, it has been observed that Au clusters grow preferentially on Ga+ FIB
irradiated areas of highly-oriented pyrolytic graphite substrates (ﬁgure 5.11). The
growth temperature is 400 K, which allows the gold atoms to diffuse on the sample
surface to ﬁnd the irradiated areas acting as pinning centers [145].
Nanofabrication
5-25

[Page 203]
5.5 Focused ion beam induced deposition
5.5.1 Fundamentals
As mentioned in previous sections, one of the most relevant applications of FIBs is
their capability to locally induce the growth of a material in a single step when used
in combination with a precursor material. The ease of use and ﬂexibility of the
technique make it appealing for applications that require unique and highly speciﬁc
designs: custom devices for prototyping, circuit repair, etc.
To perform FIBID, a precursor material in gaseous form must be delivered to the
surface of the sample, usually referred to as substrate. Si or Si/SiO2 substrates are the
ones most typically found, although it has been shown that is indeed possible to
perform the technique using exotic substrates such as insulating, ﬂexible polycar-
bonate [146]. The amount of precursor that reaches the substrate strongly depends on
the angle and distance of the GIS nozzle respective to the sample, on the temperature
of the precursor reservoir, and on the controlled pressure of the GIS system, if
applicable. In most cases, during deposition the pressure of the process chamber is
increased about one order of magnitude above its base value during deposition.
Once they reach the substrate, the precursor molecules adsorb to it and constitute a
molecular monolayer in dynamic equilibrium, as the molecules that leave it (according
to their speciﬁc residence time) are swiftly replenished by the continuous precursor
delivery ﬂux, which is (conventionally) kept constant during growth. Cryo-FIBID is a
notable exception to these operation principles, as will be discussed later in this chapter.
The FIB is then scanned over the sample tracing a pattern deﬁned by the operator,
locally inducing the decomposition of the precursor as described in the following
section. Once the scanning is complete, with the total time deﬁned by the dimensions
of the pattern and on the selected parameters (beam current, dwell time, dose, etc),
the precursor delivery is stopped, and the GIS is retracted, with the vacuum system
pumping away the remnants of the precursor within the chamber (ﬁgure 5.12).
Precursor decomposition
After SEs are emitted from the substrate upon interaction from the beam, they
induce a decomposition reaction of the adsorbed molecules of precursor material,
dissociating them into volatile and non-volatile parts. The volatile part gets pumped
away by the vacuum system, while the non-volatile constituent remains permanently
deposited on the substrate.
For instance, for the W(CO)6 precursor material, one can visualize the decom-
position reaction as:
+
→
+
↑+
W(CO)
electrons
W
6CO
electrons
6
The previous decomposition reaction is, however, an oversimpliﬁcation, since the actual
precursor decomposition is never fully achieved, nor does it occur in a perfectly ideal
environment. Firstly, the ﬁnal deposit always shows ‘leftover’ constituents from partial
precursor separation—in the case of deposition of W(CO)6, as will be discussed in the
following sections, the deposits always show a very signiﬁcant amount of carbon.
Secondly, the cleanliness and quality of the vacuum level of the process chamber
Nanofabrication
5-26

[Page 204]
(usually with a base level of around 10−6 mbar and raised to 10−5 mbar during
deposition) play a signiﬁcant role on the ﬁnal composition of the deposit, as the
FIB also induces the decomposition of present contaminant species such as water
molecules, hydrocarbons, and pump oils [92], if they are present.
Precursor molecules delivered by the GIS can both chemically and physically adsorb
to the substrate. The precursor molecular monolayer [147] that forms on the substrate
is a dynamical entity whose density depends on the adsorption and spontaneous
thermal desorption rates, on the surface molecular diffusion, and on the FIB-induced
decomposition rate. The adsorption and desorption rates are, respectively, governed by
the GIS ﬂux and the residence time of the molecules, while diffusion depends on the
nature of the substrate and of the precursor [92]. An efﬁcient decomposition rate relies
on the SEs having an energy close to the binding energy of the precursor molecules,
and on the scanning parameters being balanced with the residence time of the
molecules. Detailed discussion on the various mechanisms contributing to the
precursor dissociation has been carried out in the context of the growth of Pt pillars
by He+ FIBID [148], which illustrates these principles.
The electrical conductivity (or lack thereof) of the substrate also plays a signiﬁcant
role on the outcome of the process. FIB irradiation on substrates with insulating-like
behavior results in the appearance of charge imbalance in the substrate, which leads to
imaging artifacts, drift, and growth disruption. Charge imbalance can be avoided by
performing the irradiation in the vicinity of discharging conducting pads previously
grown on the substrate [146]. In systems where the irradiation is expected to leave the
sample always positively charged (i.e., irradiating with positively-charged ions and
expecting negatively charged SEs to come out of the sample), a ﬂood gun that injects
electrons into the chamber can be used to neutralize it. The most commonly used
substrates in FIBID are Si-based, such as boron-doped Si, SiO2, or SiN.
Figure 5.12. FIBID. While the sample stage is shown horizontal for simplicity, in most FIB SEM instruments
it will be actually tilted so the FIB incidence is normally into it. (a) General overview, (b) principle of FIB
induced deposition using a gas injection system for local deposition of 3D nanostructures by dissociating the
physisorbed molecules. Gas related processes include adsorption/desorption and surface diffusion to replenish
the FIB dissociated adsorbates. Note that deposition only occurs when the sputter yield is smaller than the
deposition yield. Shorubalko et al ‘Direct-write milling and deposition with noble gases’ in [17]. Copyright
Springer International Publishing 2016. With permission of Springer.
Nanofabrication
5-27

[Page 205]
Depending on the ratio between the number of generated SEs and the amount of
precursor available for decomposition, FIBID takes place in either an electron-limited
regime, or in a precursor-limited regime. In the electron-limited regime, the precursor
material is constantly delivered and replenished at a rate much higher than that of the
SEs inducing its decomposition. On the other hand, in the precursor-limited regime, the
SEs decompose the available precursor faster than it is replenished, resulting in either
substrate milling, or milling of the growing material itself. The competition between
growth and milling is a fundamental issue that must be addressed when determining
the conditions of an FIBID process. For example, the use of low ion beam current is
helpful in order to minimize milling effects with respect to deposition.
General properties of FIBID deposits
As with any other nanopatterning technique, the intrinsic nature of the growth
procedure sets a series of constraints and features on the ﬁnal samples.
One paramount limiting factor is the serial nature of the technique. Since FIBID is
performed with a single beam sequentially scanning one spot after another, spending an
amount of time on each point that depends on the required dose (rather than, for
instance, exposing a photo-sensitive resist through a mask in optical lithography), the
throughput of the technique is rather limited, and in most cases, not scalable for
industrial applications. The work ﬁeld area is, on its most extreme case, limited by the
size of the aperture used to deﬁne the beam, within the order of millimeters, with the
growth being typically performed with a ﬁeld of view in the order of a few microns.
Patterning very large areas requires high processing times and beam drift has to be dealt
with. As a consequence, FIBID-grown deposits are usually in the micron-size range.
The attainable resolution strongly depends on the growth conditions, mostly the
ion species, the acceleration voltage, the ion beam current, and the substrate thickness
and composition. All of these play a fundamental role deﬁning the FIB-substrate
interaction volume, which, as discussed before, eventually determines the resolution
of the technique. The smallest probe size is attainable at high voltages and low
currents, yielding a lateral resolution in the in-plane deposit around 20–30 nm for
Ga+ FIBID, and 10 nm for He+ FIBID.
As described before, ion-substrate interactions result in material amorphization
and ion implantation. Deposits grown by FIBID are expected to show both of these
features, which must be taken into account when assessing whether the procedure is
viable for a certain material or application. The magnitude of these events strongly
depends on the ion species that is being used, taking high signiﬁcance when Ga+ ions
are used, and being mostly absent for He+ ions.
It is not possible to establish a common generalization of the purity of the
deposits, since it largely depends on multiple growth parameters; as well as on any
post-processing methods, if applicable. It will sufﬁce to mention that it is expected
for the deposits to show elements present in the precursor material (typically C and
O), in variable quantity. In addition, signiﬁcant amounts of the ionic species used to
induce the growth can be present as implanted ions.
FIBID is often compared to its sister technique, focused electron beam induced
deposition (FEBID) [149]. The fundamentals and applicability of FEBID largely
Nanofabrication
5-28

[Page 206]
remain similar—a beam of charged particles is used to induce the decomposition of
a gaseous precursor, with all ion-substrate interactions being replaced by electron–
substrate interactions. FEBID lacks the growth-milling competition and ion implan-
tation characteristic to most FIBID processes, at the cost of a reduced growth rate
due to the FIB-induced SE yield being greater than that induced by a FEB. This
generally implies a reduction in the metallic content of the deposit due to the FEB
being less efﬁcient than the FIB in dissociating precursor molecules.
Growth under cryogenic conditions
At room temperature, the gaseous precursor adsorbs on the surface of the substrate
upon delivery, forming an individual monolayer that is continuously replenished
as the FIB causes it to decompose and/or the molecules leave the surface according
to their residence time. However, if the temperature of the substrate is lowered
below the condensation temperature of each speciﬁc precursor material (within the
cryogenics range of operation), instead of constituting this dynamical monolayer, a
multilayered precursor condensate forms, much thicker than the molecular mono-
layer occurring in gas phase.
Since the amount of available precursor for decomposition is vastly enhanced when
compared to conventional FIBID at room temperature, the volume per dose associated
greatly increases. Consequently, the growth speed of a structure increases by two orders
of magnitude when grown in cryogenic conditions [147]. Such a signiﬁcant modiﬁca-
tion in the required dose also results in differences in the properties of the grown
material, both compositional and structural in nature (e.g. absence of implanted
Ga, and of substrate and deposit amorphization). This technique has been recently
described for the growth of metallic W–C nanostructures, and it has been coined as
Cryo-FIBID [147] (ﬁgure 5.13).
Figure 5.13. Steps of Cryo-FIBID. (a) Formation of a condensed precursor layer at low temperature. (b) Pattern
tracing over the condensate layer, inducing the deposition of the irradiated areas. (c) Removal of the unexposed
areas by heating the substrate above the condensation temperature. (d) Final deposit after complete evaporation
of the precursor layer. Adapted by permission from [147]. Copyright (2019) Macmillan Publishers Ltd.
Nanofabrication
5-29

[Page 207]
5.5.2 Applications of FIBID
This section presents a review of the most relevant materials that can be grown via
FIBID, summarizing their properties and applications. When no ionic species is
explicitly mentioned, references are to be understood as having employed Ga+
FIBID—the usage of a different ionic species will be clearly indicated.
Platinum
FIBID of platinum is a common technique within the nanofabrication framework, very
frequently used as a protective barrier against ion-induced damage in nanoprocessing
for TEM sample preparation and cross-sectional FIB/SEM imaging. Pt FIBID nano-
wires also ﬁnd application as intermediate contacts for electrical measurements.
The most common gaseous precursor used in the growth of FIBID Pt is trimethyl
(methylcyclopentadienyl) platinum (IV) (MeCpPtMe3), which has a melting point of
30 °C. The usage of CpPtMe3 has been widely reported as well.
Characteristic growth conditions and deposit parameters from a selection of
reports on Pt FIBID nanostructures are shown in table 5.4.
The presence of impurities, naturally stemming from the FIBID process, raises
the resistivity of the deposited materials over its bulk value of 10.4 μΩ cm [150] along
a wide range of reported values. Such a dispersion in reported data was accounted
