5-30

[Page 208]
for in the systematic study performed by Fernández-Pacheco et al [163], in which the
thickness of the contacts was found to have a signiﬁcant inﬂuence on the ﬁnal value
of the resistivity, accounted for by changes on the composition of the deposit.
This compositional dependence on thickness is a consequence of the difference in
SE yield between the bare substrate and the already-grown deposit on top of which
more precursor is deposited. Namely, the presence of an energy gap on SiO2 substrates
[164] results in a lower SE yield than that occurring when deposited Pt–C is further
irradiated. The reduced SE yield that drives growth over the bare substrate results in a
lower metallic content on the ﬁrst grown nanometers.
The temperature dependence of the resistivity, ρ(T), is also different between distinct
samples. In fact, the actual sign of the thermal coefﬁcient dρ/dT differs between reports.
Fernández-Pacheco et al account for this effect by proposing a metal-insulator
transition (MIT) between distinct regimes as a function of thickness (ﬁgure 5.14).
In situ resistivity measurements reveal a signiﬁcant decrease in ρ with increasing
thickness up to 50 nm, tending to saturation around 700 μΩ cm when the thickness is
further increased. Speciﬁcally, the threshold of 3000 μΩ cm for metallic conduction
[165] is reached around 50 nm in thickness, setting the ground for the justiﬁcation of
the MIT, also supported by the fact that the metallic content is also low (<33% in
atomic percentage).
Thus, below 50 nm in thickness, FIBID Pt–C deposits behave as semiconductors,
with conduction governed by variable range hopping processes; whilst above 50 nm, an
increased metallic content yields a metallic conduction behavior. Ambient exposure is
also found to increase the resistivity of the nanowires, associated with oxidation of the
top part of the deposits.
As indicated in table 5.4, most TEM studies show the material exhibiting a
microstructure of nanograins with crystalline structure embedded in an amorphous
carbonaceous matrix. De Teresa et al [162] performed a high-resolution TEM study
Figure 5.14. (a) Resistivity of FIBID Pt deposits as a function of deposit thickness. The inset shows a TEM
image of the cross-section of a deposit, in which a difference in contrast on the ﬁrst 50 nm can be observed,
accounting for the differences in composition. (b) Atomic composition percentages for FIBID-Pt nano-
structures as a function of deposit thickness. Reprinted ﬁgures with permission from [163]. Copyright (2009) by
the American Physical Society.
Nanofabrication
5-31

[Page 209]
of thin deposits (20–50 nm) grown on Cu grids, for which fast Fourier transforms of
the obtained images were consistent with several orientations of crystalline planes of
fcc Pt (lattice parameter of 0.3924 nm). These crystallites are ellipsoidal in shape and
their size ranges from 2 up to 10 nm in the reviewed literature.
Several post-processing methods destined to modify the chemical and structural
properties of FIBID Pt can be found in the literature. One of the most intuitive,
annealing, has been found to have a from moderate to non-existent effect on the
resistivity of the nanostructure. However, as previously mentioned, annealing does
have a bending effect on freestanding nanowires [62]. This phenomenon is a
consequence of the compositional and microstructural anisotropy of the FIBID Pt
nanopillars that arises from the ﬁxed position of the GIS with respect to the standing
nanostructure, which results in one side of the nanopillar being signiﬁcantly more
exposed to the precursor gas ﬂow than the other. Cui et al used this thermal
treatment to grow 3D electrical contacts for freestanding nanostructures, and ‘nano-
cages’ to encapsulate nano-objects (ﬁgure 5.15).
The injection of a second gas during the growth procedure to favor the formation
of new volatile chemical species as a post-processing method has also been
investigated. When FIBID Pt is grown with water vapor being injected along the
organometallic precursor, an increase in Pt atomic content from 40% to 55% occurs
(at the expense of a decrease in C and Ga contents, and an increase in O content),
with an associated decrease in resistivity from 5000 to 1000 μΩ cm [156]. If O2 is
injected instead, a change in 0.61–0.27 in the C/Pt ratio is reported, with a decrease
in resistivity from 823 to 281 μΩ cm [168], whilst maintaining a linear resistive
behavior. Moreover, thickness is found not to have a signiﬁcant inﬂuence in the
resistivity when the deposits are grown using the O2 puriﬁcation method.
Post-processing based on electron beam irradiation after growth, which is
somewhat common for Pt grown via focused electron beams, has no sensitive effect
on Pt grown via FIBID [158, 168].
Figure 5.15. (a) 3D Pt nanohelix grown by Ga+ FIBID. Reprinted with permission from [166]. Copyright
(2015) American Chemical Society. (b) Hammerhead Pt nanoneedle grown on top of an AFM tip by He+
FIBID. Reproduced with permission from [167]. Copyright 2015 American Vacuum Society. (c) Pt nanocage
used to encapsulate a ZnO particle, constructed by thermally-induced shape modiﬁcation of 3D Ga+ FIBID
pillars. Reprinted by permission from [62]. Copyright (2013) Springer.
Nanofabrication
5-32

[Page 210]
Despite Ga+ ions being the most popular option for most FIBID processes, the
relatively recent commercialization of FIB columns of He+ and Ne+ ions has made
possible the investigation of growth of Pt FIBID deposits using different ion types,
with associated changes in the ﬁnal nature of the deposits.
For both Ne+ and He+ FIBID processes, the resistivity of Pt deposits strongly
decreases with increasing beam current. However, the lowest room-temperature
resistivity for these contacts is much higher for He+ (180 000 μΩ cm) than for Ne+
(600 μΩ cm) [169]. Their microstructure consists in Pt grains embedded in a
carbonaceous matrix, 3.2 and 4.5 nm in average size for He+ and Ne+, respectively.
The Pt content is roughly the same in both cases, around 16% in atomic percentage.
Tungsten
Along with platinum, tungsten can be considered the ﬂagship of the whole family
of metallic materials grown by FIBID. Tungsten–carbon deposits can be grown
using tungsten hexacarbonyl, W(CO)6, as a precursor, and present enhanced
mechanical properties [170] and relatively-low room-temperature resistivity that
enables them to be used as electrical contacts. Most reports agree on room-
temperature resistivity values near 200 μΩ cm, which permits the usage of W–C
nanodeposits as intermediate contacts for in situ and ex situ electrical measure-
ments of previously-grown samples.
Undoubtedly, the most prominent feature of FIBID W–C nanodeposits is the
presence of superconductivity appearing in the vicinity of 5 K, where the resistivity of
the material drops from a ﬁnite value (normal state) to zero (superconducting state)
with decreasing temperature (ﬁgure 5.16).
Superconductivity occurs in single-crystal tungsten at much lower and thus
harder-to-get-to temperatures (15 mK) [171], and its occurrence in FIBID nano-
deposits is, at the writing time of this chapter, a matter of debate. First, an important
distinction between the ionic species used to induce the growth needs to be made, for
the deposited materials are signiﬁcantly different in terms of composition and
crystallinity—most Ga+ FIBID deposits show around a 40% W metallic content,
whilst He+ FIBID nanostructures exhibit up to 70%; and while Ga+ FIBID W–C is
either amorphous or has very short-range order only (1 nm sized nanocrystallites),
He+ FIBID is crystalline. On that basis, the origin of superconductivity on both
types of deposits is also expected to follow different reasons.
In the case of Ga+ FIBID, Sadki et al [172] relate the occurrence of super-
conductivity in the deposits to their amorphous microstructure, which is further
enhanced by the presence of C and Ga impurities, originating from the precursor
and the FIB, respectively. This claim is compatible with the occurrence of super-
conductivity in amorphous W alloys. On the other hand, for He+ FIBID, the
superconductivity is associated with the presence of a superconducting crystalline
phase. The experimental results of Córdoba et al [54] point towards the presence of
large (20–30 nm) grains whose crystallinity is compatible with a WC1-x phase
(1-x being between 0.13 and 0.36), accounting for the superconducting behavior.
The superconducting phase is bound by the temperature of the material, the
magnetic ﬁeld applied to it, and the current density that ﬂows inside. These
Nanofabrication
5-33

[Page 211]
Figure 5.16. (a) Temperature-induced superconducting transition observed in a W–C nanowire grown by Ga+ FIBID. Inset shows a detailed view of the transition.
(b) Magneto-resistance at different temperatures, showing the temperature dependence of the upper critical magnetic ﬁeld. Reproduced from [173]. Copyright Springer
2014. With permission of Springer. (c) Scanning tunneling spectroscopy image of a Ga+ FIBID W–C ﬁlm, on which the Abrikosov vortex lattice can be seen. Each
dark spot corresponds to a superconducting vortex, where a single quantum of magnetic ﬂux threads the material. The triangular arrangement of the lattice is outlined.
Reprinted by permission from [180]. Copyright (2009) Springer.
Nanofabrication
5-34

[Page 212]
parameters all have critical values, which are typically used to characterize the
superconducting behavior of the material. The most straightforward one is the
critical temperature, at which the superconducting transition occurs. The reported
range of values for this parameters is found from 4.2 to 5.1 K [173] for Ga+ FIBID,
and 6.2–7.1 K for He+ 3D FIBID [54].
The critical magnetic ﬁeld, that is, the maximum magnetic ﬁeld the material can
withstand without going back to the dissipative state, is found to lie in the 7 to 8.5 T
range [173–175] at a working temperature of 2 K, while it is extrapolated to fall around
the 9.5 T value at zero temperature [176]. Values for the critical current density vary
between reports, sample geometries, and working conditions. Most of them fall within
the 0.01–0.1 mA cm−2 range [173, 176–179]. Additionally, two parameters typically
used in the Ginzburg–Landau theory of superconductivity, the coherence length and
penetration depth, have been determined for this material to be, respectively, 6.25 and
850 nm for Ga+ FIBID, and around 5 and 850 nm for He+ FIBID [54, 180]. Table 5.5
summarizes the superconducting parameters for both Ga+ and He+ FIBID W–C.
FIBID W–C deposits exhibit a particular type of superconducting behavior
referred to as type-II superconductivity. When a magnetic ﬁeld is applied to a type-II
superconductor, it penetrates the material in a quantized and ordered way, forming
individual entities named superconducting vortices. A superconducting vortex is a
small non-superconducting tubular region threaded by the magnetic ﬁeld ﬂux lines,
and screened by a circular supercurrent. Each vortex encloses a ﬁnite, quantized
amount of magnetic ﬂux (a ﬂux quantum). The supercurrents that surround each of
the vortices repel each other and vortices arrange in the (usually) triangular
Abrikosov lattice, the density of which depends on the magnitude of the applied
magnetic ﬁeld. An external current injected in the material also interacts with the
circular screening supercurrents of the vortices and moves them, resulting in the
appearance of electrical dissipation as the non-superconducting cores of the vortices
Table 5.5. Comparison of compositional, microstructural and superconductivity parameters of W–C grown
by Ga+ and He+ FIBID.

Nanofabrication
5-35

[Page 213]
drift within the superconducting bulk. This phenomenon requires the splitting of the
previously described critical magnetic ﬁeld into two different values—the lower
critical magnetic ﬁeld, upon which vortices start to thread the material, and the
upper critical magnetic ﬁeld, upon which superconductivity is destroyed. The latter,
which can be easily measured, is the one found in most reports in the literature.
The composition and microstructure studies of Ga+ FIBID W–C nanodeposits
are also mostly in agreement across the literature, with a W atomic content in the
40%–50% range, as well as with signiﬁcant fractions of C (30%–40%) and implanted
Ga (10%–20%). One notable exception to this trend is that of nanowires grown as
suspended bridges between two supporting features, on which the W atomic content
can be as high as 70% [170].
On table 5.6, relevant parameters from a selection of reports on Ga+ FIBID W–C
nanodeposits are shown.
The patterning ﬂexibility of FIBID makes W–C nanostructures an appealing ﬁeld
of study of several superconductivity-related effects, particularly those related to a
ﬁnite size or one particular sample geometry. Moving vortices cause measurable
resistivity shifts, such as a magnetic ﬁeld-driven re-entrance in the superconducting
state caused by vortex conﬁnement, observed in narrow nanowires (50 nm) [182]; or
the appearance of ﬁnite voltages in regions of the structure through which the
driving current does not ﬂow, solely generated by passing vortices that are being
pushed by exerting force on them in another region of the nanostructure [185].
He+ FIBID of W–C has also proven to be of particular signiﬁcance in the growth of
complex 3D nanostructures with outstanding aspect ratio, such as hollow nanowires
[54] and nanohelices [53]. The critical temperature of the deposited nanostructures, in

[Page 214]
the 6.2–7.1 K range, is up to 1.5 times higher than that of Ga+ FIBID nanostructures of
similar dimensions. TEM-based characterization studies indicate a higher amount of W
in the 3D nanostructures than those grown by Ga+ FIBID (up to 70% of W in atomic
content, compensated by a decrease in the amount of C); and the presence of 20–30 nm
sized crystalline grains, that it is close to the value of the nanowire diameter. As
anticipated, the composition range is compatible with the occurrence of the fcc WC1−x
phase on these grains [54].
The aforementioned fabrication of superconducting crystalline hollow nanopillars
[54], which can be also thought of as standing nanotubes, is a noteworthy example of
the capabilities of 3D He+ FIBID. The presence of a longitudinal hole piercing the
pillar is a consequence of a ﬁne balance between the milling and decomposition
mechanisms: to grow a pillar, the highly focused beam is steered at a single point
during the whole irradiation time. In the particular conditions of the experiment, the
precursor gas is decomposed and deposited over the surface of the sample and newly
grown material in the vicinity of said spot, while simultaneously being removed by
precise FIB-induced milling at the center of the spot, where the beam is focalized. A
3D hollow nanostructure as small as 32 nm in diameter and aspect ratio of 200 such
as this one proves the outstanding capabilities of FIBID.
Anther remarkable application example of WC He+ FIBID is the fabrication of
3D nanohelices with on-demand geometries by tuning some key deposition
parameters [53]. Córdoba et al report the smallest and more-densely-packed super-
conducting nanohelix ever grown so far, as small as 100 nm in diameter, and with an
aspect ratio of 65 (ﬁgure 5.17).
Lastly, the very recent demonstration of the capabilities of FIBID in cryogenic
conditions previously mentioned was assessed by Córdoba et al on the growth of W–C
Figure 5.17. (a) SEM images of the standing hollow crystalline W–C 3D superconducting nanopillar grown by He+
FIBID. (b) Cross-sectional TEM image of the hollow nanowire. The bright central area corresponds to the hole that
pierces the nanostructure. (c) High-magniﬁcation SEM image of the nanotube. The internal conduct can be detected
by the slight change in contrast it produces along the axis. Reprinted with permission from [54]. Copyright (2018)
American Chemical Society. (d) and (e) Superconducting W–C 3D nano-helices grown by He+ with different
geometry parameters. Inset in (d) shows the top view. Reprinted with permission from [53]. Copyright (2019)
American Chemical Society. Further permissions to the material excerpted should be directed to the ACS.
Nanofabrication
5-37

[Page 215]
nanostructures as well [147] (ﬁgure 5.18). As aforementioned, the most remarkable
feature of the technique is its extreme increase in processing speed, with a reported
decrease in growth time from 14 h down to 85 s for a large array of micron-sized W–C
squares. TEM-based analyses of the nanostructures show homogeneity in deposit
thickness, as well as a composition gradient, with the atomic percentage of W increasing
from 10% on the lower part of the deposit up to 22% on the top part.
Concerning their electrical properties, the deposits show higher room-temper-
ature resistivity than those grown at room temperature, but are, however, suitable to
be used as electrical contacts, as evidenced by a proof-of-concept contacting test of a
Pt nanowire. In addition, and contrary to the deposits grown at room temperature,
W–C nanostructures grown by Cryo-FIBID do not present a superconducting
transition down to 2 K. The precise reason for this behavior is still unresolved,
although the severe differences in deposit composition between the two techniques
are likely to play a role on it.
Notably enough, a lateral patterning resolution of 7 nm, signiﬁcantly higher than
that achieved for Ga+ FIBID at room temperature, is also reported for Cryo-FIBID.
The proposed justiﬁcation for this ﬁnding is related to the fact that the precursor
decomposition mechanism at cryogenic temperatures could potentially differ from
the conventional SE-induced decomposition at room-temperatures. Speciﬁcally, the
deposition is suggested to be directly induced by the ions in the primary beam,
allowing for much higher localization of the growth. Cryo-FIBID is, however, a
newborn technique, and further research is required to better understand the
underlying mechanisms that govern it.
Copper
In the electronics ﬁeld, the inclination towards nanostructuring copper stems from
the excellent room-temperature properties of the bulk material, including its
resistivity (1.7 μΩ cm). Deposition of copper ﬁlms is achieved by electrochemical
deposition, physical and chemical vapor deposition, and sputtering [186].
Figure 5.18. (a) Colored SEM micrograph of a high aspect ratio W–C nanowire grown by Cryo-FIBID. (b)
Two adjacent W–C nanowires deposited by Cryo-FIBID, showing an enhancement in lateral resolution when
compared to room-temperature FIBID. (c) W–C contacts grown by Cryo-FIBID (in green) placed on a Pt
nanowire, intended for a four-microprobe measurement. Despite their relatively large size, the growth time of
the full set is 50 s, roughly 700 times faster than if the process had been performed at room temperature.
Reprinted by permission from [147]. Copyright (2019) Springer.
Nanofabrication
5-38

[Page 216]
FIBID plays a modest role in this scenario, as the resistivity of the deposits is
sensitively increased due to the impurities that are intrinsically introduced by the
used precursor material (copper (I) hexaﬂuoroacetylacetonate trimethylvinylsilane
(C10H13CuF6O2Si), or Cu(hfac)TMVS for short). Table 5.7 summarizes the reported
ﬁndings.
The temperature of the substrate plays a fundamental role on the ﬁnal composition
of the deposit, with early reports of the process ﬁnding a Cu purity close to 100%
when the process is performed at 100 °C [187]. Laser-induced heating for this purpose
has been proposed and successfully tested [56]. Later investigations, however, have
not been able to fully reach this purity under similar growing conditions [57].
Gold
In a similar fashion, the targeted deposition of gold, which has a bulk resistivity of
2.4 μΩ cm, is highly appealing, with its potential applications encompassing circuit
repair, lithography mask repair, and the growth of plasmonics nanostructures, among
others.
The precursor material most commonly used to deposit gold via FIBID is dimethyl
gold hexaﬂuoro acetylacetonate (C7H7F6O2Au). Table 5.8 presents several reports on
the procedure. The work of Blauner et al [188] experimentally shows several relevant
dependences of the features of the deposit with the growth parameters, including: the
thickness of the deposit increasing with the ion dose, but being largely independent of
the gas pressure; the carbon content of the deposit increasing with precursor pressure;
and a strong decrease of deposition yield with increasing current density.
Ro et al [58, 59] report the compositional and microstructural dependences of Au
FIBID. The ionic species is found to signiﬁcantly inﬂuence the purity of the deposit,
with atomic percentages of Au of 40%, 45%, 80%, and 80% for Ne, Ar, Kr, and Xe
ions, respectively. As for the microstructure, three distinct ranges are differentiated
—using 70 kV Ar+ ions, the microstructure strongly depends on the current density:

[Page 217]
at 7 μA cm−2 it is granular, with the deposit exhibiting an atomic percentage of C
higher than 50%; whilst the microstructure becomes columnar at 0.7 μA cm−2, with
the carbon content decreasing to 30%–50%. Moreover, maintaining the low current
density conditions and increasing the substrate temperature at around 120 °C yields a
polycrystalline microstructure, and results in the carbon content being reduced to less
than 30%. In the ﬁrst two cases, the grains or columns of gold are embedded in a
carbonaceous matrix, and in all three the crystallinity corresponds to that of pure gold.
In the case of both gold and copper, the outstanding conductive properties of the
bulk material do not carry to its FIBID-grown counterpart, with the resistivity
increasing by up to four orders of magnitude. As a consequence, considering their
conductivity only, the utility of FIBID-Pt and W surpasses that of FIBID-Cu and
Au. Hence, the actual applicability of the latter is rather limited.
Other materials
The applicability of FIBID is of course limited to the existence of a viable precursor
material. These are unfortunately not available to every single material one can
think of, restricting the spectrum of typically grown materials to mostly those
discussed in the previous section. Other precursors used to induce the growth of
different materials, however, do exist, and although their usage and study are found
less frequently in the literature, a couple of them are brieﬂy reviewed here as well.
One notable example of such materials is cobalt. The usage of dicobalt octacarbonyl,
Co2(CO)8, as a gaseous precursor, is notably absent in the literature concerning its
deposition induced by an FIB. However, the usage of this precursor in combination
with an FEB is widely reported and studied, mostly thanks to the magnetic properties
the deposits exhibit [191]. Lapicki et al, however, do report the fabrication of arrays of
cobalt dots using this precursor on various substrates, achieving results of micron-sized
[192] and 150 nm sized dots [193]. The dots exhibit ferromagnetic properties in both
cases, giving the arrays the potential to improve thermal stability in magnetic recordings
[192]. Table 5.9 summarizes the reported ﬁndings.

[Page 218]
Sanz-Martín et al [196] report the milling-deposition competition that takes place
when inducing the decomposition of Co2(CO)8, which limits the thickness of the
deposits to around 60 nm within their available growth conditions (contrary to Co
FEBID, where 3D growth can be achieved). A Co content of around 50% is found
on most samples, and metallic conductivity with ρ ~ 20–40 μΩ cm is observed,
partially promoted by Ga doping (ρ ~ 6 μΩ cm for bulk Co). Wu et al [197] report
He+ FIBID of Co using the same precursor material, achieving higher Co purity,
and ohmic conductivity with ρ ~ 65–75 μΩ cm.
In parallel, Gazzadi et al [195] also report the usage of a different precursor
material, cobalt tricarbonyl nitrosyl, Co(CO)3NO. In combination with a Ga+ FIB,
the deposition of micron-sized lines with metallic behavior is achieved (with a Co:O:
Ga:N:C atomic content ratio of 54:17:9:13:7). Contrary to deposits grown using
Co2(CO)8, these lines are non-magnetic as deposited. However, they do exhibit
ferromagnetic behavior if they are annealed at 400 °C after deposition.
One other less common material is niobium. Porrati et al [198] report 2D and 3D
growth of niobium nanostructures, making use of Nb(Nme2)3(N-t-Bu) as a
precursor. The samples exhibit a 29:43:15:13 Nb:C:Ga:N atomic composition ratio,
with a polycrystalline microstructure consisting of 15–20 nm sized nanocrystallites.
With room-temperature resistivities of 550 and 380 μΩ cm for the 2D and 3D
nanostructures, respectively, the nanowires show superconducting behavior similar
to that of the extensively studied W–C FIBID nanostructures. A critical temperature
of 5 K is detected for the 2D nanowires, while the freestanding 3D nanowires exhibit
wide superconducting transitions in the 4–11 K range.

5.6 Other applications of focused ion beams
Deposition, milling and modiﬁcation of materials are the most conventional applica-
tion of FIBs. The applicability of them, however, is not restricted to those three at all,
for the focused nature of the beam provides with an invaluable tool that can be
exploited for other purposes, such as imaging or compositional analysis. In this section
some of these somewhat less extended applications are brieﬂy presented.
5.6.1 FIB-based imaging
In both scientiﬁc and industrial frameworks, SEM is usually the technique of choice for
micro- and nanostructure imaging, thanks to its ease of use, its ample range of
application, and its relatively wide equipment availability and affordability. In FIB/
SEM instruments with a Ga+ FIB, FIB imaging is usually performed only incidentally,
mostly due to the relatively high material damage the FIB can induce on the sample.
On this framework, examples of FIB usage include taking fast images to precisely
position a pattern over the sample, or carrying a ﬁne adjustment of beam parameters
by focusing and adjusting it over a test sample speciﬁcally used for this purpose.
Notwithstanding, the usage of focused beams of charged ions as a probe for image
retrieving is indeed a solid and established technique, which, by comparison with the
SEM counterpart, goes by the name of scanning ion microscopy (SIM) (ﬁgure 5.19)
[199].
The basis of imaging with charged particles is grounded on the beam-material
interactions that occur upon irradiation. In SEM, the focused electron beam induces
Figure 5.19. Examples of images obtained by HIM imaging. (a) and (b) Images in successive magniﬁcation of
a mitotic HeLa cell. Notice the high depth of ﬁeld in (a). Reprinted by permission from [37]. Copyright (2013)
Springer. (c) Butterﬂy wing (Papilio ulysses) Reproduced with permission from [202]. Copyright Wiley
Periodicals, Inc. (d) and (e) Images in successive magniﬁcation of endothelial cells of a rat kidney. Reproduced
with permission from [203]. Copyright 2013 Rice et al.
Nanofabrication
5-42

[Page 220]
the emission of both secondary and backscattered electrons, whose associated signal
can be used to generate images, each providing different contrast (SEs are typically
used to study the surface topography, whilst the contrast of BSEs relates to the
composition of the sample). In SIM, the beam that induces the emission of these
electrons is a FIB, but the working principle remains the same.
The key difference between the two approaches is the fact that the de Broglie
wavelength associated with the ions in an FIB is smaller than that of the electrons in
SEM. This fact, coupled with a smaller interaction volume on the surface, results in a
signiﬁcant resolution enhancement, down to 0.25 nm for He+ ions [199]. An important
drawback is that contrary to electrons, the ions in an FIB can induce surface milling,
material amorphization, and ion implantation.
In principle, any ionic species can be used for the purposes of SIM imaging, but
lighter ions are preferred, since their associated sputtering rates are reduced. For this
reason, whilst Ga+ SIM is indeed feasible [200], the low sputtering rate and small probe
size achievable with He+ FIBs make them the preferred choice for SIM [17, 199]. In
fact, it has just been relatively recently that HIMs have entered the ﬁeld of FIBIP, for
they had been mostly used for imaging purposes so far [17]. The usage of other novel
sources, based on more exotic species such as Li+, has also been reported [201].
Some notable functional differences that arise from the usage of ions rather
than electrons, are a higher SE yield, the emission of SIs, and the occurrence of
ion channeling. All of them can be exploited for imaging purposes—high
resolution imaging, compositional contrast, and crystallographic contrast,
respectively [17].
A remarkable feature of SIM is its relatively simple capability to overcome charge
imbalance, which is an important issue when imaging electrically insulating samples
in SEM. In HIM, since the incident ions are positively charged and the vast majority
of the particles emitted by the samples are negatively charged SEs, in all cases the net
charge accumulated on the sample is positive (this is not the case in SEM, where the
incident particles are negatively charged, rendering a less evident charge balance). The
aforementioned ﬂood gun, commonly found in HIM equipment, is used to diffuse low
energy electrons over the surface of the sample as it is being irradiated with the FIB. By
synchronizing and adjusting the operating parameters of the gun to ‘match’ those of
the FIB, it is possible to completely compensate the charge imbalance, eliminating all
associated artifacts and improving the quality of the image [17].
One other remarkable feature is the fact that, compared to SEM, HIM imaging
allows for a much larger depth of ﬁeld. Since the He+ FIB ends as a very sharp
probe, with the beam having a very small divergence angle, several planes of the
sample away from the focusing point can be still kept ‘focused’ at the same time,
without appearing blurry on the images. This feature is particularly appealing when
imaging large 3D structures, such as biological samples.
5.6.2 FIB-based tomography
In general terms, tomography is an imaging technique on which the three-dimensional
structure of an object is extracted by sequentially obtaining images of successive cross-
Nanofabrication
5-43

[Page 221]
sectional views of it. Depending on the nature of the probe used to obtain the image,
tomography can either be performed by physically slicing the sample and imaging each
cut, or by retrieving the signal yielded by penetrating radiation that bounces on it (e.g.,
a medical CT scan).
Commonplace in medical, life, geological, and material sciences (among others),
the applicability of this technique is large, as is the number of available probes and
approaches that exist for it. The milling/slicing capabilities of a dual FIB/SEM
instrument make a ﬁne addition to that list of probes, for the equipment is capable of
achieving notably high resolution (with a size of reconstructed ‘volumetric pixel’ of
tens of nm, down to 5 nm [31]).
FIB-based tomography is performed in FIB/SEM instruments by positioning the
sample at the eucentric height, tilted 52°. The working volume is deﬁned by using the
FIB to mill trenches on its sides, deﬁning a cube-like volume with edges in the 10 μm
range [31]. Then, the FIB is used to mill away a ﬁne slice of the material along the
direction of the cube that is parallel to it, i.e., its lateral face that faces the electron
beam. An SEM image is taken, and the process is repeated along the whole volume
under study. Due to the imaging plane effectively changing height as material slices
are milled away during the process, in order to keep the sample in focus, the focus is
dynamically tracked and corrected. In addition, due to the extended time the process
consumes, it is also necessary to account for beam drift, particularly if the procedure
is performed in combination with EDX analysis (which further extends the process-
ing time).
The operating parameters strongly depend on the material under study and its
expected microstructure—the thickness of each slice imposes a strong constraint on
the quality of the study, potentially obscuring any features smaller than each slice
thickness. Moreover, the operating voltage of the SEM column must be adjusted so
the interaction volume of the electron beam does not exceed the volume of the
features and/or the thickness of the slice. An interaction volume greater than that
thickness would include information about the layers below the one under study,
also shadowing the validity of the study.
The ﬁnal step is the 3D reconstruction and data extraction from the retrieved
image stack, which is a complex procedure whose nature speciﬁcally depends on the
material under study and involves corrections for contrast and brightness, image
drift, and others. In addition, it can involve statistical and compositional analysis of
the revealed features. Currently, some manufacturers provide automated software
that handles the procedure and subsequent analyses.
FIB-based tomography ﬁnds a strong ﬁeld of application in the life sciences, for
it provides the capability to gain insight into the internal structure of a cell [73]
(ﬁgure 5.20). This includes, for example, assessing the way magnetic nanoparticles
are distributed within loaded cells [204, 205] or 3D reconstruction of various types
of animal tissues [206]. Other applications are more closely related to materials
science, including the study of porous and granular materials [31].
Nanofabrication
5-44

[Page 222]
5.6.3 Secondary-ion mass spectrometry
The secondary ions (SIs) that are sputtered away from a material when it is irradiated
with an FIB (recall that the term ‘secondary’ is used to differentiate them from those of
the FIB itself, which would be considered the ‘primary’ beam) contain compositional
and structural information on the sample. Adequately obtaining these SIs, retrieving
them, and performing the subsequent analyses are the fundamentals of SIMS [17].
The procedure begins by irradiating the sample of interest with an FIB, which
induces sputtering and ejection of SIs. By applying a potential difference in the
chamber, the electrically charged SIs are redirected towards a mass spectrometer, on
which they follow separate trajectories depending on their mass, and eventually
reach detectors that count and track all contributions.
Since the ejection of sputtered atoms primarily occurs at the surface of the
material that is being irradiated, the extent to which the information retrieved from
the SIs is applicable is limited to the surface of the sample. Depending on the
magnitude of the delivered ion dose, SIMS is performed in either static mode, with a
moderate dose that allows for the study of a ‘ﬁxed’ surface; or in dynamic mode, in
Figure 5.20. A pancreatic carcinoid human cell imaged by FIB/SEM tomography. (a) Tomogram of BON
cells. Inset shows nuclear membranes, a part of the Golgi apparatus, and chromogranin A-containing vesicles.
(b) Segmentation of the plasma membrane and of three cell nuclei. Reproduced from [207] with permission of
Springer. Copyright Springer-Verlag 2012.
Nanofabrication
5-45

[Page 223]
which a much higher ion dose results in constant removal of the irradiated surface,
which allows for depth proﬁling.
Mass spectra retrieved by SIMS provide information on the surface composition,
both statically and dynamically (depth proﬁling), achieving a depth resolution that
ranges from a few nm [208] down to sub-nm resolution when low-energy FIBs are
used [17]. In addition, it is possible to reconstruct images of the surface based on the
mass ﬁltering. This SIMS imaging is achieved by synchronizing the counting and
binning of the data with the sequential scanning of the FIB on the surface, so that for
each irradiation spot, a corresponding mass spectrum is stored.
While SIMS is typically performed in dedicated instruments, which include both
the FIB and the mass spectrometer [209], a SIMS module can also be incorporated
into HIMs.
5.6.4 Current developments and future challenges
The well-established status of FIBIP and the pressing needs from science and
manufacturing settle the grounds for future evolution and improvement of the
instruments and techniques that surround it.
Concerning instrumentation advancement, the one obvious effervescent ﬁeld is the
development of different ion sources, capable of delivering high enough current
densities with small probe sizes. Apart from the previously mentioned ones (LMAISs
and PFIBs), laser-cooled low temperature ion sources (LOTISs) can be highlighted
[34]. These make use of a beam of cold (microkelvin) gaseous beam of neutral Cs
atoms that are photoionized using a laser [210].
Within the imaging framework, a very recent development is that of transmission
helium ion microscopy (THIM), a technology that makes use of FIB-like beams (with
acceleration voltages below 50 kV) as imaging probes analogously to TEM, using
transmitted ions to retrieve information on the nature of the sample. Mousley et al [211]
report the usage of the technique in a custom instrument, used for imaging several
powders in TEM grids. THIM images of uncoated samples feature a series of bright
spots, associated with ion deﬂection arising from electrostatic charging of the sample.
Although these spots are artifacts in nature, they could prove useful in imaging of larger
samples, for the ions from the beam are deﬂected according to the surface topography,
eventually yielding the pattern.
The growth of increasingly complex nanostructures using FIBIP is also a strong
trend in the ﬁeld, with 3D nanopatterning being at the forefront. Some noteworthy
achievements include the fabrication of custom nano-tools such as manipulators or
rotors [95].
5.7 End-of-chapter summary
Focused ion beam processing is an umbrella term used to refer to the wide variety of
techniques that make use of a focused beam of charged ions as the main driving
engine. FIBIP encompasses the usage of FIBs for material removal and modiﬁcation
(milling, trimming, ion implantation), material deposition via FIBID, imaging, and
compositional and structural analysis (SIMS, FIB tomography).
Nanofabrication
5-46

[Page 224]
FIBIP is performed in dedicated instruments that hold an ion source, whose nature
depends on the ionic species (typically, LMIS for Ga+ beams and GFIS for He+/Ne+),
an ion column with several apertures and lenses used to construct and focalize the
beam, and a processing chamber that holds the specimen as well as nanofabrication
modules (gas injection system, nanomanipulator) and electron and ion detectors.
The fundamentals of each speciﬁc technique are related to the nature of the
physical interactions between the ions from the beam and the atoms of the material
they impinge on. On the one hand, for milling and trimming purposes, mostly nuclear
energy losses come into play, with the energetic ions from the beam transferring
energy to the atoms in the substrate and displacing them.
On the other hand, growth of material via FIBID is grounded on electronic
energy losses, which manifest themselves by the emission of secondary electrons
from the atoms in the substrate. These electrons induce the decomposition of
gaseous precursors previously adsorbed on the surface of the material, which allows
for patterning at will by tracing the required design with the FIB.
Providing both great ﬂexibility, relative ease of use, and a large range of
processable materials, FIBIP has established itself as a solid and popular technique
in nanofabrication, and it is not unreasonable to expect for it further developments
and innovations in the foreseeable future.
5.8 Students’ corner
• A video animation on FIB milling and FIBID growth can be imaged at
https://iopscience.iop.org/book/978-0-7503-2608-7.
• A video animation on Cryo-FIBID growth can be imaged in reference [147].
We encourage the reader to go deeper into Cryo-FIBID technique, where
exciting developments are quickly underway [212, 213].
• Compare the advantages and disadvantages of FEBID and FIBID in these
cases: growth of electrical contacts to a nano-object, growth of a 3D magnetic
nanostructure, and growth of a protective layer on a given material.
• Compare the advantages and disadvantages of FIB and FEBIE for optical
mask repair.
• Compare the advantages and disadvantages for compositional analysis of
EDX using a SEM and SIMS using a HIM.

IOP Publishing
Nanofabrication
Nanolithography techniques and their applications
Jose´ Marı´a De Teresa
Chapter 6
Scanning probe lithography
Ricardo Garcia
The atomic force microscope enabled a variety of approaches to modify surfaces
with nano or atomic-scale features. Some of those approaches had the robustness
and nanoscale accuracy to evolve into advanced nanopatterning and nanolithog-
raphy methods. This chapter describes the principles of scanning probe lithography
(SPL) and some of its applications in materials science and nanotechnology. The
focus is placed on selected SPL methods based on oxidation, thermal and deposition
processes, which offer high-resolution nanolithographic applications and are applied
by several groups.
6.1 Introduction: from atomic-scale modifications to scanning probe
lithography
The inventions of scanning tunneling microscope (STM) [1] and atomic force
microscope (AFM) [2] brought about a rich variety of methods to modify and/or
manipulate surfaces at atomic and nanometer scales [3–10]. The local character of
the tip–surface interaction opened several routes to pattern surfaces and materials
which eventually led to the establishment of scanning probe lithography (SPL) [6].
This process was the result of a combination of factors, among them, a technological
context of the late 1990s that favoured the development of alternative lithographies,
the curiosity of some scientists and the appeal of combining the powerful metrology
provided by probe microscopy with nanoscale patterning.
Several STM and AFM methods demonstrated the capability to modify or
manipulate surfaces with atomic-scale resolution [5, 11–13]. Those approaches were
inspiring due to their atomic-scale features but they lacked the required throughput
for large scale nanopatterning or device applications. There were some remarkable
exceptions such as the fabrication of atomic-scale transistors [14, 15] or very high-
density memories [16] by manipulating a few atoms in each processing step.
Both STM and AFM were used to perform nanolithographic experiments.
However, the majority of SPL applications are performed with a force microscope.
doi:10.1088/978-0-7503-2608-7ch6
6-1
ª IOP Publishing Ltd 2020. All rights,
including for text and data mining (TDM), artificial intelligence (AI) training, and similar technologies, are reserved.

[Page 237]
The AFM is very convenient for nanolithography because the feedback parameter
that controls the imaging process [17] does not depend on the conductivity of the
sample surface. In addition, the AFM might be operated at its highest spatial
resolution in air, liquid and vacuum.
This chapter aims to provide an introduction to scanning probe lithography
(SPL) by focusing on the probe-based methods that have a very high degree of
repeatability by patterning a large number of nanometer-scale features and/or
enabled the fabrication of electronic or optical nanoscale devices. The described
methods are oxidation SPL [3, 6, 18], thermal SPL [6, 19, 20] and deposition SPL,
which includes dip-pen nanolithography [4] and some variations [21, 22]. At the end
of the chapter, some considerations will be provided for other SPL methods. A
general scheme to classify SPL methods is shown in ﬁgure 6.1, which also serves the
purpose for the description of the physical and chemical interactions relevant in
SPL.
A variety of physical and chemical interactions were applied to pattern surfaces
by using an AFM, as shown in ﬁgure 6.1: electrochemical reactions [3, 8],
mechanical forces [9, 23–25], bias-induced processes [26–29], thermal-induced
processes [19–21, 30, 31], chemical reactions including catalytic reactions [32, 33]
or the controlled deposition of molecules [4, 22, 34]. SPL methods either add or
remove material from the surface. The local character of the interactions in a probe
microscope interface gives rise to the formation of nanoscale features.
6.2 Oxidation SPL
Oxidation SPL is based on the formation of nanoscale oxides on a surface. The
oxidation reaction is driven by the application of a voltage bias between the AFM
tip (negative) and the sample surface (positive) in the presence of oxygen species
(oxyanions). The oxygen species are usually provided by the presence of a
Figure 6.1. (a) Types of tip–surface interactions in SPL. (b) Classiﬁcation of SPL methods according to the
dominant interaction/process used for nanopatterning [6].
Nanofabrication
6-2

[Page 238]
spontaneous water bridge joining tip and surface. The polarity of the tip indicates
that the oxidation happens in the positive electrode (anode).
There are some similarities between the local oxidation process in o-SPL and the
macroscopic anodic oxidation happening in a conventional electrochemical cell. The
tip (cathode), the sample (anode) and the water bridge (solution) can be considered
the elements of a nanoscale electrochemical cell. The absence of a reference electrode
is a key difference between o-SPL and an anodic oxidation in a conventional
electrochemical cell.
The ﬁrst evidence of a probe-based oxidation process was reported by John
Dagata and co-workers in 1990 [35]. They observed that a hydrogen-terminated
silicon surface was modiﬁed while imaging it with an STM operated in ambient air.
At about the same time, Thomas Thundat and co-workers used an STM to smooth
tantalum surfaces [36]. In both cases a voltage of a few volts was applied between the
STM tip and sample surface. High-spatial resolution spectroscopic measurements
showed that the modiﬁcations were due to the oxidation of the surfaces [37, 38].
A relevant feature of o-SPL is that the patterning and the imaging processes
operate independently. This means that the patterning processes could be inter-
rupted at any time to examine its metrology aspects. Long range van-der-Waals
forces and/or short-range repulsive forces are used to image the surface while an
external voltage is applied to induce the local oxidation of the surface [17]. High
resolution images of the sample can be obtained and used for precise alignment or
assessment of the lithography without introducing further modiﬁcations on the
fabricated nanostructures.
6.2.1 Key aspects of o-SPL
The main factors that participate in the nanopatterning process by o-SPL are the
water bridge, the electric ﬁeld and the oxidation kinetics (ﬁgure 6.2(a)). A brief
description of those factors is given below. Full details of o-SPL are provided in
comprehensive reviews [3, 6, 18] and/or in original publications.
The water bridge. The formation of a nanoscale water bridge (meniscus)
connecting the tip and the sample is a key feature in o-SPL (ﬁgure 6.2). The water
bridge provides the oxygen species (mostly OH−) needed to oxidize the surface. It
also conﬁnes spatially the oxidation reaction. The water molecules forming the
bridge might come from the humid air or from water molecules previously adsorbed
on the surface [39, 40]. The water bridge acts as an effective electrostatic lens which
focuses the ﬁeld lines within the water bridge limits. The focusing effect is enhanced
by the differences in the electric permittivities inside and outside the liquid bridge,
respectively, 81 and 1.
The bias voltage and the electric ﬁeld. To drive the anodic oxidation process, the
sample is positively biased with respect to the tip. In an AFM interface, the tip–
surface separation is very small (~1 nm). Those tip–surface distances imply that the
application of a voltage in the 10 V range might generate extremely high electric
ﬁelds of ~10 GV m−1. The electric ﬁeld in o-SPL has three main roles. First, it
induces and stabilizes the formation of a water bridge (or meniscus). Second, it
Nanofabrication
6-3

[Page 239]
produces the hydrolysis of the water molecules. This process generates the oxyanions
that participate in the oxidation process. Third, it drives the oxyanions through the
growing oxide.
Kinetics. In o-SPL, the oxide height shows a linear dependence on the applied
voltage and a logarithmic dependence on the oxidation time (power-of-time law)
[42, 43]

(6.1)

0
where h, V, t are the total thickness of the oxide, applied voltage and oxidation time;
h0, t0 and γ are ﬁtting parameters; γ depends on the material. For Si(100) and MoS2,
the γ values are, respectively, ~0.2 and ~0.5. The power-of-time law dependence was
theoretically explained by assuming that the rate-controlling step of the oxidation
reaction involves the interaction of oxyanions with holes, electrons and defects at a
material–oxide interface. To deduce the above equation, it is necessary to consider
that the oxide tends to acquire an excess of negative charge. The production of
charged defects leads to a buildup of space charge within the oxide that causes a
reduction of the oxidation growth rate for longer voltage pulse times.
In general, for a metallic surface the half-cell reaction (anode) can be described as

6.2.2 Nanolithography
The electric ﬁeld that drives the local anodic oxidation process is generated by
applying a voltage bias between the tip and the sample. The voltage can be applied
either continuously or as a pulse. In the ﬁrst case, the tip is displaced along the
sample while the voltage is switched on. In the second case, the tip rests on a ﬁxed
position of the surface during the application of the voltage pulse, then the voltage is
switched off and the tip is displaced to another position.
The value of the bias and the oxidation time depend on the material and tip’s size.
Typical ranges for voltage and oxidation times are, respectively, 5–30 V and 0.0005–
0.1 s. It is worth mentioning that most commercial AFMs might be easily trans-
formed into an o-SPL instrument [3, 6, 18].
The direct result of an o-SPL process is the generation of a thin and narrow oxide
nanostructure on a surface. Those structures might have three different function-
alities: dielectric barriers, masks for an etching process or templates. Those elements
were used either independently or in combination to generate of a large variety of
nanopatterns and nanodevices. Some examples are shown in ﬁgures 6.2 and 6.3.
Nanopatterning. The minimum feature size achieved by o-SPL was of ~6 nm.
Some examples were provided by deﬁning a tunnel junction on a Ti ﬁlm [44] and by
patterning a periodic array of silicon oxide lines with a periodicity of 15 nm [18]. The
minimum feature size seems to be limited by the tip’s size, the lateral extension of the
liquid bridge and the local oxidation kinetics [41].
A large variety of materials were patterned by o-SPL, among them, semi-
conductors (Si, GaAs, InAs, SiC), metals (Ta, Ti, Nb, or Hf), dielectric materials,
self-assembled monolayers, polymers, proteins, complex oxides, correlated oxide
materials, and two-dimensional layered materials [3, 6, 7, 18, 45–52]. The direct
writing capabilities of o-SPL found a niche of applications in the nanopatterning
single and a few layer materials such as graphene [53, 54], few layer MoS2 [55, 56],
WSe2 [57] and black phosphorus [58].
Several examples illustrated how the physical and chemical properties of the
nanopatterns were used as templates to promote the growth or ordered alignment of
materials such as organic semiconductors [59], proteins [60], nanoparticles [61–63],
block-copolymers [50] or quantum dots [64].
Nanodevices. Oxidation SPL was used to fabricate a metal-oxide transistor where
the dielectric barrier was a local oxide. This was considered the ﬁrst transistor with
truly nanometer-size features [65]. Similarly, one of the ﬁrst single-electron tran-
sistors that operated at room temperature was fabricated by using an STM tip to
Nanofabrication
6-5

[Page 241]
produce the dielectric barriers (local Ti oxides) that separated a Ti nanoscale dot
from the metal electrodes [66]. Since then, a large variety of devices such as sub-10
nm silicon nanowire FET transistors [67], optical cavities or quantum devices were
produced [3, 7, 18].
The direct and resistless features of o-SPL drew the interest of the 2D materials
community. A variety of quantum and nanoelectronic devices were fabricated based
on 2D and few-layered materials [7, 10, 18, 68–72]. Figure 6.3 illustrates the
procedure to fabricate a nanoFET channel transistor on a monolayer MoS2 [55] and
a quantum dot on graphene [69].
6.2.3 Beyond oxidation processes
The experimental set-up used in o-SPL might be easily reconﬁgured to produce other
chemical reactions or to modify the local electronic properties of a material. For
example, the chemical composition of the nanopatterns was transformed from
oxides to carbon-based materials by changing the solvent in the liquid bridge [73]. It
was also shown that the electrical properties of complex oxides heterostructures can
be reversibly switched by changing the polarity of the AFM tip during the
nanolithographic process [51, 74, 75]. Similarly, the AFM was used to control the
phase transitions at the nanoscale in strongly correlated materials such as VO2 [76].
Figure 6.3. (a) Optical image of the metallic contacts on a MoS2 ﬂake. (b) Scheme of the o-SPL fabrication of
a MoS2 FET transistor [55]. (c) AFM phase image of a MoS2 thin layer FET. The narrowest section of the
channel is 200 nm wide. The dielectric barriers prevent the current ﬂowing outside the channel. Reprinted with
permission from [55]. Copyright 2015 American Institute of Physics. (d) Scheme of the fabrication of a
graphene quantum dot; o-SPL generates very narrow graphene oxide lines that enclose a graphene quantum
dot [6]. (e) AFM image of a single quantum dot. (f) Coulomb blockade diamond of the quantum dot measured
at T = 50 mK. Panels (e) and (f) reprinted with permission from [69]. Copyright 2013 American Institute of
Physics.
Nanofabrication
6-6

[Page 242]
The above results might be important in ﬁelds such as energy conversion and
storage, catalysis or to fabricate reprogrammable electronic devices.
6.3 Thermal SPL
Thermal scanning probe lithography (t-SPL) is based on the use of heat to generate
local modiﬁcations on a polymer layer (resist). The heat applied by the tip produces
the melting and removal (evaporation) of the polymer molecules under the tip. The
resist is modiﬁed by the combined effects of mechanical forces and temperature
[6, 19, 20]. This method was proposed by John Mamin and Dan Rugar en the mid-
1990s for data storage applications [19]. Since then, t-SPL experienced a number of
evolutions [20, 30, 31, 77–79]. In some cases, the resulting patterns were made of a
material with structure and chemistry different from the original one. This t-SPL
process was called thermochemical SPL [80].
6.3.1 Key aspects of t-SPL
The patterning process is controlled by three elements, the heater (cantilever-tip
system), the heat ﬂow and the resist.
Heater. In the ﬁrst t-SPL experiments, a laser was used to heat a transparent
polymer under the AFM tip [19]. Later, the heater was integrated into the AFM
cantilever system [20, 30, 31, 77–79].
In integrated systems, the tip is resistively heated by a current ﬂowing in the
cantilever legs. The cantilever legs are highly doped except for the region where the
tip is positioned. In one design, the microcantilever (ﬁgure 6.4(a) and (b)) consists of
three highly-doped sections (legs) and a lower-doped heater region [78]. Three
voltage inputs at the base of the legs selectively power the resistors. The central leg
Figure 6.4. (a) Scheme of t-SPL [31]. Vh is the voltage applied to the heater to control the temperature at the
tip’s base; Vf is the voltage applied between the substrate and the cantilever to control the tip–polymer distance.
The region in red marks the position of the temperature sensor. (b) SEM image of a silicon cantilever designed
for t-SPL. Reproduced with permission from [78]. Copyright 2011 IOP Publishing. (c) Scheme of heat ﬂow
between the heater and the surface of the resist that is in contact with the tip. (d) Molecular structure of PPA.
The minimum temperature and the time needed to decompose PPA are indicated [78].
Nanofabrication
6-7

[Page 243]
comprises a large area platform in order to provide an additional capacitive force.
The capacitive force was applied to control the tip–surface distance and, thus, the
force exerted on the resist layer (cantilever force constant of ~1 N m−1).
Heat ﬂow. For silicon-based cantilevers, the maximum sustainable temperature at
the heater position is in the 800 °C–1000 °C range. Electro-migration of the dopants
across the legs and the type of dopants are the factors that limit the maximum
temperature. The thermal time constant of the integrated heaters was in the 5–100 μs
range [79]. Those values enabled for fast switching of the thermal stimulus. The
effective temperature at the polymer surface depends on the ratio of thermal
resistance of the substrate and of the combined resistance of the tip and tip–sample
interface [20]. For sharp tips, say with a radius of 5 nm, and a polymer layer thicker
than the lateral size of the contact, the temperature at the polymer surface might be
reduced by a factor of two with respect to the heater’s temperature (ﬁgure 6.4(c)).
Materials. The ﬁrst step in t-SPL is to pattern the resist layer. This requires the use
of highly temperature sensitive materials. The etching properties of the resist
determine the throughput and the minimum feature size of the patterning process.
Several polymer materials were studied, among them, polymethylmethacrylate
(PMMA), phenolic compounds, poly(p-phenylene) vinylene, and polyphthalalde-
hyde (PPA) [20, 81–83]. Thermally responsive PPA is the most common resist in
t-SPL because it quickly decomposes into volatile monomers when a hot tip touches
it. The process leaves a void in the resist layer whenever the tip’s temperature is
higher than the decomposition temperature of PPA ~140 °C [82]. In particular, PPA
was used for high-throughput nanopatterning because the ﬁssion of a single bond
was ampliﬁed by the spontaneous decomposition of the remaining polymer chains.
By using PPA, a 4 × 104 μm2 s−1 throughput was reached at a patterning rate of 2 μs/
pixel [78].
Imaging. In some instruments optimized for high-speed nanopatterning, the
thermal heater acted as a sensor to track the topography. Imaging was achieved
by using only electrical control without the need for an optical beam deﬂection
scheme [17]. This scheme made the t-SPL instrument more compact and faster.
However, this method is unsuitable for tracking the topography of rough surfaces.
6.3.2 Nanolithography
Thermal SPL operates by modifying a resist layer to generate a local void in the
resist. A succession of etching steps enables transferring the pattern written on the
resist to a material of interest (ﬁgure 6.5(a)). In one of the fastest t-SPL protocols
[78], for each programmed pixel, two different voltages Vh and Vf are applied for a
few microseconds to write the patterns; Vh produces the current that heats the tip
and Vf provides the force exerted on the resist by pulling the tip into contact with the
surface. The heater might sustain temperatures of about 1000 °C for several days.
During patterning, the tip is displaced across the substrate at a deﬁned distance from
its surface.
Resolution. In t-SPL it is necessary to distinguish the minimum feature written on
the polymer resist and the minimum feature size transferred to the material of
Nanofabrication
6-8

[Page 244]
interest. An array of parallel lines with a periodicity of 20 nm was generated in PPA
while Si nanowires were patterned with a width of 10 nm [84]. The minimum feature
size transferred to a material is limited by the thickness of the polymer layer which
for PPA is about 6–7 nm. This value is a consequence of the low etch resistance of
the PPA.
Greyscale nanopatterning. A singular feature of t-SPL is the capability to generate
nanopatterns of different depths in the resist. Those changes might be transferred to
a silicon substrate by using an etching protocol. The control of height comes from
the reproducible motion of the cantilever, the applied force and the stability of the
heater temperature. Those features enabled the generation of three-dimensional
greyscale height nanopatterns [6, 31, 82]. The patterning depth in a PPA polymer
layer was controlled to about 1 nm, this is, less than the linear size of a single resist
molecule. Figure 6.5(b) shows a greyscale pattern of a portrait of R Feynman who in
1959 wrote a visionary essay about nanotechnology. The same method was applied
to fabricate nanoﬂuidic ratchet-shaped Brownian motors [85].
Nanopatterning. Detailed descriptions of the nanopatterning capabilities of t-SPL
might be found elsewhere [20, 86]. Let’s underline some representative results.
Figure 6.5. t-SPL nanopatterns and devices. (a) Main steps in the fabrication process of metal contacts to a 2D
materials ﬂake [95]. (b) Greyscale patterning of a photograph of Richard Feynman. Adapted with permission
from [6]. Copyright 2014 Springer Nature. The original portrait (not shown) belongs to the Archives,
California Institute of Technology. (c) Topographical image of a fractal pattern made of 880 × 880 pixels
written in 12.8 s. Adapted with permission from [78]. Copyright IOP Publishing. (d) Optical image of a 2D
material FET device. Scale bar, 5 μm. (e) Optical image of a top-gate contact fabricated on an exfoliated h-BN
ﬂake. Scale bar, 4 μm. Panels (d) and (e) adapted with permission from [95]. Copyright 2019 Springer Nature.
Nanofabrication
6-9

[Page 245]
Thermal SPL achieved the fastest nanopatterning application of SPL. The pattern
shown in ﬁgure 6.5(c) contains 880 × 880 pixels. It was written in less than 12.8 s [78].
A combination of greyscale nanopatterning and template growth was applied to
control the density of amine groups on a polymer surface. By attaching ﬂuorescence
labels on the amines, the resulting pattern could be imaged by ﬂuorescence micro-
scopy [87]. Thermal SPL was used to deprotect active groups such as carboxylic acid
and amine groups which can subsequently be used for biochemical conjugation of
nano-objects. Multifunctional patterns of proteins, DNA and C60 were obtained
[88, 89] as well as guided assembly of block copolymers [90] and gold nanorods [91].
PPA surfaces patterned by t-SPL were used to build artiﬁcial models to mimic the
tissue environment [92].
Nanodevices. Hot probes were applied to promote the thermal reduction of
graphene oxide and graphene ﬂuoride layers. Sub-20 nm wide nanoribbons of reduced
graphene oxide were fabricated as elements of a FET [93]. The process enabled tuning
the conductivity of the nanoribbons over four orders of magnitude. Similarly, the
locality of the thermal interaction was used to transform pentacene precursors into a
semiconducting pentacene nanostructured transistor [94] or to pattern metal contacts
on monolayer 2D materials [95]. Other devices fabricated by t-SPL include magnonic
structures [96] or supramolecular polymer-based color-switchable QR codes [97]. It is
worth mentioning that in some of the above examples [93], the direct thermal
interaction of the hot probe with a material generates functionality. In other words,
an etching process is not always needed to reach device functionality.
6.4 Deposition SPL
Deposition SPL is based on the controlled delivery of a material from the tip to the
surface [4, 21, 34, 98, 99] (ﬁgure 6.6). Dip-pen nanolithography and nanoscale
dispensing were the ﬁrst approaches meeting the above deﬁnition. Nanoscale
dispensing uses hollow cantilevers integrated with ﬂuidic channels to deliver small
liquid drops onto a surface [99–101]. This method has yet to reach the sub-100 nm
patterning resolution. For that reason, nanoscale dispensing methods will not be
discussed in the chapter.
Deposition SPL (d-SPL) provides a straightforward approach to transform the
chemical and biological functionality of surfaces at the 100 nm range. The ﬁrst
deposition of organic molecules (octadecanethiols) from an AFM tip onto a surface
(mica) dates back to 1995 [102]. This process was comprehensively developed for
nanopatterning purposes under the name of dip-pen nanolithography (DPN) [4, 34].
The use of liquid inks and polymer-based materials [21, 103, 104] expanded the
capabilities initially envisioned by DPN [4]. For that reason, we prefer to use the
term d-SPL to include all the probe-based methods based on the controlled transfer
of material previously deposited on a tip to the sample surface.
6.4.1 Key aspects of deposition SPL
Molecular and liquid inks. Deposition SPL was performed by using a variety of inks,
including organic and biological molecules, polymers, colloidal particles, and metal
Nanofabrication
6-10

[Page 246]
ions [4, 21, 34, 98, 104–106]. The inks might be divided into diffusive and liquid inks.
Diffusive inks are soluble in water. Liquid inks are polymer-based such as molten
polyethylene, polysterne, PMMA or polyelectrolyte complexes. Solid inks include a
sub-category of liquid inks. The deposition of liquid and, in particular of solid inks,
is performed with a heated tip to promote the ﬂow of the polymer from the tip to the
surface [21].
Ink transport. The transport of the ink molecules from the tip to the surface
depends on the type of ink. In the case of diffusive inks, the transport process might
be divided into three steps: dissolution of the ink molecules in the water, motion of
the ink molecules in the water meniscus formed between the tip and surface and
assembly of the molecules on the surface through chemical reactions [107, 108].
The transport of liquid inks from the tip to the surface is rather complex. It
depends on the ink composition, viscosity, surface tension, temperature, volume of
the ink on the tip and interfacial energy [109]. The tip temperature can be used to
control the ink deposition. The rise of the temperature of the tip causes solid ink to
melt and wet the tip [110]. The advantage of this approach is two-fold. The ink ﬂow
can be turned on and off by changing the temperature. This is a major difference
with respect to other d-SPL methods where the ink deposition happens continuously
as long as the tip stays in contact with it. In addition, the temperature provides a
mechanism to control the rate of the ink diffusion.
Figure 6.6. Deposition SPL methods. (a) Top, dip-pen nanolithography scheme [4]. Bottom, self-assembled
monolayers written on a Au(111) surface. Bottom panel reproduced with permission from [111]. Copyright
1999 AAAS. (b) Top, d-SPL scheme by using a heated tip to deposit a solid ink onto a 2D materials surface.
Bottom, SEM image of a 36 nm wide monolayer MoS2 nanoribbon device. Adapted with permission form
[112]. Copyright 2019 American Chemical Society. (c) Top, scheme for delivering materials layer-by-layer in
3D nanoprinting. Bottom, AFM image of a square pyramid made of 15 layers. Bottom panel reproduced with
permission from [22]. Copyright 2016 American Chemical Society.
Nanofabrication
6-11

[Page 247]
Resolution. The feature size depends on the balance between the ﬂow from the tip,
deposition time and surface diffusion. For diffusive inks sub-20 nm feature sizes
were reported [111]. For liquid and solid inks, the feature size might be inﬂuenced by
several factors among them the size of the liquid meniscus, temperature and
deposition time [109]. Feature sizes in the sub-50 nm range were claimed [112].
6.4.2 Nanolithography
Deposition SPL functions by facilitating the direct transport of molecules to
surfaces, much like the transfer of ink from a macroscopic dip-pen to paper. With
independence of the type of ink, d-SPL follows three major steps: ﬁrst, the tip is
loaded with the ink; secondly, the ink is transferred to a pre-selected position of the
substrate. For liquid and, especially solid inks, this step is facilitated by using hot
probes; in a third step, the deposited ink is processed to reach a determined
functionality. This last step might be divided into several sub-steps.
Nanopatterning. Deposition SPL enables generating a range of chemical func-
tionalities on the same substrate by depositing different kinds of molecules. Some
examples of molecules deposited by d-SPL include oligonucleotides [105], nano-
particles [113] or block-copolymers [114]. The use of polymer-based inks has
enhanced the patterning and device applications. It has been shown that poly
(N-isopropylacrylamide), a type of protein adhesion molecules, can be reproducibly
written from the melt [110]. These nanostructures reversibly bind and release
proteins when actuated through a hydrophilic–hydrophobic phase transition. A
layer-by-layer process led to a 3D nanoprinting approach [22]. The local deposition
of resists led to the fabrication of different devices such as nanomechanical
resonators [115], microﬂuidic channels and 2D materials nanoribbon transistors
[112, 116].
6.5 Other SPL methods
Voltage, mechanical or chemical interactions led to other methods of patterning
surfaces [3–10, 24, 32]. Those approaches have yet to become widely used or to reach
a common set of protocols. For those reasons, they have not been explicitly
described in this chapter. It is worth realizing that some SPL-based approaches
might combine two or more interactions. For example, in t-SPL a mechanical force
is always applied to deform the resist while in o-SPL electric ﬁelds and chemical
reactions co-exist. Let’s provide a brief overview of bias, mechanical and catalytic
SPL.
Mechanical SPL or nanomachining (m-SPL) uses the mechanical force exerted by
the tip to produce the selective removal of material from a surface. Almost any
AFM could be ﬁtted to perform nanolithographic applications by controlling the
force. For this reason, m-SPL was widely applied to modify materials [24, 32] such
as self-assembled monolayers [23, 32], polymers [25], proteins [32, 117], supra-
molecular ﬁlms [118] or even metallic ﬁlms [119]. The tip’s lifetime is a major issue in
m-SPL. The mechanical force applied to modify the material might eventually wear
or contaminate the tip.
Nanofabrication
6-12

[Page 248]
Bias SPL. A great variety of probe-based nanopatterning methods beyond o-SPL
were based on the application of a voltage bias between tip and surface. The tip’s
proximity and the application of a voltage generates very high electric ﬁelds (high
electric ﬁelds ~10 V nm−1 or 10 GV m−1). Those ﬁelds were used to induce the
desorption of atoms from the tip and to deposit them on the sample [26, 27, 120] or
to decompose the gas molecules surrounding the tip [28, 121]. In other examples, the
electronic current produced by the voltage bias applied between the tip and a
conductive substrate was used to break molecular bonds of a polymer layer or soft
material deposited on the substrate [29, 122–124]. The electric ﬁeld at the tip–surface
interface was controlled to invert the polarization of a small region in a ferroelectric
ﬁlm. This generated a nonvolatile ferroelectric domain. This mechanism was
proposed for data storage [125].
Oxidation SPL might be the best example of using electrochemical reactions for
nanopatterning but other electrochemical processes were also explored by probe-
based patterning methods [3, 8, 73, 126–128].
Catalytic SPL. The deposition of catalysts in the AFM tip enabled performing
and controlling the spatial extension of catalytic reactions [129]. Examples of
catalytic reactions using AFM tips included transition metal coatings, metal
complexes, organic catalysts, photocatalysts or enzymes. A nanodevice application
of c-SPL was the reduction of insulating graphene oxide in the presence of hydrogen.
Nanoribbons with widths ranging from 20 to 80 nm were fabricated [130].
The potential of bias SPL goes beyond the ﬁeld of nanolithography. The method
has been applied to understand new processes to decompose very stable chemical
species such as carbon dioxide [129], to decompose organic solvents [131, 132] or to
study the diffusion of metal ions [126].
6.6 End-of-chapter summary
A scanning probe microscope interface provides a wealth of physical and chemical
approaches to modify a surface with nanometer-scale precision. Scanning probe
lithography methods have drawn the attention of the nanoscience and nanotechnol-
ogy communities for two main reasons. First, they provide relatively inexpensive
routes to pattern surfaces or devices with nanoscale precision. Secondly, SPL enables
studying materials and processes at the nanoscale with an ease that is unparalleled.
Several SPL approaches have evolved to become widely applied for nanopattern-
ing and nanodevice fabrication. Thus, oxidation, thermal and deposition processes
are commonly applied in scientiﬁc laboratories for designing novel surfaces and
prototyping nanodevices. Table 6.1 summarizes the ﬁgures of merit of these
techniques in terms of minimum feature size and single-dot patterning time. The
three methods demonstrated a very high degree of repeatability by generating well-
deﬁned structures and patterns with feature sizes in the sub-50 nm range. Oxidation
SPL has generated arrays of silicon oxide lines with a periodicity of 15 nm [18].
Almost the same periodicities were obtained by t-SPL while patterning arrays of
silicon nanowires and slightly higher values by d-SPL while patterning SAM
monolayers [111]. Larger differences are observed in the patterning time of a single
Nanofabrication
6-13

[Page 249]
dot. Thermal SPL can operate in the μs range while o-SPL operates in the
submillisecond range and d-SPL might operate in the subsecond range.
The development of user-friendly AFMs and powerful software tools to control
the tip’s displacement together with the availability of AFMs will ensure the use of
SPL by the scientiﬁc community for years to come. The transformation of SPL from
a scientiﬁc tool to a technology for large-scale nanomanufacturing seems far at best.
The sequential character of scanning probe microscopy and the relatively short
lifetime of an AFM tip imposes limits to the size of the patterned areas and the
throughput. Critical breakthroughs are needed to bring the SPL technology closer to
large scale (~cm2) nanolithography applications.
6.7 Student’s corner
• Which of the following SPL methods do pattern a resist in the ﬁrst
nanolithographic step?
A. oxidation SPL;
B. thermal SPL;
C. deposition SPL;
D. dip-pen nanolithography.
• Select from the list the factors that inﬂuence the feature size in oxidation SPL.
A. the tip’s radius;
B. the temperature of the tip;
C. the applied voltage;
D. the thickness of the resist.
• What are the functionalities of the oxide structures fabricated by o-SPL?
• (a) Describe thermal scanning probe lithography. (b) Mention the dominant
factors that control the feature size and (c) its order of magnitude (1, 10, 100
or 1000 nm?).
• Dip-pen nanolithography is based on the deposition of diffusive inks on a
surface. Describe the main steps of the transport process of diffusive inks.
• Provide an estimation of the electric ﬁeld generated by applying 10 V for a tip
that is placed 1 nm above a gold surface? Is this ﬁeld larger or smaller than
the average electric ﬁelds applied in electron beam lithography?

• Design a micro-optical tag made of multi-layered MoS2 that alternates blue,
