Nanofabrication
Nanolithography techniques and their applications
Jose´ Marı´a De Teresa
Chapter 7
Soft thermal nanoimprint and hybrid processes
to produce complex structures
Isabel Rodríguez and Jaime Hernández
This chapter is particularly dedicated to illustrating soft thermal nanoimprint
lithography (T-NIL) and hybrid processes as enabling technology to create complex
surface nanoarchitectures such as high aspect ratio and hierarchical micro and
nanostructures with high ﬁdelity and resolution in a cost effective manner. The
ability to produce complex surface nanoarchitectures has opened up new possibil-
ities to create a large variety of functional and multifunctional nanostructured
surfaces that can provide materials with exceptional properties beyond those
expected from the bulk material itself, and very often, they are inspired from
surface structures found in nature. In the ﬁrst part of the chapter, the basic NIL
processes are introduced. Then, a comprehensive description of the basic tools and
processes including the preparation of soft molds is given. In the second part of the
chapter, the main processing challenges concerning the production of high aspect
ratio and multilevel hierarchical structures using soft T-NIL are described. Finally,
some of the most efﬁcient methodologies to produce multiscale hierarchical surface
structures with high precision using soft T-NIL in combination with other
technologies are reviewed.
7.1 Fundamentals of nanoimprint processes
7.1.1 Introduction
Nanoimprint lithography (NIL) is a high-resolution patterning technology which,
after intense development in the last 25 years, has evolved to be one of the most
resourceful and versatile technologies for nanofabrication and nanomanufacturing
of products integrating nanoscale features [1, 2]. The fundamental principles
underlying the technology, the materials and processing methodology of the current
state of the art of NIL processes have been well documented by a vast number of
publications and books [3–6]. The main applications enabled by NIL have also been
doi:10.1088/978-0-7503-2608-7ch7
7-1
ª IOP Publishing Ltd 2020. All rights,
including for text and data mining (TDM), artificial intelligence (AI) training, and similar technologies, are reserved.

[Page 259]
reviewed [7–9]. NIL is indeed a replication technology for mechanically molding or
embossing onto a polymeric material by means of pressure exerted through a mold
whose patterns are then transferred to the polymer material and ﬁxed either by
heating and cooling or UV light crosslinking.
The practical worth of NIL derives from several distinct advantages over other
lithography methods. First and foremost is its inherent simplicity, only involving a
mold patterned with the features to transfer and a suitable ﬂowable resist material. It
only requires a simple instrumentation that does not imply expensive optical or high
vacuum components. Due to its purely mechanical nature, NIL offers a high
resolution not limited by light diffraction or beam scattering like traditional
photolithographic processes. In fact, NIL offers almost unlimited pattern resolution
that is basically decided by the availability of the mold. A pattern resolution below
10 nm was realized since the early developments of NIL [10] and, recently, atomic
scale resolution has also been demonstrated [11].
Nanoimprint is a parallel processing technology that can provide a high throughput
at nanoscale resolution. Additionally, through roll-to-roll (R2R) or roll-to-plate (R2P)
techniques, the processing capabilities of NIL can be up-scaled to produce nano-
patterns in a continuous fashion. The technology can be applied to a large variety of
materials, generating functional patterns in almost any kind of substrate, extending
the applicability of nanoimprint technology enormously. Because NIL is a cost
effective and versatile lithographic tool offering high performance in terms of both
resolution and throughput, it has emerged as one of the most promising technologies
for the fabrication and commercial production of nano-enabled products.
Nanoimprinting was born as a lithographic technique and was proposed
originally as a high resolution technique for pattern transfer from a mold to a
substrate for subsequent post-processing. In the early times, a thermoplastic polymer
was employed as a resist material. Thus, the technology was referred to as thermal
nanoimprint lithography (T-NIL) [12, 13]. Subsequently, UV-curable resins were
introduced as resist material, giving rise to UV-NIL whereby the structures are ﬁxed
via photopolymerization or crosslinking.
Due to the material patterning versatility of NIL, the technology soon evolved
from being a lithographic pattern transfer technology to being a technology to create
functional nanostructured surfaces where the imprinted structures themselves
remain as active components providing for functionality. Hence, because of simple
processing and large choice of material options, NIL has become a nanofabrication
technology ideally suited for applications beyond microelectronics. NIL has
demonstrated to be particularly useful to produce functional nanopatterns in areas
such as polymer nano-optics and photonics [14, 15], biomimetic functional surfaces
[16–18] and biological and tissue culture applications [19].
7.1.2 Basic nanoimprint processes and variants
The NIL processes have evolved into different approaches and these can be broadly
classiﬁed into two technologies: T-NIL or UV-NIL. Figure 7.1 shows schematics of
the general processing steps involved.
Nanofabrication
7-2

[Page 260]
In T-NIL, a thermoplastic thin polymer ﬁlm is ﬁrst produced by spin casting a
solution of the polymer. Alternatively, a free standing polymeric ﬁlm can be used if
required. The ﬁlm is pressed and imprinted with a mold under elevated temperature.
This process has been also referred to as nano hot embossing whereby the thermo-
plastic polymer is mechanically deformed by the mold under pressure at an elevated
temperature. In practice, this temperature is set at about 50 °C–90 °C above the
polymer’s glass transition temperature (Tg) so that the polymer reaches the viscous
ﬂow state. The typical pressure employed is within the range of 10–50 bar depending
primarily on the pattern resolution and geometry of the cavities to ﬁll in the mold [3].
After the process, the mold-substrate assembly is cooled down below the Tg,
where the polymer recovers its hard elastic, glassy properties and then separated
revealing the replica of the imprinted mold nanopattern on the thermoplastic
polymer ﬁlm.
In UV-NIL, a liquid UV sensitive resin (formulation of monomers and photo
initiators) is deposited by spin coating on a suitable substrate. Then the mold is
brought into contact with the substrate and a low to moderate pressure (0–5 bar) is
applied to ﬁll the mold cavities. In this case, the mold or the substrate needs to be
UV transparent so that by illumination through, it is possible to induce crosslinking
and hardening of the resin. Since UV-NIL is performed using low-viscosity resins, it
can be realized at room temperature and at low pressures allowing for a much faster
processing and as a result, the imprint throughput can be signiﬁcantly larger than
that of the more traditional T-NIL.
In both processes, a so-called thin residual polymer layer is left underneath the
mold relief features. For lithographic applications where post-processing is
Figure 7.1. Schematic illustration of standard thermal nanoimprint lithography (T-NIL) and UV nanoimprint
lithography (UV-NIL).
Nanofabrication
7-3

[Page 261]
required to transfer the pattern into the underlying substrate, this residual layer
needs to be removed to have the substrate exposed. This is typically accomplished
by a short anisotropic oxygen plasma treatment to dry etch the polymeric material
[3]. The thickness of this residual layer depends upon the initial ﬁlm thickness of
the resist and the size of the features to pattern. To minimize this layer it is
necessary to apply the right amount of material since too thick initial layers lead to
large residual layers [20]. However, too thin applied layers are more difﬁcult to
pattern, particularly when using hard mold and or substrates because these are
more prone to wear or damage. The residual layer etching step is obviated when
NIL is directed to create functional topographical patterns. In this case, NIL is
indeed ideal to pattern directly the surface of polymeric materials in the form of
free-standing ﬁlms.
Initially, hard molds made in silicon, quartz or nickel were used for the NIL
process. However, hard molds have several drawbacks such that they do not adapt
well to the surface of another substrate and often make a poor conformal contact
[21]. This has a number of implications on the ﬁdelity of the pattern transfer and on
the number of imprint defects on the patterned area, including poor uniformity and
defects due to particles that propagate to a large extent within the substrate.
Additionally, nonconforming molds undergo a signiﬁcant mechanical stress under
the applied pressure which translates into a high mold wearing and breaking rate
with the resulting associated high cost. An additional source of defects arises from
air trapped in the gaps between resist and mold cavities due to poor gas dissolution
within the imprint materials causing defects such as air bubbles on the imprinted
features [22, 23].
In addressing these challenges, new methodologies have appeared allowing the
expansion of NIL as a versatile nanopatterning tool. These can be categorized
broadly in two groups including those that rely on step-and-repeat imprint
approaches and those that make use of a soft mold allowing for conformal contact
over large substrate areas.
The ﬁrst approach relies on the use of a small area mold that is repeatedly
imprinted by the relative displacement of the substrate over the mold to ﬁnally
pattern a large area substrate. This technology was termed initially step and ﬂash
imprint lithography (S-FIL™) and is a form of UV-NIL process where low-viscosity
UV-curable resist is dispensed sequentially ﬁeld by ﬁeld using an inkjet process and
cured by a UV light while a transparent template is then pressed into the resist, and
this process is repeated in a step-and-repeat fashion. The technology has been
termed more recently jet and ﬂash imprint lithography (J-FIL) as it uses inkjet
techniques to dispense variable picoliter volumes of low-viscosity UV curable resists,
matching pattern size variations on the template [24]. J-FIL steppers have made
noteworthy improvements in reducing defectivity in NIL by making use of
rectangular (∼26 mm × 33 mm) bendable molds that initiate contact to the substrate
at the middle avoiding trapping gas bubbles. Furthermore, J-FIL steppers dispense
well distributed and with high precision a resin volume according to the volume of
the mold cavities to imprint. This technology was devised at the University of Texas
and developed for semiconductor fabrication by Molecular Imprints Inc. Now, the
Nanofabrication
7-4

[Page 262]
technology is part of Canon Corporation and it is likely to lead the commercial
fabrication of cutting-edge semiconductor integrated circuits (ICs) [25].
A second approach to improve upon the conformal contact relies on the use of
soft molds made of plastics or elastomers ensuring conformal contact with the
surface of the substrate even on curved or rough substrates. The concept of soft
lithography using ﬂexible poly(dimethylsiloxanes) (PDMS) molds was implemented
into NIL processes through the use of molds made of an elastomeric material like
PDMS or other polymers like ﬂuoropolymers, polyurethanes or inorganic–organic
hybrid polymers. However, PDMS is by far the most widely utilized soft material.
Polymeric soft molds are typically permeable, particularly at high temperature,
which advantageously helps to eliminate the trapped air bubbles in the resist layer
and in addition, facilitate diffusion and elimination of the resin solvent through the
mold. Due to the ﬂexibility of the elastomeric stamp, it ensures a perfect conformal
assembly onto the substrate even with curved or ﬂexible substrates, thus allowing
high ﬁdelity replication on areas up to several tens of cm2. Moreover, polymeric soft
molds allow reducing the cost of the mold substantially as the expensive master can
be replicated easily by soft lithography while it is preserved.
Soft UV-NIL is considered today the most promising NIL variant to replicate
patterns in the sub-50 nm range for mass production at low cost. In industry settings,
some variations of soft UV-NIL have been developed, these include the substrate
conformal imprint lithography (SCIL) developed by Philips and SUSS [26] and the
Soft SmartNIL® developed by EVG [27]. In SCIL, a ﬂexible composite mold (made
of two layers of PDMS of different modulus, soft and hard) is mounted on a
bendable glass carrier and kept in place by vacuum applied through an array of
grooves on the carrier. Wafer-scale imprints are made using the SCIL tool to execute
a sequential imprinting process by the stepwise release of the vacuum in the grooves,
causing the progressive contact of the ﬂexible mold with a wafer substrate which is
coated with a thin resist layer. After the imprinted resist is cured by UV exposure,
separation of the mold from the substrate is performed by switching on the vacuum
in the grooves in reverse sequence to the conformal imprint process.
The Soft SmartNIL technology uses a conformal bilayer polymer stamp on a
transparent polyethylene (PET) carrier that is pressed by a roll onto a UV-resin
coated substrate, allowing for automated sequential molding and demolding [28].
These new developments in UV-NIL have allowed the technology to meet the
industry manufacturing requirements and therefore, are gradually being adopted by
industry. The J-FIL is the process primarily implemented in semiconductor IC
manufacturing [24, 29]. The Soft UV-NIL modalities are currently being applied as
patterning tool in application ﬁelds like ﬂexible electronics [30], photovoltaics [28,
31], photonics [15, 32], photonic crystals in LEDs [33] and ﬂexible displays [34]. The
T-NIL is being used primarily in the fabrication of metallic nanoplasmonic devices
[35–37] and in emerging application such as bioinspired surfaces [16] or biotechnol-
ogy applications [38, 39].
This chapter is particularly dedicated to illustrate soft T-NIL processes, discus-
sing the critical process issues to produce complex architectures such as high aspect
ratio and hierarchical nano and micro patterns as functional topographical surfaces.
Nanofabrication
7-5

[Page 263]
7.2 Soft thermal nanoimprint lithography (soft T-NIL)
7.2.1 Thermoplastic polymers for soft T-NIL
The proper selection of the thermoplastic polymer material for an imprinting task is
important as its properties determine to a large extent the selection of process
parameters such as processing time, temperature, pressure and also inﬂuence the
outcome of the process in terms of pattern ﬁdelity and stability.
In table 7.1, the main thermoplastic polymers and their properties with relevance
in soft T-NIL processing are collected. These properties will be referred to
throughout the chapter as the optimization of the T-NIL processing parameters is
discussed.
7.2.2 Molds for soft T-NIL
Molds are an essential component in NIL and they are the decisive element for
resolution, uniformity and reproducibility. Since NIL is a contact technique, the
quality of the imprint directly depends on the quality of the mold. The fabrication of
the original master molds with well-deﬁned features at the micro- and sub-micro-
metric scales is carried out by direct writing lithographic techniques, including the
established silicon based clean room technologies such as UV and electron beam
lithography
(EBL)
and
more
recently
direct
laser
writing
by
two-photon
polymerization.
7.2.2.1 Mold fabrication techniques
Traditional master molds have been originated in silicon using deep UV lithography
and electron beam lithography (EBL). Indeed, EBL is already an established
technology for patterning at the nanometer range with high precision (see chapter 3
in this book). However, EBL has limited applicability when large area or high
throughput patterning is required. In this case, other technologies such as deep UV
lithography (at 248 nm light wavelength), immersion optical lithography (at 193 nm)
and an extreme UV lithography (light at a wavelength of 13.5 nm) have been utilized
[47]. However, these technologies require advanced and costly equipment and their
utilization is for the most part limited to the semiconductor industry (see chapters 1
and 2 in this book).
After the lithographic process step, the pattern produced is normally transferred
to the silicon wafer substrate by a reactive ion etching process. Traditionally, in
order to create deep nanostructures with vertical side walls, the Bosch process has
been employed. This is a two-step process of etching and passivation using sulfur
hexaﬂuoride (SF6) and octaﬂuorocyclobutane (C4F8) gases, respectively. The
process enables the generation of structures with aspect ratio up to 100:1.
However, this process usually produces scalloping of the side walls that increases
the side wall friction hampering the process of demolding in NIL [48]. This problem
can be improved using the silicon cryo-etch process, (see chapter 3 in this book),
which today has emerged as an alternative process for high aspect ratio silicon
etching [49]. The cryo-etch is a continuous process that uses SF6 and O2 gases at low
Nanofabrication
7-6

[Page 264]
temperatures (typically below −100 °C) and allows for the formation of a
passivation layer of SiOxFy and ﬂuorine radicals for the etching action. The
technique produces somewhat lower aspect ratio structures with typical aspect ratio
about 50:1, depending on the process.
Direct laser writing by two-photon polymerization (2PP-DLW), with resolution
down to 100 nm (see chapter 2 in this book), is emerging as a new and exceptionally
versatile technique that allows for preparing high aspect ratio 3D structures with
high complexity to serve as master stamps [50, 51]. Compared to EBL, 2PP-DLW
can achieve higher writing speed and a larger working area of up to several
centimetres [52]. This combined with the ability to directly form 3D structures,
makes 2PP-DLW a remarkable technique for rapid mastering of complex structures.
The master structures can then be replicated via soft lithography by PDMS into
working molds allowing for repeatable nanoimprint processing. Hence, the combi-
nation of 2PP DWL and NIL is particularly advantageous as it combines great
ﬂexibility in terms of structural design together with the scalability of NIL.
A concern to take into account when using molds replicated from masters created
with 2PP-DWL is the lateral wall roughness due to the laser scanning trajectories
and layering build up. This issue has been solved or minimized by H Schift and co-
workers by the process termed TASTE (temperature activated selective thermal
topography equilibration) [53]. TASTE is a process suitable for thermoplastic
imprinted materials and it involves the application of post-processing steps to
smoothen the surface [54, 55]. This includes the radiation of the surface with vacuum
UV (VUV) light at a wavelength of 172 nm emitted from an excimer lamp which

causes polymer chain scission reactions and consequently the reduction of the
molecular weight of the polymer at the outermost surface only (up to 400 nm). This
allows for decreasing the glass transition temperature compared to the bulk region,
and by thermal heating, a selective polymer reﬂow of the top exposed surface can be
achieved with the consequent smoothening effect while the bulk and overall
geometry is unaffected. The process allows reducing the roughness from about
400 nm to sub-10 nm values while preserving the sub-micrometric structural details
[53]. The smoothening effect obtained on micro-prism PMMA structures can be
observed in ﬁgure 7.2, before and after post-processing by TASTE.
7.2.2.2 Coating technologies for molds and anti-sticking treatments
An essential requirement for molds employed in NIL is to have a low surface energy
to allow for easy demolding of the imprinted polymer structures after the process.
This is critical to accomplish a good imprint quality without structural defects and
also to protect the mold from failure while increasing its life time. During the
demolding stage, the imprinted polymer is subjected to several forces as it is
detached from the mold including the friction force, the adhesion force, and the
thermal contraction force caused by the different shrinkage coefﬁcients between the
imprinted polymers and the mold material. As a result of these forces acting on both
the polymer and mold during demolding, fracture, elongation, or structural
deformation of imprinted structures may take place.
These issues can be readily improved by anti-adhesive coatings that lead to the
reduction of surface energy and the associated interfacial friction arising when the
mold and polymer replica are being separated. Fluoroalkyl trichlorosilanes with
different carbon chain lengths are the most commonly used anti-adhesive layers
because of their low surface energy, high surface reactivity, and high resistance
Figure 7.2. SEM images of a 5 × 5 micro-prism array imprinted in PMMA and obtained from a 2PP-DLW
original master mold (a) before post-processing and (b) after VUV exposure and thermal reﬂow of the surface
selectively modiﬁed (overview and close-up: scale bar 10 μm). Reproduced from [53] with the permission of
SPIE.
Nanofabrication
7-8

[Page 266]
against temperature and pressure [56]. The most popular ﬂuorosilanes employed
in NIL are 1H,1H,2H,2H-perﬂuorodecyltrichlorosilane (FDTS) and 1H,1H,2H,
2H-perﬂuorooctyl-trichlorosilane (FOCTS).
The anti-adhesive functionalization is achieved through the process known as
silanization whereby silane functional compounds form a robust Si–O covalent
linkage with a surface that is rich in hydroxyl groups. This process results in the
formation of a monolayer that acts as a coupling agent for the ﬂuorocarbon chains
turning the surface into highly hydrophobic. Trichlorosilanes have been commonly
utilized to modify glass, silica, alumina, titania, and other metal oxide surfaces
which are rich in hydroxyl groups. For polymer surfaces, the silanization process can
be enhanced via oxygen plasma treatment for the introduction of hydroxyl groups
on the polymer surface which are the anchor points for covalent grafting of
trichlorosilane molecules.
The silanization process is best carried out by chemical vapour deposition (CVD)
because vapors are more efﬁcient than liquids in penetrating into the nanoscale gaps
of a mold, producing a more effective coverage. CVD with silanes can be carried out
at ambient temperature under a moderate vacuum. In a typical process, prior to
coating, molds are exposed to gentle low power oxygen plasma (20 W, 30 s) and
immediately after, they are placed into a vacuum chamber (100 Pa) whereby the
liquid silane evaporates and reacts with the mold surface. Typically, the deposition
takes about 2 h, after which the molds are heated upto 120 °C for in-plane silane
crosslinking.
Schift and co-workers [57] developed a silane coating process using mixtures of
mono- and trichlorosilanes that allowed for higher molecular packing of the
alkylﬂuorochains self-assembled on the surface while the roughness caused by cross
polymerization between silane molecules was reduced. This surface coating produc-
ing higher density of ﬂuoroalkyl molecules, showed improved anti-adhesive
properties.
7.2.2.3 Flexible soft molds based on PDMS
The use of PDMS has been widely popularized through the development of the soft-
lithography family of patterning techniques [58]. PDMS, typically referred to as the
commercial thermocured siloxane polymer Sylgard 184 (Dow), is today the most
widely used polymer as soft-mold material in NIL due to a number of reasons, but
particularly for providing high ﬁdelity replication, easy processing and low cost.
PDMS, also called s-PDMS, is an elastomeric material with low Young’s modulus
(∼2.0 MPa) and a low surface energy of 25 mJ m−2. This allows for conformal
contact to non-planar surfaces and easy release from both the master mold or from
the imprinted patterns, generally without the need for anti-sticking agents as
required for rigid molds. PDMS holds a fairly high toughness with a high elongation
at break (>150%) which allows for signiﬁcant deformation before failure during
demolding. It is also highly transparent in the visible and in the UV spectrum being
suitable for UV-NIL photocuring based processes. A critical advantage for NIL is
its high gas permeability, which allows for the efﬁcient elimination of the air trapped
within the soft mold and the imprinted substrate through the soft mold itself.
Nanofabrication
7-9

[Page 267]
The common steps to produce PDMS soft molds by soft lithography or replica
molding to form a master are shown in ﬁgure 7.3. Conventional PDMS molds are
produced by ﬁrst mixing the siloxane oligomers and crosslinking agent in a
proportion of 10:1. The mixture is then degassed in a low pressure vacuum chamber
and casted over a master template. PDMS can be cured over the master at 80 °C for
2 h. However, a longer curing time of 24 h at a temperature of 60 °C is recommended
in order to allow for spontaneous degassing and to avoid tension build-up due to
thermal shrinkage. Cured PDMS can be thermally aged to reduce the low molecular
weight chains through a higher temperature treatment at 120 °C for 60 min. Both the
curing thermal treatment and the crosslinker to pre-polymer ratio inﬂuence the
mechanical properties of a PDMS. Thus, they should be controlled precisely for
reproducible and repeatable imprint results. The stiffness of PDMS can be slightly
modulated by varying the crosslinker-to-PDMS base ratio over the range 19:1 to 2:1,
and by varying the curing temperature. Within two extreme curing processes, i.e.,
120 min at 100 °C and 2 d at 165 °C, it was possible to vary the stiffness from
800 kPa to 10 MPa [59].
However, conventional soft PDMS (sPDMS) has two important shortcomings
when it comes to replicating nanofeatures of small size and/or high aspect ratio.
Figure 7.3. Schematic illustration of the process to make a soft mold of PDMS from an initial master mold
involving the steps of imprinting the pattern features on an intermediate polymer substrate (IPS) that serves as
template to cast PDMS and produce a replica mold of the original master. On the right, the PDMS replica
mold is used for T-NIL process on a free standing thermoplastic polymer.
Nanofabrication
7-10

[Page 268]
The ﬁrst drawback is its high viscosity of 3900 mPa s. With such high viscous ﬂuid, it
is not possible to ﬁll nanocavities below 200 nm and consequently, it will not be
suitable for mold replication below these dimensions. Secondly, sPDMS has a very
low Young’s modulus which often limits the replication of features with high aspect
ratio below 500 nm since these sorts of features will be very prone to deformation or
collapse. Both these two issues have been tackled by using crosslinking formulations
with variable proportions of vinyl and hydrosilane pre-polymers in the polymer-
ization mixture. In the pioneer work by Schmid and Michel at IBM [60], they
showed that hydrosilane-to-vinyl ratio in the polymerization mixture determines the
degree of crosslinking of the polymer and as a result, the hardness. They also
provided some guidelines and formulation examples on how to manipulate the pre-
polymer ratios to tune the hardness of the resulting material as desired. These
formulations have a low viscosity in their precursor state, which facilitates an
accurate replication of sub-500 nm master structures. In addition, the formulation
can be tuned to provide for different hardness according to the dimensions of the
feature to replicate. Formulation examples employed in our lab are shown in
table 7.2 with high (hPDMS) and mid-range hardness (mPDMS).
One needs to bear in mind that as the elastic modulus and hardness of the siloxane
polymer material increases, it also becomes increasingly more brittle and it will not
be able to withstand the bending forces applied during demolding, nor would it be
adaptable to conform to a substrate. The surface energy also increases slightly to
reach a value of 35 mJ m−2 for hPDMS. Hence, to have both beneﬁcial properties
within a mold, bi-layer composite stamps of soft and hard PDMS have been
developed whereby a thin hPDMS layer bearing the patterns is supported by a thick
sPDMS backing that provides for ﬂexibility [61]. As such, composite molds are able
to provide for both, high resolution patterning and a good compliance to a substrate.
The fabrication procedure involves ﬁrst preparing an appropriate siloxane mixture
for hPDMS which after degassing, is spin-coated over a nanostructured master mold
and soft cured. Subsequently, a degassed liquid precursor mixture of sPDMS is
poured on it and thermally cured forming the ﬂexible mold backing. It is important
Table 7.2. PDMS formulations with two different proportions of vinyl and hydrosiloxane pre-polymers and
stiffness offered by the material.

(7.0%–8.0%) Vinylmethylsiloxane-dimethylsiloxane copolymer

1.7 g

(1:2)—CATALYST

9 ± 2

Nanofabrication
7-11

[Page 269]
to control the thickness of the hard layer to be as thin as possible and restrict it to
ﬁlling just the mold features. This is because hPDMS is highly brittle, and small
bending or ﬂexing forces on the mold will lead to cracking of this layer. As a general
rule, sPDMS would be appropriate only to pattern structures above 500 nm, below
this dimension, hPDMS composite molds would be more suitable to pattern features
down to 15 nm [62].
The main drawback of PDMS-based soft molds is the short life time giving out
only a limited number of nanoimprinting cycles depending on the particular process.
It is a consequence of the porous nature of PDMS whereby organic solvents easily
penetrate, causing swelling and deformation of the mold features. In addition,
monomers from resist mixtures are also able to penetrate and due to inter cross-
linking, cause hardening and embrittlement to the material over time. These events
cause the deterioration of the molds and have a negative effect on reproducibility in
the course of repeated patterning.
7.2.2.4 Soft molds based on fluoro-polymers
Some of the difﬁculties with PDMS molds are precluded by ﬂuorinated polymers.
Fluorinated polymers are formed by linear chains of multiple carbon–ﬂuorine
bonds. These bonds are intrinsically much stronger than carbon–hydrogen bonds
and as such, ﬂuorinated polymers offer high temperature and solvent resistance.
Particularly employed in NIL are the perﬂuoropolyether (PFPE) acrylates based
elastomers because they have a number of advantages as intermediate molds
including a combination of higher stiffness and a lower surface energy which allows
for sustaining higher imprint pressures reducing the friction and adhesion forces
during demolding, thus facilitating the ﬁnal detachment of the imprinted polymers.
These characteristics allow having a signiﬁcantly improved mold life time compared
to that for PDMS [63].
With a molecular weight ∼2000 g mol−1, a dynamic viscosity at 20 °C of 430 cP
and a low surface energy, PFPE based materials can be casted directly onto the
master mold ﬁlling nanocavities down to 50 nm [64]. Due to their high thermal
stability (up to 288 °C) [65] PFPE based molds are suitable for T-NIL processing.
The mechanical properties of the PFPE ﬁlms depend upon the processing conditions
and precursor formulation including the concentration of the radical initiator. The
Young’s modulus of the PFPE materials ranges from several MPa for the acryloxy
PFPE to several 10 s MPa for the dimethacryloxy form (DMA-PFPE), and over
150 MPa for the trimethacryloxy form (TMA-PFPE) [66].
Commonly employed in NIL is the acryloxy PFPE (denoted as α-PFPE)
commercialized as
Fluorolink®
MD700 perﬂuoropolyether
(Solvay
Speciality
Polymers) with a modulus of 10.5 MPa and a lower surface energy (12.7 mJ m−2).
For replica molding, it is typically mixed with a 3%–4% w/w alpha-hydroxyketone
type of photoinitiator (2-Hydroxy-2-methylpropiophenone or Darocur 1173) and
crosslinked by UV-light (365 nm, 25 mW cm−2) for 2–3 min depending on the ﬁlm
thickness. The exposure needs to be performed in the absence of oxygen to avoid the
inhibition of the free radical photocuring reactions, and it can be done by covering the
photo resin with a ﬁlm of polyethylene terephthalate (PET) while layered on the mold
Nanofabrication
7-12

[Page 270]
to replicate, or else by carrying out the process in N2 atmosphere. The PFPE
replicated substrates can be used as free-standing molds. However, for large area
molds, the PFPE structured material can be supported on thin, ﬂexible PET sheets as
backing ﬁlm allowing for more practical handling. Sylgard 184 PDMS backing layers
have also been utilized by curing the PDMS on the PFPE cured material before
peeling it from the master mold [67].
7.2.2.5 Soft molds based on hybrid organic–inorganic materials
OrmoStamp is the most common organic–inorganic hybrid material and it has been
formulated by Microresist Technology GmbH speciﬁcally for the fabrication of NIL
transparent working stamps. The material is a UV curable liquid with viscosity of
0.75 Pa; this relatively low viscosity allows for efﬁcient ﬁlling of the master template
cavities. After curing and thermal post treatment, the polymer becomes durometric,
which means that no glass transition takes place when heating, exhibiting a modulus
of elasticity of 0.650 GPa and hardness of 0.036 GPa. These values of elasticity and
hardness make OrmoStamp useful for imprinting dense nanostructures as well as
high-aspect-ratio features.
OrmoStamp working molds have a high UV-transparency at 350 nm that is
preserved even after thermal exposure at 270 °C. As such, OrmoStamp working
molds can be used for both thermal and UV-NIL processes. However, the material
has a high surface energy and therefore after UV-curing and separation from the
master mold, the replicated OrmoStamp molds must be post-baked to harden the
polymer and an antistiction layer must be deposited before the stamp can be used.
OrmoStamp working molds can be used for up to 50 imprints with almost no pattern
degradation, and features sizes down to 50 nm have been successfully replicated [68].
OrmoStamp working molds are commonly prepared as ﬂexible composite molds
on a backing support. On the choice of this backing material, compatibility aspects
need to be considered. Firstly, it is the large shrinkage due to crosslinking by UV
light exposure and hard baking of approximately 6% that will cause bending of the
backing ﬁlm. This can be minimized by decreasing the OrmoStamp layer thickness
or/and using a thick backplane [69]. A second important aspect to consider is the
interfacial adhesion between the OrmoStamp and the backing ﬁlm material. Sylgard
184 PDMS roughened by UV exposure and treated with adhesion primer was found
to produce a suitable and stable interface, and at the same time it allows for good
conformation to the substrate [70, 71].
Table 7.3 summarizes the key physical properties for common polymers
employed in the construction of ﬂexible soft NIL molds.
7.2.2.6 Sequential soft mold replication
Because of the low surface energy characteristic of most polymers, and in particular
for the group described above, it is possible to apply them in sequential replication
processes of molding, soft lithography and nanoimprinting. The combination of
materials and processes allows for producing a second generation of replica molds in
soft materials. It also provides for an intermediate step to invert the relief features to
create the negative or the positive contrast proﬁle from the original master on the
Nanofabrication
7-13

[Page 271]
desired functional materials. Figure 7.4 illustrates a multiple replication sequence to
transfer the hierarchical structures generated on a photoresin/PMMA template onto
a PMMA substrate in three replication steps involving soft lithography and T-NIL.
However, it should be taken into consideration that at each polymer replication step,
shrinkage of the replication material takes place due to curing or thermal
contraction [73]. The level of shrinkage will vary depending on the particular
material, molecular weight and curing conditions. Typically it ranges from shrink-
age ratios about 2% (PDMS) [74] to 6% (PFPE, OrmoStamp) [70]. Hence, when
accuracy is required, appropriated early corrections onto the master mold should be
implemented.

7.3 Complex structures by soft thermal nanoimprint and hybrid
processes
7.3.1 Introduction
The ability to carry out nanoimprinting with soft molds has opened up new
possibilities to create a large variety of complex functional surface structures with
high ﬁdelity and resolution in a cost effective manner. These surfaces may exhibit
unique properties and have often been inspired by surface structures found in nature.
Indeed, the complex topographies found in nature reveal important design principles
to impart advanced functionalities into materials and commonly these involve high
aspect ratio structures often organized within a hierarchical multiscale arrangement
of micro- and nanostructures.
Multilevel and or hierarchical structures including micro- and nanoscale features
are certainly ubiquitous in nature and provide biological materials with exceptional
properties beyond those expected from the bulk material itself. Some of the well-
known examples include the lotus leaf covered with hierarchical micro- and
nanostructures that provide for anti-wetting or superhydrophobicity [44, 76, 77],
the branched nanopillars found in gecko feet that provide for dry adhesive properties
[78, 79], butterﬂy wings with a multiscale arrangement of microridges and crossing
nanoribs on the wing´s surface that provide for structural coloration [80–82] or moth
eyes with subwavelength multiscale ommatidial arrays enclosing conical structures
conferring antireﬂection and bactericidal properties [83, 84].
A close look into many of these natural surfaces shows that they often comprise a
multi-scale/hierarchical arrangement of nano- and microscale features to achieve
optimal properties for species adaptation to their living environment. Multi-
functionality in these natural systems is generally accomplished due to convergence
of both the structural elements and the material chemical composition. Hence, to
follow nature design principles, it is essential to be able to produce patterned
structures in a broad variety of materials, adequate to the target application.
Inspired by nature to achieve functionality, a number of artiﬁcial materials with
advanced physical or mechanical properties have been engineered. A summary of
the different technologies available to create bioinspired or biomimetic multi-
functional surfaces has been published in several reviews [16, 85, 86]. The interest
in engineering high aspect ratio and multilevel hierarchical topographies to impart
advanced functionalities into manufactured consumer products has been recognized
in different ﬁelds of application [87], including energy harvesting [82], membrane
technology [88], anti-icing [89], photonics and optical devices [14, 15], robotics [90],
tissue engineering [91], micro/nano-ﬂuidics [92] and fog harvesting [93] among
others.
T-NIL is one of the most promising approaches to produce artiﬁcially complex
multifunctional surfaces for its high resolution and large choice of thermoplastic
materials that can be imprinted. Some examples of natural high aspect ratio and
hierarchical micro- and nanostructures and their bio-inspired replicas created by
T-NIL technology are shown in ﬁgure 7.5. The next sections will describe in detail
Nanofabrication
7-15

[Page 273]
the basic processing issues to produce complex high aspect ratio and multilevel
hierarchical structures by soft T-NIL and combined lithographic process.
7.3.2 High-aspect ratio imprinting
Nanopatterned features with high aspect ratio (HAR) are interesting to bring out the
true effect of the topography without interfering effects from the underlying
substrate. This is especially true for micro-optical elements and photonic devices
[14, 15, 97], SERS substrates [98, 99] or substrates for cell biology studies [91]. HAR
imprinting entails additional processing challenges related to cavity ﬁlling and
demolding due to the huge increase in mold–substrate contact area [100]. During the
imprint process of HAR structures, the polymer needs to ﬂow over long distances to
ﬁll the cavities of the mold. This would require very high applied pressure and long
imprinting times sufﬁcient to drive the ﬂow along the nanocavities. Nonetheless, the
ﬁlling process is often enabled by capillary forces as the heat-softened viscous
polymer is able to wet the surface of the mold and, as a result, the pressure driven
viscous ﬂow is assisted by capillary action.
On the other hand, a low surface energy is required to reduce the adhesive forces
and wall friction to ease the separation of the mold from the imprinted structures
during demolding, avoiding possible stretching or brakeage of the small imprinted
structures. Accordingly, a HAR imprinting process demands reaching a balance
between mold wetting properties for capillary ﬁlling action and good mold release
properties for demolding. Considerations on these two process steps during HAR
imprinting are given below.
7.3.2.1 HAR mold filling
The imprint process of HAR ﬁlling relies on the external imprint pressure as well as
on capillary driven ﬂow within the cylindrical nanocavities. Capillary pressure arises
due to the ability of the viscous heated polymer to wet the surface of the mold
cavities. The capillary pressure across the interface polymer—air is the Laplace
pressure given by the difference between the pressure of the viscous polymer (Pl) and
the gas phase (Pg) at the interface,
Figure 7.5. Some bioinspired hierarchical micro and nano structures created by T-NIL and hybrid methods.

American Chemical Society, butterﬂy eye [95], rice leaf [96] Royal Society of Chemistry, butterﬂy wings [80]
John Wiley & Sons.

where γ is surface tension of the viscous polymer, θ is the solid–liquid contact angle
and r is the capillary radius.
The Lucas–Washburn equation describes the rate dh/dt of ﬂuid ﬂow through a
cylindrical capillary of radius r as a function of the driving capillary pressure.
Making the assumptions that ﬂow is laminar, viscous and incompressible and that
the capillary is much longer than it is wide, Washburn applied Poiseuille’s Law to
describe the pressure drop in a ﬂuid ﬂowing through a cylinder, deriving the
following equation:
γ

where η is the viscosity of the heated polymer and h is the height of polymer inside
the nanocavity.

The Lucas–Washburn equation predicts that when the contact angle is 90°, there is
no Laplace pressure across the interface and, as such, there is no driving force to
generate ﬂuid ﬂow. For a contact angle above 90°, the sign changes and there will be
a negative Laplace pressure opposing the ﬁlling of the capillary cavities. In these
cases, the external imprint pressure applied has to be increased substantially to
overcome the Laplace pressure. In the situation when the contact angle of the
viscous polymer increases due to poor mold wetting, the pressure or the imprint time
to ﬁll the cavities may be excessively long. Figure 7.6 illustrates these situations.
Predicted by equation (7.2), the ﬁlling of cylindrical nanocavities with viscous
polymers via capillary action is very slow due to the small value of r and the high
viscosity of polymer typically in the range of 104–107 Pa s. To give an estimate, the
time needed for complete ﬁlling of 0.5 × 2 μm pore cavities in a mold with a polymer
with viscosity about 106 (Pa s) and surface tension ∼30 mN m−1 and wetting contact
angle of 85° calculated with equation (7.2), it will take about 102 min. Reducing the
viscosity to 105 Pa s will decrease drastically the ﬁlling time to 10 min, or by reducing
the contact angle to 60°, it will take 17 min.
The process of ﬁlling HAR structures is highly favoured in T-NIL working at
high temperature since viscosity and surface tension decrease dramatically with
temperature at the same time that wetting increases reducing the contact angle.
These circumstances facilitate the viscous polymer ﬂow entry into the mold cavities
and ﬁlling by Laplace pressure. High temperature is also beneﬁcial to facilitate gas
diffusion, particularly in the case of soft polymer molds where the gas enclosed in the
cavities can be eliminated through the mold permeable material [23].
Nanofabrication
7-17

[Page 275]
High energy mold materials that provide lower contact angle and higher
wettability will be also advantageous to increase Laplace pressure, thus allowing
to signiﬁcantly reduce the imprinting time or the imprinting pressure. However, high
energy materials will increase adhesion and will preclude the separation of the stamp
from the imprinted polymer during demolding. As such, there is a need to ﬁnd a
suitable surface energy to balance these two processes of molding and demolding for
each particular imprinting task.
7.3.2.2 HAR mold releasing
The process of demolding involves the detachment or separation of the mold,
releasing the imprinted structures. The required demolding force is related to the
adhesion force between the mold and the polymer, the friction force of the structures
on the side walls as they are being released and the deformation force due to the
presence of undercuts or roughness at the sidewalls of the mold structures [101]. In
addition, there may be a thermally-induced residual stress at the polymer–mold
interface arising during the cooling stage due to the difference in thermal shrinkage
between the polymer within the cavities and the mold, which results in high contact
stress and, in turn, contributes to the demolding force.
Demolding forces are directly determined by the geometrical parameters of the
mold, the aspect ratio and density of the structures. Due to the large increase in
contact area, structures with HAR are susceptible to ripping at the base where the
stress is concentrated. When the density of structures is high, the resulting force will
be extremely high such that whole areas of the polymer may break or detach from
the supporting substrate. Likewise mold-ﬁlling, surface energy and temperature are
two parameters critical in demolding as both directly inﬂuence the friction and
adhesion forces involved. Furthermore, the thermal induced stress and the mechan-
ical strength of the polymer-imprinted features are directly related to temperature
Figure 7.6. Schematic of capillary ﬁlling situations when (left) the polymer wets the capillary (θ < 90°) and
(right) when the polymer does not wet the capillary (θ > 90°). The Laplace pressure across the interface is
indicated by the arrows.
Nanofabrication
7-18

[Page 276]
[102, 103]. Hence, the choice of demolding temperature needs to be made wisely.
The recommended demolding temperatures were predicted by measuring the
variation of demolding force with temperature [102, 104]. It was found that there
is an optimum demolding temperature when the adhesive force is decreased and the
friction force is at its lowest. This is a direct consequence of the inverse contribution
of temperature on the friction or adhesion forces. This optimum demolding
temperature was found to be somewhere slightly below the Tg and for PMMA, it
was around 70 °C [101, 103]. In practice, this implies that demolding at lower
temperature may cause the imprinted patterns to break off due to high adhesion and
brittleness arising from thermal induced stress. On the other hand, higher temper-
atures may allow easy demolding due to softness and higher elasticity but it may
result in losing the replication ﬁdelity due to stretching and collapse of the structures
as a result of low mechanical strength. These issues will be particularly more severe
as the aspect ratio increases.
The mold surface energy is a determinant factor on adhesion, and as such, low
values of mold surface energy and of polymer surface tension will lead to low
adhesion. Conversely, low surface energy values will reduce wetting and the
capillary force during mold ﬁlling which is not helpful in HAR imprinting.
Consequently, all parameters need to be taken into account and balanced for a
particular imprinting task. It should be borne in mind that silane-based anti-sticking
agents allow adjusting the surface energy within the range of 10–20 mJ m−2.
Soft molds with high elasticity and inherent low surface energy can provide a
number of advantages when it comes to demolding HAR structures. Soft polymeric
molds, having similar thermal expansion coefﬁcient as the polymer imprint material,
will generate less thermally induced residual stress during the cooling stage, thus
inducing less damage during demolding. Due to their elastic nature, soft molds will
be more tolerant towards deformation forces and demolding shear due to inhomo-
geneous mold walls. A determinant beneﬁcial factor of soft molds is that demolding
is realized by a sequential action of peeling the mold from the imprinted substrate.
This effectively results in demolding a smaller area at the time, and as a result, a
lower detachment force for demolding needs to be applied, reducing the probability
of damage to the imprinted structures or the mold, improving the uniformity and
reproducibility and, in general, the yield of the process.
7.3.2.3 Stability of soft molds with mechanical and thermal loading
Since T-NIL is a mechanical process, the mechanical properties of the mold and
dependence with temperature are decisive to the nanoimprint replication result.
Constraints on thermal nanoprinting with soft molds are imposed primarily by the
stamp deformation at high temperature and high pressure.
While choosing the mold material, the ﬁrst consideration to make is the
geometrical characteristics of the features to imprint, as this will determine the
degree of deformation. Molds with features protruding will bear a considerable
compressive stress on the small feature areas when imprint pressure is applied and
consequently, these small areas will experience a large compressive strain and
generally a reduction of the mold features’ dimensions with the force. Hence, in the
Nanofabrication
7-19

[Page 277]
case of protruding mold features, there will be a need for materials with high
modulus to resist the compressive stress and deformation. Typically, this situation
calls upon the use of ﬂexible composite molds consisting of a structured thin rigid
layer of a high modulus material bonded to a ﬂexible supporting backing such as
those described earlier (section 7.2.2) including h-PDMS, OrmoStamp or PFPE
composite molds.
In the situation of molds having structural intruding features like pores, the elastic
modulus of the material is not as critical since the compressive stress will be basically
borne by the large area comprising the mold cavities. In this case, it is possible to
employ molds made homogenously of the standard s-PDMS elastomer; imprints of
pillar features with high ﬁdelity have been demonstrated in several works [105, 106].
While choosing the material for producing soft molds, it is imperative to consider
its mechanical and wetting characteristics and adjust the process parameters
accordingly. It is the interplay between pressure, temperature and process time
that will determine the compressive strain on the mold features and, as a result, the
replication ﬁdelity. While working with soft molds, two premises should be followed:
the imprint materials should have a Young’s modulus lower than that of the mold,
and the imprint pressure applied should be lower than the modulus of the polymer
mold. Since thermoplastic materials generally have an elastic modulus higher than
that of the elastomeric mold, pressing the mold against these materials will cause a
severe compressive strain onto the mold features. Hence, in this case, the initial step
of an imprint process should be to heat up the thermoplastic materials to increase the
mobility of the polymer chains reducing its elastic modulus (the modulus decreases
by a factor of about 103 above the glass transition or melting temperature [107]) and,
in a subsequent step, pressing the soft mold into the viscous material will cause the
ﬂow of the viscous polymer into the mold cavities, minimizing the mold
compression.
Working with soft molds, one would typically require reducing the imprint
pressure in order to decrease the compressive strain. To this end, materials like
h-PDMS with surface energy in the range of 35 mJ m−2 that can sustain capillary
forces, would be helpful in assisting the ﬁlling process and, as a result, reduce the
level of the required external pressure. The elastic recovery of the mold material
under compression is an additional factor to consider during the imprint process in
order to achieve high imprinting ﬁdelity. Schift and co-workers experimentally
observed that even after complete ﬁlling of the mold, in some cases, a small
deformation on the imprinted structures could still be observed [108]. To explain this
observation they put forward the hypothesis that during the initial stage of the
embossing process, the features on a soft mold would be partially compressed.
Following this, as the mold features are ﬁlled with viscous polymer, the compression
is normalized and with sufﬁcient time, the mold is allowed to elastically relax and
recover its initial shape. This relaxation time for elastic recovery was found to have a
direct relationship with temperature, that is, at higher temperature, the viscosity of
the imprint polymer is lower producing less compressive stress and at the same time
facilitating the compressive recovery. In addition, it was found that the elastic
recovery was also time dependent. The analysis of the results indicated that
Nanofabrication
7-20

[Page 278]
depending on the imprinting temperature and related polymer viscosity, there was a
time span required for compressive strain recovery. Thus, the ﬁndings in this work
signify the need to take into account within the imprint processing time, the time for
the compressive recovery of the mold material in connection with other process
parameters such as pressure and temperature, and this should be customized for a
particular mold structural design.
7.3.2.4 Material limitation and pattern collapse in high aspect ratio imprinting
During the replication process of HAR features, it is important to consider the
mechanical stability of the features in relation to their effective stiffness to predict
the upper limit in terms of the feature aspect ratio to possibly imprint. HAR
imprinted patterns are prone to feature collapse primarily driven by adhesion forces
between the pillars and between pillars and the substrate [109]. In this process,
besides stiffness, other factors such as pattern density and surface energy of the
imprinted materials have a direct inﬂuence on the stability or otherwise adhesive
failure of the imprinted features. In general terms, two failure mechanisms can take
place. If the spacing between patterns is larger than their height, the imprinted
features can undergo ground collapse. On the other hand, if the pattern density is
high, that is, if the spacing between the patterns is smaller than their height, with
only a small bending, the imprinted features in close proximity may experience
adhesion forces toward the neighbouring patterns causing a lateral collapse failure.
The critical aspect ratio of ground collapse, (h/d)c, under which the pillars are
stable against ground collapse, was derived by Roca-Cusachs and co-workers [109]
by balancing the bending energy for ground collapse and the energy of the pillar
adhesion to the substrate, arriving at the following expression:
v

workers [110] for a square array of posts by balancing the bending energy and energy
of deformation of laterally adhered pillars with their energy of adhesion and it was
shown to be for arrays of cylindrical and square shape pillars, respectively:

These equations allow making predictions on the mechanical stability of
structures that can be replicated via nanoimprinting and show that the polymer
elastic modulus plays a critical role on the stability of polymer nanostructures. The
stability can also be enhanced by reducing the surface energy of the material. As a
general guideline, the typical aspect ratio range for sub-micrometer pillars will vary
from 2 to 10 as we move from elastomeric materials such as PDMS (modulus on the
MPa range) to stiffer materials such as PMMA with elastic modulus on the GPa
range [111].
7.3.3 Multilevel hierarchical complex nanoimprinting
Extensive efforts have been undertaken to develop methodologies leading to the
creation of multiscale hierarchical surface structures, particularly those inspired by
nature. The fabrication processes to create these complex structures in a controlled
manner often require several steps and generally involve a high level of complexity
compared to simple structures. Among the methodologies employed, NIL is one of
the most practical to produce multilevel and hierarchical micro- and nanostructures
with high precision. Top–down approaches involving lithographic processes typi-
cally confer a higher degree of controllability and repeatability over the geometrical
dimensions of the generated structures. Appropriate combinations of NIL and other
lithographic approaches, particularly photolithography, have permitted the fabri-
cation of hierarchical topographies with well-deﬁned geometry and feature control.
Some of the most efﬁcient methodologies enabling the scalable and high-throughput
fabrication of multiscale hierarchical surface structures with high precision using T-
NIL in combination with other technologies are described below.
7.3.3.1 Sacrificial layer mediated nanoimprint (SLAN)
The basis of the sacriﬁcial layer mediated NIL technology relies on the use of a
sacriﬁcial layer to protect the structures produced on a ﬁrst NIL step before a second
NIL step is performed. After the ﬁnal step, the sacriﬁcial layer is removed typically
by solvent dissolution. The function of the sacriﬁcial layer is to encapsulate and ﬁx
the structural patterns produced in a ﬁrst step to prevent deformation during
additional thermal imprint process steps. The main requirement for the sacriﬁcial
material is that it should remain thermally stable during the second thermal NIL
processing conditions. In addition, it should allow for selective dissolution using an
orthogonal solvent to the imprinted material. Another consideration that should be
kept in mind is the chemical compatibility and adhesion energies between the
different polymer materials involved.
Raut and co-workers demonstrated the fabrication of moth’s eye inspired multi-
scale hierarchical ommatidial arrays on a PC substrate [83]. In a ﬁrst step, the moth’s
eye nanocone structures were fabricated by imprinting the suitable mold onto a PC
ﬁlm. Subsequently, these structures were covered with a thin (500 nm) sacriﬁcial
layer of poly(sodium 4-styrenesulfonate) (PSS) by spin coating a 20 wt. % solution in
water. Then, a second thermal imprint step of a microlens array was performed at a
temperature higher than the glass transition temperature of PC. Because the PSS has
Nanofabrication
7-22

[Page 280]
a higher melting temperature (Tm = 450 °C) than that of the PC (Tg = 155 °C), the
temperature during the second nanoimprinting did not soften the PSS and the
nanocone patterns being encapsulated into the PSS sacriﬁcial layer remain ﬁxed and
undeformed. After demolding the micro structures, the imprinted substrate was
immersed in water for the dissolution of the PSS sacriﬁcial layer releasing the
nanocone imprinted structures on the microlenses.
On a second approach, the sacriﬁcial method has also been employed to fabricate
hierarchical ﬁbrillar gecko inspired dry adhesive structures [112]. The geometrical
characteristics of this particular design includes micropillars of 3 μm in diameter,
with an aspect ratio of 2, and ﬁbrils structures of about 200 nm in diameter with an
aspect ratio of 10, which was below the critical aspect ratio for lateral collapse for
the PC ﬁbrillar arrays estimated to be 13. The processing steps are schematically
depicted in ﬁgure 7.7. Initially, micropillar arrays are imprinted onto a PC sheet.
Subsequently, the arrays are immersed in a sacriﬁcial matrix of PSS such that only
the pillar tips remain exposed. This is achieved by controlling the spin coating
conditions. Next, the ﬁbrillar structures are imprinted on top of the micropillar
arrays using a porous anodic aluminium oxide template (AAO). PSS exhibited
excellent thermal stability at elevated temperatures and the underlying micropillars
enclosed within the PSS matrix did not show any appreciable deformation. After
demolding the AAO template, the imprinted sheet was immersed in water for the
selective dissolution of the PSS sacriﬁcial layer to expose the hierarchical HAR array
structures. In this step, the evaporation of the water after the dissolution of PSS
caused capillary-driven coalescence of the ﬂexible ﬁbrils. This issue was solved by
freeze-drying the substrate in liquid nitrogen to freeze and ﬁx the free-standing
ﬁbrillar nanostructure, followed by freeze-dry elimination of the water.
The SLAN approach has revealed itself as a process capable of efﬁciently
generating nanoimprinted hierarchical structures with high resolution, high uni-
formity and reproducibility. It appears particularly beneﬁcial for the fabrication of
HAR structures and hierarchies where demolding of HAR nanostructures can lead
to damage of the imprinted features. It provides for relative high freedom in
Figure 7.7. Schematic of the sacriﬁcial layer mediated nanoimprint process for the fabrication of hierarchical
ﬁbrillar gecko inspired structures. Reprinted with permission from reference [112]. Copyright (2018) American
Chemical Society.
Nanofabrication
7-23

[Page 281]
choosing the geometrical characteristics of the features and it can be scaled-up over
large areas. Dissolving the sacriﬁcial polymer materials appears to be a critical step
as it may cause elastocapillary coalescence or structural collapse due to solvent
evaporation, or the dissolved material may leave a residue on the imprinted
structures if it is not rinsed sufﬁciently.
7.3.3.2 Nanoimprint hybrid techniques
7.3.3.2.1 Combination of nanoimprint and other lithographic techniques
The combination of several nanofabrication techniques, particularly lithographic
technologies with T-NIL and or UV-NIL, has permitted the generation of a variety
of hierarchical topographies with well-deﬁned geometry.
The combination of nanoimprint and photolithographic (CNP) processes was
ﬁrst described by Guo and co-workers [113] using a hybrid mask-mold consisting of
a transparent quartz substrate with metal patterns acting as a photomask together
with nanoscale relief features. The hybrid mold is utilized to imprint by T-NIL onto
a photoresist that is subsequently exposed to UV light. After demolding, the
substrate is developed to eliminate the non-crosslinked resist. This methodology
can be carried out with many of the exiting photoresists exhibiting a thermoplastic
behaviour. This methodology produces self-aligned nano and micro patterns of high
precision. However, the preparation of hybrid mask-molds requires a challenging
clean room fabrication process. An alternative for more practical methodology to
produce these mask-molds was proposed by Schift et al, by producing the mold
nanofeatures using OrmoStamp material over a metal pre-patterned glass substrate
[114]. This approach allowed for more ﬂexibility and simpler fabrication of the mold
design. The downside is that due to the ﬁnite residual thickness of the OrmoStamp
layer on top of the masking metal layer, there are some diffraction effects at the
edges causing some edge shadowing effect on the imprinted patterns.
T-NIL has also been combined with e-beam lithography to produce multilevel
patterned topographies. An exemplary process is outlined in ﬁgure 7.8 [115]. The
process involves initially performing T-NIL on an electron sensitive thermoplastic
polymer such as PMMA with the appropriated molecular weight. Subsequently,
grey-scale e-beam exposure is carried out on the patterned polymer ﬁlm creating
sloped reliefs. Finally, a TASTE process of polymer reﬂow is performed to eliminate
the sidewall roughness caused by the dose dependent e-beam exposure to produce
smooth inclined slopes, while the T-NIL surface patterns stay unaffected.
7.3.3.2.2 Combination of nanoimprint and UV ﬂood exposure
The fabrication of hierarchical topographies with nanopatterns entirely covering the
micron-scale surface was demonstrated by Steinberg and co-workers [116]. The
methodology involves two T-NIL steps with an intermediate step of surface
hardening. Initially, nanofeatures on an SU-8 precursor-ﬁlm are produced by
T-NIL and subsequently, the surface is subjected to VUV short treatment (3–10 s)
which exposes the SU-8 surface-near only, triggering the polymer crosslinking of a
Nanofabrication
7-24

[Page 282]
limited ﬁlm depth. Finally, a second T-NIL is performed, using a mold containing
the micrometric structures, generating the hierarchical nano- and microstructures.
Figure 7.9 illustrates this process sequence.
The procedure applies only to negative tone photoresist materials like SU-8 (when
exposed to UV light, the exposed areas become crosslinked). SU-8 in the
non-crosslinked state displays a thermoplastic behaviour, that is, it becomes
viscoelastic above the Tg and almost liquid at high temperatures. The Tg of
unexposed SU-8 is in the range of 35 °C–50 °C. After UV treatment, the post-
exposure bake promotes and accelerates the crosslinking and increases the elastic
modulus of the material. The VUV exposure dose determines the thickness of the
crosslinked top layer; if the exposure is too low, the layer may be too thin and the
stability insufﬁcient. On the other hand, long treatment times may produce an
excessively thick layer reducing the ﬂowability and imprinting ability during the
second imprint step. Therefore, in this approach, the VUV-exposure dose and
process temperature are critical processing parameters to adjust in conjunction with
the geometrical parameters of the features in order to ﬁnd the adequate process
window for producing the hierarchical structures.
7.3.3.3 Multilevel hierarchical topographies
The combination of consecutive T-NIL and direct laser writing processes allows for
the fabrication of complex multiscale hierarchical structures with high precision in a
Figure 7.8. (a) Process sequence to create multilevel PMMA resist structures based on the combined sequential
processes of T-NIL, grey-scale e-beam exposure and thermal reﬂow by TASTE process. (b) The process allows
for the selective smoothening of the roughness created by grey scale e-beam patterning of prism structures onto
NIL prepatterned gratings, which remains unchanged (scale bar = 2 μm). Reprinted from [115], with the
permission of AIP Publishing.
Figure 7.9. Process steps for the fabrication of hierarchical nano–micro structures on a negative tone
photoresist material (SU8). (a) T-NIL of the nano-structures; (b) VUV ﬂood exposure of the surface-near
only; (c) second T-NIL of the microstructures. Reproduced with permission from [116], Copyright (2017), with
permission from Elsevier.
Nanofabrication
7-25

[Page 283]
practical and reproducible manner [75]. This process comprises three steps involving
ﬁrst a T-NIL step on a ﬁrst layer of a thermoplastic material to create a ﬁrst level of
nanopatterns. In a second step, a photoresist second layer is spin coated on top of it
and texturized by a second T-NIL process. The approach is enabled by the
thermoplastic behavior of most photoresists before they are photo-crosslinked. In
a ﬁnal step, to deﬁne the micron size features, optical lithography is carried out by
direct laser writing on the nanopatterned photoresist second layer. Alternatively, a
photolithography step through a photomask can also be performed. After develop-
ing the micropatterns by eliminating the exposed photoresist, a hierarchical top-
ography is obtained. Figure 7.10(a) illustrates schematically the fabrication steps.
The noteworthy advantage of this fabrication approach is that it allows custom-
izing the nanostructure geometry independently at each level of the hierarchy by
simply using the molds with the desired features for the ﬁrst and the second T-NIL
steps. Thus, the process allows for an enormous versatility in terms of topographical
designs that can be produced. Some examples are shown in ﬁgures 7.11(a) and (b)
which show SEM images of two-level, second order and two-level, third order
topographies, where the order refers to the number of different topographical
Figure 7.10. (a) Outline of the fabrication process to produce multilevel hierarchical topographies. First, a
polymer ﬁlm of PMMA is spin coated on a silicon wafer and imprinted with nanofeatures via T-NIL. Second,
a photoresist ﬁlm is spin coated over the imprinted polymer and a process of T-NIL is carried out with the
same or different geometrical nanofeatures. Thirdly, direct laser writing of micron size features is performed.
Finally the exposed resist is eliminated. (b) Fabrication of multilevel fractal-like hierarchical structures. This
process uses the same sequential fabrication steps but makes use of a two-level hierarchical mold obtained by
soft-lithographic replication of a previously produced hierarchical substrate.
Nanofabrication
7-26

[Page 284]
features. In these examples, the hierarchical topographies include combinations of a
moth-eye topography or 500 nm periodic gratings at two different levels of micron-
size features (pillars, ridges or cubes).
When using thermoplastic polymers, it is necessary to select among those with
sufﬁcient differences in glass transition temperature (Tg) to avoid softening of the
material of the ﬁrst layer during the second imprinting step. Nonetheless, since the
photoresist is imprinted at a low temperature of about 100 °C most thermoplastics
would be suitable. Alternatively, UV sensitive thermosetting resins can be used for
the ﬁrst layer while they will be imprinted via UV-NIL. This approach can be
combined with replica molding to produce multilevel hierarchical topographies. In
this case, a two-level hierarchical mold obtained by soft-lithographic replication of a
previously produced hierarchical substrate is employed to imprint the ﬁrst layer or
even the second layer following the same sequential fabrication steps as described
above (see ﬁgure 7.10(b)).
When the process is repeated sequentially, complex 3D fractal-like topographical
arrangements of self-similar structures at reducing length-scales can be generated.
An example of such, showing a third-order, four-level multi-scale topography
produced by this process, it is displayed in ﬁgure 7.11(c). The process requires
increasing the size of the microfeatures during the successive laser writing steps to
accommodate the pre-exiting features on the substrate. In the case of isotropic
structural designs, the enclosed features are self-aligned into the previous level, and
alignment is not required. The maximum number of structural levels to possibly
accommodate will depend on the material and geometrical characteristics of the
different levels and importantly, the feature density and aspect ratio as these
parameters will lead to the increase in contact area, and as such, the friction and
adhesion forces involved in demolding. In general, as the architectural complexity
increases, the possibility to cause damage or ripping of the structures due to
demolding also increases, and this will be the main constraint. This strategy is an
easy-to-implement fabrication process to produce multilevel hierarchical structures
with very high feature control over large areas and a wide range of materials. As
such it appears as very attractive strategy to enable many advanced nanofabrication
possibilities.
Figure 7.11. SEM images of (a) two-level, second order; (b) two-level, third order and (c) four-level third order
hierarchical structures.
Nanofabrication
7-27

[Page 285]
7.4 End-of-chapter summary
NIL performed with soft polymeric molds has emerged as the most viable
technology for the realistic production of nanoenabled products. Particularly, soft
T-NIL, due to its versatility in materials processing, has become especially useful to
produce functional nanopatterns where the imprinted structures themselves are the
active components to provide for functionality. To fulﬁl this purpose, these nano-
patterns require speciﬁc geometrical designs that are often inspired by nature and
involve complex architectures such as high aspect ratio and hierarchical nano- and
micropatterns.
This chapter has reviewed the most important processing issues including the
properties and requirements on materials for the fabrication of soft polymeric molds
and imprint process to produce these functional complex structures by soft T-NIL.
The fabrication of the required soft molds is realized by replica molding from
masters created using direct writing techniques. In addition to traditional silicon
micromachining technologies, we can now take advantage of the huge ﬂexibility in
terms of structural design of direct laser writing by two-photon polymerization tools
for rapid mastering of complex structures.
The most popular materials employed to produce ﬂexible molds were discussed.
One important consideration to take into account when selecting a polymer material
is the viscosity of the liquid precursors since it limits the minimum feature size to
possibly ﬁll. On the other hand, the Young’s modulus and surface energy of the
crosslinked material impact the imprinting and demolding processes. In HAR
imprinting, the surface energy is one of the most important parameters to take
into account as it conditions the mold ﬁlling through the generation of capillary
ﬁlling action and also the demolding through the adhesion forces between the mold
and the polymer. The mechanical properties of the imprint material will determine
the maximum aspect ratio that can be achieved before patterns collapse takes place.
The composite mold approach, whereby the nanostructures are formed by a stiffer
material on a ﬂexible backing should be implemented when the mold contains
protruding high-resolution features.
Generally, the production of hierarchical topographies with well deﬁned geom-
etry and feature control involves a signiﬁcant engineering challenge. These have
mostly been created by appropriate combinations of NIL and other lithographic
approaches, principally photolithography. In particular, it has been shown that the
combination of consecutive T-NIL and direct laser writing processes allows for the
fabrication of complex multiscale hierarchical structures with high precision in a
practical and reproducible manner.
7.5 Student’s corner
• How is it possible to use s-PDMS as a mold for T-NIL of nanostructures on
PC if the elastic modulus of the PC is three orders of magnitude higher?
• During T-NIL of HAR nanostructures on PMMA it is observed that a large
number of nanopillars are affected by ripping. What could be done to
improve the replica quality in this case?
Nanofabrication
7-28

[Page 286]
• During the fabrication of nanopillars by T-NIL on a PS ﬁlm, it is observed
that upon demolding, a large number of pillars are taller than expected
according to the mold dimensions, what could be the reason? How would you
select the demolding temperature?
• How does the aspect ratio inﬂuence the detachment force during demolding
of nanoimprinted structures? What is the advantage of using ﬂexible molds
during the demolding step of HAR nanostructures?
• Which are the main factors related to the pattern collapse of HAR?
• Given a polymer with viscosity =106 (Pa s), surface tension ∼20 mN m−1 and
wetting contact angle of 80°:
a. What is the time needed to ﬁll 0.5 × 2 μm pore size cavities by capillary
action?
b. What viscosity should the polymer have to reduce that time to about
7 min?
c. If mold silanization is accomplished in order to facilitate the demolding
process, how is the polymer wetting contact angle expected to be
affected?
• What provides the gecko with its amazing climbing capability?
