Nanolithography techniques and their applications
Jose´ Marı´a De Teresa
Chapter 8
Stencil lithography
Oscar Vazquez-Mena and Luis Guillermo Villanueva
Stencil lithography is an ancient method that has been used for centuries to transfer
patterns on different substrates. In recent decades, it has been applied to micro- and
nano-fabrication. It is a resist-less method that allows wafer-scale transfer of nano-
structures using deposition, etching and implantation. In this chapter we describe the
technique, highlighting its advantages and challenges, and we provide a detailed
guide on how to achieve an optimized result. We ﬁnalize the chapter by brieﬂy
enumerating some of the most interesting applications that stencil lithography has
been used for.
8.1 Introduction
Stencil lithography, hereinafter also referred to as ‘SL’, is a lithographic technique
that is based on the use of a solid mask to locally block/allow the ﬂux of certain
atoms/molecules/particles onto our substrate resulting in pattern generation. The
idea is similar to the classical analog, where a shape is cut-out in a solid material,
which we call stencil or mask, and we use it to locally stop/allow the ﬂow of, e.g.
spray paint (see ﬁgure 8.1).
This concept, which macroscopically has been used for centuries, can also be used
to transfer patterns in the micro-/nano-scale [1]. In this latter case, the masks must be
made separately on a solid material with apertures in the shape of the pattern that
we want to transfer. To perform these apertures, we must use different lithographic
techniques, as will be explained later in the chapter. The solid mask will stop/allow
the ﬂux of be atoms, ions, molecules, or even particles (see video 8.1 available at
https://iopscience.iop.org/book/978-0-7503-2608-7). The available processes to per-
form the pattern transfer are deposition, etching or ion implantation. We will see
later in the chapter how the choice of patterning method determines the type of mask
to be used and has different implications in the shape of the transferred pattern.
In contrast to more conventional types of lithography, based on resist processing,
stencil lithography typically presents a distance (gap) between the stencil and the
doi:10.1088/978-0-7503-2608-7ch8
8-1
ª IOP Publishing Ltd 2020. All rights,
including for text and data mining (TDM), artificial intelligence (AI) training, and similar technologies, are reserved.

[Page 292]
substrate (see ﬁgure 8.2(a)). The existence of this gap has implications on both the
advantages and disadvantages of the technique. To give two quick examples: on the
one hand, it is possible to pattern transfer without touching the substrate, which is
useful for fragile substrates. On the other hand, however, there are no physical walls
limiting the landing and spreading of the material on the substrate, so the patterns
will not be transferred 1:1 but they will observe some change of the lateral
dimensions (this is what we call blurring).
Figure 8.2 shows the canonical use of SL–metal deposition through a stencil
membrane. We can see the membrane with micro-/nano-apertures that stands
Figure 8.1. Macroscopic stencils. (Left) a paper stencil with the symbol for wiﬁcut out. Material could go
through the holes in the paper to transfer the pattern on a surface. (Right) street grafﬁti made using another
paper stencil where the letters have been cut out.
Figure 8.2. Canonical example for SL–metal deposition. (a) Schematic of the metal deposition setup, where a
membrane with micro-/nano-apertures is placed between the metal source and the substrate to stop metal from
passing except from the regions where the apertures are opened. (b) Scanning electron microscope (SEM)
micrographs showing on the left a membrane with nanoapertures and on the right the same pattern replicated on
the surface with 75 nm thick Au layer. Image with permission from [2] copyright 2012 American Chemical Society.
Nanofabrication
8-2

[Page 293]
between the metal source and the substrate, masking locally the ﬂow of atoms. This
membrane is in most cases fabricated on silicon or silicon nitride, which are durable
materials to reduce fracture and allow re-utilization of the stencils.
8.1.1 Brief history of stencil in micropatterning
The use of stencils in micromachining has a long history. We can go back to 1969,
where a stencil was proposed to etch glass by hydroﬂuoric acid [3]. In the 1970s, SL
was used to mask the deposition of metal by sputtering, and achieving lateral
resolution in the millimeter range [4]. Later, SL was demonstrated for the fabrication
of thin ﬁlm Josephson devices [5], and to deposit contacts on solar cells, thus
avoiding photoresist processing [6]. The minimum attained lateral dimensions have
been improving signiﬁcantly since then, with several milestones on the way, such as:
10 μm in 1982 [7], 3 μm in 1996 [8], 500 nm in 2003 [9]; and a record of 50 nm in 2008
[10]. This reduction in the lateral resolution has indeed been possible thanks to the
advances in the other lithographic processes used for the fabrication of the stencil
membranes: electron beam lithography (EBL) [11], deep-UV lithography [12] and
focused ion beam (FIB) [10], nanosphere lithography [13] and nano-imprint
lithography (NIL) [14].
8.1.2 Why is stencil lithography interesting?
SL has the following advantages that make it attractive for nanopatterning:
Resistless processing: SL does not involve the coating of the substrate with any
resist, avoiding contact with solvents, organic materials, or energy radiation.
This enables the use of the technique on chemically fragile surfaces such as
polymers or self-assembled monolayers. At the same time, it also allows the
patterning on mechanically fragile substrates and structures, such as bad
adhesion materials or released micromechanical structures (membranes,
cantilevers, etc). In addition, SL can also be performed on substrates that
show a 3D topography, i.e. substrates where resist casting (via spin or spray
coating) is challenging or even impossible.
Easy manipulation and implementation: SL can be used by anyone that has
access to a deposition, etching or implantation machine. Once the stencil
(mask) is made, one can ﬁx it on a substrate even with practical methods, like
adhesive tape or mechanical clamps, and insert the coupled system into the
processing machine. The stencil will mask the entities and thus allow the
transfer of the pattern into the substrate.
Stencil reusability: Following the previous point, the stencil (mask) is the
fundamental part of the process. Even though the fabrication might be
complicated (see next section), the stencils can be reused many times. This
makes the pattern transfer via SL quite cost-effective. For deposition of
nanostructures (through nanoscale apertures), reusability is limited by the
so-called clogging, i.e. the progressive closing of the apertures due to lateral
deposition. For etching and ion-implantation, there is no lateral deposition,
Nanofabrication
8-3

[Page 294]
but the membranes might be damaged during the process if proper protection
is not implemented.
Parallel patterning: As opposed to other nanopatterning techniques, SL can be
used to pattern in parallel a full wafer. The results, as we will cover later
below, might not be optimum when using large stencils, since the gap
between the stencil and the substrate might be larger, but it is still possible
to do the pattern transfer and use blurring corrective methods.
Dynamic stencil lithography: Since (typically) stencils do not stick to the
substrate, there exists the possibility of moving them with respect to the
substrate during pattern transfer. This has been tested with deposition and it
opens up a rainbow of different options: variable thickness structures, multi-
material structures with different patterns using a single mask, complex
designs, etc.
8.1.3 Why is stencil lithography challenging?
Not everything is positive regarding SL. There are several challenges to overcome
for SL to become a mainstream option, namely blurring, clogging, shadowing and
membrane stability. These three points are very much referring to the use of SL for
nanopatterning. When apertures are in the micron-size, these points are much less
important.
Blurring: As we can see in ﬁgure 8.2, there is always a gap between the stencil
and the substrate. This means that when species travel through the apertures
they are not laterally conﬁned, and thus the lateral resolution of the technique
is compromised. This can be caused by surface diffusion of the deposited
material and by the divergence of the material ﬂux, which in turn depends on
the process pressure and the collimation of the ﬂux. Blurring becomes more
relevant with decreasing structure size, since the absolute value of the
dimension loss remains constant, thus in relative terms grows inversely
proportional to the structure size. At the nanoscale, the dimensions of the
blurred zone can be as large as the structures themselves. A deeper analysis
and strategies for blurring reduction are discussed later in the chapter.
Shadowing: In the case that the ﬂux of material is not collimated, but divergent,
depending on the aspect ratio of the membrane apertures, we could also have
a partial masking of the line of deposition into the substrate. This generates
patterns that do not have a uniform thickness over the width of the aperture.
Clogging: In the case of deposition through a stencil mask, there is an issue that
arises when the deposited material thickness is of the same order or greater
than the lateral size of the stencil aperture. In that case, the deposition of
material on the aperture sidewall signiﬁcantly reduces the size of the aperture.
This implies, essentially, that the aperture width dynamically changes from
the beginning till the end of the deposition. Indeed, if the deposited thickness
is larger than the stencil aperture width, it could happen that the apertures
close during a given deposition. Clogging also affects the reusability of the
stencil membrane since the subsequent deposition will start at a narrower
Nanofabrication
8-4

[Page 295]
aperture width. However, clogging can also be used to fabricate conical or
pyramidal patterns [15, 16], which is a distinct feature compared to other
types of lithography.
Membrane stability (and low ﬁll factor): To perform pattern transfer in the nano
(sub-micron) scale, we need to etch apertures in the stencil membrane with
lateral dimensions in the nanoscale. This is only possible if the membrane
thickness is also in the nanoscale, since otherwise the aspect ratio of the
aperture makes it extremely hard to be opened. The previous effect,
shadowing, is another reason to have small aspect ratio in the stencil
apertures. Having thin membranes means that they are more fragile and
that they are more prone to mechanical deformation arising from material
deposition, or damage during etching or implantation. The ﬁrst logical
solution to this is to make the membranes smaller in lateral dimensions, so
that they are sturdier mechanically. However, this also means that the area to
be patterned is smaller, and the overall surface of the wafer cannot be used
within a single deposition, reducing the ﬁll-factor available with SL.
8.2 Fabrication of stencil membranes
In SL everything starts with the fabrication of the membranes. It is a process very
much analogous to the fabrication of masks/reticles for optical lithography and the
fabrication of stamps for nanoimprint lithography.
8.2.1 Membrane material
Unlike optical lithography, where the typical mask material is quartz, stencil
membranes can be made of different materials, depending on the design
requirements.
First, a ﬂat membrane is favored over a wrinkled one. This is something that
happens in certain structures and is called buckling [17]. In order to avoid this
buckling, membranes made out of materials with compressive stress should be
avoided.
Second, as we just mentioned in the previous section, if one needs nano-apertures,
the thickness of the membrane must also be in the sub-micron range. If one needs
micro-apertures, then the thickness can be much larger. This has a direct impact in
the choice of material. For small apertures, in order to gain stability of the
membrane with a small thickness, a material with tensile stress should be used,
and the most used one is silicon nitride [18]. For larger apertures, the material choice
opens up, but people have mostly used either silicon [19–21] or certain polymers [22].
8.2.2 Fabrication steps
The steps to fabricate a stencil membrane are relatively straightforward. They will
depend, of course, on the actual material chosen for the membrane itself and the
dimensions of the apertures. Here we give what we consider to be the canonical
example of stencil membrane fabrication: a ﬂat silicon nitride membrane with nano-
apertures (see ﬁgure 8.3).
Nanofabrication
8-5

[Page 296]
A silicon wafer is coated on both sides with silicon nitride (ﬁgure 8.3(a)). Here,
one typically chooses low-stress silicon nitride (made by biasing the stoichiometry
and enriching the silicon content). Then a lithography is performed on the frontside
(ﬁgure 8.3(b)) to deﬁne the apertures on the frontside SiN layer via dry etching
(ﬁgure 8.3(c)). Subsequently, windows are opened on the backside SiN layer
(ﬁgure 8.3(d)) that will allow KOH to etch the bulk Si and thus release the
membrane (ﬁgure 8.3(e)). These steps have been described thoroughly in the
literature [23].
Variations of this process ﬂow can be found for example on the bulk Si etching,
which in some cases has been made combining deep reactive ion etching (DRIE) and
KOH, or even only DRIE. The main reason to do this is to be able to place
membranes closer together, since KOH deﬁnes an angle of 54.7º in the sidewalls and
that limits the number of membranes that can be placed per area.
8.2.3 Membrane reinforcement
As we have described previously, membrane stability is an important concern for
SL. We have mentioned that it is possible to improve the membrane stability by
increasing the thickness (if the aperture size allows it) and by reducing the lateral
dimensions of the membrane (if the required ﬁll-factor allows it).
There might be cases where none of these options is a possibility, meaning that
nano-apertures over a large area are required. This would imply that the membranes
must be thin and large and thus very fragile mechanically. In order to improve
membrane yield and endurance, it is possible to add mechanical reinforcements to
the membrane. This is typically done (but there are other ways) using corrugations in
the membrane, as we can see in ﬁgure 8.4.
Figure 8.3. Canonical example for stencil membrane fabrication. (a) Si wafer with a thin layer of SiN coated
on both sides. (b) A lithography is performed on the frontside using EBL, NIL, DUV… or any nano-
lithography technique we include in this book. (c) Frontside SiN is patterned, and resist is removed. (d) SiN on
the backside is etched to deﬁne the windows for a subsequent etching step. (e) KOH etching that removes the Si
and releases the membrane.
Nanofabrication
8-6

[Page 297]
8.3 Challenges of stencil lithography
We have already listed the main challenges or this technique, but now we are going
to perform a deeper analysis on each of them.
8.3.1 Blurring
Blurring is the increase in lateral dimensions of the desired pattern when being
transferred from the membrane to the substrate. Depending on the fabrication step
where we use the stencil, the behavior of the species travelling through the apertures
is different. A schematic for each case can be seen in ﬁgure 8.5.
Figure 8.4. Canonical example for stencil membrane fabrication with corrugations. (a) Si wafer is patterned
with trenches where the reinforcements are required. (b) SiN is deposited on the wafer, conformally covering
the trenches. (c) SiN on the frontside is patterned using lithography and dry etching. (d) SiN on the backside is
etched to deﬁne the windows for a subsequent KOH etching that removes the Si and releases the membrane,
revealing the corrugations that add mechanical stability to the membrane.
Figure 8.5. Schematic for the different blurring effects on stencil lithography depending on the fabrication
technique. (a) Deposition. Since the beam of material is not collimated, the deposition yields a zone of penumbra
that corresponds to the so-called geometric blurring, which is continued by a surface diffusion zone. (b)
Implantation. Even though in this case the beam is quite collimated, the geometric blurring always exists. (c)
Etching. On top of the geometric blurring due to the material not being collimated, we can also ﬁnd an isotropic
etching component that is caused by free radicals which are not directed by the bias voltage on the substrate.
Nanofabrication
8-7

[Page 298]
8.3.1.1 Deposition
The most common case, based on the typical use of stencil lithography, is for the
deposition of material and, more in particular, evaporation. In this case
(ﬁgure 8.5(a)), the material source is heated around an area of diameter (d) and
placed at a distance (D) of the membrane/substrate, which are separated by a gap
(G). Via a simple geometrical analysis, it is possible to see that the aperture of width
(w) transfers onto the substrate forming a structure which does not have constant
thickness across the width. On the ﬁnal structure, we can see three distinct regions.
The darker part, that we can call the central region, corresponds to the zone with the
desired thickness (dark grey in ﬁgure 8.5(a)). Moving laterally, we observe a region
where not all the points from the source material reach the substrate. This zone is the
one that corresponds to the geometric blurring. It happens because the material
emerging from the source is not perfectly collimated. Finally, the last portion shown
in the schematic (in light gray) is a very thin layer of material due to surface
diffusion.
Geometric blurring is relatively easy to calculate. Considering that the gap is much
smaller than the distance to the source (G ≪D, please note that the drawing in
ﬁgure 8.5 is not made to scale) and that the thickness of the membrane is smaller
than the aperture width (t ≪w), we can see that the deposited structure will have a
part where the thickness is maximum, and then a zone where the thickness decreases
linearly. The dimensions can be calculated by equation (8.1):
Δ

where wmax corresponds to the width of the region with maximum thickness. From
equation (8.1) it is clear that this effect disappears when G = 0, as we mentioned
before. The fact that the effect reduces with the ratio d
D is intuitive if we think of the
collimation of the material emanating from the source. In an analogy to a light
source, rays arrive collimated when the source is distant and punctual, i.e.
→0
d
D
.
In practical terms, we can see that to have better results when depositing material
through a stencil, we should seek large deposition chambers, where the source and
the substrate are as far away as possible. This typically happens in evaporators with
only one chamber. At the same time, we would like to reduce the size of the source.
This implies that the best technique is e-beam evaporation (since it is possible to
minimize the heated area); then thermal evaporation (with a small heating crucible);
then multi-target sputtering with a small target size; and ﬁnally single-target
sputtering. The difference in d between evaporation and sputtering is at least of
one order of magnitude, and this is the main reason why a stencil is rarely used with
sputtering. In addition to this purely geometrical effect, the fact that sputtering takes
place at a much higher chamber pressure than evaporation makes the collimation
even harder, even though in some cases a substrate bias could be applied to convey
directionality to the deposition.
The second contribution to blurring during deposition is surface diffusion. The size
of this contribution, which has also been called halo, is difﬁcult to calculate a priori
Nanofabrication
8-8

[Page 299]
since it depends on many factors, for example the state of the substrate surface [24],
the material being deposited [25], the energy of the material when reaching the
surface, etc. The only realistic way to estimate the width passes through experiment-
ing and measuring its size. However, this can be proven to be quite cumbersome
since this halo is only a few nanometers thick (typically < 5 nm), and it is not visible
under the scanning electron microscope (SEM). To facilitate the visualization, a
technique called structures development is used, where a mild dry etching is
performed on the underlying substrate, therefore revealing the size of the halo. A
clear example of this can be seen in ﬁgure 8.6(a) [25], where we show respective SEM
micrographs of an aperture in a stencil membrane, the deposited aluminum ﬁlm on
Si, and the developed structure after a mild dry etching, revealing the size of the halo.
By using this technique, it has been possible to analyze the dependence of surface
diffusion on different parameters. Figure 8.6(b) shows how the gap makes larger the
geometric blurring, but also how it increases the size of the halo. Figure 8.6(c) shows
that for larger deposition rates we observe a larger surface diffusion. This is probably
because material arrives at the surface more abundantly and thus there is more
Figure 8.6. Examples of blurring. (a) Method to reveal the size of the halo created by surface diffusion. From
left-to-right, SEM micrographs of an aperture in the SiN; the metal deposited on the substrate making it
difﬁcult to observe the thin halo; the same substrate after a mild dry-etching of the substrate material, where
the halo acts as a mask, therefore making it visible. (b) Dependence of blurring on the gap. SEM micrographs
showing deposited structures from the same aperture width (waper) but different gaps. An increase in the width
of the central structure (

geom) with the gap is evident, as it is an increase of the halo. (c) SEM
micrographs showing the dependence of the halo with the deposition rate. Higher rate means larger surface
diffusion, probably because atoms arrive with more energy to the substrate. (d) SEM micrographs showing the
effect of material and gap on the size of the halo. Different materials, depending on their adhesion (surface
diffusion coefﬁcient), expand more on the surface than others. (e) Technique to reduce the effect of blurring,
specially the one coming from surface diffusion. SEM micrographs showing a stencil aperture and the result of
the deposition of 50 nm-thick amorphous-Si layer. A mild dry-etching is performed for different times,
reducing the size of the halo until it is completely gone after 15 s etching. Images adapted from [25] copyright
IOP Publishing, and [26] copyright IEEE.
Nanofabrication
8-9

[Page 300]
energy to allow the atoms to travel faster. Another example can be seen in
ﬁgure 8.6(d), that evidences the difference in material and, at the same time, due
to the gap. This latter point can also be seen in ﬁgure 8.6(b). However, the
dependence on the material is very much striking and indicates the importance of
studying this issue beforehand.
Even though this halo can reach very large dimensions, since its thickness is quite
small, it is also possible to bypass the issue by removing it before continuing the
processing. This can be done via a mild dry etching very selective to the material we
want to remove. An example of this can be seen in ﬁgure 8.6(e) [26], where 50 nm of
amorphous silicon were deposited on silicon oxide. The visible halo is removed by a
mild silicon etching. After 15 s of etching, the nanowire-like shape is recovered with
no visible halo.
8.3.1.2 Implantation and etching
We group implantation and etching because both present rather collimated beams of
ions going to the substrate. In the ﬁrst case (ﬁgure 8.5(b)), as we know from the
functioning principles for ion implanters, the ions are generated from a source,
accelerated, ﬁltered and then collimated with the aid of thin slits along the way.
However, this collimation is not perfect and thus the pattern will suffer a slight
distortion. We can see this in the examples of ﬁgures 8.7(a) and (b), where the stencil
apertures (of 140 nm (a) and 310 nm (b)) are used to let ions through and the ﬁnal
dimensions of the implanted region, according to AFM measurements are slightly
larger (around 100 nm) [27]. This is right after implantation. Depending on the
subsequent annealing process, the overall loss of dimensions can be somewhat
larger.
Figure 8.7. Effect of blurring for implantation and etching. (a) SEM micrograph of a 140 nm stencil aperture;
followed by an AFM topography image of the substrate after implantation has been performed; and the proﬁle
of the implanted region. The 10 nm protrusion might be due to a faster native oxidation rate within the doped
region. (b) Same as (a) but with an aperture of 310 nm. (c) SEM micrographs showing trenches in silicon being
etched by means of a deep reactive ion etching (Bosch® process). Geometric blurring can be observed, as well
as the effect of a shallow isotropic etching emanating from the free radicals. (d) SEM micrograph showing the
dependence of blurring on the gap, where the rightmost part has been patterned with a gap that is 50 μm larger
than the section on the left.
Nanofabrication
8-10

[Page 301]
In the case of dry etching, as schematically shown in ﬁgure 8.5(c) and mentioned
above, the ionized molecules within the generated plasma will be attracted towards
the substrate thanks to the applied bias. This generates quite collimated beams of
ions that will etch the substrate providing some geometrical blurring. It is possible to
observe this in ﬁgure 8.7(c), in the cross section, where the corners of the trenches are
being rounded up by non-purely directional etching. In addition to this, we also
observe the effect of the free-radicals that are not collimated by the bias, since they
are charge-neutral. In this case, an isotropic component of the etching appears and
damages the surface. This is shown in ﬁgures 8.7(c) and (d). Finally, as in the case of
the deposition, it is easy to see that blurring for etching and implantation increases
with the gap, as is shown in ﬁgure 8.7(d).
8.3.2 Shadowing
The second challenge that we must face when using SL is the so-called shadowing.
This is purely a geometrical effect so, as for the case of the geometrical blurring, it is
more important when the source material is not collimated. Therefore, we will focus
our discussion on the deposition. In contrast to blurring, shadowing does not depend
uniquely on the gap (G), but also on the thickness of the membrane. From that point
of view, shadowing can be considered an issue for any type of lithography that uses a
(thick) resist with a non-collimated process (e.g. lift-off).
Figure 8.8 shows a schematic of the shadowing effect with its two main
consequences: reduction in thickness and displacement of the structures with respect
to the center of the aperture.
The ﬁrst effect relates to the part of the source that can send material to the
substrate in an effective way. We can also call it the ‘line of sight’ from the substrate.
It is also a purely geometrical effect, as the geometric blurring. As we can see in
ﬁgure 8.8(a), the central part of a structure always receives more material than the
borders. It is possible to calculate the width of the portion with maximum thickness,
similar to what we did in equation (8.1), only that now we will not neglect the effect
of the membrane thickness (t):

8.3.3 Clogging
The third challenge for SL is the so-called clogging. This is an issue solely affecting
deposition and it consists of the fact that the material being deposited through the
membrane will also be deposited on the membrane and partially on the sidewalls of
the apertures. It is an issue that affects mostly the patterning of nano-structures since
it becomes important when the aperture width (waper) is of the same order as the
thickness of the deposited material (tdep) [28, 29].
Two consequences can be seen from the clogging of an SL membrane: the
reduction of structure width along the deposition, which can even yield in some cases
(
<
w
t
aper
dep) that the structure does not reach the intended thickness, as we can see in
ﬁgure 8.9. The second effect, a direct consequence of this latter point, is that clogging
Figure 8.9. Clogging effect. Schematic showing the reduction in width along the thickness of the structure due
to a reduction in the aperture width caused by the deposited material on the side walls. When the aperture
width is very large compared to the thickness (
≫
w
t
aper
dep), this effect can be neglected. But when this ratio
starts being large, we can even reach the point where the aperture closes completely, therefore ending up with
structures that do not reach the maximum thickness overall. The different colors represent different regions of
the deposited structure with different thickness. For simplicity, and better illustration, we show in this
schematic the case where blurring and shadowing can be neglected, i.e. the case with a collimated beam of
material arriving at the stencil membrane.
Nanofabrication
8-13

[Page 304]
might render the stencil unusable, since the apertures are closed, and they cannot be
used anymore.
The deposition of material on the sidewalls depends on the material being
deposited and the deposition method. It has been seen, for example, that Al shows
that the thickness deposited on the sidewalls is half that deposited on the ﬂat
substrate [29], but other materials behave differently. It also depends on the
material, treatment, and state of the membrane. As two examples, surface treatment
on the membrane [15] and high membrane temperature during deposition [30, 31],
have both been shown to signiﬁcantly reduce the membrane clogging.
The second issue arising from clogging that we mentioned is that the apertures are
completely blocked, thus rendering the stencil unusable. In order to solve this, the
easiest option is to perform wet etching of the covered membranes to remove the
additional material. This has been proven for some metals [24, 29] as is shown in
ﬁgure 8.10, and it is a useful and easy method to use whenever a suitable etching
solution can be found.
8.3.4 Cracking and bending
The last challenge for SL that we will mention here is the bending and cracking of the
membranes. This is inherent to SL as we use (thin) membranes to perform the
patterning. It is important for all the different processes we can do with SL
(ﬁgure 8.5—deposition, implantation and etching), however, the reasons are quite
different.
In the case of deposition, the material that is deposited on top of the membrane
causes a mechanical stress that can induce bending, buckling or even rupture of
the membrane. As we mentioned before, in the fabrication section, these effects are
more important when the thickness of the membrane is small, and they can be
minimized by including mechanical reinforcements in the membrane, as for example
corrugations. A clear example is shown in ﬁgure 8.11 [33], where we compare
the deformation of a stencil membrane before and after deposition. The intrinsic
stress in the metal has a much smaller effect if reinforcements are placed within the
membrane.
In the case of implantation, the ions are much more energetic when arriving at the
membrane than in the case of evaporation and thus, they penetrate the membrane
material. This is an important point since the thickness of the stencil needs to be
Figure 8.10. Clogging and cleaning. SEM micrographs of (left) a nanoslit in a stencil membrane after
fabrication; (center) the same aperture after deposition of metal (100 nm of evaporated Al), showing almost
complete clogging; (right) the same aperture after cleaning in a wet chemical etchant for Al. Images reprinted
with permission from [32].
Nanofabrication
8-14

[Page 305]
large enough to stop the ions. The actual value of the thickness depends on (i) the
ions being sent (size and mass) and (ii) the energy at which they are accelerated. With
these two parameters it is possible to estimate via simulations what is the penetration
depth within the membrane, and thus the minimum thickness required for the
membrane to be functional as a mask for implantation. In any case, the ions
penetrating the membrane cause signiﬁcantly larger damage than in the case of
deposition. This is one of the main reasons why SL has not been used for
implantation very often, but only in some cases [27, 34, 35].
When performing etching through a stencil membrane, the situation is also much
more aggressive than for deposition. In this case, the energy of the ions is not enough
to penetrate the membrane material, but the etching process can thin the membrane
rendering it more fragile. In fact, it is necessary to use a membrane material that is
compatible with the type of etching that is going to be performed. In principle, we
want a selectivity with the material on the membrane that is large enough so as to
not have any etching. An example is the use of aluminum to coat the membrane as a
preparation for its use to etch silicon in the substrate. Si etching (based on SF6) does
not etch aluminum (selectivity Al:Si is larger than 1 : 106). Thus, with a protection
layer of 50 nm Al it is possible to etch through a full Si wafer (525 μm) [36]. As in the
case of the implantation, this process has not been used very frequently because it is
much more aggressive than deposition [3, 36–39].
8.4 Design considerations
Following the discussion on challenges, we would like now to offer a synthesis and
to elaborate in the different design considerations to obtain a successful patterning
with SL.
8.4.1 Process as collimated as possible
As we have seen in the previous section, blurring and clogging are associated with
geometrical effects that would minimize if the entities (ions, atoms …) arriving at the
stencil membrane come as parallel as possible. To do this one can: (i) collimate the
ions via electronic lenses; (ii) use a very small source size; and (iii) at a large distance
Figure 8.11. Membrane bending. SEM micrographs showing two stencil membranes after deposition of 50 nm
of Cr on top. Since Cr is a metal with a large intrinsic stress, we can see the bending of the cantilever-like part
of the ﬂat membrane (left), whereas the structures in the corrugated membrane (right) remain ﬂat. Images
from [33] reprinted with permission from Elsevier.
Nanofabrication
8-15

[Page 306]
from the substrate; and (iv) reduce the pressure of the process to increase the mean
free path, thus avoiding unnecessary collisions between the atoms.
8.4.2 Thin but robust membranes
We have also shown that shadowing is increased by having thick membranes. In
addition, fabrication of high-aspect ratio apertures is challenging. Therefore, smaller
aspect ratios are preferred (
≫
w
t). However, thin membranes are more fragile, thus
one might consider adding mechanical reinforcements and the use of a proper
material. In addition, as explained above, in the case of implantation we need a
minimum thickness of the membrane that is larger than the penetration depth of the
ions into the membrane material. Otherwise the membrane does not stop the ions,
i.e. no masking is performed.
8.4.3 Durable material (both mechanically and chemically)
In order to ensure a long lifetime of the stencil membranes, we have to choose a
material that is durable/robust mechanically. Materials with high Young’s modulus,
maybe tensile stress, and non-brittle are ideal from the mechanical point of view. In
addition, to ensure reusability of the membranes after different processes, the
material should be chemically stable, i.e. not easy to etch via chemical methods.
These points are the main reason why silicon nitride (low stress, silicon rich, silicon
nitride to be precise) is the most used materials for stencil membranes.
8.4.4 Surface treatment
Chemical stability is beneﬁcial to ensure reusability of stencils after deposition. If the
membranes are used for etching, as we mentioned before, it is necessary to coat the
membranes with a selective material in order to protect the membrane. The selection
of this protective material depends on the type of dry etching to be performed. To
reduce the effect of clogging we also mentioned that some surface coatings can be
used, for example some self-assembled monolayers.
Another way to reduce clogging goes through the fabrication of more complex
membranes, which include electrodes to enable heating during deposition. This
temperature increase reduces clogging, but it makes fabrication (extra steps to place
metallic heaters on the membranes) and implementation (electrical connection must
be ensured during the deposition) more difﬁcult.
8.4.5 Reducing gap
As we mentioned in the introduction, the major difference between patterning
through a stencil membrane and through a layer of resist is that the membrane is not
conformal to the substrate, but it has a gap (G) in between. We have seen how this is
a parameter of fundamental importance for both blurring and shadowing. Thus,
many people have devoted time and effort into solving this issue.
The simplest way to reduce the gap is to reduce the size of the stencil. The overall
curvature of the stencil mask (wafer) yields a larger gap the larger the size of the
Nanofabrication
8-16

[Page 307]
mask, and this goes roughly with the square of the mask dimensions. This means
that moving from a 10 mm square chip to a 100 mm wafer, the gap might be up to
100 times larger. This is the reason why nanopatterning with SL at the full wafer
scale has seldom been accomplished and most of the time it has been done using
single chips. Importantly, here we refer to the size of the mask, not the size of the
membranes. Clearly, this solution compromises a lot on the interest of the technique
since it limits the size of the area to be patterned on the substrate.
If full-wafer patterning is desired, the compromise comes from somewhere else.
One explored option follows from the mentioned solution for clogging: fabricating
membranes with integrated heaters. Not only can a reduction in the clogging rate be
observed, but also via heating the gap is reduced [30, 31]. However, as mentioned
above, the implementation and fabrication become more complicated, partially
losing the advantage of the technique.
Another possibility is to only complicate the fabrication, while remaining with a
simple implementation. This solution [40, 41] consists of fabricating membranes that
are recessed from the wafer-level, and attached to the wafer via mechanical (MEMS)
springs. This way, when clamping together stencil mask and substrate, the recessed
membranes will approach the substrate notably minimizing the gap, as shown
schematically in ﬁgures 8.12(a) and (b). Figures 8.12(c) and (d) show the dramatic
consequence of the use of these compliant stencils, with a substantial reduction in the
halo as well as a much better deﬁned central structure.
8.5 Alignment
Any nano-lithography process must address two major points to become relevant.
The ﬁrst is the resolution, which we have already widely analyzed in the previous
sections, and then is the alignment. This is a clear challenge mostly for novel
lithography methods that do not have the history of, e.g. optical or electron beam
lithography.
Figure 8.12. Compliant stencil. (a) and (b) Schematic drawings of a stencil mask on a substrate, showing the
change in gap across the mask, for standard and compliant membranes, respectively. (c) and (d) SEM
micrographs showing the results of depositing 50 nm Al on top of Si through standard or compliant
membranes, and after a mild dry etching to reveal the size of the halo. The difference in the size of the central
structure (dc) and the total structure (dT) is signiﬁcant. Images adapted from [40] with permission of The Royal
Society of Chemistry.
Nanofabrication
8-17

[Page 308]
In the case of SL, alignment needs to be performed between two solid pieces that
need to stay together during the processing. The most comfortable solution to
perform this alignment is using bonding aligners. These machines are speciﬁcally
made to align two wafers and to clamp them, allowing the pair then to be
transported to the bonding equipment. Figures 8.13(a) and (b) show pictures of
an example of a clamping tool used to perform bond alignment of two full 100 mm
wafers. Figure 8.13(c) shows an SEM micrograph of an example of alignment, where
a nanowire-like structure in aluminum was deposited on some pre-deﬁned platinum
contacts. Alignment accuracy is better than 2 μm in this case.
In order to enable this alignment, one needs to prepare membranes that contain the
alignment marks, to allow the microscope to see through. The accuracy of the
alignment depends on the machine being used and can be better than 500 nm. The
main issue with this process is that, after alignment, the full assembly (wafers and
clamping device) must be introduced into the processing equipment. This is doable for
deposition machines (in particular evaporators), but for implantation and etching it is
more difﬁcult. It is rare to ﬁnd commercial implanters and etchers that allow us to
introduce this type of assembly in the processing chamber. This is the reason why
alignment at full-wafer scale has been only done with evaporation [10].
Alignment at the chip level can be performed using dye bonder machines. The main
point is the clamping mechanism which, in some cases, must be made manually. With
lower resolution, one can also perform manual alignment using a microscope.
However, achieving a better resolution than 10 μm is rather difﬁcult manually. Still,
this has been used to demonstrate complex integration of systems, even fabricating
transistors fully by SL (implantation, etching and metallization [42]).
8.6 Dynamic stencil
One of the most unique and attractive features of SL is the fact that the movement of
the mask with respect to the substrate is allowed during the deposition. This, in turn,
enables the fabrication of any shape in situ, and without the need to break vacuum.
In this sense, it is similar to what one can do with focused ion/electron beam induced
deposition (FIBID/FEBID), but in this case the patterning can happen at full wafer
Figure 8.13. Alignment in SL. (a) Commercial transfer chuck for a bonding aligner. The chuck is used to
clamp both wafers (substrate and stencil) and then transfer them into the processing equipment. (b) Detail of
one of the clamps holding both wafers. (c) SEM micrograph showing the alignment of two depositions. First,
electrode contacts in Ti/Pt are deposited, and subsequently an aluminum layer is deposited contacting the Pt
below. Image from [10] with permission from American Chemical Society.
Nanofabrication
8-18

[Page 309]
scale and at many places simultaneously. However, in order to perform this, it is
required to add the functionality of moving the stencil mask with respect to the
substrate in vacuum. This, as is the case for the alignment that we discussed before,
is exceedingly difﬁcult to perform for etching and implantation, and it has only been
done for deposition. Furthermore, only a handful of groups in the world have been
able to do dynamic patterning with SL.
First, some groups have performed it at the chip level, using a combination of SL
and scanning probe microscopy (SPM), where the cantilever played the role of solid
mask, being patterned with nano-apertures. To do this, they modiﬁed setups for
SPM in ultra-high vacuum (UHV) to also enable deposition. One of the advantages
is that it is possible to image the dimensions of the deposited patterns in situ [43–47].
For full-wafer dynamic stencil, one approach can be seen in ﬁgure 8.14. Three
motors are connected to the stencil mask in order to enable movement in all three
axes, and they are placed inside an evaporator. Both dynamic (motion while
deposition is happening, see video 8.3 available at https://iopscience.iop.org/book/
978-0-7503-2608-7) and quasi-dynamic (deposition happens when the stencil is
static, and motion when the deposition is stopped, see video 8.2 available at
https://iopscience.iop.org/book/978-0-7503-2608-7) SL can be performed [48]. One
of the challenges is to perform alignment with former structures on the wafer or with
the axis of the motors. A solution could be to include optical microscopes inside the
chamber, or to use nanoelectromechanical sensors (NEMS) to detect where
deposition is happening [49].
Figure 8.14. Dynamic SL. (a) Schematic of the concept for a full wafer dynamic (and quasi-dynamic) stencil.
Three different motors are connected to the stencil mask and are controlled by an external computer. The full
setup is inserted in an evaporation chamber to enable patterning of the substrate. (b) SEM micrographs
showing the drawing of (i) a rectangle in silver and (ii) the roof of a house in gold, without breaking vacuum.
Images adapted from [48] with permission of AVS.
Nanofabrication
8-19

[Page 310]
8.7 Applications
SL is a very promising technique. It offers a different way to perform nano- and
micropatterning that in some cases enables certain applications that otherwise would
not be possible. We do not wish to make here an exhaustive list of the applications
for which SL has been used, there are other works where one can follow that [1], but
we just aim to give a short idea.
Since SL does not require any resist, people have used it to deposit sensitive
materials like organic conductive molecules [50–53], magnetic alloys [54], complex
oxides [55, 56], and fullerenes [44]. In addition, SL has been used on delicate and
unconventional substrates such as non-planar substrates [36, 42, 57], CMOS devices
[58], SAMs [59], and organic layers [2, 60–62].
SL has been used to fabricate periodic structures on the back reﬂectors of a-Si
solar cells: to improve their quantum efﬁciency [63–65]; to pattern electrical contacts
on 2D materials to keep them cleaner [66, 67]; to fabricate transistors on non-planar
substrates [42]; to fabricate infrared plasmonic nanostructure antenna arrays such as
nanodots [68]; to realize metal nanopatterns to promote binding of biomaterials [69];
to fabricate pentacene thin ﬁlm transistors (TFT) [53, 70]; to fabricate NEMS
resonators integrated with CMOS circuitry [58]; and many more that we will not
mention but we refer the interested reader elsewhere [1].
8.8 End of chapter summary
In this chapter we have covered the functioning principles of SL, its major challenges
and advantages. We have shown how this ancient method for surface patterning has
reemerged and it has proven to be very reliable for nanopatterning a wide variety of
materials and on different substrates.
We have covered the different processes that can be performed, enumerated some
of the major applications, described how to fabricate a stencil membrane, …. But,
above all, we have put special emphasis on the different points that need to be
checked in order for a successful implementation of SL, in order to minimize the
impact of each of the four main challenges present in SL. As such, this chapter can
be taken as an initiation guide to SL.
8.9 Student’s corner
• If you want to deposit 100 nm structures, and you can afford ±10 nm, would
you use sputtering, e-beam evaporation, or thermal evaporation? Why?
• Considering a distance source–substrate of 75 cm and a typical source size of
2 mm, how much should the gap be to ensure the requirements above?
• If these 100 nm structures are supposed to be 200 nm thick, what are the main
issues that we would face, and why?
• How would you pattern with SL a ring of 10 μm in diameter and 200 nm in
width, plus 20 nm thick?

Nanofabrication
Nanolithography techniques and their applications
Jose´ Marı´a De Teresa
Chapter 9
Ice lithography
Anpan Han, Ding Zhao and Min Qiu
We present ice lithography (IL), which is electron beam lithography (EBL) using ices
or condensed gases as resists. Examples of ices are water-, alcohol-, and diesel ice.
Water ice can be used as a positive tone resist, while the organic ices serve as negative
tone resists. Instead of spin-coating of resist on mirror polished wafers, the ice resists
can be condensed onto cold surfaces with complicated geometries. This is demon-
strated by patterning on suspended carbon nanotubes. In the future, IL is promising
for 3D printing of functional materials such as metals and semiconductors, and
entirely new applications might emerge.
9.1 Introduction and historical perspective
This book chapter is organized into ﬁve sections. The ﬁrst section is a brief introduction
to the organization of the chapter, and how the different sections are organized to give
the reader the best possible experience. In the second section, to understand the
mechanisms of ice lithography and how to make nanostructures using ices and
electrons, we start with refreshing three important and key fundamental topics; the
energetic electron, condensing gases inside vacuum, and electron–matter interactions.
We will introduce the reader to concepts and terminology that are necessary and
facilitate the following chapters. In the third section, we present ice lithography, which
was invented by Gavin King, Gregor Schurman, Jene Golovchenko and Dan Branton
in 2005 [1]. Here, we present instrumentation, materials and processes. Section 9.4
focuses on published applications of ice lithography, which contains the main results
obtained by this emerging technology. Finally, we discuss future research challenges
and opportunities, which include fundamental research on electron–matter interac-
tions, applications in lithography and additive manufacturing (3D printing), and we
introduce the new concept of Icetronics.
For historic perspectives, it is up to debate when to start; nevertheless, we think it
should start in 1956 when Dr Steere at UC Berkeley built an apparatus to freeze and
fracture biological specimens for electron microscopy studies [2]. After a post-doc at
doi:10.1088/978-0-7503-2608-7ch9
9-1
ª IOP Publishing Ltd 2020. All rights,
including for text and data mining (TDM), artificial intelligence (AI) training, and similar technologies, are reserved.

[Page 317]
ETH Zurich, Dan Branton, returned to the US and used the reﬁned Swiss freeze
fracturing method to conclude that cell membranes were lipid bilayers, which later
led to the discovery of membrane proteins, and the development of the ‘ﬂuid mosaic
model’ of the cell membrane in 1972 [3]. In 1990, the Human Genome Project was
launched, and it had the aim and goal to determine human DNA. Dan Branton and
Jene Golovchenko joined forces to research and develop nanopore sequencing,
which is a commercial success today [4]. During their push for nanopore sequencing
in 2005, they needed a pristine nanolithography method for building nanopore
sequencing devices. Because Jene was involved in proton–ice interactions earlier at
Bell labs, and Dan worked with freeze fracturing which involved freezing samples at
liquid nitrogen temperatures and electron microscopy, they had the enabling
knowledge, and agreed to push for ice lithography. Anpan Han joined the Nanopore
group to build the ﬁrst dedicated ice lithography instrument [5]. Min Qiu and Ding
Zhao became involved in ice lithography in 2012. The ﬁrst organic ice work was
reported in 2017 [6], and 3D metallic structures were reported in 2018 [7]. Our research
is still in its infancy, and we see great potential that we share in this book chapter.
9.2 Fundamentals
9.2.1 Energetic electrons
The ﬁrst ingredient in ice lithography is the electrons. The electron is a fundamental
particle, and evidently the literature concerning the electron is colossal. In this section,
we will present its importance in two areas that are relevant for ice lithography. First,
its role in chemistry will be presented. Second, its role in scanning electron microscopy
(SEM) for nanometer imaging and electron beam lithography will be summarized.
First, in the Bohr model of the hydrogen atom, the positive charged proton nucleus
is in the center of the atom, and the negatively charged electron forms a shell around
the nucleus. If the electron is removed from the hydrogen atom, the remaining
positively charged nucleus is referred to as an ion, which is chemically unstable, and it
will react with other atoms or molecules to reduce its chemical energy. Two hydrogen
atoms can form the more energetically favorable hydrogen molecule. Here, the
covalent bond between the two atoms consists of the two electrons that form an
electron pair (Lewis pair). The Lewis pair form a common shell around the two nuclei
that is separated by a distance deﬁned as the bond length. If one electron is removed
from the hydrogen molecule, there is an unpaired valence electron, and a dihydrogen
cation is formed. In chemistry, these energetic dihydrogen cations with unpaired
valence electron are also referred to as radicals. The same picture can be translated to
most molecules.
In the next sections, free electrons will be interacting with matter, which will cause
chemical reactions. In other words, the free electrons will interact with the electrons
bound to the nucleus. We choose to approach chemical reactions by considering and
focusing on the energy exchange. Breaking a chemical covalent bond, i.e. removing
an atom from a molecule requires energy. The bond-dissociation energy (BDE) is
the energy needed for breaking chemical bonds, i.e. removing electrons from the
Lewis pair, which is also known as radicalization. The energy is between 1 and 12 eV
Nanofabrication
9-2

[Page 318]
per bond. The BDE for C–C and C–H bond in alkanes is about 4 eV. Nitrogen
bonds are 9.79 eV, and ﬂuorine bonds are 1.63 eV.
While breaking chemical bonds happens in physical processes and provides the
upper limits of required energy, one central subset of chemistry is the science of electron
exchange and sharing between atoms and molecules. Examples are reduction–
oxidation (redox) reactions in which the oxidation states of atoms are changed. In a
redox reaction, the molecule that loses an electron is oxidized, and the molecule that
gains an electron is reduced. Note that oxygen does not have to be present in a redox
reaction, but electrons are essential. The energy exchange in redox reactions is in the
order of 100 kJ mol−1 or 1 eV per molecule. Some redox reactions are spontaneous,
and others require the addition of energy. For reduction to take place, electrons do not
have to come from another molecule, and they can be delivered by an external electron
source. This leads to electro-chemistry, and here the electrons required for redox
reactions are directly injected or removed by an external source. The energies that are
involved in electrochemical cells are also in the order of 1 eV. For galvanic Daniell
cells, the produced energy is 213 kJ mol−1 or 2.1 eV per molecule. In conclusion, the
energies required for many chemical reactions are in the order of 1–10 eV.
Second, electrons play a central role in transmission electron microscopy (TEM)
and SEM. For imaging applications, please refer to Williams and Carter [8],
Goldstein et al [9], and other excellent textbooks in electron microscopy. In brief,
inside the SEM vacuum chamber, free electrons are extracted from point electron
sources and an external electric ﬁeld is applied. Provided by the applied ﬁeld, the
electron energy can be adjusted from 1 keV to 30 keV. Using electrostatic
or electromagnetic lenses, the electrons are tightly focused into an electron beam
(e-beam). Modern SEMs create beams with diameters less than 1 nm. The e-beam
current ranges between a few pA to mA. The SEM image is formed by scanning the
e-beam across the sample. When the e-beam interacts with the sample, secondary
electrons (SE) are ejected from the sample and collected by a detector. The signal is
transduced into an electrical signal, digitized, and displayed on a computer screen.
The displayed signal is correlated with the number of ejected SE, such that a brighter
signal corresponds to a larger number of SE. Each pixel on the screen corresponds to
the scanning e-beam hitting a spot on the sample.
As in optical lithography, in EBL the electron microscope is adapted for lithography
applications (see chapter 3 in the present book). For a book on EBL please refer to [10],
chapter 3. Regarding instrumentation, for most research applications, Gaussian EBL
systems are used. The electron optics are very similar to SEMs, and many EBL systems
are adapted SEMs. Some SEM companies offer EBL as an optional module. The EBL
option consists of an electrostatic e-beam blanker which switches the beam on-off
quickly, a pattern generator which takes control of the scanning coils in the SEM to
position the e-beam and draws user-designed patterns, a picoampere meter that
measures the beam current, and lithography software.
9.2.2 Ices, condensed gases in vacuum
The second ingredient for ice lithography is ice. We deﬁne ices as solids of condensed
gases. Water ice is an example of ice. Other notable ices are CO2 ice, which is also
Nanofabrication
9-3

[Page 319]
known as dry ice, ammonia and methane ices, which are found on cold planets and
asteroids. A large group of matter that are gases or liquids at ambient conditions are
solids in vacuum and low temperatures. Many organic molecules, as we normally
know them, are gases or liquids. Examples are octane which is petrol and isopropanol
which is used for, e.g. cleaning. Theoretically, most organic gases and liquids condense
to solids or ices at low temperature in vacuum. The condensation condition of matter is
given by its phase diagram (ﬁgure 9.1). Under closed equilibrium conditions, depend-
ing on the temperature and pressure, matter can be in four different phases; solid, gas,
liquid or supercritical ﬂuid. To change phases the materials often have to pass the ‘lines
of equilibrium’. At certain conditions two or three phases coexist. For example, at the
triple point the material takes the forms of gas, liquid and solid, and all these phases are
in equilibrium. At the lines of equilibrium, the matter contains two phases. An example
is the liquid–air interface of water; here the water vapor (gas) is in equilibrium with its
liquid phase. At the surface of solids, the solid phase is in equilibrium with the gas
phase. The vapor pressure (VP) is given by the semi-empirical Antoine equation, which
is derived from the Clausius–Clapeyron relation.
=
−
+

(9.1)

p is VP, A, B, C are material speciﬁc constants, T is temperature.
Moving away from the closed system, and into a dynamic open system, the rate of
phase change becomes important. The sublimation rate from a planar surface of
water is derived by Estermann [11].
Figure 9.1. Phase diagram illustration of a selected material for ice lithography. Depending on the pressure (P)
and temperature (T ) the material can exist in three different phases. At the lines of equilibrium, the material
exists in two different phases. Inside the GIS, the vapor phase of the material is in equilibrium with the solid or
liquid phase. The GIS temperature can be adjusted such that the vapor pressure can be adapted, and the blue
line highlights the material phases in the GIS environment. The magenta dashed line and the four letters
illustrate the ice condensation process. The material vapor in the GIS (A) is injected to the low-pressure
vacuum chamber (B), and the vapor is condensed on the cold sample on the cryostage (C). The material vapor
can be sublimated by increasing the cryostage temperature (D). The green line shows the material phases on
the cryostage.

S is the sublimation mass ﬂux and the unit is given in kg m−2 s−1, Mw is the molecular
weight, R is the universal gas constant. Equation (9.2) states the sublimation rate is
proportional to the VP at equilibrium, and less dependent on the molecular weight. For
water ice, at 70 K, the sublimation rate is 1 molecule cm−2 h−1 [11], and hence under
the appropriate conditions, ice is very stable.
The phase diagram, and equations (9.1) and (9.2) guide our selection of materials for
ice lithography applications. We follow the phases and phase changes of the selected
material during the ice deposition process (ﬁgure 9.1); ﬁrst the selected material is
loaded into the gas injection system (GIS, see section 9.3 for process and instrumenta-
tion), and inside the GIS the vapor phase is in equilibrium with the solid or liquid phase
of the material. Second, the vapor is injected into the SEM vacuum, and for a fraction
of a second, the material is only in the vapor phase. Third, the material is then
condensed into a solid that is in equilibrium with its vapor phase at low temperature.
Finally, we can heat the cryostage to sublimate the ice. Following this process, a
suitable ice must have two desired properties. First, at room temperature (298 K), the
material must have a suitable VP which could be practically controlled by the GIS.
The GIS temperature could be adjusted to control the VP of the selected material.
Second, the ice must be stable at cryogenic temperature in vacuum (10−6 Torr in the
SEM chamber), which requires its VP must be sufﬁciently low such that the
sublimation is small. The low VP is also required to maintain a stable SEM vacuum
for subsequent e-beam processing. The cryostage temperature could be adjusted for
freezing ice.
In table 9.1, we present selected materials that have and have not the two desired
properties. We give the extrapolated VP values at 60, 150 and 298 K. 298 K is room
temperature, 150 K is easily obtained by liquid nitrogen (LN2) cooled cryostages,
and the more expensive helium based cryotechnology is needed to cool samples
below 60 K. A selection of the materials in table 9.1 has already been used in our
publications, while others are being evaluated or considered for future studies.
Speciﬁc Antoine constants of materials are from www.webbook.nist.gov, however,
the constants are not measured at low temperatures, and hence the extrapolated VPs
are rough estimates and there might even be large deviations. We use water as a
benchmark material. The water VP at room temperature is 23.5 Torr, and VP of
hydrated salts are a few Torr, which work nicely with GIS. Water VP at 150 K is
5 × 10−8 Torr, which is much smaller than the SEM vacuum pressure. Water VP at
60 K is extrapolated to 2 × 10−57 Torr, which is extremely low, and it cannot be measured
yet (UHV pressures are in the range of 10−9 Torr). To condense Xe, CO2, the
cryostage must be cooled by He based cryogenic technology. Methane, hydrogen and
oxygen are volatile at 60 K. Linear alkanes with more than seven carbons, and small
alcohol molecules can be used for ice lithography using LN2 cooled cryostages. The
styrene and naphthalene aromatics can be used with LN2 based cryostages, while
benzene needs He based cryotechnologies. Importantly, the aromatics are the basic
building blocks of graphene, carbon nanotubes, and organic semiconductors. Many

[Page 322]
different halogens containing organic chemicals can be used for LN2 based cryo-
stage. Another interesting group of organic materials is organometallics, which is
often applied for chemical vapor deposition (CVD) and atomic layer deposition
(ALD). Most elements can be incorporated into organic chemicals. Many organo-
metallics are suitable for ice lithography applications. It is important to mention the
safety concerns; some organic chemicals are very reactive, even at room temper-
ature. In table 9.1, while most chemicals have a sufﬁciently low VP at 150 K, a few
chemicals have low VP at 298 K too (e.g. tetradecane, naphthalene), which means
that the gas injection process and hence ice formation can be very slow. Fortunately,
higher VP pressures and ice deposition rates can be obtained by increasing the GIS
temperature.
The condensed gases, solids, can be crystalline or amorphous (glassy) or a mixture
of both. Crystals have well organized lattice structures, while atoms in glasses are
packed in a random fashion. Glasses are formed when the phase change is extremely
rapid such that crystal formation is not possible. Because of the difference in
structure, the material properties are very different, which we will not discuss in
detail. One important difference is the density of glasses can be signiﬁcantly smaller
than crystals. Because the atoms in glasses are not organized in the positions with the
least energy as in the case of crystals, if the temperature is ﬁnite, the atoms in the
glass will slowly reorganize to form stable crystals. At high temperatures, the lattice
vibrations enhance the glass-to-crystal transition process. The critical temperature is
referred to as the glass transition temperature (Tg). For ice lithography, the gases
are condensed on a cold surface, and a glass thin-ﬁlm is often formed. If the cold
surface temperature is near or below the Tg, nanocrystalline thin-ﬁlms are formed in
the condensation process. If a glass thin-ﬁlm is heated to the Tg, the glass thin-ﬁlm
reorganizes into nanocrystalline thin-ﬁlms (unpublished results). If the sample
temperature approaches the glass transition temperature, crystals are formed, and
voids in the original ﬁlm can be observed because glasses have a smaller density
than crystals. The transition of glasses to crystals takes place at high temperatures,
and it is often associated with increased sublimation.
As for the fundamental research of organic glasses, an extremely relevant research
thrust led by Edigar and colleagues, studies organic glass thin-ﬁlms made by physical
vapor deposition (PVD). Unlike with our work, they started to work with organic
molecules with Tg higher than room temperature. In 2007, they discovered that the
glasses prepared by PVD were exceptionally thermodynamically stable [12]. The
stability is manifested by a higher density. Furthermore, during heating organic glasses,
here, the thin-ﬁlm sublimates at an ‘onset temperature’ (Tonset) which can be 20 K
higher than the Tg [13]. For unstable organic glasses Tonset is equal to Tg. Following
this discovery, an ongoing world-wide effort has the aim to understand the funda-
mental mechanisms behind this stability. The theoretical foundation was laid down by
de Pablo and team [14]. In 2017, Edigar and team published condensation of organic
molecules such as isopropanol at cryogenic temperatures [13]. Their fundamental
ﬁndings are nicely presented and discussed in a 2017 review by Edigar [15]. For ice
lithography application, the three most relevant ﬁndings are the following. First, some
organic molecules can form very stable glasses, while others cannot. Tylinski et al,
Nanofabrication
9-7

[Page 323]
showed that for alcohol glasses, despite their chemical similarities, there was a wide
range of kinetic stability [13]. Second, there is a sweet spot for the stability of organic
glasses, i.e. during PVD, the substrate temperature (Ts) should be slightly lower than
Tg, and the deposition should be slow such the molecules have sufﬁcient time to
densely pack. Third, depending on the molecular structure, the organic glass ﬁlm can
be anisotropic [16].
9.2.3 Electron–ice interactions
For the overview of electron–matter interactions (EMI), we refer to several excellent
textbook chapters [8, 9]. There is also a very relevant EMI for electron-beam
lithography application (chapter 3 in [10]). In what follows, we will summarize from
the above references and then apply them for electron–ice interactions.
Figure 9.2 illustrates different processes when energetic electrons interact with a solid.
The energetic electron is called the primary electron (PE). The electron–matter
interaction can be inelastic or elastic. In elastic interactions, the incoming electron
does not lose any energy and it is scattered. Backscattered electrons are ejected out of
the sample by elastic interactions, and their energy is slightly smaller than the PE. In
inelastic interactions, the incoming PE energy is reduced, and depending on the energy
Figure 9.2. (a) Three energetic primary electrons (PE) interacting with an atom in solid matter. Note that the
change in trajectory is highly exaggerated. The ﬁrst PE (blue) interacts with a valence electron (VE), and a
secondary electron is ejected (SE). The second PE (green) interacts with the nucleus and its trajectory changes
and radiates bremsstrahlung (BS). The third PE (red) interacts with a strongly bound K-shell electron, and the
electron is ejected as an SE. The atom is ionized in the ﬁrst and third PE–matter interaction. The ionized atom
can relax to lower energies by chemical reaction using the VE, or emitting Auger SE or characteristic x-rays.
PE can also interact with the material lattice and generate plasmons and phonons. (b) The PE interacts with ice
thin-ﬁlm on a silicon substrate. A cascade of SE is generated in close proximity to the PE path. The ﬁrst
cascade of SE is illustrated in red, the second cascade of SE is in yellow and the third in purple. Later in this
section, we will see that the range of the cascade of reactions is few nm for a 100-nm-thick organic ice thin-ﬁlm.
The long-range radiation will also cause cascading interactions, however different from electrons, it is spread
over much larger distances.
Nanofabrication
9-8

[Page 324]
magnitude, different processes could take place. It is important to point out that in SEM
a high energy PE hitting a sample is likely to cause a cascade of different processes. The
likelihood of an electron–matter interaction is given by the interaction cross section. The
most likely interaction is generation of plasmons (10−22 m2), phonons, bremsstrahlung,
followed by ionization (10−23 m2), and ﬁnally SE (10−25 m2). Plasmons and phonons
are, respectively, electron and lattice oscillations in the sample, which ultimately
generates heat. The energy of bremsstrahlung ranges from a few eV to near PE energy,
and the high energy radiation can cause ionization. Energetic electrons or radiation
cause ionization, which could involve removal of electrons in the inner electron shells, or
by removing valence electrons (VE) from the Lewis pair, i.e. breaking bonds (section
9.2.1). While the energy needed to break C–C bonds is 3.6 eV, the energy needed to
remove K-shell electrons (KE) ranges from 100 to 105 eV. The energetic molecules
and atoms can release their energies in three different processes. First, they can
react with other molecules or recombine as described in section 9.2.1. At lower
electron doses, bonds are broken and larger molecules will be broken into smaller
molecules. At electron doses above a critical threshold, cross-linking to large
molecule networks takes place. We will discuss this chemistry in detail later.
Second, the energetic molecule can emit characteristic x-rays. Third, characteristic
Auger electrons can be ejected to reduce excitation state. High energy character-
istic x-rays and Auger electrons can cause further cascading ionization. While the
free mean path of x-rays is long, the short ranged electrons will cause ionization a few
nm next to its creation.1
A quantitative view of the electron–matter interaction is to consider the energy
loss of the PE as it interacts with the sample. The energy loss (-dE) along the path
(ds) is given by the Bethe equation
π
ϵ
