Here, N is the number of atoms per unit volume, which is proportional to density, Z
is the atomic number, E0 is the primary electron energy, I is the mean ionization
energy that is deﬁned as I = (9.76 + 58.8 Z−1.9)Z. As a comparison, for a 20 keV PE
interacting with carbon, the energy loss is 2.24 eV nm−1, while it is 6.94 eV nm−1 for
silver. Important for organic ices that consist mainly of hydrogen, carbon and
oxygen, the electrons will mainly interact with the carbon and oxygen. For 100-nm-
thick thin-ﬁlms deposited on a silicon substrate the energy loss is about 200 eV, and
hence sufﬁcient for about 100 chemical reactions. Most of the energy will be released
in the silicon substrate.
Based on the Bethe equations, the PE trajectory and its energy loss can be
visualized with Monte Carlo simulations. Casino freeware was used for the
1 The deﬁnition of SE is blurred in the literature. Seen from an electron–matter interaction perspective, SE are
all electrons that are generated by the cascade of PE and matter interaction. While for electron microscopist,
SE are low energy electrons that can be detected, i.e. only the electrons that escape the material surface are
considered as SE. In the following discussions, we take the electron–matter interaction perspective, and the
energy of SE ranges between a few eV to near PE electron energy.
Nanofabrication
9-9

[Page 325]
simulations (ﬁgure 9.3(a)). In the simulation, we set the e-beam diameter to 20 nm, and
PE energy to 5 keV. For water ice, the interaction volume has an onion shape. The
initial PE beam and its energy spread out. 50% of the energy is deposited in a cylinder
that is 200 nm tall and 50 nm in diameter (ﬁgure 9.3(b)). 95% of the energy is contained
in the top 600 nm of the ice. Note the energy extends 15 nm in the lateral direction. For
water ice interacting with 20 keV PE, 50% is deposited in a cylinder that is 2000 nm tall
and 50 nm in diameter (ﬁgure 9.3(c)), and the interaction volume is much larger than for
the 5 keV case. At 5 keV, the interaction volume for octane is very similar to water
(ﬁgure 9.3(d)), which can be anticipated from the Bethe equation. If the octane ice is
deposited as a 100-nm-thick thin-ﬁlm on a silicon substrate (ﬁgure 9.3(e)), we see that
because Si has a larger Z than C, there is an energy concentration at the silicon surface,
and the interaction volume is smaller than for pure octane ice. For tungsten hexaﬂuoride
ice condensed on silicon substrate, 90% of the energy is deposited in the ice, while very
little energy enters the underlying silicon substrate.
Figure 9.3. Monte Carlo simulations using Casino freeware. PE trajectories of 5 keV electrons that are interacting
with water ice (a) and energy by position distribution (b). 90% of the energy is outside the purple line, 50% of the
energy is outside the yellow line, and 5% of the energy is outside the cyan line. 20 keV electrons interacting with
water ice (c). 5 keV electrons interacting with solid octane (d) and 100-nm-thick octane thin-ﬁlm on silicon
substrate (e). 5 keV electrons interacting with 100-nm-thick tungsten hexaﬂuoride thin-ﬁlm on silicon (f).
Nanofabrication
9-10

[Page 326]
The direct consequence of the above analysis for ice lithography, using water and
carbon containing organic ices, the interaction volume is almost independent on the
chemistry. This makes the method very robust and predictable. In contrast, if
organometallic ices are used, the interaction depth will depend on the density and Z
of the metal atom in the organic molecule. The interaction volume can be adjusted
by modulating the PE energy, and 20 keV can be used for processing at least 2 μm of
hydrocarbon ice. For octane thin-ﬁlms deposited on silicon substrates (ﬁgure 9.3(e)),
the energy distribution in the ice ﬁlm has the shape of a truncated cone whose side
proﬁle angle has important consequences for resist applications.
In the electron–matter interaction volume, ionization and chemical radical
formation take place. The energetic molecules can then take part in several different
chemical reaction pathways (ﬁgure 9.4). First, recombination can take place and the
original molecule is reformed. Second, the chemical radical is formed on the ice thin-
ﬁlm surface, and it has very high VP and sublimates. As an example, for alkane ices,
these high VP radicals are small molecules that contain hydrogen and a few carbon
atoms, whose VP are in the range of methane (table 9.1). Third, the radical reacts
with another molecule and forms a new network molecule with it, which has a very
low VP such that it forms a stable deposit at room temperature. This is one of the
main ﬁndings in the paper by Elsukova et al [19], which we encourage the reader to
enjoy in full. In the same study the ultimate resolution of ice lithography is also
presented and discussed.
If the reaction tips in favor of forming the network molecule, the deposit is
stable at room temperature and it is most likely a cross-linked network composed of
elements and molecule parts of the condensed ice. For instance, in the case of alkane
Figure 9.4. Chemistry of electrons interacting with hydrocarbons and down stream chemical reaction
pathways. The energetic electrons cause bond scissions, and a small hydrocarbon radical containing one
carbon and a large radical are created. The smaller radical may vaporize, recombine, or react with other
molecules. The larger radical can recombine or react with other molecules. If the reaction with larger molecules
dominates, large networks are formed. In terms of resist, the initial ice acts as a negative tone resist. Most
organic ices published so far are considered as negative tone resists. If the evaporation dominates, no network
will be formed, and the e-beam irradiated areas will be free from molecules. In terms of resist, the initial ice acts
as a positive tone resist.
Nanofabrication
9-11

[Page 327]
ices, the cross-linked molecule is expected to have many similarities to cross-linked
polyethylenes. Meanwhile, for HMDSO ices, the cross-linked molecule is expected
to behave as silicon rubbers such as PDMS (table 9.1). Relevant to lithography,
these ices could be used as negative tone resists. In contrast, ices composed of
elements that cannot form a stable network can only change chemistry by reaction
path 2, which is the case for water and iodine. Here ices could be used as positive
tone resists.
9.2.4 Summary
The ingredients of ice lithography are the energetic electrons and thin-ﬁlms of
condensed gases. The energy of the electrons are more than sufﬁcient to facilitate
chemical reactions. When the energetic electrons interact with the ice, a cascade of
chemical reactions takes place, which leads to either cross-linking or chemical
dissociation of the initial condensed gas. The product is either a polymer substance
or smaller volatile molecules. There are thousands of different organic molecules
that could be frozen into thin-ﬁlms, and we have only studied a handful.
9.3 Lithography using electrons and ices
The basic principle of IL is simple; a vaporized material coats a cold surface and
forms an ice resist layer, which is subsequently exposed to a focused electron beam
provided by an electron microscope. This brief description shows IL has high
similarity to both EBL and FEBID. In particular, IL is an indirect fabrication
method that relies on resist masks like EBL; meanwhile, it employs a gas precursor
that interacts with electrons like FEBID. However, there are also notable important
differences among these methods, and IL provides value to the nanolithography
process and toolbox. In this section, we focus on practical IL procedures, perform-
ance of ices, and developing custom IL instruments.
9.3.1 Ice lithography process
A detailed process ﬂow of IL is depicted in ﬁgure 9.5 [22]. It reveals that IL skips spin-
coating and chemical development required in EBL, resulting in a more streamlined
process. It also shows that IL involves an electron–solid interaction, differing from
FEBID where electrons interact with a gas that is condensed onto a surface [22].
During IL, the precursor gas ﬁrstly condenses onto the precooled sample and produces
a uniform amorphous ice thin-ﬁlm. Then the e-beam bombards the ice resist in speciﬁc
regions for pattern generation. Depending on different types of electron–matter
interaction (section 9.2.3), the e-beam patterning procedure in IL can be generally
categorized into a subtractive process or an additive process.
For water ice, electrons remove the resist within the exposure areas (ﬁgure 9.5(b))
and the ice patterns subsequently are ﬁlled with required materials (e.g. metal) by
deposition (ﬁgure 9.5(d)). Finally, the sample is immersed into alcohol at room
temperature to remove all ice resist with residuals. Except for the last lift-off step or
‘melt-off’ step, the whole IL process for water ice is accomplished in vacuum.
Nanofabrication
9-12

[Page 328]
For alkane ice, cross-linking occurs during electron–ice interaction. Heavily cross-
linked molecules within the exposure areas form a solidiﬁed pattern, which remains
in vacuum even after heating the sample to room temperature (ﬁgure 9.5(g)). This
carbonaceous product is also nonvolatile and chemically inert at atmospheric pressure,
thus suitable as an etching mask for transferring the resist pattern to the underlying
material. Here, oxygen plasma cleaning can be used for resist removal.
9.3.2 Ice lithography resist
Like the resist classiﬁcation in EBL, IL resists can be divided into positive-tone resists
and negative-tone resists (section 9.2.3). We summarize and compare IL resists with
EBL resists and a FEBID precursor in table 9.2. Although several chemicals have the
potential to be IL resists (table 9.1), only water ice has been demonstrated as a positive-
tone resist to date, and used to fabricate nanostructures with sub-10 nm features [18].
Organic ice from simple organic molecules, e.g. alkanes, alcohols, shows negative-
resist-like capabilities. The linewidth of patterns deﬁned in organic ice depends on both
beam current of the instrument and hydrocarbon length of the ice resist molecules. In
SEM based IL, the optimal linewidths in nonane (C9H20) and anisole (C7H8O) ice are
usually 100 and 60 nm, respectively. However, the linewidth could be signiﬁcantly
reduced, e.g. <5 nm in octane (C8H18) ice [19], when using an e-beam provided by a
transmission electron microscope (TEM). This value is comparable to the ﬁnest
features achieved in EBL resists.
In addition to linewidth, contrast and sensitivity are also important resist proper-
ties. The plots in ﬁgure 9.6 show the contrast curves for two types of ice resists. For
water ice, the thickness can be measured in situ through tilted SEM imaging. It is
challenging to obtain the initial thickness of organic ice. Hence, we measure the
thickness of exposed resist at atmospheric pressure by AFM, which is normalized to
the thickness of completely exposed resist. Electron dose represents the number of
electrons that are applied to a unit area of resists. Contrast is the slope of the curve.
Figure 9.5. Ice lithography process ﬂow. (a) Vaporized material is frozen onto the cold sample to form a layer
of ice resist. (b and f) Focused e-beam exposes ice resist for patterning. For water ice, (c) the exposed ice
vanishes and pattern transfer is realized after (d) metallization and (e) lift-off. For alkane ice, (g) only the
exposed areas remain after heating and patterns are transferred by (h) plasma etching and (i) oxygen plasma
for resist removal. Adapted with permission from [22]. CC BY 4.0.
Nanofabrication
9-13

[Page 329]
Generally, an IL resist with high contrast enables close packing of patterned features,
while that with low contrast allows ﬂexible thickness control of partially exposed
resists, which is beneﬁcial for greyscale lithography. Sensitivity is deﬁned as the dose
at which the exposed features have half of the thickness of the initial positive-tone
resist or of a completely exposed negative-tone resist. Sensitivity is related to the
patterning speed. As shown in table 9.2, EBL resists need lower dose than IL resists,
which results in a smaller number of electrons required during EBL, and a higher
writing speed of patterning, thus a larger throughput. It is worth noting that FEBID
requires 250 mC cm−2 to deposit a single atomic layer from an organometallic
precursor. This dose stays on the same order of magnitude as IL resists. However,
actual FEBID dose increases in proportion to the thickness of deposit and many
more electrons are needed for thicker deposits. Compared to FEBID, the electron
dose in IL is less dependent on resist thickness.

9.3.3 Ice lithography instrument
Ice lithography research needs the support of cutting-edge instrument development.
Historically, the earliest experiments on nanopatterning ice by e-beam were conducted in
an SEM with a cryosystem designed for biological applications, which was actually not
suitable for IL. In 2010, the same research group demonstrated the successful IL process
for fabricating electronic devices and reported the ﬁrst dedicated IL instrument [5].
Inspired and guided by this system, another two instruments have been subsequently
constructed at Technical University of Denmark (DTU) and Zhejiang University
(ZJU), respectively. The DTU design (ﬁgure 9.7(a), from [22]) focuses on nano-
fabrication using organic ice resists. It is highly accessible and modular. Except for
the load-lock, all subsystems can be assembled or disassembled quickly, enabling
ﬂexible switching between a conventional SEM imaging system and a specialized IL
instrument. In China, a cryogenic metallization system is integrated to the IL instru-
ment (ﬁgure 9.7(b)). Therefore, this instrument can also be used for the growth and in
situ characterization of metal ﬁlms at cryogenic temperature. To our best knowledge,
the Chinese instrument is the most advanced today. However, the research ﬁeld is
Figure 9.7. IL instruments at (a) Technical University of Denmark (DTU) and (b) at Zhejiang University
(ZJU) in China. (c) Schematics of IL instrument consisting of six subsystems. Adapted with permission from
[22]. CC BY 4.0.
Nanofabrication
9-15

[Page 331]
growing, and at least four other instruments around the world are under construction
and development.
As shown in ﬁgure 9.7(c), an IL instrument usually consists of ﬁve (e.g. DTU
instrument) or six (e.g. ZJU instrument) subsystems, depending on the type of resists and
processes involved. Here, Subsystem 1 is a commercial SEM, aiming at providing a
focused electron beam and a sample stage with high-precision displacement. Subsystem 2
is an EBL module including a beam blanker, enabling rapid e-beam writing on ice resists.
Both are off-the-shelf subsystems, while the others are highly customized as follows.
Subsystem 3: A cryogenic system includes a cryostage for cooling sample and a
cold trap for shielding sample from vacuum contaminations. A heater is also
required for thermoregulation or the sublimation of ice resists in vacuum. As in the
case of DTU design (ﬁgure 9.8), the cryostage and cold trap are connected together
with a copper braid, which has a large cross-section (~1 cm) to enhance thermal
conductivity while still allowing mechanical ﬂexibility during stage moving. The
cold trap is mounted onto a custom ﬂange at the right side of the SEM, and clamped
to a long copper rod, whose external end is immersed into liquid nitrogen in a
vacuum ﬂask. Therefore, the cryostage is cooled through the copper braid with the
same liquid nitrogen source as the cold trap. Figure 9.8 also shows the detailed
design of the cryostage. Four polyether ether ketone (PEEK) pillars are used to
Figure 9.8. Schematics of the cryogenic system in DTU design. Inset shows assemblies of the cryostage and
sample holder. Adapted with permission from [20]. Copyright (2018) Elsevier.
Nanofabrication
9-16

[Page 332]
thermally insulate the upper part of the cryostage from the lower part, which is
attached to an adapted SEM stage dovetail. To improve heat transfer, a spring clip
ﬁrmly holds the sample holder down when it slides into the cryostage. Temperature
is adjusted with a cartridge heater inside the cryostage.
Subsystem 4: A gas injection system aims at introducing water or organic vapor
into the SEM chamber through a stainless-steel nozzle. A leak valve connected to the
nozzle restricts the injection rate of vapor, thus determining the thickness of ice resist.
A vacuum gauge is used to monitor the gas pressure and its evolution during vapor
deposition. Chemical precursors such as MgSO4·7H2O for water ice or C9H20 for
organic ice, are stored in a glass vial with vacuum connections. To avoid additional
vibrations to SEM, the pumping line in this subsystem is typically removed during
e-beam writing and attached only when loading a new precursor.
Subsystem 5: A load-lock allows fast exchange and transfer of samples without
breaking the vacuum and cryogenic conditions in the SEM chamber. It can be also
useful for conventional room-temperature EBL. This small side chamber has its own
turbo and roughing pump, separated from those in SEM. The roughing pump runs a
parallel line to Subsystem 4, but can be isolated and operated independently. Sample
holders are manipulated by a rotatable transfer arm.
Subsystem 6: A cryogenic metallization system is required for processing water ice
resist. A cryostage and a liquid nitrogen Dewar are included to maintain ice resist
during thermal evaporation or magnetron sputtering of materials. As an example in
ZJU design (ﬁgure 9.9), the cryostage is closely attached to the bottom of the Dewar
Figure 9.9. Schematics of the cryogenic metallization system in ZJU design. Adapted with permission from [7].
Copyright (2018) American Chemical Society.
Nanofabrication
9-17

[Page 333]
for minimizing thermal loss along the cooling path. When transferred into this
subsystem, the sample holder is rotated and then inserted into the cryostage with its
face down to the thermal evaporator.
9.4 Applications
9.4.1 Nanofabrication on 3D structures
Nanofabrication on 3D structures has attracted growing attention in various research
ﬁelds. For instance, a traditional aplanatic lens with aberration correction usually
consists of two or more optical elements, while optical metasurfaces can manipulate
light at the subwavelength scale and exhibit the expected function in a single element.
However, the fabrication of an aplanatic metasurface is challenging as one necessary
step is patterning on a 3D structure. Other examples are endoscopes and ﬁbre-
imaging systems. The realization of such miniature optical instruments also relies on
advanced 3D nanofabrication techniques.
Since spin and spray coating can only attain uniform thin resist coatings on
planar substrates, traditional EBL processing is limited to ﬂat substrates. However,
ice lithography appears highly promising for nanofabrication on 3D structures
attributed to vapor-deposited ice resists. One example is patterning on AFM
probes [18], as shown in ﬁgure 9.10. In IL, we can coat all accessible cold surfaces
with water ice, even on a pyramidal tip of the AFM probe. Beneﬁting from the high
dose for ice removal, a glance at the target structure in SEM will not affect the ice
mask seriously but can help us position exposure areas without additional marks.
Then we pattern a rectangle area centered on the tip by the focused e-beam to
remove the water ice and reveal the underlying tip surface. After metallization, Au
covers the probe including the exposed tip with an adhesion layer of Ti. Lift-off is
implemented through immersing the sample in isopropanol and melting water ice
Figure 9.10. (a) Processes for IL to cap an AFM Si3N4 tip. (b) and (c) SEM images of the AFM probe whose
tip is capped with 1-nm-thick Ti and 20-nm-thick Au. (d) Metallic nanoparticle arrays on a single Ag nanowire
with a diameter of 160 nm. SEM images with red and green frames show the nanowire after e-beam exposure
and metallization, respectively. Adapted with permission from [7, 18]. Copyright (2012, 2018) American
Chemical Society.
Nanofabrication
9-18

[Page 334]
at room temperature. Finally, a clean and residual-free AFM tip with a metal cap
is obtained.
The next example is patterning on a single nanowire [7]. As just mentioned, we are
able to observe the target structure under the ice resist through SEM imaging
immediately. This advantage is clearly illustrated in ﬁgure 9.10(d). Here, we arrange
the exposure areas upon the nanowire. After metallization and lift-off, we achieve a
line of nanoparticles located on top of the nanowire. The diameter of the nanowire is
160 nm. It is clear that our alignment accuracy is better than 50 nm.
9.4.2 Nanofabrication on fragile structures
The second advantage of IL is patterning on fragile structures [18]. For instance, silicon
nitride membranes are too fragile to withstand ultrasonication, and polymeric resists
are usually required to assist lift-off in traditional EBL processing. In IL, we could
easily pattern the surface of a fragile freestanding Si3N4 membrane, which is supported
on a Si wafer frame. As shown in ﬁgure 9.11(a) and (b), metallic nanostructures with
sub-10 nm features and bowtie structures with gaps less than 7 nm have been achieved
on the 60-nm-thick Si3N4 membrane, respectively. These TEM images also show that
the sample surface after lift-off is very clean without residuals or contaminations in the
tiny gap. Figure 9.11(c) shows the back side of the Si3N4 membrane, which is located at
the bottom of a 200-μm-deep pit and surrounded by Si. Traditional EBL processing
would hardly apply a uniform resist through this pit. However, we still successfully
perform IL and fabricate nanostructures on such a Si3N4 membrane with a thickness of
20 nm, even near the edge of the pit (ﬁgure 9.11(d)).
Another example is patterning on freely suspended single-walled carbon nano-
tubes. Here, the carbon nanotubes were located over a trench in a freestanding
Figure 9.11. TEM images of (a) three-bladed 5-nm-thick Pd and (b) 5-nm-thick Au with 1-nm-thick Cr
patterns on the planar top surface of a freestanding Si3N4 membrane. (c) and (d) SEM images of 10-nm-thick
Ag with 1-nm-thick Ti patterns on the bottom-facing surface of a Si3N4 membrane. (e) and (f) Al2O3
nanoparticles grown by ALD coaxially on freely suspended nanotubes, where Ti seeds have been fabricated
previously through IL. The white arrows indicate the areas where ice is removed. Adapted with permission
from [18]. Copyright (2012) American Chemical Society.
Nanofabrication
9-19

[Page 335]
silicon nitride membrane (ﬁgure 9.11(e)). As just mentioned, traditional EBL cannot
deal with such a fragile structure either. However, after cooling down the sample, we
can deposit water ice onto the tiny nanotubes. Then we control the e-beam to remove
the ice selectively and deposit a Ti thin-ﬁlm on the surface of the nanotube, which acts
as a seed layer for growing Al2O3 by atomic layer deposition (ALD). As a result, Al2O3
particles are formed on the nanotubes whose locations and sizes are precisely
determined by the IL patterning combined with ALD processing (ﬁgure 9.11(f)).
9.4.3 Rapid prototyping
Rapid prototyping, also known as additive manufacturing or 3D printing, refers to a
group of manufacturing processes, which enables fast 3D fabrication of unprece-
dented engineering and production possibilities. Construction of a part is usually
done by adding layers of one material on top of one another, and materials such as
metals, ceramics and plastics are involved in the fabrication. Examples of rapid
prototyping methods are inkjet printing, stereolithography, two-photon polymer-
ization, and FEBID. In a sense, EBL can also be regarded as a rapid prototyping
processing, since a stacking layer strategy is adopted for fabricating 3D structures.
However, the fabrication of each layer needs a series of procedures including spin-
coating, exposure, developing, deposition and lift-off, and additional markers
should be fabricated previously for registration and alignment between layers. All
these complex steps limit the 3D processing speed of EBL.
IL opens a new possibility for nanoscale rapid prototyping with e-beam [7]. For
instance,we are able to perform a marker-less fabrication with waterice,whichresults in
many fewer processing steps for 3D nanofabrication. Figure 9.12 shows detailed IL
fabrication steps for a pyramidal nanostructure, just through repeating ice deposition,
e-beam exposure and metallization three times. To realize a similar structure, at least 19
processing steps and 8 load–unload operations are involved in traditional EBL
processing. In comparison, IL simpliﬁes the processing, that only requires ten steps
Figure 9.12. 3D layered and suspended nanostructures produced by IL. In situ SEM images after different
processing steps including ice deposition (a, d, and g), e-beam exposure (b, e, and h) and metallization (c, f, and
i). A pyramidal nanostructure obtained using water ice (j) or organic ice (k), with corresponding line scan by
AFM. Similar to greyscale lithography, IL can also realize 3D mushroom-like (l) and bridge-like (m)
nanostructures. Adapted with permission from [6, 7]. Copyright (2017, 2018) American Chemical Society.
Nanofabrication
9-20

[Page 336]
and one load–unload operation. If using organic ice (ﬁgure 9.12(k)), the processing steps
could be further compressed into the cycle of ice deposition and e-beam exposure
followed by one-time lift-off, although alignment markers are needed. In IL, we also
successfully fabricate partially suspended nanostructures like a mushroom (ﬁgure 9.12(l))
or a bridge (ﬁgure 9.12(m)), inspired by the strategy of greyscale lithography. Applying
and combining these approaches will bring new and exciting opportunities in 3D
nanofabrication.
9.4.4 High-resolution patterning
Resolution is a key criterion for the evaluation of a nanolithography method. Sub-10 nm
features have been obtained using water ice. Meanwhile, the resolution of organic ice in
SEM-based IL is usually above 50 nm. It is mainly due to the instrumental limitations of
SEM, such as a broad beam spot and optics instabilities. To minimize these inﬂuences
and achieve higher resolution, we perform IL in an environmental TEM equipped with
a gas cell and a cryostage [19]. The TEM operates at 80 keV and the substrate is a 5-nm-
thick Si3N4 membrane. Therefore, the incident electrons can be highly concentrated into
a tiny spot (∼1 nm) and pass through the substrate swiftly with a small cross section of
scattered secondary electrons, ﬁnally resulting in expected high-resolution patterns.
As mentioned in section 9.2.3, two competing chemical reactions, chain scission
and cross-linking typically take place in the resist during exposure. Here, cross-
linking of the molecules dominates when exposing the organic ice layer to a focused
e-beam, and forms new molecules with an increased molecular weight. The thickness
of ﬁnal solidiﬁed pattern is proportional to the number of molecules cross-linked
enough to become nonvolatile at room temperature. As shown in ﬁgure 9.13, three
organic alkane resists with different molecular lengths are tested. All these molecules
have a simple linear structure with only C−C and C−H bonds. For each organic
resist, the line patterns get wider with increasing beam current, due to the larger
e-beam diameter. Furthermore, a positive correlation between the linewidth and the
molecular length can be achieved under the same beam current. It demonstrates
that the minimal feature size is fundamentally determined by the resist molecules.
These evidences not only show IL has the capability for high-resolution patterning,
but also set a clear direction for approaching the ultimate resolution of IL.
9.5 Future research, opportunities and challenges
9.5.1 Fundamental science research
A careful reader will quickly realize most of the fundamental scientiﬁc questions
are wide open. Starting with the organic ice itself, there are thousands of organic
molecules that can be frozen in vacuum. We have only reported eight chemicals,
and we had poor measurements of freezing points of even fewer. Dr Schick’s
and Dr Ediger’s teams used macroscopic AC nanocalorimetry to measure the
transition temperature of alcohol ices [13, 29]. However, most physical properties
of the organic ices are unknown. Technology is pushing ahead, and we must
answer the most fundamental scientiﬁc questions:
Nanofabrication
9-21

[Page 337]
Figure 9.13. Line patterns on octane (a−c), undecane (d−f), and tetradecane (g−i) in TEM. Linewidth plotted
as a function of (j) beam current and (k) resist molecular length. Adapted with permission from [19]. Copyright
(2018) American Chemical Society.
Nanofabrication
9-22

[Page 338]
1. What is the structure of organic ices? Are they crystalline or amorphous, or
both?
2. What are the condensation, sublimation, glass transition temperatures?
3. What are the electrical properties such as conductivity, dielectric constant,
and bandgap?
4. What are the mechanical properties such as thermal conductivity, density,
and Young’s modulus?
5. One big chemistry question is, what happens if we mix these ices together?
Critical readers will question if valuable efforts should be made to explore this empty
space in science. If space travel is the future, then we should spend valuable effort in
the research of organic ices. Space is the ﬁnal frontier, and the environment is very
different. It is cold and there is vacuum. Most organic chemicals, which are liquids or
gases on Earth, will freeze to organic ices (solids) in space. Future space applications
of organic ices might range from dynamic coatings of spaceships to lubricants at low
temperatures.
9.5.2 Icetronics research
While the applications of organic ices themselves seem very distant and remote,
the earthly applications of e-beam processed organic ices range from quantum
technologies, telecommunications, to advanced production of computer chips.
Lithography applications for e.g. MEMS, sensors, 2D materials, and photonics
research are plentiful. The careful reader with a materials science background
will quickly realize that it is possible to create new materials whose properties are
completely unknown. Indeed, we envision nanoscale additive manufacturing
using e-beam assisted engineering of electronics as an important research direction,
i.e. to 3D print computer chips including functional materials. This technology can
make patterns down to 10 nm, and enable acceptable production speed-up, which
drives applications below 1 μm and in three dimensions. The main hypothesis is that
by selecting and freezing organic compounds and exposing them with an optimized
e-beam, it is possible to print functional materials such as metals, dielectrics and
semiconductors. Because the two required ingredients are ice and electrons, and we
aim to make electronic devices, we introduce the term ‘Icetronics’.
The three future research areas of Icetronics are:
1. To deposit metal conductors by e-beam patterning of organometallic ices.
2. To deposit organic dielectrics whose chemical compositions are similar to
polymers.
3. To deposit organic semiconductors for ﬁeld effect transistor devices.
For metal deposition, many metals could be deposited by using the full range of
organometallics. We would like to focus on Au, because it has good electrical
properties, and it is used in electronics as contact, electrode, and wiring material.
Furthermore, Au is biocompatible, and it is widely used in medical implants. In 2016,
Shawrav et al added water vapor to Au organometallic FEBID process to deposit
Nanofabrication
9-23

[Page 339]
almost pure Au, which have much better electrical properties than prior art [30].
Based on Shawrav et al studies, we hypothesize the formation of an almost pure and
porous Au ﬁlm. We showed that thin-ﬁlm Au can be annealed and densiﬁed at 300 °C
[31], which will increase the electrical performance of the deposit.
For dielectric deposition, we deposited alkanes and aromatic compounds [6]
and silicone elastomer (PDMS) compounds [21]. Unpublished results show that
the optical properties of the deposits are very different; this suggests that the
original organic chemistry plays an important role in the deposit formation.
This is fundamentally different from FEBID deposits, which is independent of the
hydrocarbon precursor [32]. We believe this is caused by fundamental differences
between electron–gas–surface (FEBID) and electron–solid–surface interactions
(Icetronics). The e-beam cross-links the condensed organic ice ﬁlm, and we
hypothesize the resulting composition of the deposits and its physical properties
can be tailored by selecting the appropriate chemistry. It is likely that the deposits
properties are very similar to polymers, which are good barrier material for metal
wires and common dielectric materials.
We can freeze and pattern, e.g. alkanes, siloxanes, and ﬂuorocarbons such as C4F8.
The deposited material is expected to have similar properties to polyethylene, silicone
elastomers, and Teﬂon. Cyclic siloxanes can also do ‘ring opening’ polymerization
reaction, and we hypothesize that this chain reaction could be initiated by direct
injection of electrons using the e-beam. Furthermore, the reaction could be controlled
by co-freezing inhibitors.
For semiconductor deposition, organic semiconductors (OS) are divided into small
molecule OS (SMOS), and polymeric OS (POS) [33]. The fundamental building block
of SMOS is the basic conjugated π-orbital units, which are benzene and thiophene.
High mobility SMOS thin-ﬁlms (e.g. pentacene) are polycrystalline, and the mobility is
related to the size of the crystals. The correlation between thin-ﬁlm structure and
mobility is complicated, and studies show that larger crystals have higher mobility [34].
Apart from pentacene and its derivatives, other SMOS molecules are rubrene, and
anthracine derivatives (9,10-diphenylanthracene, DAP) which is used for OLEDs
[35, 36]. Anthracene, DAP and tetracene have low melting points and sufﬁcient VPs
for injection into the e-beam chamber and frozen to ices.
We could build ﬁeld effect transistor (FET) devices using semiconducting organic ices,
whose process is similar to our previous work on carbon nanotube FETs [17, 18]. For
SMOS, the crystallinity is strongly correlated with the electrical performance. Hence, we
anticipate that there is a strong co-relation among the number of electrons used for cross-
linking of organic semiconducting ices, the crystallinity of the cross-linked product, and
the electrical performance. The electrons needed for cross-linking will also damage and
introduce defects into the ice crystal. The question is how robust these materials are?
Finally, our goal is to 3D print a functional FET using Icetronics technology.
9.5.3 Applications
We have touched on applications throughout the chapter. It is important to point
out that this is a scalable technology thanks to the development of multi-beam
Nanofabrication
9-24

[Page 340]
technologies, which are instruments that have 260 000 e-beams (www.ims.co.at). We
classify applications into two areas. First, in IL applications, the ice serves as a
masking or sacriﬁcial material to pattern a functional material such as metals or
semiconductors. IL is an additional tool in the powerful toolbox of nanolithography.
Second, cross-linked organic ices could be used as functional materials, which opens
up nanoscale 3D printing or rapid prototyping. The impact of this technology might
be equivalent to the impact of 3D printing for mechanical and manufacturing
engineering. Entirely new and unconventional applications might emerge.
9.6 End-of-chapter summary
IL has been used to deposit and pattern water ice and organic ice resists. Due to
the high degree of conformity, IL has seen successful results in performing
lithography on 3D structures, as well as nanofabrication on fragile structures.
Nanoscale rapid prototyping can be done using the IL instrument. Furthermore,
3D structures are made by adopting layer-by-layer processing approach, which is
similar to additive manufacturing. Finally, an important application is high resolution
patterning, and we show line-width down to few nanometers by utilizing high energy
e-beam exposure in a TEM instrument.
9.7 Student’s corner
• What is the electron energy range in an SEM?
• Is it possible to condense CO2 in an SEM using a liquid nitrogen based
cryostage?
• What are the reaction products of electrons’ interaction with CO2 ice?
• What are the chemical reaction pathways for electrons–ice interactions?
• Which chemical would you want to work with and why?
• Can IL and EBL be used for lithography on 3D structures?
• List the factors that determine the patterning resolution of IL process.
• What device or structure would you like to build with IL?
