Nanofabrication
Nanolithography techniques and their applications
Jose´ Marı´a De Teresa
Chapter 10
Magnetic nanopatterning via thermal scanning
probe lithography
Edoardo Albisetti, Daniela Petti, Riccardo Bertacco and Elisa Riedo
Finding new ways for controlling magnetism at the nanoscale is of great importance
both for enabling fundamental studies on highly controlled systems and for
developing novel device concepts. Recently, we introduced a technique called
thermally assisted magnetic scanning probe lithography (tam-SPL), which allows
one to craft the spin texture of magnetic multilayers with high ﬂexibility and
tunability. In this chapter, we review the principles and applications of tam-SPL.
First, we introduce the main concepts and discuss the patterning of magnetic
domains in exchange bias multilayer systems. Then, we describe different strategies
for stabilizing complex spin textures with lower dimensionality and tailored proper-
ties, such as magnetic domain walls and solitons with deterministic topology. We
show how to extend the technique to different magnetic systems, such as synthetic
antiferromagnets. Finally, we discuss the application of tam-SPL to the ﬁeld of
magnonics, and realizing reconﬁgurable spin-wave circuits and optically-inspired
nanomagnonic platforms.
10.1 Introduction
Searching novel tools for controlling the magnetic properties of thin ﬁlms with
nanometric resolution is of capital importance for studying fundamental phenomena
and developing new device concepts. A notable example in this sense is the rise of
engineered spin textures, such as magnetic domains, domain walls [1], and
topological solitons (such as vortices [2, 3] and skyrmions [4]), as active elements
in magnetic nanodevices for memory and computing.
The most widely used techniques for patterning magnetic materials in order to
spatially control their magnetic properties include conventional micro- and nano-
fabrication, such as optical and electron-beam lithography [5], and ion-beam
irradiation [6–8]. In the ﬁrst case, the functionality is determined by shaping the
doi:10.1088/978-0-7503-2608-7ch10
10-1
ª IOP Publishing Ltd 2020. All rights,
including for text and data mining (TDM), artificial intelligence (AI) training, and similar technologies, are reserved.

[Page 344]
magnetic material via additive or subtractive manufacturing, i.e. by masking,
deposition and subsequent lift-off, or by ﬁrst depositing the material, then masking
and subsequently etching away the unprotected material, e.g. via ion-milling. These
methodologies represent the wide majority of the used approaches for fabricating
e.g. domain-wall conduits, hall bar structures, spin-valves and magnetic tunnel
junctions, spin-wave waveguides and magnonic crystals.
Ion-beam irradiation based-techniques, on the other hand, allow producing
spatially controlled alterations of the magnetic properties of continuous ﬁlms and
multilayers. In the most common scenario, ﬁrst the magnetic stack is masked via
conventional lithography, then the whole sample is irradiated with a tunable dose of
ions for either controllably damaging the magnetic material or multilayer interfaces,
or producing localized chemical transformations which in turn change the local
magnetic properties. Direct focused ion-beam writing can also be used, instead of
masking, for irradiating nanoscale regions [9].
Finally, fabrication of three-dimensional magnetic nanostructures has been
demonstrated by direct growth via focused electron-beam induced deposition
(FEBID) [10], or using two-photon polymerization lithography in combination
with electrochemical deposition [11].
In this chapter we will discuss a recently developed technique, called thermally
assisted magnetic scanning probe lithography (tam-SPL) [12] which employs a
heated nanometric scanning probe for reversibly controlling the magnetic properties
of thin ﬁlm heterostructures. In section 10.2 we introduce the working principle and
main features of the technique, and show its application to the patterning of tailored
spin textures in different systems. In particular, we discuss the stabilization of
magnetic domains, domain walls and topological solitons in in-plane magnetized
ﬁlms and synthetic antiferromagnets. In section 10.3, we discuss the application of
tam-SPL for manipulating spin waves in the emerging ﬁeld of magnonics, focusing
on the realization of reconﬁgurable spin-wave waveguides and nanoantennas based
on tailored spin textures. Finally, we give an overview of future perspectives for the
application of tam-SPL.
10.2 Thermally-assisted magnetic scanning probe lithography
Thermal scanning probe lithography (t-SPL) is based on scanning a nanoscopic
heatable probe on the surface of the sample, e.g. in an atomic force microscope
(AFM) system or dedicated thermal lithography tool. Such thermal probes were
originally developed by IBM for data storage applications [13, 14], and consist of
specially designed cantilevers which integrate a resistive micro-heater located above the
tip. By ﬂowing an electric current, the heater temperature can be precisely controlled
from RT up to ~1100 °C. As the heat diffuses towards the apex of the nanometric tip, the
lateral dimension of the hot spot becomes comparable to the tip size, and it can be used
for producing a wide range of highly localized thermally-induced surface modiﬁcations.
Throughout the years, several proof-of-principle demonstrations of applications of t-SPL
to different systems were proposed [15]. For example, t-SPL can be used for locally
removing or displacing material via thermally assisted nanoindentation or degradation,
Nanofabrication
10-2

[Page 345]
and therefore creating three-dimensional topographic patterns [16], or lithographic
masks enabling the subsequent nanofabrication steps [17]. Another proliﬁc ﬁeld of
application of t-SPL is the direct conversion of materials in the ‘thermochemical
nanolithography’ approach, where the heated tip is used for promoting spatially
conﬁned thermochemical reactions in a wide range of systems [18]. Examples in this
framework include the local deprotection of functional groups on the surface of
polymeric ﬁlms [19], which can then be used for immobilizing proteins and protein
gradients [20], the direct fabrication of nanostructures from precursor materials [21],
or the modiﬁcation of the surface properties of 2D materials [22].
Tam-SPL is performed by sweeping the heated tip on the surface of a magnetic
multilayer in the presence of an external magnetic ﬁeld, for producing local,
stable modiﬁcations to its magnetic properties.
10.2.1 Concept
One of the most widely studied and technologically relevant phenomena in magnet-
ism is the exchange bias [23, 24]. It occurs at the interface between an antiferro-
magnetic material (AF) and a ferromagnetic material (FM) causing a shift in the
hysteresis loop of the ferromagnet due to the exchange interaction between the two
materials. In other words, the exchange bias introduces a unidirectional magnetic
anisotropy in the ferromagnet, which contributes to determining its magnetization
ground state. Exchange bias is widely used in magnetic recording for pinning the
magnetization in magnetoresistive read-head devices such as spin valves or magnetic
tunneling junctions. If, as in most cases, the Curie temperature of the ferromagnet is
higher than the Néel temperature of the antiferromagnet, it is possible to set the
exchange bias pinning direction by cooling the sample across its blocking temper-
ature TB (which is usually comparable to the Néel temperature of the antiferro-
magnet) while applying an external magnetic ﬁeld, the so-called ﬁeld cooling.
Tam-SPL consists of sweeping the heated probe on the surface of an exchange
bias magnetic system in the presence of an external ﬁeld, for performing a highly
localized ﬁeld cooling and locally re-setting the exchange bias anisotropy point-by-
point with nanoscale resolution. In turn, this allows one to deterministically stabilize
complex magnetization conﬁgurations. Figure 10.1 shows the working principle of
the technique in the simplest case of an exchange bias FM/AF bilayer magnetized in
the plane of the ﬁlm. First (a), a global ﬁeld cooling is performed for setting a
uniform exchange bias and therefore a uniform magnetization orientation across the
whole sample. The global pinning direction is determined by the direction of the
‘initialization’ ﬁeld Hi. (b) Shows the corresponding hysteresis loop, which is shifted
from the center of a quantity He called exchange bias ﬁeld. The patterning (c) is
performed by sweeping the tip, heated above the blocking temperature TB, on the
sample surface while applying an external ‘writing’ ﬁeld Hw. As the tip is displaced
across the sample, the previously heated region undergoes a highly localized ﬁeld
cooling, and the local exchange bias is re-set in the direction of the magnetization,
set by Hw. As the external ﬁeld and the heated tip are removed, the new magnet-
ization ground state is determined by the unidirectional anisotropy landscape
Nanofabrication
10-3

[Page 346]
‘stored’ within the system via tam-SPL. This is visualized at the bottom of panel (c)
by the opposite exchange bias shift, and therefore opposite unidirectional aniso-
tropy, and in the local hysteresis loops of the non-patterned (orange loop) and
patterned regions (red loop).
10.2.2 Patterning magnetic domains, domain walls and solitons
By controlling the patterning conditions and the geometry of the patterned area, it is
possible to deterministically stabilize a wide range of spin textures with tailored
properties and dimensionality. Here, we discuss the case of two-dimensional
magnetic domains, one-dimensional domain walls and zero-dimensional topological
solitons patterned in prototypical CoFeB/IrMn exchange bias bilayers magnetized
in the plane of the ﬁlm.
In general, magnetic domains of controlled size and magnetization orientation are
patterned via tam-SPL by raster-scanning the heated tip on the sample, in a similar
fashion as in atomic force microscopy imaging. The main technique used for
characterizing the magnetic patterns at remanence was magnetic force microscopy
(MFM), which shows the magnetic contrast associated with the stray ﬁeld gradients
arising in regions with non-uniform magnetization, such as the domain walls
surrounding a uniform magnetic domain. Figure 10.2 shows the MFM images of
arbitrary shaped domains with magnetization at remanence pointing in-plane in the
opposite direction with respect to the magnetization outside the patterned regions.
In particular, square, triangular and diamond shaped domains with size ranging
from 1.5 to 2.5 μm are stabilized in Ru(2)/Ir20Mn80(7)/Co40Fe40B20(5)/SiO2(1000)/Si
(thickness in nm) multilayers grown via magnetron sputtering. The patterning was
performed by raster-scanning the heated tip at a 3 μm s−1 speed on the surface of the
sample while applying an external writing ﬁeld Hw ~ 700 Oe in the opposite direction
Figure 10.1. Sketch of tam-SPL performed on an exchange bias ferromagnet (FM)/antiferromagnet (AF)
bilayer. (a) Initialization consists in performing a global ﬁeld cooling in order to pin the magnetization of the
FM in the direction of the external initialization ﬁeld Hi. (b) tam-SPL is performed by sweeping a heated
scanning probe on the sample in the presence of an external magnetic ﬁeld Hw in order to locally reset the
exchange bias. (c) Upon removal of the external ﬁeld, the spin texture in the ferromagnet is stabilized by the
local exchange bias ﬁeld. The bottom panels show the hysteresis loops of the initialized sample (a) and of the
patterned sample (c).
Nanofabrication
10-4

[Page 347]
with respect to the initialization ﬁeld Hi. In this speciﬁc case, the heater temperature
Th was set to 560 °C, 675 °C and 765 °C for squares, triangles and diamonds,
respectively. These values ensured that the surface temperature, which is lower than
the heater temperature due to thermal dissipation, was kept above the blocking
temperature of the exchange bias system TB ~ 160 °C. Noteworthy, the topographic
image associated with the MFM image (ﬁgure 10.2(b)) does not show any visible
pattern, conﬁrming the purely magnetic nature of tam-SPL patterning. For assessing
the minimum domain size achievable in this system, stripe-shaped domains were
patterned by sweeping the heated tip along a single 12 μm long line. Figure 10.2(c)
shows the high-resolution MFM image of the resulting domain, which consists of
two 180° Néel domain walls separated by 250 nm. This case provides a clear
demonstration of the fact that the minimum domain size achievable via tam-SPL is
related to the domain wall width of the magnetic system (which in turn depends on
the micromagnetic properties of the system), rather than the spatial extension of the
thermal proﬁle generated by the tip, which could reach sub-10 nm resolution [25]
The possibility of vectorially orienting point-by-point the magnetization opens up
a plethora of possibilities which go beyond the writing of two-dimensional domains.
In fact, by controlling the magnetization within the domains, it is possible to tailor
the properties and shape of the domain boundaries, as well. This capability is well
clariﬁed by the micromagnetic simulations shown in ﬁgures 10.2(d)–(g), which
Figure 10.2. (a) Magnetic force microscopy image of tam-SPL written triangular, diamond and square shaped
domains with different dimensions. Black arrows indicate the direction of the magnetization in the patterned
and non-patterned regions. Scale bar: 2 μm. (b) Topography image of patterned region. Scale bar: 4 μm
(c) High-resolution MFM image of a stripe domain written with a single pass of the heated tip. Scale bar: 500
nm. (d–g) Micromagnetic simulations showing the spin conﬁguration of the domain walls of square, triangle,
diamond and stripe domains, respectively. Adapted by permission from [12], copyright (2016) Springer.
Nanofabrication
10-5

[Page 348]
visualize the spin-conﬁguration at the boundaries of the magnetic domains shown in
ﬁgure 10.2(a), where the local spin-orientation is indicated by black arrows while the
color code indicates the calculated MFM contrast, which allows one to directly
compare the experiments with the simulations. Figures 10.2(d)–(g) show the
stabilization of different types of domain walls, enabled by the control of the shape
of the magnetic patterns with respect to the orientation of the writing ﬁeld.
Speciﬁcally, panel (d) shows the stabilization of a tail-to-tail 180° wall at the right
boundary of the square domain, and the stabilization of side-to-side 180° walls at the
top and bottom boundaries of square and stripe domains (panel (g)). In such domain
walls, the spins at the center of the wall are oriented parallel (tail-to-tail) and
perpendicular (side-to-side) with respect to the wall proﬁle. Furthermore, domain
walls characterized by a mixed character and complex shape proﬁle develop at the
boundaries of triangular and diamond shaped domains, and are shown in panels
(e) and (f). This provides a compelling demonstration that tam-SPL allows the
stabilization of complex tailored one-dimensional spin textures in continuous ﬁlms
e.g. by imposing non-uniform boundary conditions via domain writing.
By generalizing this concept, it is possible to develop a methodology [26] for
patterning zero-dimensional magnetic structures such as vortices and Bloch lines
within domain walls. Such topological spin textures are raising interest due to their
potential in data storage and computing, so that ﬁnding novel ways for controlling
their properties beyond the capabilities of conventional nanofabrication is highly
appealing. For example, one of the most used strategies for stabilizing vortex conﬁg-
urations is by exploiting the ﬂux closure in round or elliptical magnetic dots [27].
However, this does not allow one to straightforwardly control the circularity of the
texture (clockwise or counter-clockwise), and its vorticity (vortex or antivortex).
In the following, we show two strategies for stabilizing solitons with deterministi-
cally controlled properties in continuous exchange biased systems, by designing
vectorially the unidirectional anisotropy landscapes via tam-SPL. Figure 10.3
shows the stabilization of vortex–antivortex (VA) pairs with tailored circularity.
For doing so, as shown in panel (d), tam-SPL wasused for patterningtriangular shaped
regions with counter-clockwise unidirectional anisotropy and a shared central apex.
For obtaining a spatially rotating unidirectional anisotropy, each region was sequen-
tiallypatterned with a different orientation of the writing ﬁeldHw. Thearrows markthe
direction of the exchange bias ﬁeld in each region, which sets the direction of the
magnetization at remanence, after patterning. This non-uniform magnetization con-
ﬁguration in turn generates four 90° domain walls, a potential minimum for the vortex
core in the center of the patterned area (pink dot) and an antivortex located at the
bottom left corner (orange dot). Panels (e) and (f) show the MFM image and
micromagnetic simulation of the patterned structure, respectively, where the black
arrows mark the local magnetization orientation, and the color contrast in the
simulations indicates the divergence of the magnetization which is related to the
experimental MFM contrast. It is worth noting that the circularity of the vortex and
the position of the antivortex is determined by the sense of rotation of the unidirec-
tional anisotropy pattern.
Nanofabrication
10-6

[Page 349]
Panels (a, c) show a second strategy, which allows combining the patterning of
domain walls and solitons. First, two magnetic domains with opposite magnet-
ization orientation (e.g. pointing upwards and downwards, respectively) are pat-
terned as shown in panel (a). This leads to the stabilization of a Néel side-to-side
180° wall at the domain boundary. Then, two domains with magnetization pointing
horizontally (e.g. pointing towards the right and the left, respectively) are patterned
at the two ends of the wall. As a result, the wall chirality (i.e. the direction of the
magnetization within the wall) is determined, at each end, by the magnetization
direction of the corresponding domain. A Bloch line is stabilized within the wall if
the wall chirality is different at the two ends. Panel (a) shows the case of a
vortex-type Bloch line with clockwise circularity (pink dot), stabilized within a
straight Néel wall. Noteworthy, by controlling the relative direction of the magnet-
ization of the four domains, it is possible to control the vorticity of the Bloch line
(vortex or antivortex type), and its circularity (clockwise or counter-clockwise).
Panels (b) and (c) show the MFM image and corresponding micromagnetic
simulations, where dark arrows mark the local direction of the magnetization.
The presence of the Bloch line is conﬁrmed by the abrupt change in the wall
chirality, which is visible in the MFM magnetic contrast. Interestingly, the domain
wall is constituted by metastable points for the Bloch line, which could therefore be
displaced along the wall, e.g. via external ﬁelds or currents.
10.2.3 Writing spin textures in synthetic antiferromagnets
So far, we focused on writing spin textures in exchange bias FM/AF bilayers,
however, the very same tam-SPL concept can be generalized to more complex
magnetic multilayers. In the following, we show the application of tam-SPL
Figure 10.3. Patterning topological solitons. (a, d) Sketch of the direction of the exchange bias, blue arrows,
for stabilizing a vortex Bloch line (pink dot) within a Néel wall (a) and a vortex (pink dot)-antivortex (orange
dot) pair (d) in a continuous exchange bias system. (b, e) MFM image of the patterned regions at remanence.
Black arrows indicate the direction of the magnetization. Scale bars: 3 μm (b) and 2 μm (e). (c, f)
Micromagnetic simulation showing the spin conﬁguration and the position of the vortex cores and antivortex.
Adapted from [26], with the permission of AIP Publishing.
Nanofabrication
10-7

[Page 350]
patterning to synthetic antiferromagnets (SAFs) [28], i.e. magnetic multilayers com-
prising two ferromagnetic layers exchange coupled antiferromagnetically via RKKY
coupling [29, 30] across a thin nonmagnetic layer. Throughout the years, SAFs played
a pivotal role in the development of spintronic devices, for example in spin valves [31]
and magnetic tunneling junctions [32], and more recently they have been proposed for
racetrack memories [33] and in the emerging ﬁeld of magnonics [34].
In ﬁgure 10.4(a) we show the hysteresis loop and the magnetization direction of
the top (purple arrow) and bottom (orange arrow) ferromagnetic layers of a Ru(2)/
Ir20Mn80(10)/Co40Fe40B20(45)/Ru(0.9)/Co40Fe40B20(45) system measured by vibra-
tion sample magnetometry (VSM). The two lateral lobes mark the rotation of the
ferromagnetic layers, while the plateau at low ﬁelds indicates robust antiferromag-
netic coupling. In this region, the magnetization direction of the top CoFeB layer is
set by exchange bias with the 10 nm IrMn layer, while the magnetization of the
bottom layer is oriented antiferromagnetically. tam-SPL patterning is performed, as
described above, by sweeping the probe heated above the blocking temperature of
the system on the sample surface while applying a writing external ﬁeld Hw strong
enough to saturate the system [35]. This allows one to spatially control the exchange
bias direction, and therefore the magnetization orientation of the top layer at
remanence. The magnetization of the bottom layer is oriented point-by-point in the
opposite direction by the antiferromagnetic coupling. Figure 10.4(b) shows a sketch
of a 180° domain wall. Figure 10.4(c) shows two experimental examples of domain
walls stabilized by writing oppositely oriented magnetic domains in SAF. In
particular, MFM images show the magnetic contrast arising at the domain walls
in the case of straight angled walls (top) and parabola-shaped domain walls with
different curvature (bottom). In the following section, we show how tam-SPL
written spin textures in SAF can be employed as tunable and highly controlled spin-
wave emitters.
Figure 10.4. Patterning spin textures in synthetic antiferromagnets. (a) Hysteresis loop of a CoFeB (35)/Ru
(0.6)/CoFeB (35)/IrMn (10) nm SAF system. The direction of the magnetization in the top (bottom) layer is
indicated by the purple (orange) arrow. (b) Structure of a domain wall, in pink, in a synthetic antiferromagnet.
(c) MFM images of straight and curved domain walls patterned via tam-SPL. The direction of the
magnetization of the top FM layer is indicated by purple arrows. Scale bars 3 μm. Adapted from [36] John
Wiley & Sons.
Nanofabrication
10-8

[Page 351]
10.2.4 Features and capabilities of tam-SPL
Here, we review some of the features, capabilities and limitations of tam-SPL, taking
as an example the simplest case of spin textures stabilized in exchange bias FM/AF
bilayers.
As explained above, tam-SPL allows the vectorial point-by-point tuning of the
unidirectional anisotropy. Below, we show that by varying the writing temperature
during patterning it is possible to precisely tune the exchange bias strength in a
continuous fashion. Figure 10.5(a) shows the MFM image at remanence of two
squares patterned with writing ﬁeld Hw in the opposite direction with respect to the
initialization ﬁeld Hi (top). The central and bottom panels show the corresponding
(color coded) local hysteresis loops measured via magneto-optical Kerr effect
microscopy (μ-MOKE) along the exchange bias direction. Each pattern was
performed with a different heater temperature TH = 240 °C (central square),
TH = 435 °C (left square). The non-patterned sample (top yellow square) does not
show any magnetic contrast in the MFM image at remanence, as expected from its
hysteresis loop, which is completely shifted from the center and featuring an
exchange bias ﬁeld He of −50 Oe set during initialization. By increasing the
patterning temperature around the TB of the system (central green square), the
local loop shifts towards the center, and a magnetic contrast appears in the MFM
image. By further increasing the patterning temperature above TB (left red square),
the loop completely shifts on the opposite direction up to Hep = 50 Oe, and a well-
deﬁned magnetic domain is retrieved at remanence in the MFM image. This
capability is enabled by the precise temperature control allowed by t-SPL, in
combination
with
the
statistical
distribution
of
blocking
temperatures
in
Figure 10.5. Tunability of the unidirectional anisotropy and evolution of the domains as a function of the
external ﬁeld. (a) Top panel: MFM image of a non-patterned area (yellow), a square domain patterned with
TH = 240 °C (green) and a square domain patterned with TH = 435 °C (red). Central and bottom panels:
local μ-MOKE hysteresis loops color-coded as above. (b) μ-MOKE images of the domains shown in
ﬁgure 10.2(a) acquired at different external ﬁelds, showing the evolution of the patterns as a function of the
external ﬁeld. In the bottom (top) panel the forward (reverse) ‘loop’ is shown. Scale bars 2 μm. Adapted by
permission from [12], copyright (2016) Springer.
Nanofabrication
10-9

[Page 352]
polycrystalline exchange bias systems, which arises from the distribution of grain
sizes within the ﬁlm [24].
Another appealing feature of tam-SPL is its reversibility. The process of setting
the exchange bias via ﬁeld cooling is initiated by temporary weakening (or destroy-
ing) the antiferromagnetic order with temperature. It is therefore a reversible
process, provided that heating does not induce other irreversible transformations
in the ﬁlms. In this sense, heating polycrystalline sputtered multilayers at high
temperature is known to cause interdiffusion and eventually damage the interfaces.
In turn, this can cause changes in the magnetic properties of the ﬁlm (coercivity,
saturation magnetization) and weakening of the exchange bias. It is worth noting
however, that the temperature resilience of sputtered magnetic materials has been
extensively studied in the framework, e.g. of heat-assisted recording, MTJs (see e.g.
[37, 38]), so that it is indeed possible to suitably design interfaces which are resilient
to thermal treatment. In this framework, tuning the blocking temperature of the
system by engineering the composition of the antiferromagnetic layer represents a
viable additional route to reduce the patterning requirements in terms of peak
temperature [39].
The reversibility of the patterning process leads to fully reconﬁgurable domains.
In particular, as shown in [12], writing, erasing and rewriting patterns can be done
simply by sweeping the heated tip on the same region while applying a different
writing ﬁeld Hw. Noteworthy, as explained in ﬁgure 10.3, the reconﬁgurability of
tam-SPL patterns is at the core of the ability to write complex spin textures, such as
vortices and Bloch lines, and represents a distinctive feature with respect to the vast
majority of nanopatterning methodologies.
While, so far, we mainly focused on the patterns at remanence, now we steer our
attention to the evolution of the patterned domains as a function of the external
ﬁeld. For doing so, we acquired μ-MOKE images of the patterned domains as a
function of the external ﬁeld applied in the plane of the ﬁlms. Figure 10.5(b) shows
the image sequence and corresponding hysteresis loops of the domains shown in
ﬁgure 10.2(a). In particular, the top (bottom) sequence was acquired by sweeping the
ﬁeld along to the exchange bias direction from +100 Oe to −100 Oe (from −100 Oe
to +100 Oe). It is worth noting that two different regimes can be observed, one at
low ﬁelds (around 0), one at high ﬁelds, comparable to the exchange bias ﬁeld.
At low ﬁelds, the patterned domains, which appear as dark regions on bright
background, are modulated by the application of the external ﬁeld. In particular,
they expand when the external ﬁeld and their magnetization point in the same
direction, and they shrink when they point in the opposite direction. This is due to
the fact that the domain walls can be displaced upon application of relatively small
ﬁelds. After removing the external ﬁeld, the original domain conﬁguration is
retrieved. The possibility to reversibly and precisely tune the spin textures via
external stimuli provides an additional ﬂexible strategy to design real-time reconﬁg-
urable devices.
At higher external ﬁelds, i.e. when the ﬁeld becomes comparable to the exchange
bias ﬁeld, the domain pattern disappears and a uniform magnetization orientation in
the whole sample is obtained, as shown by the uniform dark (bright) contrast in
Nanofabrication
10-10

[Page 353]
the μ-MOKE images acquired at large negative (positive) ﬁelds. Importantly, after
removing the external ﬁeld the same magnetization conﬁguration at remanence is
recovered after 2000 loops. This stability of the pattern stands from the resilience of
the patterned exchange bias against external magnetic ﬁelds.
10.3 Nanopatterned spin textures for magnonics
The ability to stabilize multidimensional spin textures in continuous ﬁlms opens up a
plethora of possibilities in the emerging ﬁeld of magnonics. Magnonics seeks to
control the excitation, propagation and transduction of spin waves, i.e. propagating
perturbations in the spin texture of magnetic materials, for building highly-
integrated and energy-efﬁcient computing devices [34, 40, 41]. The main powerful
enabling features of spin waves are the absence of Joule losses associated with spin-
wave propagation, and the short spin-wave wavelength in the GHz–THz range,
which can go down to a few nm, i.e. several orders of magnitude shorter than
electromagnetic waves. These two features, together with the extremely-rich spin
wave phenomenology and the improvement of micro-nanofabrication techniques
makes magnonics a highly active research ﬁeld. In this framework, the use of tam-
SPL engineered spin textures for controlling the excitation and propagation of spin
waves is an extremely promising avenue, ensuring reconﬁgurable functionality and
high design ﬂexibility towards both fundamental studies and fabrication of mag-
nonic nanodevices. In this paragraph, we review our recent results on the use of
2D–1D and 0D engineered spin textures for controlling the emission, propagation
and spatial conﬁnement of spin waves in exchange bias single layer systems and
synthetic antiferromagnets.
10.3.1 Controlling the spin-wave excitation and propagation with magnetic domains
Here, we show that the excitation efﬁciency and propagation of spin waves can be
spatially controlled by patterning magnetic domains in a continuous ﬁlm [12].
Figure 10.6(b) shows the MFM image of 2.5 μm wide vertical stripe-shaped domains
with magnetization pointing towards the left and upwards direction (black arrows),
patterned via tam-SPL in a CoFeB (5)/IrMn (7) nm exchange bias bilayer. The
presence of sharp 90° Néel domain walls is conﬁrmed by the dark/bright contrast. In
order to excite spin waves, Ti (7)/Au (200) nm coplanar waveguides (CPW) were
patterned via EBL on the top of the ﬁlm after depositing an electrically insulating
SiO2 layer. An optical image of the sample is shown in ﬁgure 10.6(a). For exciting
spin waves, an RF current is injected in the CPW, so that an RF Oersted magnetic
ﬁeld Hrf is generated in the vicinity of the CPW. In panel (a), the direction of Hrf is
marked by red lines, and the domain walls separating alternating 90° domains are
indicated by dashed orange lines.
For studying spin waves with high spatial resolution, we used micro-focused
Brillouin light scattering (μ-BLS). In BLS, incident photons (generated by a laser
focused on the sample surface) are scattered inelastically by the interaction with
magnons. By analyzing the intensity, the wavevector and the frequency shift of the
scattered photons, it is possible to retrieve information on the dispersion and
Nanofabrication
10-11

[Page 354]
intensity of the spin waves travelling within the magnetic ﬁlm. Furthermore, by
scanning the focused laser beam, it is possible to build one- and two-dimensional
intensity maps with spatial resolution limited by the size of the laser spot, which in
our case is around 250 nm. Figure 10.6(c) shows the one-dimensional spin-wave
intensity map acquired along the x direction (parallel to the CPW) right outside the
CPW. Spin waves were excited by running a 4 GHz current in the CPW. The spin-
wave intensity is color-coded, the domain walls are indicated by dashed lines and the
direction of the magnetization within each domain is indicated by the white arrows.
Noteworthy, we observe well-deﬁned maxima in the spin-wave intensity within the
domains where the magnetization lies parallel to the CPW (Damon–Eshbach
conﬁguration, DE) and minima where the magnetization is perpendicular to the
CPW (backward volume conﬁguration, BA). Panel (d) shows the spin-wave spectra
acquired in the center of regions a and b, showing a 36-fold higher excitation
intensity in the DE stripes with respect to the BA stripes. This is due to the fact that
the spin-wave excitation efﬁciency is maximum when the CPW Oersted ﬁeld couples
well with the dynamical magnetization of the spin-wave mode. Such coupling is
more efﬁcient in the DE conﬁguration, because the Oersted ﬁeld has a strong
component along the y direction, i.e. along a major axis of the elliptical precession of
the dynamic magnetization. In contrast, in the BA conﬁguration, the coupling is less
efﬁcient, resulting in a much lower spin-wave intensity. In [42] we also showed that
the spin-wave excitation efﬁciency within tam-SPL domains can be reversibly
Figure 10.6. Controlling spin waves with magnetic domains. (a) Coplanar waveguide (CPW) for spin-wave
excitation. The vertical dashed orange lines mark the domain walls. (b) MFM image showing vertical 2.5 μm
stripe domains separated by 90° domain walls. The direction of the magnetization within the domains is
indicated by black arrows. (c) Line-map of the spin-wave intensity, color-coded, along the x direction, acquired
right outside the CPW. Dashed lines mark the domain walls and white arrows indicate the direction of the
magnetization within the domains. (d) Spin-wave spectra acquired in the center of stripe a (Damon–Eshbach
conﬁguration) in red and stripe b (backward-volume) in blue. The spectra were acquired right outside the
CPW. (e) Line-map of the spin-wave intensity, color-coded, acquired along the y direction in stripe a (top
panel) and b (bottom panel). (f) Spin-wave spectra acquired at 2.25 μm from the CPW in stripe a (red) and b
(blue). Adapted by permission from [12], copyright (2016) Springer.
Nanofabrication
10-12

[Page 355]
modulated in real-time via an external static ﬁeld, by temporarily reorienting their
magnetization. Figure 10.6(e) shows a one-dimensional μ-BLS map acquired along
the y direction (i.e. perpendicular to the CPW). The top panel, related to the DE
conﬁguration, shows the detection of propagating spin waves up to 4 μm from the
CPW, while the bottom panel, related to the BA conﬁguration, shows detection up
to 2 μm from the CPW. Figure 10.6(f) shows the corresponding spin-wave spectra
acquired at 2.25 μm from the CPW, demonstrating that while spin waves are still
detectable in the DE stripe, no spin-waves are detected in the BA stripes. This
demonstrates that, by locally controlling the direction of the magnetization, it is
possible to straightforwardly spatially modulate in a reversible way the spin-wave
excitation, propagation and conﬁnement in a continuous ﬁlm, without any physical
patterning, and allowing for both off-line reconﬁgurability of the domain structure
at remanence via tam-SPL and real-time reconﬁgurability via external stimuli.
Another appealing application of tam-SPL-written magnetic domains is the
realization of reconﬁgurable magnonic metamaterials made by periodically modu-
lating the magnetic properties (e.g. unidirectional anisotropy and/or magnetization
orientation) with high spatial resolution. Figure 10.7 shows micromagnetic simu-
lations presenting the design of a spin-wave phase-shifter based on a single 250 nm
stripe domain. Panels (a, b) show the unidirectional anisotropy direction and
resulting spin conﬁguration of a single stripe, respectively, while panel (c) shows
the effect of a single stripe on the spin-wave propagation, in contrast to the case of
uniformly oriented magnetization. In particular, the presence of the stripe causes a
3π phase-shift in the propagating spin wave excited at 10 GHz (red and green line),
with respect to the case of uniformly oriented magnetization (blue line). The phase-
shifting mechanism is based on the strongly anisotropic spin-wave dispersion
Figure 10.7. Micromagnetic simulations of phase-shifters and magnonic crystals. (a) Unidirectional aniso-
tropy direction of a single 250 nm wide stripe. (b) Resulting micromagnetic conﬁguration at remanence.
(c) Amplitude of the spin waves propagating in presence of the stripe (green and red curve) and in the
uniformly magnetized ﬁlm (blue). (d) Sketch of the simulated magnonic crystal comprising 50 repetitions of the
stripe shown in (a). On the right, proﬁle of the effective refractive index. (e) Simulated transmission of the
magnonic crystal. Adapted by permission from [12], copyright (2016) Springer.
Nanofabrication
10-13

[Page 356]
depending on the relative angle between the wavevector k and the magnetization M,
which causes the spin waves to have a different wavelength when travelling within
different magnetic domains. Such phase-shifting element can be used, e.g. as
building blocks of Mach–Zehnder interferometers.
This change in the refractive index can be exploited for building magnonic
crystals, i.e. periodic magnetic nanostructures for controlling the transmission of
spin waves [43]. Panel (d) shows the micromagnetic conﬁguration of a crystal (left),
comprising 50 repetitions of the 250 nm wide stripes, and the periodic modulation of
the spin-wave refractive index (right). Panel (e) shows the transmission of the crystal
as a function of the frequency in the 3–5 GHz range, calculated with a one-
dimensional transfer matrix method, displaying the characteristic magnonic band-
gaps. The position and bandwidth of the gaps can be tuned by changing the width of
the stripe, the number of repetitions and the separation between the stripes.
Interestingly, as shown before, the magnonic crystal properties can be fully
reconﬁgured by performing tam-SPL again, or in real time by applying an external
ﬁeld for temporarily re-orienting the magnetic domains.
10.3.2 Nanoscale spin-wave circuits based on spin textures
In the previous paragraph, we demonstrated that 2D micrometer-sized domains can
be employed as waveguide for spin waves. Here, we show that tailored 1D spin
textures can act as nanoconduits, for conﬁning and steering spin waves and realizing
spin-wave circuits [44]. Straight and curved domain walls were patterned via tam-
SPL in a CoFeB (20)/IrMn (10)/Ru (2) nm exchange biased system, grown on Si/
Si3N4 200 nm thick membranes via magnetron sputtering, as described previously.
On top of the patterned samples, we fabricated 2 μm × 30 μm microstrip antennas
via maskless optical lithography and lift-off, after depositing a 50 nm thick SiO2
insulating layer via magnetron sputtering. The stripline consisted of a Cr 5 nm/Cu
200 nm bilayer deposited by thermal evaporation. We studied the conﬁned spin-
wave modes propagating along the spin textures via space and time-resolved
scanning transmission x-ray microscopy (STXM), where monochromatic x-rays,
tuned to the Co L3 absorption edge (photon energy of about 781 eV), are focused
onto a spot on the sample, and the transmitted photons are recorded using an
avalanche photodiode. The sample is scanned using a piezoelectric stage, and the
transmitted x-ray intensity is recorded for each pixel to form the image. Magnetic
contrast arises from the x-ray magnetic circular dichroism (XMCD) of the circularly
polarized light employed. In the particular conﬁguration we used to obtain the results
reported in this paragraph, the black-white contrast is associated to the in-plane
component of the magnetization (Mx).
In ﬁgure 10.8(a), we show the static STXM image of a curved 180° Néel domain
wall, obtained by patterning two domains with antiparallel remanent magnetization.
The orange arrows indicate the direction of the magnetization in the two domains.
At the bottom of the ﬁgure, the edge of the antenna used for spin-wave excitation is
visible. Panel (c) displays a sequence of STXM images showing the propagation of
spin waves along the domain wall. The images are taken stroboscopically,
Nanofabrication
10-14

[Page 357]
generating the spin waves with a sinusoidal excitation at 1.1 GHz and without any
applied static external ﬁeld. The normalized Mx signal reported in panel (c) is
calculated as the magnetic deviation ΔMx(t) from the time-averaged state 〈Mx(t)〉
and shows spin waves, conﬁned at the domain wall, propagating away from the
microstrip located at the bottom. In order to better understand the geometry of the
propagating modes, in ﬁgure 10.8(b) are presented micromagnetic simulations of
the spin-wave excitation in the presence of the curved domain wall. In particular, the
left panel shows the magnetic deviation ΔMx(t) from the time averaged value 〈Mx(t)〉,
while in the right panel the Mz component is reported. The excited modes are called
Winter magnons [45–47] and are characterized by a ﬂexural motion of the domain
wall, associated with an elliptical spin precession within the wall. Panel (d) reports the
spatial map of the amplitude of the spin-wave excitation, which is obtained by
plotting for each pixel the amplitude of the sinusoidal ﬁt of the time-trace (raw data)
acquired in it. The lateral extension of the spin-wave mode corresponds to 115 nm, as
calculated from the full-width-at-half-maximum (FWHM) of the horizontal proﬁle
extracted from the spatial map at x = 0.6 μm from the stripline (panel (d)) and is
reported in panel (e). This conﬁrms that the mode is conﬁned at the domain wall, and
Figure 10.8. Conﬁning and steering spin waves with domain walls. (a) STXM image of a curved 180° Néel
wall patterned in a CoFeB (20)/IrMn (10) nm system. Orange arrows indicate the direction of the magnet-
ization within the domains. (b) Micromagnetic simulations showing spin waves propagating within the curved
domain wall. (c) Time-resolved STXM images showing spin waves excited at 1.11 GHz, propagating along the
curved 180° Néel wall. (d) Spatial map of the spin-wave excitation amplitude showing conﬁnement at
the domain wall. (e) Horizontal proﬁle extracted from (d) at a distance of 0.6 μm from the antenna.
(f) Experimental (ﬁlled orange) and simulated (empty blue) dispersion of spin waves propagating within a
straight domain wall. Scale bars: 500 nm. Adapted from [44].
Nanofabrication
10-15

[Page 358]
that no spin waves are propagating within the domains. In ﬁgure 10.8(f), the
experimental (ﬁlled orange dots) and simulated (empty blue dots) dispersion relation
is reported in the case of a straight 180° Néel domain wall. The experimental values
were acquired from measurements at different excitation frequencies, while the
simulations were obtained from a sinc-shaped ﬁeld pulse excitation. The propagating
character of the modes is conﬁrmed by both simulations and experiments, displaying
a positive dispersion.
A small bandgap is visible below 0.3 GHz and can be attributed to the presence of
a residual effective ﬁeld in correspondence of the domain wall, arising from
crystalline anisotropy and the exchange bias. The spin-wave group velocity
vg = ∂ω/∂k, extracted at k →0, has been calculated both from the experimental
data and from the simulations and is vgExp = 2.77 ± 1.4 km s−1 and vgSim = 2.30 ±
0.34 km s−1, respectively. Noteworthy, the spin-wave modes conﬁned at the domain
wall are the only ones supported in this frequency range, which falls within the
bandgap of the propagating modes in the uniform ﬁlm. The conﬁnement of the
modes at the domain wall is therefore guaranteed. Noteworthy, propagating
distances exceeding 4 μm have been measured in curved domain walls, with a
conﬁnement width < 200 nm. This fact, together with the positive dispersion
conﬁrms that shaped spin textures and the corresponding guided modes can be
used for transporting information within integrated circuits.
In order to verify the possibility to fabricate nanocircuits, we patterned spin
textures comprising two domain walls sharing a common apex. Figures 10.9(a) and
(b) show the static STXM images of the two domain walls when a small static
magnetic ﬁeld of 2 mT and 1.68 mT was applied in the plane of the ﬁlm, as indicated.
It is possible to notice that the distance between the two domain walls (dashed white
Figure 10.9. Reconﬁgurable spin-wave circuits based on domain walls. (a, b) STXM image of a spin texture
comprising two 180° Néel walls (white dashed lines) sharing a common apex, acquired applying a static 2.00
mT (a) and 1.68 mT (b) external ﬁeld H. The direction of the magnetization is indicated by the orange arrows.
(c, d) Spin-wave excitation amplitude along the proﬁles indicated by the dashed green lines in panel (a) and (b),
respectively. (e, f) Time traces extracted in correspondence of the colored stars in panels (c) and (d). When
H = 2.00 mT the modes are spatially separate. By reducing the external ﬁeld, the two modes can be tunably
superimposed. Scale bars: 500 nm. Adapted from [44].
Nanofabrication
10-16

[Page 359]
lines) can be tuned by applying a small external ﬁeld. Indeed, by decreasing the ﬁeld,
the two domain walls, initially spatially separated (panel (a)), are brought closer and
in panel (b) they share a common apex. As shown previously, the propagating spin
waves are conﬁned at the walls, so that by controlling the position of the walls it is
possible to displace the spin-wave excitation with high accuracy.
The amplitude of the spin-wave modes along the horizontal proﬁles of panels
(a-b) (green dashed lines) in the two conﬁgurations is shown in panels (c, d) and
allows one to better appreciate the progressive overlapping of the two modes. The
two guided excitations are visible as two peaks, with FWHM around 200 nm. In
panel (c) the applied magnetic ﬁeld corresponds to 2 mT and the two modes do not
overlap, with a peak-to-peak distance of 810 nm. At a ﬁeld of 1.68 mT, the two
modes are separated by 340 nm and therefore partially overlap (panel (d)). In panels
(e, f), by plotting the sinusoidal ﬁt (time traces) extracted in correspondence of the
stars (color coded) in panels (c, d), it is possible to better analyze the conﬁned spin-
wave modes as a function of the applied external ﬁeld. Three curves are shown: those
with higher amplitude (orange, blue) are related to the magnetization dynamics of the
two guided modes, in correspondence of their maximum intensity. The phase
difference of the two guided modes, as expected, depends on the applied ﬁeld, which
modiﬁes the spin conﬁguration and modulates the geometry of the nanowaveguides.
The third, with lower amplitude (red), corresponds to the dynamics in the region
where the two modes overlap. For an applied ﬁeld of 2.00 mT, panel (e) shows that
the two modes do not overlap, therefore no excitation is measured (red dashed line).
Conversely, for lower ﬁelds, the two modes are spatially superimposed and the third
lower-amplitude curve is visible in panel (f). This result shows that it is possible to
achieve a sizeable modulation of the excitation amplitude and phase in the overlap
region. The control of the superposition, phase difference and amplitude of the
conﬁned modes via external stimuli such as ﬁelds or currents, allows one to envision
the implementation of logic devices based on nanoscale spin textures, such as Mach–
Zehnder-type spin-wave interferometers.
10.3.3 Optically inspired nanomagnonics in synthetic antiferromagnets
In this paragraph, we discuss the use of patterned spin textures to generate spin
waves for implementing an optically-inspired spin-wave platform [36]. We patterned
domain walls and vortices in a Ru(2)/Ir20Mn80(10)/Co40Fe40B20(45)/Ru(0.9)/
Co40Fe40B20(45) nm synthetic antiferromagnetic system (SAF) [48] (see section
10.2.3), grown on Si/Si3N4 200 nm thick membranes via magnetron sputtering.
As described in the previous paragraph, we patterned 5 μm × 30 μm microstrips of
Cr 5 nm/Cu 200 nm on a 50 nm thick SiO2 deposited by thermal evaporation. We
studied the spin-wave modes generated the spin textures via space and time-resolved
STXM. In ﬁgure 10.10(a) a micromagnetic simulation sketches the concept of
magnonic nanoantennas based on nanoscale patterned magnetic domain walls. The
domain wall is set in oscillation by an external magnetic ﬁeld HRF, generated by a
stripline located in close proximity to the wall, run by RF currents. In particular, the
out-of-plane component of the oscillating HRF ﬁeld outside the microstrip couples
Nanofabrication
10-17

[Page 360]
with the out-of-plane component of the magnetization of the SAF in correspondence
to the wall. The coupling between the out-of-plane components has the advantage of
being independent from the relative orientation of the stripline and the spin textures,
so that curved, angled walls and vortices can be excited.
In ﬁgure 10.10(b), we show an experimental STXM image of the directional
emission from a curved domain wall (red line) and focusing of spin-wave beams. The
blue/red line corresponds to the emitted wavefronts and the white arrows represent
the direction of the static magnetization of the upmost ferromagnetic layer. In this
experimental conﬁguration, the black/white contrast in the image corresponds to the
oscillation of the out-of-plane component of the magnetization, normalized to the
average value measured over a period of oscillation of the RF sinusoidal signal of
the microstrip. The frequency of excitation is 1.43 GHz. The focus of the spin-wave
beam is located at a distance of 2.5 μm from the spin texture, as can be evinced also
from the change of concavity of the wavefronts. Panel (c) shows the spatial proﬁle of
the beams at the focal point, extracted along the yellow dashed line of panel (b).
Consistently with a description analog to diffractive optics, the FWHM of the beam
amplitude, which is f = 340 ± 50 nm, is close to the value obtained considering the
spin-wave wavelength and the numerical aperture of the source, i.e. the wall
geometry: f = λ/2sin α = 363 nm. Panels (d, e) display the experimental STXM
image and the corresponding micromagnetic simulation of spin waves emitted from
Figure 10.10. Domain wall based magnonic nanoantennas in synthetic antiferromagnets. (a) Simulations of
the emission of a focused directional spin-wave beam by a domain wall based nanoantenna excited by an
external oscillating ﬁeld Hrf. uk indicates the spin-wave propagation direction. On the bottom, the spin
precession along in single oscillation is sketched. (b) STXM image showing the directional emission and
focusing of convex spin-wave wavefronts by a curved wall. (c) Spatial proﬁle of the spin-wave amplitude along

10-18

[Page 361]
an extended curved domain wall. In this case, the RF frequency was 1.57 GHz.
Wavefronts retaining the same form of the domain wall are visible for several
microns, limited only by the image acquisition window. Indeed, in ﬁgure 10.10(f) the
experimental spatial proﬁle extracted along the yellow dashed line of panel (d) shows
a strong spin-wave signal visible for more than 15 periods, featuring a spin-wave
wavelength of 300 nm. These experiments demonstrate how a straightforward
wavefront engineering is possible by shaping the spin texture.
Figure 10.11 demonstrates that robust spin-wave interference patterns, spanning
for several wavelengths, can be generated by patterning multiple spin-wave sources.
In particular, panels (a-b) show the experimental STXM image (a) and micro-
magnetic simulations (b) of planar and radial wavefronts excited simultaneously by
a straight domain wall (red line) and a vortex (red dot) located within the domain
wall. The radial and planar wavefronts are highlighted by curved and straight red/
blue lines, respectively, and the white arrows represent the direction of the magnet-
ization of the top layer. The excitation frequency was 1.29 GHz.
As the black/white contrast represents the variation of the out-of-plane compo-
nent of the magnetization at a speciﬁc time, constructive interference fringes are
visualized as high intensity black/white regions. On the other hand, destructive
interference is visible as low-intensity, grey areas and corresponds to regions where
the spin waves sum in anti-phase. The region of the ﬁrst interference maximum is
indicated by white lines in both the panels. Figure 10.11(c) shows the spatial map of
the spin-wave amplitude, obtained by ﬁtting the time-trace of every acquired pixel
of panel (a) with a sinusoidal function and plotting the corresponding amplitude.
Figure 10.11. Generation of interference patterns using multiple nanoantennas. (a, b) STXM image and
corresponding simulations of the interference from radial wavefronts emitted by a vortex (red dot) and a
planar wavefront emitted by a straight wall (red line) excited at 1.29 GHz. The individual wavefronts are
indicated by blue and red lines. (c) Spatial map of the spin-wave amplitude, showing the ﬁrst ﬁve interference
maxima (yellow) and minima (blue). (d) Amplitude proﬁle extracted along the dashed line in panel (c) and
corresponding simulations. Scale bars: 500 nm. Adapted from [36]. John Wiley & Sons.
Nanofabrication
10-19

[Page 362]
The characteristic interference fringes, arising from the constructive (maxima) and
destructive (minima) interference, are represented by yellow and blue regions,
respectively, and are numbered in the panel. In panel (d), we show the experimental
(red) and simulated (grey) spatial proﬁle corresponding to the red dashed line of
panel (c). The ﬁrst ﬁve maxima, spaced by 450 nm are plotted, demonstrating a very
good agreement between experimental and simulated data.
The spatial engineering of spin-wave wavefronts via tailored magnonic nano-
antennas, the focusing of spin-wave beams down to the nanoscale, and the
controlled generation of robust multibeam interference patterns are essential
ingredients for analog processing. Future applications include reconﬁgurable micro-
wave ﬁlters, isolators, and devices for pattern and speech recognition based on the
emission, manipulation, and interference of coherent spin-wave wavefronts at the
nanoscale.
10.4 End-of-chapter summary
In this chapter, we discussed our recent results on the development of the patterning
technique tam-SPL, for reversibly controlling ferromagnetic spin textures with
nanoscale precision. In particular, we presented the working principle and the
main features of the technique. Figure 10.12 shows a summary of the capabilities of
tam-SPL which were discussed in this chapter, and an overview of the magnetic
systems where tam-SPL was applied. In particular, we demonstrated that tailored
2D–1D and 0D structures can be stabilized in continuous ﬁlms (panel (a)): 2D
magnetic domains with different shapes and extensions, 1D domain walls, such as
Néel walls with tailored spin conﬁguration, and 0D topological solitons, such as
vortices and Bloch lines. We showed (panel (c)) that our technique can be applied to
exchange biased systems with in-plane magnetization, but also to more complex
systems such as synthetic antiferromagnets, where the magnetization orientation of
the top layer at remanence can be patterned by controlling the exchange bias, while
the magnetization of the bottom layer is set accordingly by the antiferromagnetic
coupling. In perspective, tam-SPL can be applied also to exchange biased systems
with
perpendicular
magnetic
anisotropy.
Interestingly,
in
these
systems,
Dzyaloshinskii–Moriya interaction stabilizes topological spin textures such as sky-
rmions and chiral domain walls [49]. The nucleation, dynamics and annihilation of
these topological spin conﬁgurations could be therefore controlled by patterning via
tam-SPL.
Panel (b) presents some applications we demonstrated in the ﬁeld of magnonics.
In particular, we showed the spatial modulation of the spin-wave excitation and
propagation in tailored magnetic domains, and the development of magnonic
crystals based on the periodic modulation of the effective ﬁeld. We then demon-
strated that nanoscale 1D spin textures act as nanowaveguides, which effectively
channel and steer spin waves, and can be the basic elements of tunable nanoscale
circuits allowing for the control of the superposition and interference of conﬁned
spin waves. Finally, we presented a versatile, optically inspired platform using spin
waves, in which magnonic nanoantennas based on tailored spin textures are used for
Nanofabrication
10-20

[Page 363]
launching spatially-shaped coherent wavefronts, diffraction-limited spin-wave
beams, and generating robust multi-beam interference patterns. In perspective,
one of the most interesting applications in magnonics is represented by the study and
control of spin waves in fully three-dimensional spin textures.
From a broader point of view, the full potential of engineered spin textures has
still to be unveiled. For instance, the study and patterning of spin textures in
different magnetic systems such as ferrimagnets and antiferromagnets represent
extremely promising routes for exploiting the ultrafast velocity of domain walls [50],
the spin dynamics up to the THz frequency range [51] and novel physical
phenomena that can be eventually disclosed by tailoring the magnetic properties
of these systems.
Figure 10.12. Current capabilities and applications of tam-SPL. (a) Multidimensional spin textures such as 2D
magnetic domains [12, 42], 1D domain walls [44, 52] and tailored 0D topological solitons [26] are written
reversibly in continuous ﬁlms. (b) Applications of tam-SPL in the ﬁeld of magnonics. Top panel: magnetic
domains are used for spatially modulating the spin-wave excitation and propagation [12]. Bottom panels:
nanoscale spin textures such as domain walls are used for building reconﬁgurable tunable circuits [44] and
optically-inspired nanomagnonic platforms [36]. (c) Exchange bias systems where tam-SPL has been
demonstrated so far: in-plane FM/AF bilayers, synthetic antiferromagnets and systems characterized by
perpendicular magnetic anisotropy.

10.5 Student’s corner
• What are the main factors determining the minimum magnetic domain size
which can be patterned with tam-SPL?
• What are the factors determining the minimum tip temperature needed for
tam-SPL patterning?
• What are the factors determining the maximum tam-SPL writing speed?
• What determines the resilience of tam-SPL patterns with respect to time,
magnetic ﬁeld and temperature?
• Would it be possible to pattern different spin textures in two stacked
exchange bias antiferromagnet/ferromagnet bilayers? If so, how?

Chapter 11
Nanofabrication of three-dimensional magnetic
structures
Dédalo Sanz-Hernández, Claire Donnelly, Lucas Pérez and
Amalio Fernández-Pacheco
The extension of nanoscale magnetism to three dimensions provides exciting
opportunities to explore new physical phenomena, and at the same time, opens
fascinating prospects to create 3D magnetic devices which could be exploited in
future sensing and data storage technologies. The move of nanomagnetism to three
dimensions faces, however, similar challenges to other nanotechnology ﬁelds: the
fabrication of 3D nanoscale materials is extremely difﬁcult and requires new
patterning methods better suited for this purpose. In this chapter, we review some
of the most promising emerging fabrication techniques for 3D nanopatterning of
magnetic systems. We focus ﬁrst on direct-write methods such as focused electron
beam induced deposition (FEBID) and two-photon lithography, which have the
ability to 3D print nano- and micro-structures with great precision, and which can
either directly write magnetic materials, or create 3D scaffolds for subsequent
deposition of magnetic ﬁlms. We complement the chapter with a section dedicated to
electrodeposition of magnetic materials, a technique with the ability to conformally
deposit magnetic metals and lead to a wide variety of magnetic systems, including
nanowires, multilayers and complex objects. Our text introduces ﬁrst the funda-
mentals of these techniques, followed by an in-depth discussion of recent key
examples where these techniques have been used to explore new 3D magnetic effects.
An extensive list of references and materials is included, to help those interested in
the 3D nanofabrication of magnetic systems.
11.1 Introduction
11.1.1 3D magnetic nanostructures for the future
Magnetic materials play a large role in our lives in a large variety of ways, due to
their tailorable properties [1]. On the macro scale, their interaction with external
doi:10.1088/978-0-7503-2608-7ch11
11-1
ª IOP Publishing Ltd 2020. All rights,
including for text and data mining (TDM), artificial intelligence (AI) training, and similar technologies, are reserved.

[Page 369]
phenomena is key: permanent magnets, that display high coercivities and produce
high stray magnetic ﬁelds, are used widely in energy production and harvesting,
particularly for renewable energy, making them a key component of the ﬁght against
climate change. Inductive magnetic materials, on the other hand, that are highly
susceptible to external ﬁelds, are widely used as sensors.
On the micro- and nanoscales, the impact of miniaturised magnets is no less
extensive. Multilayered magnetic ﬁlms are used as sensors in the automotive
industry, whilst grainy and patterned magnetic thin ﬁlms are most widely used in
perhaps the most well-known application of magnets: data storage. Following the
discovery of effects such as giant magnetoresistance (GMR), the possibility for the
electrical detection of the magnetic state has revolutionised data storage capabilities
[2]. With the advent of big data, and the Internet of Things, data and its storage is of
increasing importance in our lives, and magnetic storage devices—both tapes and
hard drives—remain one of the most reliable long-term options.
Over the years, progress has relied on the continuous development of magnetic
materials with improved properties—however, for further progress, including higher
functionalities and more efﬁcient and ‘green’ devices, a paradigm shift is required.
One example is the case of permanent magnets: since the discovery of NdFeB in the
1980s, slight progress has been made via the doping of grain boundaries with exotic
metals (Dy). However, progress on the level of the game-changing improvements
provided by the discovery of NdFeB have been elusive. Recently, exciting develop-
ments in the 3D printing of bonded permanent magnets [3] have demonstrated that
higher functionality can be obtained by patterning the magnetic material in speciﬁc
3D geometries: therefore providing signiﬁcant improvements in device properties via
the geometry, and not the material itself.
There is growing excitement/consensus in the ﬁeld that such a change in
dimensionality may provide the much-needed paradigm shift for nanomagnetism
[4, 5]. Indeed, up until now, micro- and nanomagnetism have been limited to planar
systems that are essentially two-dimensional, and much progress has relied on the
highly controlled deposition of high quality magnetic thin ﬁlms. As with permanent
magnets, with the extension to three dimensions, there is the potential for control
over the geometry to provide completely new functionalities. In curved magnetic
ﬁlms, for example, there have been predictions of curvature-induced magnetic
anisotropy,
as
well
as
curvature-induced
Dzyalozinskii–Moriya
interactions
(DMI) that induce chirality into the system [6]. As a result, the introduction of
curvature to a ﬁlm provides an alternative route to tailoring the magnetic material
properties, with the potential for high levels of control, and access to properties not
achievable in two dimensions. When one moves to three-dimensional nanostruc-
tures, such as magnetic nanotubes, or cylindrical magnetic nanowires, theoretical
predictions indicate that the new geometries available will lead to domain wall
velocities signiﬁcantly higher than those observed in two-dimensional systems, as
well as the introduction of new degrees of freedom, such as magnetochirality, non-
reciprocal spin wave propagation, amongst others [7]. As a result, three-dimensional
nanomagnetism could offer the paradigm shift that the magnetism community has
been waiting for.
Nanofabrication
11-2

[Page 370]
11.2 Overview of nanofabrication techniques for magnetism
Despite the huge potential envisaged for 3D nanomagnetism provided by theoretical
and computational studies, the experimental realization of 3D magnetic nano-
structures has so far been scarce, due to the huge difﬁculty in patterning nanoscale
materials in three dimensions [4]. This fundamental bottleneck faced by nano-
magnetism is common to other branches of nanotechnology, which would also
beneﬁt from moving to 3D. As an introduction to new types of 3D nanofabrication
techniques, and speciﬁcally to those covered in this book chapter, let us ﬁrst brieﬂy
outline an example of how the fabrication of a standard magnetic nanostructure
device takes place, consisting of the combination of thin ﬁlm physical vapour
deposition (PVD) and standard lithography (optical/electron-beam lithography)
processes. In a PVD step, a single or multilayered thin ﬁlm with nanometric
thickness is deposited on a substrate at high or ultra-high vacuum conditions via
techniques such as evaporation or sputtering. This results in a high-quality planar
magnetic material, where stoichiometry, crystallographic structure and defect
density can be very ﬁnely controlled. Importantly, materials deposited by PVD
also beneﬁt from high-quality interfaces, where sophisticated magnetic interfacial
effects [8] such as surface perpendicular magnetic anisotropy, indirect exchange
coupling or antisymmetric exchange coupling can be engineered. Complementing
PVD processes, lateral patterning of thin ﬁlms at the nanometric scale is achieved by
a series of lithography steps, where an organic resist is ﬁrst spun on the substrate,
followed by its exposure to a well-controlled photon/electron dose that modiﬁes its
solubility and enables it to be selectively removed by a chemical developer. The
remaining resist is then used as a mask for subsequent steps, where the ﬁlm areas not
protected by this mask are selectively removed, a process that involves, e.g. physical
etching (via ion irradiation) or chemical etching processes. This type of patterning
approach to obtain a magnetic nano-device may comprise tens of steps involving the
deposition of metallic and insulating layers, metallization steps, lift-off procedures…
all of them critical for the ﬁnal realization of the device. Despite this, 2D patterning
is now a mature technology used in industry and research institutions around the
world.
These denominated top-down methods for nanofabrication, where material is
removed appropriately to form a magnetic nanostructure (see ﬁgure 11.1), excel in the
fabrication of planar systems, and can readily reach nanoscale resolutions if the
correct method is used. However, despite some remarkable advances in realizing 3D
nanostructures [9], these are not designed, and in general are not suitable for 3D
nanopatterning. The ﬁeld of 3D nanomagnetism thus requires a different set of
nanofabrication methods following a bottom-up approach, where small building
constituents are put together to form a nanostructure. There exists a great variety of
bottom-up techniques currently under development for nanotechnology, such as block
co-polymer technology [10], DNA self-assembly [11], vapor–liquid solid methods [12],
to cite a few. In this book chapter, we focus on two particular methodologies that are
starting to provide exciting new experimental results in 3D nanomagnetism, namely
(i) direct-write techniques for 3D nanofabrication, which include direct building of
Nanofabrication
11-3

[Page 371]
3D-shaped magnetic materials, as well as the possibility of creating 3D scaffolds that
are subsequently covered by magnetic thin ﬁlms, and (ii) deposition of advanced 3D
magnetic structures using electrodeposition techniques. We note that this chapter
content does not constitute a comprehensive overview of the 3D nanofabrication of
magnetic materials, but provides a good introduction to the fundamentals of direct-
writing and electrodeposition of 3D magnetic nanostructures.
11.3 Direct-write techniques for 3D nanofabrication of magnetic
materials
In recent years, signiﬁcant advances in 3D printer technologies have enabled the
fabrication of 3D geometries that are not possible with standard ‘subtractive’
techniques [13, 14], making possible the prototyping of small-scale devices in an
accessible and low-cost fashion. This ability to design and fabricate 3D patterns and
geometries at the micro- and nanoscale has led to creative developments and
advances in several ﬁelds, including optics [15–17], electronics [18–20] and bio-
medical technologies [21, 22].
For magnetic devices, which are widely used in sensing, the automation industry,
and for energy harvesting and production, 3D fabrication techniques are also of
signiﬁcant interest. Recent developments in the 3D printing of magnetic materials
have led to lightweight polymer bonded magnets that can be patterned in complex
shapes. As a result, enhancements of the properties of permanent magnets [23, 24]
and the production of speciﬁc magnetic ﬁelds [3], ideally suited for speciﬁc scientiﬁc
infrastructure applications [25], has been realised. In addition to permanent
magnets, patterned 3D magnetic systems have been incorporated in shapeable
magnetoelectronics [26] and rolled up sensor microﬂuidics [27]. An important
advantage of magnetic structures is the possibility that they provide for remote
