Figure 11.1. Schematic representation of bottom-up versus top-down lithography techniques for nano-
technology, involving systems on the scale of 1–100 nm. Some natural systems are included in the scale for
comparison.
Nanofabrication
11-4

[Page 372]
control, which has important implications for in vivo applications such as drug
delivery, and indeed has been applied in microrobots [28] for the transport of
targeted cells in mice and zebraﬁsh [29], the orientation and alignment of electronics
in 3D with ﬂoating magnetic micro-tweezer robots [30], as well as for athermal
shape‐memory [31] with the possibility of 4D bioprinting [32] in the future.
The 3D structuring of materials becomes particularly interesting when funda-
mental lengthscales are approached which, for magnetism, are the magnetic
exchange length and domain wall width (typically ~tens to hundreds of nanometres)
[33]. On this scale, the geometry starts to inﬂuence the magnetic structure via effects
such as shape anisotropy, conﬁgurational anisotropy and curvature-induced phe-
nomena. In particular, 3D conﬁned geometries have been predicted to lead to a
variety of new magnetic properties [4, 6], including new types of domain walls with
ultra-high velocities [7, 34, 35], effective anisotropies and chiral interactions in
curved magnetic shells [36–38] and torsion effects in magnetic helices [39]. First
experimental results have conﬁrmed the presence of Bloch-point domain walls [40]
and signiﬁcant curvature-induced chirality [41]. However, domain wall dynamics
have turned out to be signiﬁcantly more complex than predicted [42, 43], high-
lighting the need for extensive experimental characterisation, including sophisticated
nanofabrication.
There have been several demonstrations of the fabrication of 3D systems via, for
example, self-assembly [10, 44], rolled-up technologies [27, 45], grazing incidence
physical vapour deposition [46–48] and anodised alumina/polymer templates
[49, 50], where speciﬁc geometries or shapes can be fabricated. For the realisation
of a wide variety of geometries, however, a more ﬂexible, tunable technique is
required. In this section, we focus on recent advances in the fabrication of arbitrary
3D magnetic nanostructures via nanoscale 3D printing direct write techniques,
which provide the capability to design and fabricate arbitrary geometries.
For the fabrication of arbitrary 3D magnetic nanostructures using direct-write
techniques, there are two main routes available (see ﬁgure 11.2): the direct growth of
magnetic materials, and the growth of scaffolds, which can then be mapped into
magnetic structures via a variety of deposition methods. We will give an overview of
Figure 11.2. Direct-write of 3D magnetic nanostructures using two main approaches. (a) The direct write of
magnetic materials in 3D, with FEBID, results in isolated structures such as (i) the helix, as well as
(ii) extended lattices. (b) The patterning of non-magnetic scaffolds, which are then coated with magnetic
materials to form shell structures, resulting in (i) a domain wall conduit, (ii) an artiﬁcial spin ice lattice, (iii) an
artiﬁcial buckyball structure. (a-i) Reproduced from [51]. (a-ii) Reproduced from [52]. (b-i) Reproduced from
[53]. (b-ii) Reproduced from [54]. (b-iii) Reproduced from [55].
Nanofabrication
11-5

[Page 373]
both routes, including the different techniques available and the state-of-the-art,
along with long-standing challenges and limitations.
11.4 Focused electron beam induced deposition
11.4.1 FEBID for magnetism
As described in section 1.2, in recent years, magnetic nanostructures have mainly
been fabricated with top-down techniques that combine lithography with the thin
ﬁlm deposition of ferromagnetic materials. For the direct printing of 3D magnetic
materials, however, a bottom-up fabrication process is required. Such a process is
currently provided by FEBID, which enables the direct-write fabrication of
materials with nanoscale resolution (ﬁgure 11.2(a)). FEBID is a powerful pattern-
ing technique in which a material is locally deposited via the cracking of a
precursor gas by a focused electron beam [56] (see also chapter 4 in the present
book). Due to the ﬂexibility in the precursor used, as well as the small focal points
of electron beams in electron microscopes, it is possible to deposit a variety of
materials with very high spatial resolutions, on the order of tens of nanometres,
thus opening up exciting perspectives in condensed matter physics [57].
When one considers the fabrication of 3D magnetic materials, one of the main
concerns is the quality of the deposited material, which has critical implications for
magnetic properties of the nanoscale system. In this regard, FEBID could be slightly
concerning: generally, the organic nature of the deposition process leads to a high
carbon content of the deposited structure [56]. However, in recent years the direct
deposition of functional—and in many cases, high purity—cobalt and iron struc-
tures, with good magnetic properties has been demonstrated [58, 59]. This key
progress is possible thanks to the usage of carbonyl cobalt and iron precursor gases,
whose decomposition is particularly efﬁcient under a FEBID process [60].
Furthermore, recent advances in precursor synthesis have led to the deposition of
CoFe alloys [61]. This, together with the co-injection of several gases [62] and the
growth of core–shell geometries via sequential growth [63] paves the way for the
deposition of tailored magnetic materials. With the development of advanced
modelling and simulations of FEBID, a deeper understanding of the underlying
mechanisms has been achieved, which in turn has led to the fabrication of tuneable
materials with a range of different properties [64–66].
Of course, perhaps the most relevant aspect of FEBID for nanomagnetism is not
simply the deposition of magnetic materials, but the capability it provides for the
structuring of magnetic materials on lengthscales of the order of tens of nanometres.
At these scales, the geometry and curvature of patterned systems can be used to
dictate the magnetic properties, and thus result in magnetic nanodevices. In two
dimensions, 2D nanowire devices have been applied successfully, e.g. to domain wall
logic [67–69], while in three dimensions, freestanding pillars for nanomagnetic logic
[70] and magnetic actuation [71] have been realised. Furthermore, the ability of
FEBID for direct-writing on arbitrary substrates has been exploited to realise a
range of customised scanning-probe microscopy and spectroscopy sensors with
different 3D geometries [72–76].
Nanofabrication
11-6

[Page 374]
From ﬁrst demonstrations of the fabrication of simple cylindrical nanowires with
a static electron beam, advances in the design of stream ﬁles—that are used to direct
the electron beam—have led to enhanced control in the fabrication of high-purity
3D magnetic structures. This control has led to greater control over the growth and
magnetic properties of the more simple geometry ferromagnetic cylindrical nano-
wires [77, 78], to the ability to pattern more complex 3D structures (see
ﬁgure 11.2(a)) such as ferromagnetic nano-helices [51] and 3D artiﬁcial lattices
[52]. Yet more complex 3D structures have been achieved with recent advances in
algorithms for the nanofabrication of 3D structures [52, 79, 80] ranging from 3D
nanowire architectures to the implementation of CAD-based design for truly 3D
nanostructures, from the topologically-non-trivial Möbius strip to a variety of
curved 3D surfaces. The new feasibility of the fabrication of these more complex
structures opens up exciting perspectives for additive nano-manufacturing, thus
advancing FEBID towards a 3D printer at the nanoscale.
Whilst the direct patterning of 3D magnetic nanostructures that FEBID provides
is unparalleled by other techniques, one of the main challenges it faces is the material
properties of the deposited nanostructures. For the realisation of the predicted
potential of 3D nanomagnetism, greater control over the global magnetic properties,
such as the exchange lengths, saturation magnetisation and coercive ﬁelds are
required. Moreover, despite some attempts to control interfacial effects in FEBID
materials [81], the technique is currently not sufﬁcient to study and exploit this type
of phenomena with the level of control and reproducibility required for spintronics.
Aside from interfacial effects, higher magnetic functionality can also be obtained via
the combination of 3D nanomagnetism with alternative active materials including,
for example, magnetostrictive materials. Both routes will require the development of
further expertise in the development of new precursor molecules, as well as in the co-
deposition of materials, whilst post-processing techniques such as annealing, which
has proven effective in obtaining high purity crystalline cobalt [82], and electron
radiation in a hydrogen atmosphere [83] promise accessibility to higher purity
materials.
Although our ﬁrst thought may be to focus on developing alternative treatments
and post-processing techniques to avoid low-purity materials, it is important to
consider the advantages that ‘dirty’ carbon-transition metal alloys might provide
for magnetic devices. In fact, it has been shown that by working in the low
transition metal-content regime, effects such as intergranular tunnelling [84] an
enhanced extraordinary Hall effect [85] can be achieved. Such effects have been
successfully exploited for ultra-high sensitivity sensing in other non-magnetic
precursors for a variety of effects such as metal–insulator transitions [86], strain
[87] and gas sensors [88].
11.4.2 FEBID fundamentals
FEBID employs the beam of electrons of a scanning electron microscope (SEM), to
controllably induce the decomposition of a precursor gas into a solid deposit and
volatile fragments [79, 80, 89, 90]. The tightly-focused beams of electrons produced
Nanofabrication
11-7

[Page 375]
by SEMs enable nanoprinting structures as small as a few tens of nm and can be
computer-controlled with nanometric precision. Figure 11.3(a) schematically
describes this process.
The FEBID process, however, is not simple and involves a large set of
interactions and interlinked physical effects [56, 64, 91, 92] and newcomers diving
into the scientiﬁc literature may ﬁnd themselves overwhelmed by the large number of
variables, parameters and experimental conditions that play a role in the process
(ﬁgure 11.3(b)). In this section, we introduce the tools and models developed by the
FEBID research community for the understanding of the FEBID process through
the combination of experiments, continuum models and advanced simulation tools.
Furthermore, user-friendly empirical calibration procedures and beam-pattering
algorithms that enable non-expert users to design and fabricate structures will be
discussed to provide a starting point to those considering using FEBID for 3D
nanopatterning. Four independent subsections follow, covering resolution, basic
theory and modelling, patterning algorithms and hybrid techniques. These may be
read in series or individually.
11.4.3 FEBID resolution
The spatial resolution of the technique (deﬁned as the thinnest printable feature) is
determined by how tightly the electrons are compressed by the electromagnetic
lenses in the SEM (beam width) and how much does the beam spread when the
electrons collide with the atoms in the substrate and the growing nanostructure
(beam-substrate interaction volume). It can vary between 200 and 15 nm depending
on the conditions employed [90].
Figure 11.3. The FEBID process. (a) Schematic representation of the decomposition of precursor molecules
being performed by the electron beam of an SEM. (b) Cross-interaction between parameters during FEBID.
Three circles represent the three elements that interact during FEBID (electron beam, precursor and substrate)
and key parameters related to each element are included inside each circle. Key interactions between elements
are represented by arrows.
Nanofabrication
11-8

[Page 376]
Beyond being able to produce such small 3D volumetric features, FEBID also
excels at controlling the position of such structures, the positioning resolution of
FEBID is determined by the precision of SEM beam-scanning hardware, which can
reach sub-nm performance at high magniﬁcation. Positioning resolution is seldomly
discussed, as volume resolution is the standard benchmark across all techniques, but
it is, however, of great interest for the nanofabrication of 3D magnetic nano-
structures as magnetic coupling is highly dependent on nanostructure separation and
overlap [93].
Additionally, one may deﬁne a layer resolution as the thinnest possible layer that
the technique can deposit. In theory, the layer resolution in FEBID is limited by the
shortest pulse that the SEM can produce and sub-monolayer deposition of material
could be possible using a sufﬁciently low precursor ﬂux and a sufﬁciently short pulse
of electrons. In practice, however, this process is limited by vacuum quality in the
SEM chamber. Depending on the microscope’s base pressure, monolayers of
residual gas will adhere to the surface at different rates [94]. This residual gas is
always decomposed by the electron beam and limits the smallest layer thickness that
can be deposited without suffering from excessive contamination. Layer thickness in
the order of a few nanometers is generally employed during 3D nanofabrication [80].
11.4.4 FEBID theory, modelling and practical recommendations
The FEBID process involves three key physical entities: a precursor gas which is
decomposed into a solid deposit and volatile fragments, a focused beam of electrons,
and a substrate where the precursor molecules adsorb to generate nanostructure
growth. Once the nanoprinting process has started, the nanostructure being printed
becomes the substrate. These three entities are represented as solid circles in
ﬁgure 11.3(b), and key experimental parameters related to each are included inside
the circles. This section describes this relationship diagram in detail, discussing
different tools available to gain a deeper understanding of some of the interactions
represented.
Starting with the electron beam, its acceleration potential determines the kinetic
energy with which electrons arrive at the substrate and is critical for the scattering
that takes place once electrons interact with the substrate [95]. This beam–substrate
scattering is indicated as a double arrow in ﬁgure 11.3(b) labelled interaction volume
and results in a cloud of electrons that forms inside and around the substrate after
beam impact. The shape of this interaction volume depends both on the energy of
the beam and the substrate material and density. It also strongly affects the
resolution of the process and can generate the appearance of parasitic co-deposition
below or around the exposed regions [79, 89, 90]. Figure 11.4(a) shows as an
example, the interaction volume for an electron beam incident onto a Pt tip with an
energy of 3 keV (left) and 30 keV (right), calculated using Monte Carlo simulations.
At 3 keV most electrons are stopped or backscattered by the tip leading to a high
interaction volume, whereas at 30 keV most of the beam passes through with little
scattering.
Nanofabrication
11-9

[Page 377]
The amount of scattering also impacts the energy deposited on the substrate and
consequent heating. If electrons are stopped by the substrate, the kinetic energy of
the beam will directly be converted into heat, whereas if the electrons are mostly
subject to elastic scattering events, negligible energy is transferred. A versatile and
freely accessible tool to model and understand electron–substrate interactions is
CASINO [96].
The second critical parameter related to the incident beam is the beam current,
which determines the amount of electrons that impact the substrate every second. A
high beam current density will likely cause a fast depletion of the precursor in the
regions where the electron concentration is higher. It will also result in a higher
release of energy into the substrate (heating). High current beams are associated with
larger beam spot sizes (due to electrostatic repulsion inside the beam), and will result
in higher growth rates as more molecules are dissociated per second [64, 65, 89, 99].
We continue this discussion with the precursor. In FEBID, the precursor is
injected into the chamber of an electron microscope using a small nozzle or gas
injector system (GIS), with a typical diameter of a few hundreds of microns. A GIS
needs to be employed in order to inject the gas locally to the growth region only,
keeping a high vacuum in the rest of the chamber. This enables electrons to travel
with negligible scattering, at the same time making sure that no parasitic decom-
position of the precursor gas takes place at other parts of the chamber, which could
result in damage if a metallic (or insulating) ﬁlm was accidentally deposited on them.
The use of a GIS means that the concentration of precursor gas is not uniform across
Figure 11.4. Simulation and modelling tools. (a) Electron trajectories at 3 keV (left) and 30 keV (right),
simulated using CASINO [96]. Reproduced from [97]. (b) Simulated gas ﬂow from a gas injector system.
Reproduced from [64]. (c) Simulated nanostructure growth using a ﬁnite difference model including temper-
ature, precursor diffusion and electron trajectories. Reproduced from [98]. (d) Analytical solution of precursor
concentration and growth rate as a function of fundamental FEBID frequencies represented in a 2D map.
Reproduced from [65].
Nanofabrication
11-10

[Page 378]
the whole sample, but is space dependent [100]. Figure 11.4(b) shows a simulated gas
distribution, where a central maximum corresponding to the GIS axis is observed,
with a progressive decrease in gas concentration as one deviates from this axis.
Additionally, the arrival of the gas is also directional, and shadowing effects can
occur during the nanofabrication process. Due to this spatial dependence of the gas
ﬂow, a critical experimental parameter when performing FEBID is the GIS align-
ment. Due to the small size of the nozzles employed (50–300 μm diameters) an
incorrect alignment of the GIS can easily lead to very little gas arriving at the growth
region and unsuccessful growth. In general, to achieve maximum gas ﬂow, one
should make the center of the GIS axis coincide with the point where growth will
take place. It is important for inexperienced users to keep in mind that this alignment
occurs in 3D space and cannot be assessed solely from the 2D image provided by the
SEM. The height encoder of the sample stage should be employed to assess the
height of the GIS during the alignment process, to ensure that the axis of the GIS
truly aligns to the growth region and not below or above it. The sketch of
ﬁgure 11.3(a) may serve as a rough illustration of this type of GIS misalignment;
in this ﬁgure, the GIS is pointed to the top of the nanowire, but the gas lands on the
substrate very far to the right, where the electron beam cannot reach. In a real
experiment the nanowire of the ﬁgure would never have started growing in the ﬁrst
place!
The chemistry and composition of the precursor is a second variable critical to the
process, as it determines the composition of the deposit. Due to their availability, the
ﬁrst FEBID precursors were borrowed from the ﬁeld of chemical vapour deposition
(CVD). CVD chemistries [56], however, are optimized for thermal instead of
electron decomposition, and the search for more suitable FEBID precursors is an
active ﬁeld of research [101]. For the direct nanofabrication of magnetic nano-
structures, Co2(CO)8 and Fe(CO)5 are two commonly employed precursors due to
the high purity of magnetic deposits that they produce [59, 102]. Although not
directly related to the precursor itself, we include vacuum quality as a precursor
variable. Inevitably, the vacuum in an SEM chamber is not perfect and there always
exists a residual level of gases in the chamber that compete with the precursor for the
adsorption sites available on the substrate. The number of monolayers of residual
carbon impinging on the substrate per second decreases with increasing vacuum
quality [94] and therefore low chamber pressures and the use of cryogenic traps
inside the chamber is recommended to achieve high-purity growth of magnetic
materials.
Finally, the substrate plays also a key role. Its composition and microstructure
not only determine the amount of electron scattering during a FEBID process, but it
may also play a critical role in the growth mechanisms via its interaction with the
precursor. When a precursor molecule falls on the substrate, it could potentially
form a bond that will depend on the chemical state of the precursor molecule and the
substrate’s surface. One or more adsorption energies may be associated with the
formation of this bond, and complex adsorption potentials, with barriers and wells
may be present [103]. If the bond between the substrate and the precursor results
from a strong chemical interaction, catalytic effects may occur, and the strength of
Nanofabrication
11-11

[Page 379]
such bonding will also affect precursor diffusion, which is the movement of precursor
molecules over the substrate due to concentration gradients.
The precursor dynamics related to all the above energy barriers is critically
inﬂuenced by the substrate temperature. A high substrate temperature, for example,
can enable the formation of chemical bonds that are otherwise prevented by an
energy barrier [103], or may cause a strong desorption of the precursor when the
thermal energy of the molecules increases above the adsorption energy [65].
Cryogenically cooled substrates, for example, are employed in order to allow the
absorption of a large number of precursor molecules on the substrate and generate
accelerated FEBID growth [104].
In addition to the elementwise interactions discussed above, two second-order
interactions are possible between the electron beam, the precursor, and the substrate.
These are surface activation and surface poisoning. Surface activation consists of the
creation of chemically active species by the electron beam on the surface of the
substrate that reduce the energy of adsorption of the precursor or cause autocatalytic
effects. On the other hand, surface poisoning refers to the effect in which the
decomposition of precursor by the electron beam results in chemically inactive
species covering the substrate, inhibiting potential autocatalytic activity [105].
To understand the consequences of the large number of interactions described
above, two complementary approaches are employed: continuum models [64, 65] in
which the fundamental precursor-substrate-beam variables are functions of contin-
uous variables (space and time), and ﬁnite-difference simulations (ﬁgure 11.4(c)) in
which each variable is modelled within small 3D-voxels and nanostructure growth is
calculated with ﬁxed time steps [66].
Continuum models are most suitable for the study of fundamental interactions
and establishing correlations between the different FEBID parameters. Typically
ﬁnding analytical solutions, predictions from continuum models can be computed at
low cost and can be efﬁciently ﬁtted to experimental data. To help in the
simpliﬁcation of model construction and data interpretation, the framework of
fundamental FEBID frequencies has been recently introduced [65]. These frequen-
cies correspond to the coefﬁcients found in the differential equations that drive
precursor dynamics on the surface of growing substrates and their unit is [s−1] or
[Hz]. When these frequencies are compared to each other (ﬁgure 11.4(d)), different
growth regimes can be directly inferred and allow researchers to simplify the
modelling, interpretation, and planiﬁcation of a FEBID experiment.
To model more complex 3D dynamics for FEBID growth, only ﬁnite-difference
simulations can currently incorporate the evolving nature of the growth surfaces
albeit with a signiﬁcant computational cost. The emergence of GPU (graphical
processing unit) software acceleration successfully employed in, e.g. ﬁnite-element
micromagnetic modelling [106], and the rapidly increasing ratio of GPU computa-
tional power/GPU price (driven by the strong role that GPUs play in the efﬁciency
of
artiﬁcial
intelligence
algorithms) can provide
signiﬁcant
acceleration
to
ﬁnite-difference FEBID modelling and even open the path to more complex
modelling or even iterative optimization of 3D-nanoprinting pattern generation,
discussed in the next section.
Nanofabrication
11-12

[Page 380]
11.4.5 FEBID: 3D nanopatterning algorithms
For many users of FEBID techniques, the study of fundamental interactions and the
development of growth optimization algorithms will be beyond the scope of their
application. For this reason, the FEBID community has recently made an important
effort to develop plug-and-play nanopatterning softwares that require minimum
user expertise and therefore open the use of the technique to a user base as wide as
possible. This approach was pioneered by Fowlkes et al [66] and is based on the
creation of interconnected nanowire networks. This algorithm starts with a
deﬁnition of a segment array such as the one displayed in ﬁgure 11.5(a), using a
customized software package. After deﬁning the nanowire array, a calibration
protocol is executed in the microscope (ﬁgure 11.5(b)) to measure the dependence
between segment angle and exposure time [79]. The measured dependence is then fed
into the same software and an exposure ﬁle is generated automatically. Execution of
the exposure then leads to the nanoscale printing of the nanowire array
(ﬁgure 11.5(c)). Further correction parameters to, e.g. heating during growth, can
be added during the nanostructure design process based on extra calibration
experiments or ﬁnite-element modelling [107]. A similar patterning strategy was
developed by Keller and Huth [108] and efforts are being made to integrate both
solutions into a single software package [90].
Following on the successful implementation of Fowlkes et al, a layer-by-layer
method for 3D nanopatterning was developed by Skoric et al [80] to generalise the
Figure 11.5. (a) Nanowire network deﬁned as a set of vertices. (b) Segment angle calibration example. (c) 3D
nanoprinted structures generated by the combination of (a) and (b) into dedicated nanopatterning software.
(d) 3D nanostructure deﬁned using a standard stereolithography (STL) ﬁle and segmented by dedicated
software. (e) Calibration procedure to ﬁt the geometrical properties of the electron beam interaction volume
with the substrate. (f) 3D nanoprinted structure deﬁned in the STL ﬁle of (d). (a–c) Reproduced from [79].
(d–f) Reproduced from [80].
Nanofabrication
11-13

[Page 381]
technique beyond nanowire networks. The input method for this algorithm is a
standardised .stl ﬁle that can be generated with common computer aided design
(CAD) software. In this case the .stl ﬁle is adaptively segmented (layer thickness
depends on local curvature) by a customized software package (ﬁgure 11.5(d)) that
in addition calculates the heat resistance of the nanostructure below each layer to
correct for heating effects. A two-step calibration protocol designed to measure the
space-dependent dose distribution resulting from a single dwell, and the heat
conductivity of the printed material, are implemented [80]. Step 1 of this calibration
is displayed in ﬁgure 11.5(e). Using the calibrated parameters, a dwell time solver
optimizes each layer exposure employing a per-layer proximity matrix and generates
a beam scanning ﬁle that is then executed by the microscope (ﬁgure 11.5(f)). Further
corrections such as beam defocusing and angular dependence of gas ﬂow can be
incorporated at the expense of extra calibration experiments [80].
11.4.6 FEBID and thin ﬁlm deposition: hybrid approach for 3D nanopatterning
In addition to the use of FEBID for direct-writing of ferromagnetic materials, it is
also possible to fabricate 3D nanostructures using a ‘hybrid’ scaffold-based
approach, where a non-magnetic 3D architecture is patterned, and then coated or
ﬁlled with a magnetic material, making possible the fabrication of both ‘shell’-like
and ‘bulk’-like 3D magnetic nanostructures. With this segmented approach, the
stringent requirements on the material properties of the 3D printed material are
lifted, and the geometry and dimensions of the scaffold alone can be optimised [80,
109] while processes for the deposition of thin-ﬁlms on 3D surfaces can be developed
independently. As we will see in section 11.5, this approach can be implemented with
other 3D nanofabrication techniques different from FEBID.
Regarding FEBID, a key advantage is using well-established chemistries, e.g. the
archetypical carbon–platinum compound via the MeCpPtMe3 precursor. FEBID of
such a material is a more established and well-understood process [89] than FEBID
of ferromagnetic materials, where less reproducible properties [51], electron-medi-
ated autocatalytic processes [110] and sudden changes in growth regimes [77] are
observed. Furthermore, using the scaffold approach, growth conditions can be tuned
to operate at or close to the reaction-rate-limited regime, which lead to higher spatial
resolutions and greater 3D growth ﬁdelity [56]. This is not generally possible when
directly depositing ferromagnetic materials, where conditions at the mass-transport-
limited regime are preferable to achieve high metallic purity [58, 111]. This hybrid
approach, combining FEBID with thin ﬁlm deposition, is a promising route for the
fabrication of core–shell nanowire architectures, as well as curved magnetic thin
ﬁlms [80].
In a ﬁrst demonstration, this hybrid nanofabrication approach has been employed
to demonstrate the injection of magnetic information from a 2D substrate into a 3D
nanostructure for the ﬁrst time [53]. For this, structures such as the one represented
in ﬁgure 11.6 have been used. Additionally, the versatility of FEBID enables 3D
shadowing strategies, such as the one shown in ﬁgure 11.6(d), where a 3D shade is
employed to prevent the deposition of magnetic material at the base of the 3D
Nanofabrication
11-14

[Page 382]
nanowire,
which
generates
a
magnetically-disconnected
reference
structure.
Extensive details regarding this fabrication strategy are available in [97].
11.5 Two-photon lithography
11.5.1 Two-photon lithography for 3D nanomagnetism
An alternative method for the fabrication of non-magnetic 3D scaffolds introduced
in section 11.4.6, is the use of two-photon lithography [112], see ﬁgure 11.7 (see also
chapter 2 in the present book). In two-photon lithography, an optical laser is focused
with an objective lens into a photoresist that is sensitive to UV light, and unaffected
by longer wavelength light. As a result, the chemical structure of the resist is only
modiﬁed where there is sufﬁcient photon density for two-photon absorption to take
place, which is only within the focal point of the laser beam. In this way, high spatial
resolutions (sub-diffraction-limit) can be reached and, as the resist is only modiﬁed
in the focal point of the laser, truly 3D structures can be written by moving this focal
voxel relative to the substrate.
Compared to FEBID, the main advantages of two-photon lithography are the
speed and accessibility—which currently come at the cost of spatial resolution. With
the combination of sophisticated lasers, piezo stages and galvo-mirrors, two-photon
lithography offers a signiﬁcant increase in the writing speed of 3D nanostructures—
reducing the typical writing time of a 1 μm structure from minutes to seconds—thus
opening up the possibility for high throughput processing/fabrication. In addition,
the technique makes use of a laser setup, instead of the need for an electron
microscope, and recent commercialisations have led to an increase in accessibility
for non-expert users. However, the spatial resolutions achievable with an optical
technique are still limited: although sub-wavelength spatial resolutions can be
reached, with standard two-photon lithography techniques spatial resolutions are
Figure 11.6. (a) 3D nanofabrication of a non-magnetic scaffold. (b) A layer of magnetic material is deposited
over the scaffold and the substrate. (c) Resulting hybrid 3D nanostructure. (d) A 3D nano-shade may be
employed to prevent magnetic material from being deposited at the base of the 3D nanowire and create a
magnetically-disconnected reference sample. (a–d) Reproduced from [53].
Nanofabrication
11-15

[Page 383]
still on the order of hundreds of nanometres—at the limit of the relevant magnetic
lengthscales—and with an elongation of the focal voxel in the vertical direction.
In recent years, two-photon lithography combined with a negative tone resist—
where the exposed region remains after development—has been applied to 3D
nanomagnetism, enabling the creation of structures such as an isolated artiﬁcial
Buckyball [114, 115], and extended artiﬁcial 3D spin lattices [116, 117] for the study
of magnetic frustration in 3D (see ﬁgure 11.2(b)(ii–iii)). It is also possible to combine
two-photon lithography with positive tone resists, where the exposed regions are
removed after development. These 3D molds can then be ﬁlled using electroplating
(see section 11.6), as has been demonstrated for gold metamaterials [118], as well as
for magnetic materials [117] which provide a route for the fabrication of nano- and
micro-wire and bulk magnetic architectures, albeit on larger lengthscales.
With the main challenge for two-photon lithography being the achievable spatial
resolution, it should come as no surprise that there have been signiﬁcant efforts in
recent years to reduce these to more relevant lengthscales, as described in detail in
[116]. There has been work on the development of new systems, new photoresists,
and post-processing techniques, which all show signiﬁcant promise for the fabrica-
tion of structures with sub-100 nm features. In particular, post-processing techniques
such as pyrolysis, in which the crosslinked polymer scaffold can be reduced to a
glassy carbon structure by heating at high temperatures in a nitrogen atmosphere
[119, 120], leading to the shrinking of an architecture by up to 80% and spatial
resolutions below 100 nm for 3D structures. This new technique has been recently
combined with homogeneous deposition of magnetic material to form an artiﬁcial
buckyball shell structure [114, 115]. In addition, two-photon lithography has been
mostly performed with a laser of 780 nm wavelength, which results in a minimum
lateral feature size of approximately 200 nm. Recently, higher spatial resolutions
Figure 11.7. Two-photon lithography, where a photoresist is exposed in the focal point of a focused laser,
creating a non-magnetic scaffold. Reproduced from [113].
Nanofabrication
11-16

[Page 384]
have been achieved by the move to lower wavelength incident light, resulting in sub-
100 nm spatial resolutions with a wavelength of 405 nm [121]. Comparative
increases in spatial resolution have been achieved through chemical changes that
lower the density of active radicals in the resist [122] as well as the use of overlapping
incident laser beams [123], thus demonstrating the wide range of routes to higher
spatial resolutions that are currently being explored.
As previously discussed, an advantage of the 3D scaffold + PVD approach to the
fabrication of 3D magnetic nanostructures involves the tuneable magnetic material
properties of thin-ﬁlm depositions: for the mapping of the scaffold into magnetic
materials, standard thin ﬁlm deposition techniques already optimised for the
deposition of high-quality materials can often be used, thus providing access to
signiﬁcantly higher purities, as well as the possibility for the integration of interfacial
or magnetostrictive functionalities. On the other hand, a main challenge comes with
the fact that many thin ﬁlm deposition techniques are optimised for planar
substrates. In 3D geometries, shadowing effects typically interfere with the homoge-
neous coating [114], and typically result in strongly-textured materials [124].
Such effects can be avoided with a number of chemical deposition techniques,
which are better suited for the deposition of thin ﬁlms on 3D geometries. Perhaps the
most well-known of these is atomic layer deposition (ALD), a process consisting of
multiple ‘self-limiting’ reactions that results in the deposition of homogeneous thin
ﬁlms with precise thicknesses, well-suited to high aspect ratio, or 3D surfaces. While
well-established for dielectric materials such as Al2O3, ALD of metallic materials
generally requires high temperature processes, except for particular metals including
tungsten, platinum and iridium. The direct deposition of magnetic materials via
ALD is also possible, and involves the deposition of a transition metal oxide, and its
subsequent reduction under a hydrogen atmosphere in high temperatures [125–127],
thus requiring scaffold structures with high thermal stability.
Electrochemical techniques (section 11.6) also offer a route to the homogeneous
deposition of magnetic materials on a 3D surface. With electrodeposition, a
magnetic layer can be grown on a 3D surface as long as a surface is conductive.
Indeed, recently the lower deposition temperatures possible with non-magnetic
metals have been exploited to ﬁrst deposit a conductive layer on a 3D polymer
scaffold, onto which a Ni layer was subsequently electroplated [113, 115]. An
alternative electrochemical technique that does not require a conductive layer is
electroless deposition, where a solution is used to activate a surface uniformly,
therefore making possible the deposition of a metallic layer. Magnetic materials,
both hard [128] and soft [129] have recently been deposited in this way, including on
polycarbonate membranes to produce magnetic nanotubes [129, 130].
11.5.2 Examples of scaffold lithography using two-photon lithography
As mentioned in the previous section, two-photon lithography provides a ﬂexible
route to fabricating 3D magnetic nanostructure via the fabrication of a non-
magnetic scaffold. As an example, we will now describe a case study of this
technique in detail, that was the ﬁrst demonstration of the use of two-photon
Nanofabrication
11-17

[Page 385]
lithography for 3D nanomagnetism: the fabrication of an artiﬁcial buckyball
structure [114]. The 6 μm diameter 3D artiﬁcial buckyball structure consisted of a
polymer scaffold that was coated with a magnetic material to form a 3D interlinked
tubular structure (a scanning electron microscope image is given in ﬁgure 11.2).
Along with providing an opportunity to investigate the role of vertices in a linked
magnetic nanotube structure, the buckyball geometry is also an extremely
stable structure which has been found to survive radiation damage well.
In the study, in order to provide feedback on the effectiveness of the fabrication
method, the structural and chemical properties of the magnetic nanostructure were
characterised by resonant phase tomography. To ensure a clear view of the sample
about 360° for the tomography experiments, the structure needed to be mounted on
tomography pins offering both high stability and an unobstructed view. An over-
view of the sample fabrication is given in ﬁgure 11.8.
To ensure a 360° view for the tomography measurements, the 3D magnetic
nanostructure was fabricated on pillars manufactured on a silicon substrate, that
were created via a combination of electron beam lithography to deﬁne a hard mask,
and reactive ion etching, resulting in high aspect ratio silicon pillars of height 36 μm
and diameter 10 μm (for more details we refer you to [114, 115]).
The buckyball was then fabricated directly on top of the pillars using two-photon
absorption lithography with a Nanoscribe 3D direct laser write system. Speciﬁcally,
in two-photon lithography a near infra-red laser (λ = 780 nm) is focused with an
objective lens into a photoresist that is sensitive to UV light, and unaffected by
longer wavelength light. As a result, the chemical structure of the resist is only
modiﬁed where there is sufﬁcient photon density for two-photon absorption (with an
effective wavelength of 390 nm) to take place, which is only within the focal point of
the laser beam. In this way, a spatial resolution of around 150 nm can be reached
and even surpassed by tailoring the laser power and exposure time. In addition, as
Figure 11.8. Fabrication of the artiﬁcial magnetic buckyball. (a) High aspect ratio Si pillars are prepared.
(b) The 3D structure of interest is exposed on the Si pillar using two-photon absorption lithography. (c) The
pillar is transferred to the tomography pin using the focused ion beam. Reproduced from [113].
Nanofabrication
11-18

[Page 386]
the resist is only modiﬁed in the focal point of the laser, truly 3D structures can be
written by moving this focal spot within the resist relative to the substrate. With the
‘dip-in’ mode, the objective lens of the system is immersed in the photoresist,
allowing for the 3D structure to be exposed directly on the pillar, as seen in
ﬁgure 11.8(b), which is located and brought into focus manually using the micro-
scope of the system. After exposure, the sample is developed by immersing in
PGMEA for 1 min, after which a ﬁnal cleaning process involving immersion in
isopropanol is performed.
Once the non-magnetic scaffold for the buckyball structure was successfully
fabricated on the pillars, the cross-linked polymer structure was then coated with 30
nm of Co capped with 6 nm of gold using a UHV sputtering system of base pressure
×
−
5
10 9 mbar. To optimise the homogeneity of the coating on the 3D structure, the
sample was mounted at an angle of approximately 45° with respect to the source and
during the deposition the specimen was rotated continuously.
As a ﬁnal step, the buckyball was transferred to the tomography pin using a
focused ion beam (FIB). In particular, a micromanipulator was attached to the pillar
approximately 10 μm below the top, where the buckyball was located, using focused
ion beam deposition of carbon. The pillar was subsequently cut from the Si substrate
as shown in ﬁgure 11.8(c) and transferred to the tomography pin. SEM images of the
sample mounted on the tomography pin, and the buckyball itself, are given in
ﬁgure 11.9.
The quality of the 3D magnetic buckyball structure was subsequently determined
using element-speciﬁc x-ray phase tomography, a high-resolution imaging techni-
que. In addition to providing a map of the structure in three dimensions, by tuning
the energy of the incident x-rays, this technique provides element speciﬁcity,
revealing the location of a particular element, in this case cobalt, within the sample
[114]. In the case of the buckyball, the 3D structure of the sample, and the location
of the cobalt, was determined to 25 nm spatial resolution in three dimensions. An
element speciﬁc rendering of the artiﬁcial buckyball structure is shown in
ﬁgure 11.9(c), with the resist shown in blue and the Co in orange. The resist visible
on the inside and underside of the buckyball illustrates the shadowing effects of the
Co sputter deposition [55].
As well as information on the location of the cobalt within the structure, element-
speciﬁc phase tomography provides a quantitative map of the electron and atomic
Figure 11.9. Characterisation of the artiﬁcial magnetic buckyball (a, b) Scanning electron micrographs of the
Co-coated artiﬁcial buckyball mounted on the tomography pin. (c) A 3D rendering of the artiﬁcial buckyball
with elemental contrast, with the cobalt layer shown in orange, and the polymer in blue. Reproduced from [55].
Nanofabrication
11-19

[Page 387]
density within the structure, which was used to determine information on the
oxidation state of the cobalt. In this case, it was determined that the cobalt layer on
the polymer scaffold was in fact oxidized, which was conﬁrmed with spectroscopic
measurements [114].
From the element speciﬁc phase tomography of the buckyball, two main
problems with the fabrication processes were identiﬁed. Firstly, the cobalt was
oxidised. As the oxidation of cobalt destroys its ferromagnetic properties, in order to
obtain a good quality ferromagnetic 3D structure for investigations of the effect of
the 3D structure on the magnetic properties, further optimisation of the sample
fabrication is required. As the Co was capped on the outside with a thin layer of
gold, it is likely that the oxidation and disorder in the ﬁlm is due to diffusion of
oxygen or other impurities from the polymer scaffold to the magnetic ﬁlm. To avoid
this, a protective layer could be introduced between the polymer and magnetic ﬁlm.
Secondly, the magnetic ﬁlm deposited on top of the polymer scaffold is not
homogeneous: there are rather severe shadowing effects and in some areas the
structure was not coated at all.
For the optimal production of magnetic structures, it was therefore concluded
that the deposition of magnetic thin ﬁlms onto 3D scaffolds is the most challenging
area. Although the shadowing effects of more traditional deposition techniques can
be exploited in the fabrication of 3D magnetic nanostructures based on curved
surfaces, such as in the case of the 3D artiﬁcial spin ice lattices [116, 117], the
homogeneous deposition of magnetic thin ﬁlms on a 3D surface is particularly
challenging.
11.6 Electrodeposition of 3D materials
11.6.1 Electrodeposition for 3D nanomagnetism
Complementing our discussion on direct-write nanofabrication techniques, we
focus this last section on the synthesis of nanomaterials and 3D materials via
electrodeposition. Electrodeposition has appeared in recent years as a non-
expensive and versatile alternative to 3D nanofabrication, in particular for the
growth of cylindrical nanowires and arrays of nanowires. Electrodeposition is a
chemical method for the synthesis of materials based on a cathodic process, where
ions in an electrolyte are incorporated on a conductive substrate forming a ﬁlm via
a reduction reaction [131]. As with most chemical techniques, electrodeposition is a
conformal growth technique, that allows the deposition of a wide variety of
materials, mainly metals and metallic alloys but also semiconductors [132] and
polymers [133], onto areas of complex shape. No vacuum or high temperature is
needed, which reduces the cost of electrodeposition compared with other growth
techniques and makes the process highly suitable for industrial use, mainly for the
fabrication of protective coatings but also in technological areas including micro-
electronics and magnetic recording [134]. However, there is an important draw-
back when comparing with other physical or chemical techniques normally used
for the growth of thin ﬁlms and coatings: the need for conductive substrates.
Electrodeposition is based on an electron-transfer reaction [135] and, therefore, it
Nanofabrication
11-20

[Page 388]
can only take places on metals, metallized surfaces—as we will show later—or
highly doped semiconductors [136, 137].
However, this drawback turns into an advantage for the growth of patterned thin
ﬁlms and 3D structures. The key concept is that electrodeposited material can only
grow on the conductive parts of the substrates. We can illustrate this with an
example. Let us take a silicon wafer in which a Ti/Au bilayer has been sputtered to
make it conductive. Then, we cover the wafer by a resist and, by means of e-beam
lithography and resist developing, we drill an ordered pattern of nanoholes. When
we use this structure as substrate in an electrodeposition process, the reduction
reaction can only take place on the bottom part of the holes, where the Au is in
contact with the electrolyte and the material growth from bottom to top, ﬁlling the
pores. Figure 11.10(a) shows a collection of ordered electrodeposited Co, whose
shape is exactly that of the pores—slightly conical. When reaching the top part of
the resist, the growth is not conﬁned by the geometry and their material overgrows
the resist, growing from the top part of the columns, forming ‘nano-mushrooms’
(ﬁgure 11.10(b)). This example clearly shows the two main attributes that make
electrodeposition so well suited for the growth of 3D materials: the need for
conductive substrates and conformal growth.
11.6.2 Electrodeposition of nanowires
In the above-described combination of electrodeposition and lithography, there are
no limitations in the aspect ratio of the structures [138] and, in fact, the LIGA
(lithographie, galvanoformung and abformung) technique uses electrodeposition
through molds prepared by x-ray lithography to fabricate 3D-structures with a very
large aspect ratio [139]. However, the most extended used of template-assisted
electrodeposition has been the synthesis of nanowires using nanoporous templates as
masks [140]. Among the different possible options that can be used as masks, two
stand out: etched ion-track polymeric nanoporous membranes [141] and anodized
aluminium oxide self-organized templates [142].
The etched ion-track ones are normally made of polymers (polymide, polycar-
bonate…) and their fabrication involves the irradiation of the polymers with
energetic heavy ions, forming damage areas called ion tracks, and chemical etching
Figure 11.10. Electrodeposition for the synthesis of patterned and 3D samples: an introductory example (a) Co
nanowires grown through nanoholes prepared in lithographic resist by means of e-beam lithography. The
material grows from the conductive substrate ﬁlling the holes. (b) When the material reaches the top part of the
resist, the growth is not limited by the mask, starting 3D growth from the top of the column.
Nanofabrication
11-21

[Page 389]
to selectively dissolve the ion-track to form channels. The resulting channels are
oriented parallel in the direction perpendicular to the surface [143]. The density of
pores is quite low, and they are randomly distributed (see ﬁgure 11.11(a)). The
control over the irradiation and etching conditions enables the production of
channels with different geometries and sizes. Although these templates are very
versatile, their fabrication process requires the use of large accelerator facilities, like
GSI in Germany, Brookhaven in the USA or Lanzhou in China, which are normally
not available for most researchers. Therefore, most examples in the literature using
polymeric templates used commercial ones, that can be purchased with pores
ranging from 10 to 100 nm diameter.
Alumina nanoporous templates can also be purchased from several companies,
but this kind of template can be easily prepared by electrochemical anodization of
Al, which provides a low cost and highly scalable technique to produce nanoporous
templates for the synthesis of nanowires [144]. In this case, it is possible to obtain a
highly ordered hexagonal array of parallel pores, as shown in the top view and cross
section images of a membrane in ﬁgure 11.11(b). The Fourier transformation of the
image (inset of the ﬁgure) clearly reveals the high order of the template. The pore
diameter and the interpore distance strongly depends on the anodization conditions
(applied voltage, electrolyte and temperature). Therefore, by controlling these
parameters, it is possible to tailor the templates, obtaining nanoporous with
Figure 11.11. Electrodeposition of nanowires, (a) polycarbonate template and (b) alumina template. The
difference between randomly oriented pores in polycarbonate templates and ordered in alumina templates is
clearly observed. (c) Alumina templates with different diameter. (d) Schematics of the electrochemical cell for
the growth of nanowires. (e) Evolution of current with time during the growth of nanowires. Dashed lines
divide the curve in three different regimes: (i) nucleation, (ii) steady sate growth and (iii) saturation.
Nanofabrication
11-22

[Page 390]
diameters ranging from hundreds, down to a few nanometers (ﬁgure 11.11(c)) [145].
Atomic force microscopy nanoindentations in the Al before anodization [146], as
well as lithography [147] can be used to deﬁne the symmetry and the lattice
parameter of the ordered arrays.
Once the templates are prepared, the procedure for the synthesis of nanowires is
the same in all cases. Before electrodeposition, a conductive layer, typically a gold
layer, should be evaporated, via thermal evaporation or sputtering, on one side of
the membrane. This conductive layer will act as cathode or working electrode in the
electrodeposition of nanowires, and its thickness should be thick enough to
guarantee good electrical conductivity in the whole template area. The nanowires
are normally grown under potentiostatic conditions, i.e. applying a constant
potential between the working electrode and the reference one, using an electro-
chemical cell similar to the one shown in ﬁgure 11.11(c). This control is normally
preferred when growing nanostructures, because it is almost independent of the
substrate conductive area, which is difﬁcult to know and control in template-assisted
deposition using a nanoporous template.
The evolution of the electrical current ﬂowing through the electrochemical cell can
be used to monitor the growth. Figure 11.11(e) shows a standard current-versus-time
transient state, measured during the growth of nanowires under potentiostatic
deposition. In this case, the curve corresponds to the growth of Bi in a polycarbonate
template. Three different regions can be distinguished in the curve, corresponding to
three different growth regimes. Firstly, there is a drop of the current due to nucleation
of the material onto the conductive layer. Immediately after the nucleation process,
there is a steady regime due to the constant growth rate while the channels are being
ﬁlled, since the active surface is constant. Finally, when the channels are completely
ﬁlled, the material begins to grow over the surface of the template, connecting the
different pores, similarly to the example of the nano-mushrooms in ﬁgure 11.10. When
targeting the fabrication of nanowires, the growth is stopped in the second regime.
Therefore, by template-assisted deposition, it is possible to grow metallic nano-
wires with independent control over diameter (ﬁxed by the pore diameter of the
template), length (controlled by the electrodeposition time) and composition and
crystal quality (controlled by the composition of the electrolyte and the growth
potential). It is important to have in mind that electrochemical deposition is not a
stoichiometric technique, there is therefore not a general relationship between the
ratio of electroactive ions in the electrolyte and the one of the different elements in
the growth material. In particular, in the case of magnetic alloys formed by metals
from the iron-group (Fe, Co, Ni), alloys that are particularly important in magnet-
ism, the so-called anomalous co-deposition should be considered. In this effect, taking
place when plating an alloy of two or more metals belonging to this group, the less
noble of the metals deposits preferentially to the more noble one [148]. This depends
on the electrochemical parameters, as well as on the geometry of the template [149],
making it essential to calibrate the composition under similar electrochemical
conditions, before each growth experiment.
Electrodeposited nanowires can be studied and/or used inside the template, but
also released from the template by etching the conductive layer and dissolving the
Nanofabrication
11-23

[Page 391]
template using the appropriate organic (for the polycarbonate) or inorganic (for the
alumina) chemical. When released from the substrate, their individual magnetic
properties can be studied by means of a range of microcopy techniques, such as
magnetic force microscopy [150], transmission electron microscopy [151] and
synchrotron radiation-based techniques [40, 42]. They can also be contacted by
means of focused-ion-beam induced deposition [152], e-beam lithography [153, 154],
or laser lithography [132], to study their magnetotransport properties or other
experiments involving the application of electrical currents. It is also possible to
prepare a suspension of nanowires in different solvents, functionalized them and
used in biomedical applications [155].
In recent years, there have been some proposals to create a freestanding
tridimensional structure formed by vertical nanowires released from the template.
For this purpose, the conductive layer used as working electrode should be thickened
either before or after the electrodeposition of the nanowires, but before dissolving
the template to release them. Although the efforts are focused, up to now, in energy
[156] and neurobiology [157] applications, there are emerging applications in the
ﬁeld of magnetism that will be developed in the near future.
There is a nice video description of the full procedure to fabricate nanowires,
including the formation of the porous alumina membrane that is used as the template,
the electrodeposition of the nanowires inside the template and the release of the
nanowires into a solution in a recent publication of Jürgen Kosel’s group [158].
11.6.3 Nanostructured multilayered nanowires
In the 1980s, different groups developed a method for the electrodeposition of metallic
multilayers from a single electrolyte, with control of the layer thickness down to tens
of nanometers. To illustrate how this method works, let us consider as an example the
electrodeposition of a FM/Cu multilayer, where FM is a ferromagnetic material with
reduction potential more negative that Cu. Therefore, there is a potential A at which
only Cu is deposited and a potential B at which both, Cu and FM are deposited,
forming an alloy. Pulsing the potential between A and B (pulse plating deposition), it
is possible to grow a multilayer ﬁlm consisting of alternating layers of pure Cu and a
FM–Cu alloy. By using a very low concentration of Cu2+ ions in the electrolyte, the
amount of Cu in the alloy will be small, with the magnetic properties of the FM–Cu
being very similar to those for the pure FM. Clearly, this method presents some
limitations regarding the composition of the different layers and, indeed, in the case of
a magnetic material is mostly limited to the electrodeposition of FM/Cu multilayers
were FM is an alloy of Fe, Ni and Co. Nevertheless, it allowed one to prepare
structures with high interfacial quality, showing interfacial effects normally limited to
multilayers grown by physical vapour deposition techniques, such as GMR [159],
enabling, e.g. the creation of spin-valves [160].
The combination of template-assisted growth using nanoporous templates and
pulse plating deposition allows the preparation of multilayered nanowires, consist-
ing in a stack of disks or cylinders of alternating composition along the wire axis.
This strategy allows, therefore, synthesizing materials with submicrometric structure
Nanofabrication
11-24

[Page 392]
in the three dimensions: laterally through the use of the nanoporous templates—
where the size is controlled by pore diameter—and along the growth direction by
pulse-plating electrodeposition—where the composition and length of the different
segments or layers is controlled by the electrochemical parameters and time [161].
This approach allows for a huge control over the magnetic properties (coercivity,
remanence, anisotropy) of the grown structures [162–164], even obtaining super-
paramagnetic nanowires [165] or nanowires showing magnetization reversal ratchet
effects [166].
The above-described pulse plating procedure is limited to the fabrication of
alternating ferromagnetic and non-magnetic layers along the nanowire, due to
limitation imposed by anomalous co-deposition, which makes it impossible to grow
pure Co, Ni or Fe from an electrolyte containing different ions. A different approach
to prepare all-magnetic multilayers consists of using a dual-bath technique, based on
changing the electrolyte after the growth of each layer [150, 167] This technique
opens more possibilities for lateral nanostructuration, but is slow and complex as the
number of layers in the stack increases. Recently, a novel approach has been
reported that takes into account the dependence of anomalous co-deposition on
growth potential, to produce multilayers formed by all-ferromagnetic layers using
single-bath pulse-plating electrodeposition [168]. We hereafter describe this proce-
dure in detail as an example of the preparation of multisegmented nanowires, in
particular Fe20Ni80/Fe80Ni20 nanowires.
Figure 11.12(a) shows the large variation of the Fe content in a Ni–Fe alloy when
varying the growth potential for electrolytes with different a Fe2+/Ni2+. This
variation can be tuned by changing the Fe2+/Ni2+ ratio in the electrolyte. In
particular, for a Fe2+/Ni2+ ratio of 2%, it is possible to have a variation from
Fe20Ni80 (for a growth potential of −1.5 V) to Fe80Ni20 (for a growth potential of
−1.0 V), in the case of nanowires of 200 nm of diameter. Now, we can apply a pulsed
potential
between
these
two
values
to
grow
multisegmented
nanowires.
Figure 11.12(c) shows the temporal evolution of the cathodic current during the
electrodeposition. Varying the temporal duration of each pulse, it is possible to
control the length of each segment. In this example, equispaced local changes in
composition were introduced, as shown in ﬁgure 11.12(b). The accurate control that
can be achieved for nanostructuring of nanowires using this method is revealed by
transmission electron microscopy images (ﬁgure 11.12(c)).
Interestingly, the same idea of pulse plating deposition can be also applied for
nanostructuring alumina templates. It was mentioned before that the diameter of
porous in alumina templates depends, among other parameters, on the anodization
voltage. Therefore, pulsing the voltage between two different values makes it
possible to obtain a controlled modulation, in terms of pore diameter and length
of the different segments of the pore [169]. These modulated templates can be used as
templates for electrodeposition to fabricate nanowires with modulation in the
diameter along their axis [170]. Furthermore, the different steps of anodization
can be also accompanied by wet etching and atomic layer deposition processes, in
order to widen or shrink the pores, respectively. This provides a larger versatility in
terms of control of diameter and length of the different segments [128].
Nanofabrication
11-25

[Page 393]
11.6.4 Electrodeposition of complex structures
In the last few years, exciting perspectives for the study of 3D nanomagnetism [4] and
new opportunities to apply magnetic nanostructures in different areas [146, 171–173]
are triggering the search for novel methods for the synthesis of more complex
magnetic structures. In this context, electrodeposition has been also employed for
the advanced synthesis of 3D structures using different approaches, all of them
based on the two main properties mentioned before: the need for conductive
substrates and conformal growth characteristics that enable the coating of complex
3D conductive structures [174].
For most biomedical and environmental applications, it is important to protect
the magnetic material with a functionalizable coating. For magnetic applications, a
coating can provide the material with additional contributions to magnetic aniso-
tropy. Therefore, in addition to nanostructuration along the axis of the nanowire, it
is important to tune the composition along the radial direction, making possible the
growth of 3D magnetic core–shell structures, using electrodeposition in combination
with other techniques. The easiest approaches consist of the growth of metallic
nanowires, which are partially oxidized to form core–shell structures after releasing
the nanowires from the template [175]. This technique is easy to implement but it is
difﬁcult to control the composition and thickness of the different layers. The second
approach is the so-called widening method [176]. In this method, a material is ﬁrst
electrodeposited into the porous of a template following the standard form. Then,
the template is immersed in a solution that partially removes the nanoporous walls,
leaving an annular gap around the nanowires. A second material is subsequently
electrodeposited into the gap forming a core–shell nanostructured array. Finally, the
third reported method to prepare core–shell nanostructures is again a modiﬁcation
Figure 11.12. Electrodeposition of multisegmented nanowires. (a) Variation of the Fe content as a function of
the concentration of Fe2+ ions in the electrolyte in the electrodeposition of Fe–Ni alloys. (b) Evolution of the
current with time during nanowire growth and (c) transmission electron microscopy images of the grown
nanowires in which the different periodic segments can be observed.
Nanofabrication
11-26

[Page 394]
of the template-assisted method for the synthesis of nanowires: once the template is
prepared, the inner part of the pore is covered by an insulating material using sol–gel
[177, 178], proceeding with the growth afterwards, ﬁlling the pores by electro-
chemical deposition. For applications involving the release of the nanowires, it is
important to select a material for the coating that is not etched by the chemicals and
solvent used to remove the template.
Different templates with more complex shapes can be prepared and ﬁlled with
magnetic materials using electrochemical deposition. Recently, it has also been
proposed that cylindrical nanoporous alumina membranes can be formed by
anodization of Al wires, a geometry that can be used for the synthesis of radially
distributed nanowire arrays [179, 180]. Different approaches have been also
proposed to produce nanostructured templates with a tridimensional structure of
nanopores that can be ﬁlled by electrochemical deposition. Two-photon lithography
can be used to deﬁne complex tridimensional nanopatterns in polymers [112].
Interconnected nanowire networks have been fabricated by ion-track-etching of a
polycarbonate template by using a double ion irradiation with different incidence
angles [181]. Finally, the anodization process can be also modiﬁed to produce
tridimensional networks of perpendicularly arranged nanoporous in templates [182],
templates that can be ﬁlled by magnetic materials, allowing the tailoring of the
magnetic anisotropy [168].
To sum up, electrodeposition is a very suitable technique for the growth of
tridimensional materials providing that an appropriate template is available. The
development of new nanofabrication tools in the present and future will allow, in
combination with electrodeposition, the manufacture of new magnetic materials,
with new opportunities for the study of magnetism in 3D systems, opening the door
for new applications.
11.7 End-of-chapter summary
• FEBID is a versatile nanopatterning technique that can achieve cutting-edge
volumetric resolutions as well as very high nanostructure positioning
accuracy.
• A large number of interactions take place between the electron beam, the
substrate and the precursor during the FEBID process.
• Numerous modelling and experimental tools enable an in-depth understand-
ing of these interactions.
• Automatic patterning algorithms with standardised calibration protocols
have been developed to enable a wide range of users to use FEBID for
nanoscale 3D printing.
• FEBID may be combined with other material deposition techniques in order
to access materials for which a precursor gas is not available.
• Two-photon lithography can be used, in combination with RF magnetron
sputtering, to fabricate a 3D magnetic nanostructure such as an artiﬁcial
Buckyball.
Nanofabrication
11-27

[Page 395]
• Element-speciﬁc phase tomography is a powerful technique to characterize
3D nanostructures with a spatial resolution of 25 nm. In the case of the
buckyball, this method revealed that the Co layer was not homogeneously
distributed over the 3D surface, and that it was in fact oxidized, thus
highlighting the challenges of fabricating 3D magnetic nanostructures using
3D fabrication hybrid methods.
• Electrodeposition is a growth technique for the synthesis of materials, mostly
metallic. Electrodeposited material can only grow on the conductive parts of
the substrates. This, together with its ability for conformal growth, makes
electrodeposition very suitable for the growth of patterned thin ﬁlms and 3D
structures.
• Electrodeposition has been extensively used in combination with nanoporous
template to produce nanowires with accurate control of diameter (down to a
few nanometers), length (up to hundreds of micron) and composition. These
nanowires can be also nanostructured in diameter and composition along the
axial or radial directions, to produce more complex tridimensional structures.
• The templates can be totally removed to study individual nanowires. It is also
possible to prepare suspensions of nanowires in different solvents, and also
create a freestanding tridimensional structure of vertical nanowires released
from the template.
• The use of more complex templates prepared by different techniques
(anodization of 3D networks, ion-track etching with double irradiation,
FEBID, two-photon lithography…) leads to the synthesis of more complex
3D magnetic structures.
11.8 Student’s corner
Further reading:
• Reviews: 3D nanomagnetism [4–6], magnetic nanostructures grown by
FEBID [59, 102], 3D FEBID nanoprinting [183], additive manufacturing
of metallic micro- and nanostructures [13], two-photon lithography methods
for 3D nanomagnetism [112].
• Modelling: CASINO [96], GIS [100], ﬁnite difference simulation [66], con-
tinuum models [64], FEBID frequency framework and maps [65].
• FEBID patterning algorithms: nanowire networks [79, 89], layer-by-layer [80].
• 3D magnetic hybrid systems: scaffold-based nanopatterning [97].
Exercises and further work:
• Use CASINO [96] to model the electron distribution occurring in different
materials and substrate geometries at different beam energies.
• Estimate the angle that the GIS forms with respect to the electron column in
your FEBID microscope using a ruler and basic geometry and consult the
corresponding gas distribution in [100].
Nanofabrication
11-28

[Page 396]
• Measure the vertical distance between your substrate and the GIS by slowly
rising the substrate until it touches the GIS while imaging with the SEM. (Do
this very carefully with an experienced user if it is the ﬁrst time you try!!)
• Calculate using basic geometry if the axis of the GIS is really pointing to
where the beam hits the substrate for your standard growth conditions.
(Remember this is a 3D problem)
• Familiarize yourself with the table of experimental parameters that should
always be recorded in an experiment in order to use the fundamental
frequency framework ([65] and supplementary material therein).
• Find out where the above parameters are displayed in your electron micro-
scope and familiarize yourself with their continuous monitoring during
experimental sessions and the use of the calculator provided in [65] supple-
mentary material.
• Understand why it is challenging to grow freestanding horizontal elements,
and familiarize yourself with the use of substrate tilting described in [97].
• Search bibliography dedicated to the advanced characterisation of 3D mag-
netic nanostructures using electron and x-ray microscopy techniques. Identify
the main challenges related to a 3D geometry when using these methods.
• Regarding the fabrication of 3D magnetic systems using 3D scaffolds and
deposition of magnetic thin ﬁlms, can you think of why a non-homogeneous
coating might be useful, and why it might not?
• For this method, can you think of how one might improve the fabrication
method and what techniques could be suitable?
• What are the two main characteristics of the electrodeposition technique that
make it so versatile for the synthesis of 3D structures?
• How can you combine two-photon lithography or FEBID with electro-
deposition to prepare 3D structures?
• What kind of 3D magnetic nanostructure would you like to make, and how
would you make it?
