Chapter 4 Focused electron beam induced deposition
• Which growth variables have an inﬂuence on the lateral resolution of FEBID,
and why?
In general, higher lateral resolutions are obtained by playing with numerous
growth variables:
○Higher acceleration voltage provides smaller probe size, thus potentially
higher spatial resolution.
○Lower beam current is associated to smaller spot size, thus higher
spatial resolution.
○Short growth times minimize the effect of drift and instabilities,
contributing to higher spatial resolution.
○Conducting substrates minimize unwanted charging effects, which
degrade the resolution.
○High density substrates induce a more localized emission of secondary
electrons, thus a smaller deposition area.
○If very thin substrates (such as membranes) are used, the interaction
volume will be smaller and the FEBID resolution will be better.
• Which measures would you take to reduce the halo around FEBID deposits?
There are two main strategies that minimize the halo produced around the
FEBID deposits, which are related to using the smallest-possible electron
beam spot and reducing the interaction volume. The electron beam size can
be reduced by increasing the acceleration voltage (minimizing the objective
lens aberrations) and reducing the beam current. The interaction volume can
be reduced independently of the beam properties by using high density
(Z number) substrates or very thin substrates.
• Are insulating substrates suitable for FEBID growth?
Nanofabrication
A-8

[Page 440]
In general, insulating substrates are not suitable for FEBID due to the
appearance of detrimental charging effects around the growth area. However,
there are different approaches that can be taken to enable a satisfactory
FEBID growth on insulating substrates. For instance, the fabrication of
metal pads around the growth area for charge evacuation, or the use of
(voltage biased) metallic masks such as the ARAGON-Chip discussed in
section 4.5 can be used to dissipate charge. In this case, a metallic mask
allows the evacuation of the charge generated during the FEBID growth
(Pablo-Navarro J et al 2019 Diameter modulation of 3D nanostructures in
focused electron beam induced deposition using local electric ﬁelds and beam
defocus Nanotechnology 30 505302). The use of lower voltage and beam
currents could also be beneﬁcial for insulating or poorly-conducting
substrates.
• Growth rate is one of the key issues of FEBID. For a given electron dose, which
growth parameters could you modify to increase the growth rate?
The growth rate is the result of numerous factors inﬂuencing the decom-
position and growth processes in FEBID, and not all of them are independ-
ent. For a given electron dose, the growth rate will increase by:
○Decreasing the acceleration voltage, given that the amount of secondary
electrons reaching the surface of the sample will be higher, thus being
more efﬁcient to decompose the precursor molecules (De Teresa J M
et al 2009 Origin of the difference in the resistivity of as-grown focused-
ion- and focused-electron-beam-induced Pt nanodeposits J. Nanomater.
2009 936863).
○Decreasing the beam current, as there will be less heating on the growth
area, avoiding the decrease of the precursor residence time (Mutanga E
et al 2019 Impact of electron-beam heating during 3D nanoprinting
ACS Nano. 13 5198–213).
○Increasing the precursor gas ﬂux, in case we are in the precursor-limited
regime in which the deposit does not grow faster for a lack of available
precursor molecules (Pablo-Navarro J et al 2017 Tuning shape, com-
position and magnetization of 3D cobalt nanowires grown by focused
electron beam induced deposition (FEBID) J. Phys. D: Appl. Phys. 50
18LT01).
○Increasing the refresh time, so the depleted precursor molecules have
longer time to be replenished.
○Decreasing the dwell time to values lower than the effective residence
time of the precursor gas molecules, so the molecule depletion is avoided
(Serrano-Ramón L et al 2011 Ultra-small ferromagnetic nanostructures
grown by focused electron beam-induced deposition ACS Nano 5 7781).
○Decreasing the distance from the GIS nozzle to the area of interest on
the substrate. This should result in a higher growth rate if we are in the
precursor-limited regime because it would increase the number of
available precursor molecules.
Nanofabrication
A-9

[Page 441]
○Increasing the density of the material (higher average Z), so a higher
number of secondary and backscattered electrons reach the surface of
the sample and can decompose the precursor.
○Decreasing the temperature of the substrate to increase the precursor
residence time (Huth M et al 2020 Temperature-dependent growth
characteristics of Nb- and CoFe-based nanostructures by direct-write
using focused electron beam-induced deposition Micromachines 11 28).
Chapter 5 Focused ion beam induced processing
• Compare the advantages and disadvantages of FEBID and FIBID in these
cases: growth of electrical contacts to a nano-object, growth of a 3D magnetic
nanostructure, and growth of a protective layer on a given material.
Both FIBID and FEBID are grounded on the same fundamental principle—a
focused beam of charged particles, either ions or electrons, induces the
decomposition of a gaseous precursor adsorbed on the surface of a substrate,
mostly via the emission of SEs by the latter. Due to the mass and charge
difference between the two types of charged particles, the processes involved
in the ion–matter interaction are different. Generally speaking, FIBID is
faster than FEBID (due to a greater SE yield induced by the impinging ions
when compared to the electrons), and also yields a higher metallic content
than FEBID. However, when using FIBID, ion implantation occurs, which
can change the properties of the substrate. Secondly, ion bombardment
induces substrate amorphization and defect creation, which can be detrimen-
tal in certain cases. Moreover, in FIBID a competition between growth and
milling is always present (which can be to a certain extent controlled by
adjusting the dose and precursor ﬂux), while in FEBID, milling is not
relevant.
Hence:
○In the case of electrical contacts, FIBID should be ﬁrst considered, for it
will yield metallic contacts when used with the appropriate precursor
and in short processing time. However, the sensitivity of the substrate
and of the object that is being contacted to ion irradiation must be
considered—will local amorphization of the sample (below the point of
contact placement and on its vicinity) signiﬁcantly alter the electrical
properties of the object? If so, (for example, in the case of thin samples)
then FEBID should also be considered, although the contacts may be
less metallic and therefore less conductive (Hernández-Ramírez F et al
2006 Characterization of metal-oxide nanosensors fabricated with
focused ion beam (FIB) Sens. Actuators B 118 198–203).
○Similar arguments hold for the growth of a 3D magnetic nanostructure.
Since a signiﬁcant amount of the focused beam ions will get implanted,
they may play a role on the overall magnetic properties of the sample.
Advantages of using FEBID for this application are the high resolution
and, in general, the high purity and magnetization obtained (Fernández-
Nanofabrication
A-10

[Page 442]
Pacheco A et al 2009 Magnetotransport properties of high-quality cobalt
nanowires grown by focused-electron-beam-induced deposition J. Phys. D
Appl. Phys. 42 055005). On the other hand, FIBID will be a faster growth
technique but milling effects can be detrimental (Sanz-Martín C et al 2019
High volume-per-dose and low resistivity of cobalt nanowires grown by
Ga+ focused ion beam induced deposition Nanomaterials 9 1715).
○In order to grow a protective layer over a material, FEBID is preferred
to FIBID if amorphization, implantation and defect creation are to be
avoided. However, growing large, thick layers using FEBID can take
unreasonably long processing times. A common procedure in protective
deposits for TEM sample preparation and for cross-sectional SEM
imaging is to make use of both techniques to grow such layers (FEBID
and FIBID can be easily performed in FIB/SEM instruments)—ﬁrst, a
relatively thin protective layer is deposited via FEBID on top of the
sample, and then a second thicker layer is grown by FIBID on top of the
ﬁrst, hence only damaging the (irrelevant for further experiment
purposes) FEBID-grown layer.
• Compare the advantages and disadvantages of FIB and FEBIE for optical mask
repair.
In optical mask repair, it is important not to modify the optical properties of
the underlying substrate of the mask, which is better accomplished using
FEBIE instead of Ga+-FIB given that Ga+ implantation produces optical
changes. However, the use of light-ions in gas ﬁeld FIB sources could change
this in future (Yasaka A et al 2016 Nanoscale imaging, material removal and
deposition for fabrication of cutting-edge semiconductor devices ion-beam-
based photomask defect repair technology Hitachi Rev. 65 71–75).
• Compare the advantages and disadvantages for compositional analysis of EDX
using an SEM and SIMS using an HIM.
While both SIMS and EDX are compositional characterization techniques,
their working principles, scopes and purpose are vastly different. EDX is
performed by measuring the x-rays that the sample emits when it is irradiated
by an SEM. Different elements yield x-rays of different energies, which
manifest themselves in the form of peaks in a spectrum, whose extent in terms
of energy is capped by the energy of the beam (no x-rays with higher energy
than that provided with the beam can be emitted). Applying higher voltages
to extend the spectrum results in an enhanced electron penetration (bigger
interaction volume). On the other hand, SIMS is based on the physical
sputtering of the surface of a sample, with the ejected atoms being mass
ﬁltered to separate the different constituents.
Therefore:
○EDX is simpler. It is usually readily available in most characterization
facilities, typically implemented as an additional module in instruments
that naturally use an SEM, and it is relatively easy and fast to perform.
SIMS, on the other hand, in most cases requires a dedicated instrument
Nanofabrication
A-11

[Page 443]
(although it can also be implemented in HIMs), and the procedure is
more complex.
○EDX should, generally speaking, be thought of as a bulk technique.
While low SEM energies allow only for limited spectra, high acceler-
ation voltages result in more area being probed. In addition, neighbor-
ing peaks arising from different elements may overlap, blurring the
conclusions. If EDX is performed in a TEM instrument, however, both,
its resolution and ﬁdelity, are enhanced.
○SIMS is, on the other hand, a surface technique, which enables for high-
resolution discerning of chemical elements constituting the surface of
the sample. It can also be performed in dynamic mode, tracing a depth
proﬁle of the sample.
○EDX is reasonably non-invasive (although it does yield a relatively high
electron dose on the sample), while SIMS is destructive, since it physi-
cally sputters the surface that is being analyzed.
○SIMS is, generally, more precise. The spatial resolution of an HIM FIB
is greater than that of an SEM, and the mass separation procedure
allows for more ﬁdelity in element separation than that based on x-ray
spectra analysis. It is even possible to obtain grain-boundary composi-
tions with good accuracy.
Chapter 6 Scanning probe lithography
• Which of the following SPL methods do pattern a resist in the ﬁrst nanolitho-
graphic step?
A. Oxidation SPL.
B. Thermal SPL.
C. Bias SPL.
D. Dip-pen nanolithography.
• Select from the list the factors that inﬂuence the feature size in oxidation SPL
A. The tip’s radius.
B. The temperature of the tip.
C. The applied voltage.
D. The thickness of the resist.
• What are the functionalities of the oxide structures fabricated by o-SPL ?
The oxide nanostructures might be used as dielectric barriers or masks for an
etching process or templates.
• (a) Describe thermal scanning probe lithography. (b) Mention the dominant
factors that control the feature size and (c) its order of magnitude (1, 10, 100 or
1000 nm ?).
(a) Thermal SPL uses a hot tip to pattern a resist. The tip induces the local
melting and removal of the resist. The pattern is used as a mask in an
etching process.
(b) Tip’s size, tip’s temperature and resist properties.
Nanofabrication
A-12

[Page 444]
(c) 10 nm.
• Dip-pen nanolithography is based on the deposition of diffusive inks on a
surface. Describe the main steps of the transport process of diffusive inks.
The process might be divided in three main steps: dissolution of the ink
molecules in the water, motion of the ink molecules in the water meniscus
formed between the tip and the surface, and assembly of the ink molecules on
the surface.
• Provide an estimation of the electric ﬁeld generated by applying 10 V for a tip
that is placed 1 nm above a gold surface. Is this ﬁeld larger or smaller than the
average electric ﬁelds applied in electron beam lithography?
The mean electric ﬁeld without considering the tip’s ﬁnite geometry is of 10 GV
m−1. That value is extremely high. In EBL, very high voltages are applied to
emit and accelerate the electrons. Typical voltages are in the 1–30 kV range.
Those voltages are applied over ~1–30 cm distances. The equivalent electric
ﬁeld (10 kV applied over 1 cm and without including geometry factors) is of 1
MV m−1, this is, two-to-three orders of magnitude smaller than the ﬁelds that
might be generated in SPL. This example illustrates how a small voltage might
generate an extremely high electric ﬁeld.
• Design a micro-optical tag made of multilayered MoS2 that alternates blue,
green and yellow regions. The color of MoS2 ﬂakes is related to the number of
monolayers (see scheme below). Each optical region of the tag is a square of 800
nm × 800 nm. A triangular MoS2 ﬂake of 10 μm (side length) and 30
monolayers in thickness is deposited on a Si substrate (see below). It is the
raw material for the tag. Hint: The number of MoS2 monolayers oxidized per
unit of time in o-SPL is known. The MoS2 oxides generated in o-SPL are
soluble in water (see ﬁgure 6.2(c)).
The process involves three steps. First, the initial ﬂake is patterned by o-SPL
according to the ﬁgure. The o-SPL parameters are chosen to oxidize
completely the ﬂake (30 mL). Second, the optical regions are patterned
according to the ﬁgure. The numbers in parenthesis indicate the thickness of
Nanofabrication
A-13

[Page 445]
the patterned oxides in terms of monolayers. Third, the sample is immersed in
water to remove the oxidized regions.
Chapter 7 Soft thermal nanoimprint and hybrid processes to produce
complex structures
• How is it possible to use PDMS as mold for T-NIL of nanostructures on PC
ﬁlms if the elastic modulus of the PC is three orders of magnitude higher?
The thermal NIL process is performed at temperatures well above the
polymer’s Tg, in practice this temperature is set about 50 °C–90 °C above
Tg so that the polymer reaches the viscous ﬂow state. At this high temper-
ature, the elastic modulus and yield strength decrease several orders of
magnitude and as a result, the compressive stress on the mold will be much
reduced avoiding mold deformation while the heated polymer chains ﬂow
and ﬁll the cavities of the mold. In the particular case of PC, the glass
transition temperature is about 150 °C and the elastic modulus has a value of
2.2 GPa at 25 °C. By raising the temperature up to 170 °C, the elastic
modulus already decreases three orders of magnitude, down to 2 MPa, which
is comparable to that of the PDMS.
• During T-NIL of HAR nanostructures on PMMA it is observed that upon
demolding a large number of nanopillars are affected by ripping. What could be
done to improve the replica quality in this case?
Ripping is a consequence of stress concentration at the base of high aspect
ratio nanostructures during the demolding step. The demolding defects can be
minimized by decreasing the surface energy of the mold and by adjusting the
demolding temperature. Surface energy can be tuned within the range of 10–
20 mJ m−2 by using silane-based anti-sticking layers. In the case of PMMA, a
demolding temperature of 70 °C was observed to be optimum to avoid
ripping by inducing the appropriate levels of softness and elasticity, while
reducing friction and adhesion forces.
• During the fabrication of nanopillars by T-NIL on a PS ﬁlm, it is observed that a
large number of pillars are taller than expected according to the mold dimensions,
what could be the reason? How can you select the demolding temperature?
Nanofabrication
A-14

[Page 446]
The most plausible reason to cause the stretching of nanopillars is demolding
at excessively high temperature. At temperatures close to the glass transition
of the polymer (110 °C for PS), the elastic modulus and mechanical strength is
still reduced which allows for stretching of soft material due to the strong
demolding forces that upon cooling, results in permanent plastic deformation
of the nanostructures. The optimum demolding temperature is achieved when
the adhesive force is decreased and the friction force is at its lowest. The
optimum demolding temperature should be somewhere slightly below the Tg
and in practice, it is experimentally found for each imprinting task.
• How does the aspect ratio inﬂuence the detachment force during demolding of
nanoimprinted structures? What is the advantage of using a ﬂexible mold during
the demolding step of HAR nanostructures?
The demolding process consists in the separation of the mold from the
imprinted structures. The required demolding force depends, among other
factors, on the adhesion force between the mold and the polymer and the
friction force of the structures on the side walls roughness as they are being
released. These forces are determined by the contact area which depends
directly on the geometrical parameters of the mold, the aspect ratio and
density of the structures. A ﬂexible mold allows performing a sequential
action of peeling of the mold during the demolding step, resulting in a smaller
area, thus a lower demolding force compared to a rigid mold. As a result, the
number of defects are reduced improving overall the replica quality.
• Which are the main factors related to the pattern collapse of HAR?
Principally, the limitation to the aspect ratio of a nanostructure depends on its
mechanical properties (Young’s modulus and Poisson’s ratio), which deter-
mine the effective stiffness at the nanoscopic dimensions. In addition, there
are two main factors which affect the nanofeature’s stability: surface energy
and pattern density. HAR nanostructures may suffer collapse by adhesion
forces between pillars (lateral collapse) or between pillars and substrate
(ground collapse).
• Given a polymer with viscosity = 106 [Pa s], surface tension ~20 mN m−1 and
wetting contact angle of 80°
a. What is the time needed to ﬁll 0.5 × 2 μm pore size cavities by capillary
action?
b. What viscosity should the polymer have to reduce that time to about 7
min?
c. If mold silanization is accomplished in order to facilitate the demolding
process, how is the polymer wetting contact angle expected to be affected?
Use the relations derived from the Lucas-Washburn equation:

• What provides the gecko with its amazing climbing capability?
The gecko’s climbing ability relies on the ﬁbrillar structures on its toe pads.
The pads are covered by densely packed, high aspect ratio hierarchical pillars
called setae, with diameters between 5–10 μm. Each of these setae presents
ﬁbrillar branches at the tip with diameters ranging from 200 up to 500 nm and
aspect ratios between 10 and 25. This hierarchy induces a progressive
lowering of the effective stiffness and increases dramatically the available
contact area. The hierarchical setae can then easily conform and come close
to a surface where van der Waals forces develop and give rise to strong
frictional adhesive forces.
Chapter 8 Stencil lithography

you use sputtering, e-beam evaporation, or thermal evaporation? Why?
In this case we would use a stencil membrane with 100 nm apertures. In order
to stay below 10 nm variation from the nominal value, we would have to

• If these 100 nm structures are supposed to be 200 nm thick, what are the main
issues that we would face, and why?
The main issue that would be encountered is the fact that lateral deposition
would happen on the lateral walls of the stencil membrane, thus progressively
Nanofabrication
A-16

[Page 448]
reducing the aperture dimensions and ending up with the deposition of a
pyramidal structure (triangular cross section) likely thinner than expected.
This is the phenomenon we call clogging.
• How would you pattern with SL a ring of 10 μm in diameter and 200 nm in
width, plus 20 nm thick?
A direct approach would be to do this via dynamic stencil lithography.
Patterning on the membrane a hole of 200 nm in diameter and then drawing a
circle. In the same way as is shown in the support video #3, available at
https://iopscience.iop.org/book/978-0-7503-2608-7. A second option would be
using quasi-dynamic stencil lithography, by fabricating a membrane with two
complementary patterns that together would form a full ring. These comple-
mentary patterns could be, for example, two half-rings. Finally, with stand-
ard SL, ring-like patterns are difﬁcult to perform because you cannot
fabricate a ring-shaped aperture. A solution would be to fabricate a ring-
shaped apertured split into smaller pieces, as the ﬁgure below shows. These
narrow supports for the inner circle should be narrow-enough so that blurring
would eventually join each of the apertures. Typically, they should be below
50 nm to have a conductive layer. With this method, we use one disadvantage
of SL in order to obtain the desired pattern.

Chapter 9 Ice lithography
• What is the electron energy range in an SEM?
Primary electron energy range between 100 eV to 30 keV. Secondary electron
energy range between 1 eV and 30 keV.
• Is it possible to condense CO2 in an SEM using a liquid nitrogen based cryostage?
No, because the vapor pressure of CO2 at 150 K is 6 Torr, which is much
higher than SEM vacuum.
Nanofabrication
A-17

[Page 449]
• What are the reaction products of electrons’ interaction with CO2 ice?
CO (g), CO solid network, oxygen.
• What are the chemical reaction pathways for electrons -ice interactions?
Secondary electrons cause ionization or radicalization of the organic ice. The
reactive chemicals can recombine, cross-link into networks or larger mole-
cules, or the smaller molecule fragments can vaporize.
• Which chemical would you want to work with and why?
Please contact the authors, and we can discuss your ideas.
• Can IL and EBL be used for lithography on 3D structures?
IL produces uniform and conformal thin-ﬁlms on the sample surface with
complex topography. Therefore, it is suitable for performing lithography on
3D structure. On the other hand, because EBL resists are coated by spin-
coating or spray coating, the resist uniformity is not guaranteed on a 3D
structure, and therefore, EBL is less suited for the application in question.
• List the factors that determine the patterning resolution of IL process.
Material chemistry, electron beam parameter (energy, beam spot, etc),
mechanical limitation (stability, positioning, etc), and ice property (thickness,
crystallite, etc).
• What device or structure would you like to build with IL?
All answers are good answers, and some answers can be great answers.
Chapter 10 Magnetic nanopatterning via thermal scanning probe
lithography
• What are the main factors determining the minimum magnetic domain size
which can be patterned with tam-SPL?
There are two main factors to be considered: the lateral extension of the thermal
proﬁle produced by the tip on the sample, and the magnetic properties of the
sample. In the ﬁrst case, the thermal proﬁle produced on the sample depends
both on the probe size (down to a few nm), and on the thermal conductivity of
the substrate. In general, the higher the thermal conductivity of the substrate,
the more conﬁned is the thermal proﬁle in the lateral direction. Concerning the
magnetic properties: the minimum size of the domains that can be stabilized in a
ferromagnetic ﬁlm is comparable to the domain wall width, which in turn is
determined by the micromagnetic properties of the system. Therefore, a small
minimum domain size is obtained by combining highly conﬁned thermal proﬁle
and narrow domain walls.
• What are the factors determining the minimum tip temperature needed for tam-
SPL patterning?
The minimum temperature on the sample needed for patterning should be
higher than the blocking temperature of the exchange bias system. The
temperature set on the heater (on the cantilever) is typically higher, due to the
need to compensate for the thermal dissipation across the sample.
• What are the factors determining the maximum tam-SPL writing speed?
Nanofabrication
A-18

[Page 450]
The main limiting factor is the thermal constant of the heated tip, which is
typically in the order of tens of microseconds. This poses a limit to how fast
the heat can by switched on and off controllably while sweeping the tip on the
sample. Maximum writing speeds around 1 mm s−1 can be reached in
optimized systems.
• What determines the resilience of tam-SPL patterns with respect to time,
magnetic ﬁeld and temperature?
The resilience of tam-SPL patterns arises from the stability of the exchange
bias system. Provided that the blocking temperature is well above room
temperature, the unidirectional anisotropy of the system is not affected by the
external magnetic ﬁeld, and is stable over time. The stability of the system is
lost when approaching the blocking temperature. This allows the system to be
reconﬁgured either by resetting a uniform exchange bias on the whole ﬁlm, or
by performing tam-SPL again.
• Would it be possible to pattern different spin textures in two stacked exchange
bias antiferromagnet / ferromagnet bilayers? If so, how?
In order to pattern effectively two stacked layers via tam-SPL, one should be
able to address each layer independently. In order to do so, either the
blocking temperature of the systems, or the effective temperature reached by
each layer during patterning should be different. Once this condition is
reached, different magnetic domain structures could be stabilized within the
ﬁlms independently.
Chapter 11 Nanofabrication of three-dimensional magnetic structures
• Search bibliography dedicated to the advanced characterisation of 3D magnetic
nanostructures using electron and x-ray microscopy techniques. Identify the
main challenges related to a 3D geometry when using these methods.
For the characterization of 3D magnetic structures, single 2D projections are
not always sufﬁcient to determine the 3D conﬁguration. 3D imaging
techniques such as tomography and laminography provide the opportunity
to map the 3D magnetization (x-rays), or magnetic induction (electrons)
vector ﬁelds. These advanced techniques require sophisticated setups to
enable to measurement of the appropriate data. In addition, advanced data
analysis using sophisticated reconstruction algorithms is required to recover
the 3D vector ﬁeld, which will require signiﬁcant computing power.
• Regarding the fabrication of 3D magnetic systems using 3D scaffolds and
deposition of magnetic thin ﬁlms, can you think of why a non-homogeneous
coating might be useful, and why it might not?
There are interesting uses for both homogeneous and non-homogeneous
coatings, reﬂecting the wide variety of interesting 3D magnetic systems.
Homogeneous coatings are required for the realization of structures such as
magnetic nanotubes, which are predicted to exhibit ultra high domain wall
velocities and magnetochiral effects, whilst inhomogeneous coatings can be
used to fabricate curved surfaces and frustrated magnetic systems. The key is
Nanofabrication
A-19

[Page 451]
the control over the fabrication process, and the ability to fabricate the
designed structure.
• For this method, can you think of how one might improve the fabrication
method and what techniques could be suitable?
This method can be improved by using techniques allowing nanostructuring
(PVD techniques, pulse plating electrodeposition, …). In this way, non-
homogeneous coating can be produced in a more controlled way, expanding
the possible 3D magnetic structures to explore.
• What are the two main characteristics of the electrodeposition technique that
make it so versatile for the synthesis of 3D structures?
As with most chemical techniques, electrodeposition is a conformal growth
technique. In addition, electrodeposition can only take place on metals or
metallized surfaces. Therefore, using a 3D non-conductive template with an
opening to a conductive contact, electrodeposition is going to proceed from
the contact, fully ﬁll in the template without shadows.
• How can you combine two-photon lithography or FEBID with electrodeposition
to prepare 3D structures?
We could use either of the two 3D nanofabrication techniques to create a
template for a subsequent electrodeposition process. For this, you can think
about two possible strategies. In the ﬁrst one, 3D insulating nanostructures
with the inverse design as the ﬁnal structure would be created. These would
have openings to a conductive substrate, enabling the electrodeposited
material to ﬁll these holes. In the second strategy, you could create FEBID
metallic nanostructures which would be afterwards covered by a metallic shell
via electrodeposition.
• What kind of three-dimensional magnetic nanostructure would you like to
make, and how would you make it?
Depending on the 3D magnetic structure you want to make, there are
different approaches shown in this chapter. If your desired structure is not
very demanding in terms of the quality of the magnetic materials, or if you
want a pure Co or Fe structure, you can directly write the structure using
FEBID. However, if you need a better control of the magnetic material, you
should combine different techniques. First, you need to create a non-magnetic
scaffold using FIBID of two-photon lithography and, afterwards, coat it or
ﬁll in it with a magnetic material using the electrodeposition or one of the
techniques mentioned throughout the chapter.
Nanofabrication
A-20
